questions.q: In math problems for electrical circuits, which letter stands for current (amperage)?
questions.expl: Amperage (current) is represented by the letter I in circuit math; E is voltage, R is resistance.

questions.q: One coulomb is equal to about how many electrons?
questions.expl: A coulomb is the basic unit of electrical quantity, equal to 6.28 billion-billion (6.28 x 10^18) electrons.

questions.q: Ohm's Law states that current in a circuit is:
options: Directly proportional to voltage and directly proportional to resistance
Directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance
Inversely proportional to voltage and directly proportional to resistance
Independent of both voltage and resistance
questions.short: Directly proportional to voltage, inversely proportional to resistance
questions.expl: Ohm's Law: current is directly proportional to circuit voltage and inversely proportional to resistance. In plain terms, 1 volt pushes 1 amp through 1 ohm.

questions.q: A 12-volt source is connected across a 4-ohm resistor. Using Ohm's Law (I = E / R), how much current flows?
questions.expl: I = E / R = 12 V / 4 ohm = 3 A. This is the core Ohm's Law calculation the exam expects.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.6 (Ohm's Law) / FAA-H-8083-30B p.12-39

questions.q: Electrical power (watts) is found by:
questions.expl: Watts is the basic unit of power, the product of voltage multiplied by current (P = E x I). One watt = one amp under one volt.

questions.q: The RMS (effective) value of a sine wave equals what fraction of the peak value?
questions.expl: RMS (root mean square) is the effective value of AC; it equals 0.707 times the peak value. RMS produces the same heating as an equal DC value.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.8 (Root Mean Square)

questions.q: The frequency of most AC used in aircraft applications is:
questions.expl: Aircraft AC systems commonly run at 400 Hz. Frequency = cycles per second, measured in hertz (Hz).

questions.q: In an AC circuit, capacitance causes current to ___ the voltage (remember 'ICE').
questions.expl: Capacitive reactance causes current to LEAD voltage (ICE: in a Capacitor, I leads E). Inductance does the opposite (ELI).

questions.q: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law states that in any closed circuit the algebraic sum of all voltage drops is:
options: Equal to the source voltage doubled
questions.expl: KVL: the algebraic sum of all voltage drops in any closed circuit equals zero.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.9 (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)

questions.q: A series circuit is best described as a circuit that:
options: Has more than one path for current
Has only one path for current, making current common through all components
questions.short: One path; current common to all components
questions.expl: A series circuit has only one path for current, so the same current flows through every component (connected end-to-end).

questions.q: The two most common faults found when troubleshooting circuits are:
options: Open resistors/contacts and shorts between conductors
Too many resistors and too few capacitors
questions.expl: The most common circuit problems are open resistors or contacts, and shorts between conductors.

questions.q: On a resistor color code, what value does the color GREEN represent?
questions.expl: Green = 5 (mnemonic: Big Bears Romp On Yellow Grass...: Grass = Green = 5).

questions.q: On a resistor's tolerance band, GOLD represents what tolerance?
questions.expl: Gold tolerance band = +/- 5%. Silver = +/- 10%, no color = +/- 20%.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.15 (Color Codes table)

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of the physical factors that affects an inductor's inductance?
options: Number of turns of the coil
The color of the wire insulation
questions.expl: Inductance depends on core cross-section, coil length, number of turns, and core material. Insulation color is irrelevant.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.16 (Inductors / Physical Factors)

questions.q: The time constant of a capacitor is the time needed for the capacitor voltage to reach what percent of the applied voltage?
questions.expl: One time constant = the time for the voltage across a capacitor to advance to 63.2% of the applied voltage.

questions.q: A step-down transformer, compared to a step-up transformer:
options: Has equal turns on both windings
questions.expl: A step-down transformer decreases voltage and increases current; it has more turns on the primary than the secondary.

questions.q: About how much forward voltage (of the correct polarity) does it typically take to make a silicon diode conduct?
questions.expl: On average it takes about 0.7 VDC of correct polarity to forward-bias a diode into conduction.

questions.q: A diode allows current to flow:
questions.expl: A diode is the simplest semiconductor (N-type joined to P-type). It allows current to flow in one direction only and is used to change AC into DC (rectification).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: The primary purpose of a Zener diode is to:
questions.expl: A Zener diode conducts under certain voltage conditions; its primary purpose is to regulate voltage.

questions.q: The binary numbering system operates in which base?
questions.expl: Binary uses only two digits (1 and 0) and operates in Base 2. Octal = Base 8, decimal = Base 10, hex = Base 16.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.27 (Binary Numbering System)

questions.q: An AND logic gate produces a HIGH (1) output only when:
questions.expl: An AND gate has two or more inputs and one output; it outputs 1 only when ALL inputs are 1. (EXCLUSIVE OR needs inputs to differ.)
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.29 (AND Logic Gate)

questions.q: ESD stands for Electrostatic Discharge. The recommended way to protect static-sensitive devices is to:
options: Wear a conductive wrist strap connected to ground
Rub the part with a dry cloth first
Hold the part by its bare pins
questions.short: Wear a grounded conductive wrist strap
questions.expl: ESD control: attach a conductive wrist strap that connects your body to ground, touch only components to be installed/removed, and do not touch bare pins.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.33 & p.45 (ESD / Proper Grounding)

questions.q: 'Clean As You Go' and tool accountability (shadow boards, chit systems) are practices used to prevent:
questions.expl: FOD = Foreign Object Debris/Damage. Clean-as-you-go and tool control (shadow box, tether, chit, sponge count) prevent debris from damaging aircraft.

questions.q: Galvanic corrosion occurs when:
options: A single metal sits in dry air
Two different metals are in contact in the presence of an electrolyte
questions.expl: Galvanic corrosion occurs when different (dissimilar) metals contact each other in the presence of an electrolyte.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: In the American Wire Gage (AWG) standard, as the gage NUMBER gets larger, the wire diameter:
questions.expl: AWG is the wire sizing standard: as gage numbers become larger, wire diameters get smaller. Sizing also considers power loss, voltage drop, and current capacity.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.48 (American Wire Gage)

questions.q: Which instrument is used to find a circuit problem when power is REMOVED from the circuit (it measures resistance)?
questions.expl: An ohmmeter is used with power removed and measures resistance/continuity. A voltmeter and ammeter are used with power on the circuit.

questions.q: Motion around the aircraft's longitudinal axis (nose-to-tail) is called:
questions.expl: Longitudinal axis runs nose to tail; motion about it is roll. Lateral axis = pitch; vertical axis = yaw.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p.53 (Axis of Flight)

questions.q: Which subatomic particle actually carries electricity through a conductor?
questions.expl: Electrons are the negatively-charged particles that orbit the nucleus and are involved in the conduction of electricity. Protons (+) and neutrons (neutral) stay in the nucleus and do NOT conduct.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 5-6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-1

questions.q: A proton carries what charge?
questions.expl: Protons are positively-charged particles in the nucleus. They are NOT involved in conducting electricity.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-1

questions.q: A neutron carries what charge?
questions.expl: Neutrons are neutral subatomic particles inside the nucleus and are not involved in conduction.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 5; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-1

questions.q: What is a 'free electron'?
options: A loosely-bound outer-shell electron that can move from atom to atom
An electron locked tightly in the nucleus
A proton that has lost its charge
questions.short: A loosely-bound outer electron that can travel atom-to-atom
questions.expl: Outermost electrons are loosely held and easily dislodged. Once freed, they travel atom to atom — and that flow IS electric current.

questions.q: Whether a material conducts electricity depends mainly on its number of:
questions.expl: More free electrons available = more current the material can conduct. This is what separates conductors, insulators, and semiconductors.

questions.q: Which group lists good electrical CONDUCTORS?
questions.expl: Conductors have many free electrons. Copper and silver each have one electron in their outer orbit, giving billions of free electrons.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 4; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-3

questions.q: Which material is an INSULATOR?
questions.expl: An insulator is used to prevent the passage of electricity. Insulators (glass, ceramic, plastic, rubber) make almost no free electrons.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 5; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-3

questions.q: Silicon and germanium, in their pure state, are best described as:
questions.expl: Semiconductors fall between conductors and insulators and can be made to act like either. Silicon and germanium are the most common, used in transistors and chips.

questions.q: Static electricity is best described as:
options: Electrons flowing steadily in one direction
An electrical charge built up on a non-conductive surface by friction
questions.short: A charge built up on a non-conductive surface by friction
questions.expl: Static electricity is a buildup of charge (electrons at rest) from rubbing non-conductive materials. There is no current flow, and it serves no useful purpose.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-4

questions.q: In the water-pipe analogy, VOLTAGE is like the:
questions.expl: Voltage = electrical pressure (EMF) that pushes electrons. Current = amount flowing; resistance = friction opposing flow.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-16

questions.q: The VOLT is the basic unit of what?
questions.expl: A volt is the basic unit of electrical pressure — the amount of force required to cause one amp to flow through one ohm of resistance.

questions.q: In math problems, voltage is represented by which letter?
questions.expl: Voltage (EMF) is expressed by the letter E in formulas like E = I × R.

questions.q: The AMPERE is the unit used to express:
questions.expl: The ampere measures current. One ampere = a flow of one coulomb passing a point per second. In math, current is the letter I.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 4; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-17

questions.q: One ampere equals a flow of one coulomb past a point in:
questions.expl: 1 amp = 1 coulomb per second. Current I = Q (charge in coulombs) ÷ t (time in seconds).
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 4; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-17

questions.q: One coulomb is equal to how many electrons?
questions.expl: The coulomb is the basic unit of electrical quantity: 6.28 × 10¹⁸ electrons.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 4; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-17

questions.q: The OHM (Ω) is the unit of:
questions.expl: The ohm is the unit of electrical opposition to current. A circuit has one ohm when it limits current to one amp under a pressure of one volt.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-18

questions.q: 'A circuit has one ohm of resistance when it limits the flow of current to ___ under a pressure of ___.'
questions.expl: Verbatim definition: one ohm limits current to one amp under a voltage pressure of one volt.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-18

questions.q: Which four factors affect a conductor's resistance?
questions.expl: A conductor's resistance depends on its material, length, cross-sectional area, and temperature.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 4; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-18

questions.q: If you DOUBLE the length of a wire (same everything else), its resistance:
questions.expl: Resistance is directly proportional to length. Twice the length = twice the resistance, which halves the current.

questions.q: The FAA officially defines current flow using which theory/direction?
questions.expl: Electron flow is what actually happens; the FAA officially defines current using electron theory, negative to positive.

questions.q: Ohm's Law states current is ___ to voltage and ___ to resistance.
questions.short: directly proportional to V, inversely proportional to R
questions.expl: Ohm's Law: current is directly proportional to circuit voltage and inversely proportional to circuit resistance.

questions.q: Which is the correct Ohm's Law formula to solve for CURRENT?
questions.expl: From the triangle (E on top, I and R below): cover I and you get E over R, so I = E / R.

questions.q: A 28-volt circuit has 14 ohms of resistance. How much current flows?
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-17

questions.q: A 50-ohm relay coil carries 0.5 amp. What voltage is across it?
24 volts pushes 3 amps through a heater. What is its resistance?
In a circuit, if voltage stays the same but resistance DOUBLES, the current:
questions.expl: Current is inversely proportional to resistance. Double R → half the current (e.g., 12V/3Ω=4A becomes 12V/6Ω=2A).

questions.q: The WATT is the basic unit of:
questions.expl: The watt is the basic unit of power — the product of voltage times current. One watt = one amp under one volt.

questions.q: Which is a correct power (Watt's Law) formula?
questions.expl: Power = current × voltage. The other valid forms are P = I²R and P = E²/R.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 6; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-22

questions.q: A device draws 2 amps from a 24-volt bus. How much power does it use?
24 volts is applied across a 20-ohm resistor. Power dissipated? (P = E² / R)
One horsepower equals how many watts?
questions.expl: 1 horsepower = 746 watts. A 1-hp electric motor needs 746 W of electrical energy.

questions.q: To measure VOLTAGE across a component, the voltmeter is connected:
questions.expl: Voltmeters are connected in parallel — across the two points whose voltage you want. They have HIGH resistance so they don't load the circuit. Power is ON.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 15; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-71

questions.q: A voltmeter should have a HIGH internal resistance because:
options: It makes the needle move faster
Low resistance would draw off current and give a wrong reading (load the circuit)
It is required to read AC
questions.short: So it doesn't load the circuit / steal current
questions.expl: A low-resistance voltmeter shunts current and lowers the circuit's effective resistance, changing the reading. Use a high-resistance voltmeter.

questions.q: To measure CURRENT through a load, an ammeter is connected:
questions.expl: The ammeter must be in series so all the current flows THROUGH it. It has very low resistance. Power is ON.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 14; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-69

questions.q: Why must you NEVER connect an ammeter directly across a battery or voltage source?
options: Its resistance is so low that huge current flows and destroys the meter
questions.short: Its low resistance lets huge current flow and ruins the meter
questions.expl: An ammeter's resistance is extremely low. Across a source, even a volt or two drives very high current through it, damaging the meter. Always wire it in series.

questions.q: An OHMMETER must be used with the circuit's power:
questions.expl: An ohmmeter has its own internal battery and is used when power is removed from the circuit. Outside power gives false readings and can damage it.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 11; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-71

questions.q: On an analog ohmmeter, leads held OPEN (not touching) read:
questions.expl: Open leads = full-scale = infinite resistance (open). Shorting the leads together reads zero ohms (and is used to zero-adjust the meter).

questions.q: A MULTIMETER measures voltage, current, and resistance by using a:
options: Selector switch that sets it as voltmeter, ammeter, or ohmmeter
questions.expl: A multimeter is a multi-function meter; a selector switch sets it to act as an ammeter, voltmeter, or ohmmeter. It's the most versatile tool the AET uses.

questions.q: The abbreviation 'VOM' (an analog multimeter) stands for:
questions.expl: Analog multimeters are called VOMs (Volt-Ohm-Milliammeter). Digital ones are DMMs, which read easier and load the circuit less.

questions.q: Which instrument displays a voltage WAVEFORM (shape over time), not just a DC number?
questions.expl: An oscilloscope displays the waveform of electrical signals — AC peak-to-peak values, frequency, and phase relationships.

questions.q: An aircraft storage battery is generally rated at what voltage?
questions.expl: Aircraft storage batteries are generally rated at 12 or 24 volts, providing the EMF that pushes current through the circuit.
questions.cite: AET Study Guide p. 4; FAA-H-8083-30B p. 12-16

questions.q: Aerodynamics is the science of:
options: The action of air on an object
The chemistry of jet fuel combustion
questions.short: The action of air on an object
questions.expl: Aerodynamics deals with the motion of air and the forces acting on an object in that current of air. The four relevant parts: the atmosphere, relative wind, pressure, and density.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.301 (AM.I.J.K8) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-36
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The four forces of flight are:
questions.expl: Lift (up), weight (down), thrust (forward), drag (rearward). When all four are in balance the airplane flies straight and level at a constant speed.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.302 (AM.I.J.K8) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-39
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: When LIFT is greater than weight, the airplane is:
questions.expl: Lift > weight → climbing. Thrust > drag → accelerating. Any time the forces are not in balance, something about the airplane's condition is changing.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.303 (AM.I.J.K8) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-39
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The three types of drag named on the slide are:
options: Static drag, dynamic drag, and wave drag
Parasite drag, profile drag, and induced drag
Form drag, skin drag, and ram drag
Interference drag, trim drag, and wind drag
questions.short: Parasite drag, profile drag, and induced drag
questions.expl: Drag is the resistance of the air to objects moving through it. The slide names three types: parasite, profile, and induced drag.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.304 (AM.I.J.K8) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-40
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: In a CONVERGING duct, subsonic air exits at:
options: Higher velocity and lower pressure than it entered
questions.short: Higher velocity and lower pressure than it entered
questions.expl: As the duct gets smaller, velocity increases and pressure decreases (Bernoulli). In a DIVERGING duct the opposite happens: velocity decreases, pressure increases.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.306-307 (AM.I.J.K5, AM.I.J.S7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-37
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A venturi is a tube with a constriction. At the constriction the airflow:
options: Speeds up and its pressure increases
Slows down and its pressure increases
Speeds up and its pressure decreases
questions.short: Speeds up and its pressure decreases
questions.expl: When fluid flowing through a tube reaches a constriction, the speed through the constriction increases and its pressure decreases — the convergence/divergence model of the venturi.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.308 (AM.I.J.K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-37
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: How does Newton's THIRD law explain lift?
options: Air leaving the trailing edge is forced downward; the equal and opposite reaction pushes the wing up
Air piles up under the wing and lifts it by compression alone
The propeller pulls the airplane upward
Gravity is canceled by engine thrust
questions.short: Air leaving the trailing edge is forced downward; the equal and opposite reaction pushes the wing up
questions.expl: As air travels around a wing and leaves the trailing edge it is deflected downward; the equal-and-opposite reacting force is lift. (The calculation uses Newton's 2nd law, F = Mass × Acceleration.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.310 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-38
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The slide defines an airfoil as:
options: Only the cross-section of a wing
Any device that creates a force based on Bernoulli's principles or Newton's laws when air flows over its surface
A control surface attached to the empennage only
Any surface painted with anti-glare coating
questions.short: Any device that creates a force based on Bernoulli's principles or Newton's laws when air flows over its surface
questions.expl: An airfoil is ANY device creating a force when air flows over it — wings, propeller blades, rotor blades. The wing's shape works like one-half of a venturi.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.311 & 322 (AM.I.J.K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-38
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: On an airfoil, the curvature of the upper surface is called the:
questions.expl: An airfoil has a leading edge, trailing edge, upper camber, and lower camber. The difference in curvature between upper and lower surfaces builds the lift force.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.312-313 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-38
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The angle of attack is the angle between:
options: The chord line and the relative wind
The chord line and the horizon
The wing root and the wing tip
The longitudinal axis and the lateral axis
questions.short: The chord line and the relative wind
questions.expl: Angle of attack = chord line vs relative wind. As AOA increases, lift increases — until the air separates and the wing stalls.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.315 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-40
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: If the angle of attack becomes too great, the airflow separates from the wing and lift is destroyed. This condition is called:
questions.expl: Too high an AOA → boundary layer separation → turbulent air → lift destroyed → the wing stalls (stops flying).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.315 & 317 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-40
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Vortex generators are:
options: Slots cut through the flap track fairings
Spinning drums in the leading edge that energize the airflow
Short airfoils arranged in pairs on the wing's upper surface that pull high-energy air into the boundary layer
Heated strips that prevent ice vortices
questions.short: Short airfoils arranged in pairs on the wing's upper surface that pull high-energy air into the boundary layer
questions.expl: Vortex generators pull high-energy air down into the boundary layer region and prevent airflow separation.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.319 (AM.I.J.K11) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-41
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Stall strips are small obstructions on the wing leading edge that:
options: Generate extra lift at high angles of attack
Prevent the wing from ever stalling
Force a part of the wing to stall before it normally would
questions.short: Force a part of the wing to stall before it normally would
questions.expl: Stall strips impede the smooth flow of air at high angles of attack, forcing that part of the wing (typically the root) to stall first — keeping aileron control at the tips.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.320 (AM.I.J.K11) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-41
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A wing FENCE keeps air flowing straight over the airfoil by preventing:
options: Spanwise airflow ('bleeding off' toward the wingtip)
Air from entering the engine inlet
questions.short: Spanwise airflow ('bleeding off' toward the wingtip)
questions.expl: Wing fences prevent spanwise airflow — they stop the air from bleeding off toward the wingtip.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.321 (AM.I.J.K11) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-41
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Winglets cut drag and boost fuel efficiency by:
options: Shrinking the vortex of air at the wingtips
questions.short: Shrinking the vortex of air at the wingtips
questions.expl: Wingtip vortices form as high-pressure air below the wing spills around the tip into the low-pressure air above. Winglets diminish that vortex and the induced drag it creates.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.324 & 327 (AM.I.J.K11) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-42
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A repair that leaves the aircraft skin less smooth and aerodynamic will:
options: Give the aircraft more drag and can change how it handles in flight
Increase lift by energizing the boundary layer
Have no effect if the structure is strong
questions.short: Give the aircraft more drag and can change how it handles in flight
questions.expl: When you repair aircraft skin and it is no longer smooth and aerodynamic, the airflow-inhibiting repair adds drag — repairs can change how the aircraft handles.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.323 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 (consequences of surface condition)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The three axes of an aircraft are:
questions.expl: Longitudinal (nose-to-tail), lateral (wingtip-to-wingtip), vertical (top-to-bottom). All three pass through the center of gravity.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.328 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-43
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Movement about the LATERAL axis is called:
questions.expl: Lateral axis → pitch (elevator) · longitudinal axis → roll (ailerons) · vertical axis → yaw (rudder).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.339 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-43
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Movement about the VERTICAL axis is called:
questions.expl: Yaw is rotation about the vertical axis, controlled by the rudder. Roll = longitudinal axis (ailerons); pitch = lateral axis (elevator).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.341 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-43
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: STATIC stability is:
options: The airplane's resistance to taxiing turns
The initial response an airplane displays after its equilibrium is disrupted
The time it takes the airplane to recover
questions.short: The initial response an airplane displays after its equilibrium is disrupted
questions.expl: Static stability = the initial response after equilibrium is disrupted. Dynamic stability = the time it takes to recover.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.330-331 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-43
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Directional stability — the tendency to return to alignment with the relative wind after a yaw disturbance — is provided mainly by the:
questions.expl: Directional stability is stability about the VERTICAL axis; the vertical fin causes the airplane to align with the relative wind.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.336 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-44
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Wing DIHEDRAL (tips higher than the roots) primarily improves:
questions.expl: Dihedral provides lateral stability — when a wing drops, the lower wing develops more lift and rolls the airplane back level. [Slide is a figure; wording verified in FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5, Lateral Stability.]
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.334-335 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-44
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A DUTCH ROLL is:
options: A ground-handling turn using differential braking
A full roll performed during training
A pitch oscillation caused by elevator flutter
A small oscillation around both the longitudinal and vertical axes
questions.short: A small oscillation around both the longitudinal and vertical axes
questions.expl: Dihedral roll response plus vertical-fin yaw response combine into a small oscillation about both the longitudinal AND vertical axes. Not considered dangerous but uncomfortable; airliners use yaw dampers to cancel it. [Slide is title-only; wording from FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5.]
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.337 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-44 (Dutch Roll)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: An anti-servo tab on a stabilator moves in the SAME direction as the trailing edge in order to:
options: Lock the stabilator on the ground
Reduce the control's sensitivity to pilot input
questions.short: Reduce the control's sensitivity to pilot input
questions.expl: Stabilators are very sensitive; the full-length anti-servo tab creates a force opposing the movement so the pilot cannot overcontrol. [Slide is title-only; wording from FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5, Anti-servo Tab.]
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.344 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 (Anti-servo Tab)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Which flap type attaches with a track and roller system, moving AFT as well as down — increasing wing area, camber, and chord — making it the most effective type?
questions.expl: The Fowler flap moves aft and down, increasing total wing area in addition to camber and chord line — the most effective of the four trailing-edge types, used on airliners and business jets. [Slide is title-only; wording from FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5.]
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.357 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 (Fowler flap)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Plain flaps deployed downward increase lift (and drag) by increasing the wing's:
questions.expl: Plain flaps attach to the trailing edge inboard of the ailerons; deployed downward they increase effective camber and the chord line, creating more lift and more drag. [Wording from FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5.]
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.351 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 (Plain flap)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Slats and leading-edge flaps are high-lift devices located:
options: On the trailing edge inboard of the ailerons
On the leading edge of the wing
questions.short: On the leading edge of the wing
questions.expl: Trailing-edge devices: plain, split, slotted, and Fowler flaps. Leading-edge devices: leading-edge flaps and slats.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.359-360 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 (High-Lift Devices)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A CANARD is:
options: A vertical fin mounted under the tail
A horizontal surface mounted ahead of the main wing
A movable trim surface on the rudder
questions.short: A horizontal surface mounted ahead of the main wing
questions.expl: A canard places the horizontal (pitch) surface forward of the main wing. [Slide is title-only; definition per FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5.]
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A shock wave is best described as:
options: A gust of turbulence near thunderstorms
Sound energy piling up on the airplane when it reaches the speed of sound
An electrical discharge along the wing
The trailing vortex behind heavy aircraft
questions.short: Sound energy piling up on the airplane when it reaches the speed of sound
questions.expl: At the speed of sound, pressure waves can no longer travel ahead of the airplane; the sound energy piles up — initially on top of the wing, then attaching to the leading and trailing edges.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.368 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-46
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Behind a NORMAL shock wave the airflow is:
options: Subsonic, with higher static pressure and density
questions.short: Subsonic, with higher static pressure and density
questions.expl: Normal shock waves form perpendicular to the airstream; behind them velocity is subsonic and static pressure and density are higher.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.369 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-46
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Behind an OBLIQUE shock wave the airflow velocity is:
options: Lower, but still supersonic (pressure and density higher)
questions.short: Lower, but still supersonic (pressure and density higher)
questions.expl: Oblique shock waves attach to the sharp leading/trailing edges of supersonic designs; behind them the air is slower but still supersonic, with higher static pressure and density.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.370 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-47
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: An EXPANSION wave differs from a shock wave because behind it:
options: Velocity increases while static pressure and density decrease — and it is not a shock wave
questions.short: Velocity increases while static pressure and density decrease — and it is not a shock wave
questions.expl: Where supersonic flow turns away from the flow direction (over the top of a wing), an expansion wave forms: velocity increases, pressure and density decrease. It is NOT a shock wave.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.371 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-47
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: In supersonic flight, the wing's aerodynamic center moves from about 25% of the chord back to:
questions.expl: Subsonic aerodynamic center ≈ 25% of chord; in supersonic flight it moves aft to 50%, causing significant control and stability changes. Transonic flight is the most difficult regime — part of the wing is subsonic, part supersonic.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.372 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-47
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The three main rotor system types for helicopters are:
questions.expl: Main rotor systems: fully articulated, semi-rigid, and rigid.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.375 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-49
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: In forward flight, the rotor blade moving TOWARD the relative wind is the:
questions.expl: The advancing blade moves toward the relative wind (more lift); the retreating blade moves away from it (less lift) — creating dissymmetry of lift.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.392 & 394 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-50
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Helicopter designers solved dissymmetry of lift with:
options: Locking the blades rigidly to the mast
Heavier blades on the retreating side
Spinning the rotor faster in forward flight
A hinged design that lets the rotor blade flap up with increased lift and down with decreased lift
questions.short: A hinged design that lets the rotor blade flap up with increased lift and down with decreased lift
questions.expl: Blade flapping equalizes lift: the advancing blade flaps up (reducing its angle of attack) and the retreating blade flaps down. [Wording verified in FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5, Dissymmetry of Lift.]
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.393-394 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 (Dissymmetry of Lift)
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: The COLLECTIVE pitch control lever does what?
options: Pitches all main rotor blades up simultaneously and equally
Tilts the rotor disc in the direction of desired flight
questions.short: Pitches all main rotor blades up simultaneously and equally
questions.expl: Collective = all blades change pitch simultaneously and equally (vertical control). Cyclic = changes pitch cyclically to tilt the rotor disc for directional control.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.388 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-51
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: Do weight-shift-control aircraft and powered parachutes require an A&P certificate to work on them?
options: Only powered parachutes require an A&P
Only weight-shift aircraft require an A&P
No — A&P certification is not required for either
Yes — both require a certificated A&P
questions.short: No — A&P certification is not required for either
questions.expl: Per the slides, weight-shift control (flexible wing) aircraft and powered parachutes do NOT require an A&P certification to work on them.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.397-398 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-52
sources: Block 1 Aerodynamics & Theory of Flight — Quiz

questions.q: A circular inspection plate has a radius of 4 inches. What is its area?
questions.expl: Use A = πr² with π = 3.14. Area = 3.14 × 4² = 3.14 × 16 = 50.24 square inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.124 (Circle: A = πr²) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-19

questions.q: A circular access panel has a diameter of 10 inches. What is its radius?
questions.expl: Radius is one-half of diameter. Radius = diameter ÷ 2 = 10 ÷ 2 = 5 inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.124 (radius = half the diameter) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-19

questions.q: A piston has a diameter of 5 inches. What is the area of the piston head?
questions.expl: First convert diameter to radius. Radius = 2.5 inches. Area = 3.14 × 2.5² = 3.14 × 6.25 = 19.625 square inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.124 & 138 (bore/2 = radius) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-19

questions.q: A circular aircraft part has a diameter of 8 inches. What is its circumference?
questions.expl: Use C = πd. Circumference = 3.14 × 8 = 25.12 inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.123-124 (C = D × π) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-19

questions.q: A hydraulic piston has a radius of 2.5 inches. What is the piston area?
questions.expl: Use A = πr². Area = 3.14 × 2.5² = 3.14 × 6.25 = 19.625 square inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.124 (A = πr²) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-19

questions.q: A rectangular baggage compartment is 36 inches long and 24 inches wide. What is the floor area?
questions.expl: Use A = length × width. Area = 36 × 24 = 864 square inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.114-115 (A = L × W) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-18

questions.q: A rectangular panel is 4 feet long and 3 feet wide. What is its area?
questions.expl: Use A = length × width. Area = 4 × 3 = 12 square feet.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.114-115 (A = L × W) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-18

questions.q: A triangular gusset has a base of 10 inches and a height of 6 inches. What is its area?
questions.expl: Use A = 1/2 × base × height. Area = 1/2 × 10 × 6 = 30 square inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.120 (A = ½ × B × H) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-18

questions.q: A baggage compartment is 40 inches long, 20 inches wide, and 18 inches high. What is its volume?
questions.expl: Use V = length × width × height. Volume = 40 × 20 × 18 = 14,400 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.135 (V = L × W × H) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-20/3-21

questions.q: A cylindrical container has a radius of 3 inches and a height of 10 inches. What is its volume?
questions.expl: Use V = πr²h. Volume = 3.14 × 3² × 10 = 3.14 × 9 × 10 = 282.6 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.137 (AM.I.H.S2, V = πr²h) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-21

questions.q: An aircraft wing panel is 12 feet long and 5 feet wide. What is its area?
questions.expl: Use A = length × width. Area = 12 × 5 = 60 square feet.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.126 (span × mean chord) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-20

questions.q: A fuel tank is shaped like a rectangular box measuring 30 inches by 20 inches by 12 inches. What is the volume?
questions.expl: Use V = length × width × height. Volume = 30 × 20 × 12 = 7,200 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.135 (rectangular solid) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-20/3-21

questions.q: An engine has a bore of 5 inches and a stroke of 4 inches. What is the displacement of one cylinder?
questions.expl: Displacement = piston area × stroke. Bore radius = 2.5 inches. Area = 19.625 sq in. Displacement = 19.625 × 4 = 78.5 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.138 (#cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: A 4-cylinder engine has a bore of 4 inches and a stroke of 3 inches. What is the total piston displacement?
questions.expl: Total displacement = πr² × stroke × number of cylinders. Radius = 2. Area = 3.14 × 4 = 12.56. One cylinder = 12.56 × 3 = 37.68. Total = 37.68 × 4 = 150.72 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.138 (#cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: A 6-cylinder aircraft engine has a bore of 5 inches and stroke of 5 inches. What is the approximate total displacement?
questions.expl: Find one cylinder, then multiply by 6. Area = 19.625 sq in. One cylinder = 19.625 × 5 = 98.125. Total = 98.125 × 6 = 588.75 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.138 (#cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: An engine cylinder has a piston area of 20 square inches and a stroke of 5 inches. What is the displacement of that cylinder?
questions.expl: Displacement = area × stroke. Displacement = 20 × 5 = 100 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.138 (#cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: An 8-cylinder radial engine has 75 cubic inches displacement per cylinder. What is the total displacement?
questions.expl: Total displacement = displacement per cylinder × number of cylinders. Total displacement = 75 × 8 = 600 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.138 (#cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: A 4-cylinder engine has a total displacement of 320 cubic inches. What is the displacement per cylinder?
questions.expl: Per-cylinder displacement = total displacement ÷ number of cylinders. Per-cylinder displacement = 320 ÷ 4 = 80 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.138 (#cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: Convert 24 inches to feet.
questions.expl: There are 12 inches in 1 foot. 24 ÷ 12 = 2 feet.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.127 (12 inches = 1 linear foot) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-22 Fig.3-23

questions.q: Convert 3 feet to inches.
questions.expl: There are 12 inches in 1 foot. 3 × 12 = 36 inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.127 (12 inches = 1 linear foot) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-22 Fig.3-23

questions.q: Convert 72 inch-pounds to foot-pounds.
questions.expl: Divide inch-pounds by 12. 72 in-lb ÷ 12 = 6 ft-lb.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.156 (AM.I.H.S8, ×12 / ÷12) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-22

questions.q: Convert 8 foot-pounds to inch-pounds.
questions.expl: Multiply foot-pounds by 12. 8 ft-lb × 12 = 96 in-lb.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.156 (AM.I.H.S8, ×12 / ÷12) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-22

questions.q: Area is a measurement of the amount of ___ of an object.
questions.expl: Area measures the amount of surface, usually expressed in square inches, square feet, or square meters. (Volume is the space within.)

questions.q: The formula for the area of a trapezoid is:
questions.expl: A trapezoid is a four-sided figure with ONE pair of parallel sides. Slide example: ½(16 + 8) × 7 = 12 × 7 = 84 sq in.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.122 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-19 Fig.3-13

questions.q: A parallelogram differs from a rectangle in that a parallelogram:
options: Has two pairs of parallel sides but does NOT have four right angles
Has all four sides of equal length by definition
Has only one pair of parallel sides
questions.short: Has two pairs of parallel sides but does NOT have four right angles
questions.expl: A parallelogram is a four-sided figure with two pairs of parallel sides and no requirement of right angles. Area = length × height.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.121 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-19

questions.q: The sum of the three angles in any triangle is always:
questions.expl: A triangle is a three-sided figure; its three angles always total 180°.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.117 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-18

questions.q: A triangle with three sides of equal length is called:
questions.expl: By sides: equilateral = 3 equal sides; isosceles = 2 equal sides; scalene = all sides differ.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.118 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-18

questions.q: The circumference of a circle equals:
questions.expl: Circumference (distance around the circle) = D × π (3.1416). Area = π × r². The radius is half the diameter.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.123-124 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-19

questions.q: An ellipse has semi-axes a = 14 inches and b = 8 inches. Its area is approximately:
questions.expl: Ellipse area = π × a × b = 3.1416 × 14 × 8 = 351.8592 sq in. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.125 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-19

questions.q: One square foot equals how many square inches?
questions.expl: 1 sq ft = 12 in × 12 in = 144 sq in. To convert sq ft to sq in multiply by 144; sq in to sq ft divide by 144 (720 ÷ 144 = 5 sq ft).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.127 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-22 Fig.3-23

questions.q: The volume of a cube is found by:
questions.expl: A cube is a three-dimensional solid with six square sides: V = S³.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.136 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-21

questions.q: The formula for the volume of a SPHERE is:
questions.expl: Sphere volume = 4/3 πr³, or equivalently 1/6 πD³ since the radius is half the diameter.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.139 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-21

questions.q: The formula for the volume of a CONE is:
questions.expl: A cone (circular base tapering to a point) has volume 1/3 πr²h — one third of the cylinder that contains it.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.140 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-21

questions.q: The total volume swept by all the pistons of a reciprocating engine in ONE revolution of the crankshaft is called:
questions.expl: Piston displacement is the total volume (cubic inches) swept by all pistons in one crankshaft revolution. Total = #cyl × π(bore/2)² × stroke.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.138 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 pp.3-21/3-22

questions.q: One cubic foot equals how many cubic inches?
questions.expl: 1 cu ft = 12 × 12 × 12 = 1,728 cu in. Multiply cu ft by 1,728 to get cu in (38 cu ft = 65,664 cu in); divide cu in by 1,728 to get cu ft.

questions.q: Approximately how many gallons fit inside a volume of one cubic foot?
questions.expl: 7.5 gallons will fit inside 1 cubic foot. (A gallon itself occupies 231 cubic inches.) Needed when converting a tank's cubic volume to gallons.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.143 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-22 Fig.3-23

questions.q: One cubic yard equals how many cubic feet?
questions.expl: 1 cu yd = 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 cu ft. Slide example: 450 cu yd × 27 = 12,150 cu ft.

questions.q: The surface area of a CUBE is found by:
questions.expl: A cube has six identical square faces, so surface area = 6 × S². (Sphere surface area = 4πr²; cylinder = 2πr² + πDH.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.147 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-22

questions.q: The SINE of an angle in a right triangle equals:
questions.expl: Sine = opposite/hypotenuse; cosine = adjacent/hypotenuse; tangent = opposite/adjacent. Side c (hypotenuse) is always across from the right angle.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.150-151 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-23

questions.q: The TANGENT of an angle equals:
questions.expl: Tangent is the length of the opposite side divided by the length of the adjacent side.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.150-151 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-23

questions.q: The sine of a 30° angle is 0.5. This means the hypotenuse is:
options: Half as long as the opposite side
Twice as long as the opposite side
questions.short: Twice as long as the opposite side
questions.expl: Sin 30° = opposite/hypotenuse = 0.5/1 — the ratio is 0.5 to 1, so the hypotenuse is twice the opposite side. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.152 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-24

questions.q: The two short sides of a right triangle are 7 inches and 9 inches. The hypotenuse is approximately:
questions.expl: Pythagorean Theorem: a² + b² = c². 49 + 81 = 130; c = √130 = 11.4 inches. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.153-154 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-24

questions.q: To convert inches to millimeters you multiply by:
questions.expl: Conventional to metric: inches × 25.4 mm (12 in × 25.4 = 304.8 mm). Metric to conventional: mm ÷ 25.4 (120 mm ÷ 25.4 = 4.72 in).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.155 (AM.I.H.K11) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-22 Fig.3-23

questions.q: The binary number system has only two digits because:
options: It is the oldest number system known
Two digits make arithmetic carry easier
Switches and valves have two states — on (1) and off (0)
Computers cannot store more than two values per byte
questions.short: Switches and valves have two states — on (1) and off (0)
questions.expl: Binary uses only 0 and 1; its use is based on the fact that switches or valves have two states: on = 1, off = 0.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.158 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-25

questions.q: Convert the binary number 10110011 to a decimal number.
questions.expl: Add the place values where 1s appear: 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 179. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.160 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-25

questions.q: A hydraulic system pressure is 500 psi and acts on a piston area of 4 square inches. What force is produced?
questions.expl: Use F = P × A. Force = 500 × 4 = 2,000 pounds.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.264-265 (AM.I.J.K13, F = P × A) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: A force of 600 pounds acts on an area of 3 square inches. What is the pressure?
questions.expl: Use P = F ÷ A. Pressure = 600 ÷ 3 = 200 psi.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.247 & 264 (P = F ÷ A) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-22
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: A hydraulic piston has an area of 6 square inches. If pressure is 200 psi, what force is produced?
questions.expl: Use F = P × A. Force = 200 × 6 = 1,200 pounds.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.264-265 (AM.I.J.K13, F = P × A) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: According to Pascal’s law, pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted:
questions.expl: Think about hydraulic systems. Pascal’s law says pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.264 (AM.I.J.K13) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.5-27/5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: In a venturi, as fluid velocity increases, pressure generally:
questions.expl: Bernoulli principle relationship. In a venturi throat, velocity increases and static pressure decreases.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.269 & 308 (AM.I.J.K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.5-29/5-30
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Which type of heat transfer occurs mainly through direct contact?
questions.expl: Example: heat moving through metal. Conduction is heat transfer through direct contact between materials.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.241 (conduction/convection/radiation) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.5-19/5-20
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Which type of heat transfer occurs by movement of a fluid such as air or liquid?
questions.expl: Example: warm air rising. Convection is heat transfer by movement of a fluid, including air or liquid.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.241 (conduction/convection/radiation) · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.5-19/5-20
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: If the same force is applied to a larger area, the pressure will:
questions.expl: Use P = F ÷ A. Pressure decreases when the same force is spread over a larger area.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.247 & 264 (P = F ÷ A) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-22
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Heat may be defined as:
options: The total kinetic energy of the molecules of any substance
The potential energy stored in chemical bonds
questions.short: The total kinetic energy of the molecules of any substance
questions.expl: Heat is a form of energy produced only by the conversion of one of the other forms of energy; it is the total kinetic energy of a substance's molecules.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.232 (AM.I.J.K4) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-18
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: A BTU (British Thermal Unit) is the amount of heat required to:
options: Boil 1 lb of water at sea level
Raise the temperature of 1 lb of water 1°F
questions.short: Raise the temperature of 1 lb of water 1°F
questions.expl: BTU = heat required to increase the temperature of 1 pound of water by 1 degree Fahrenheit.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.239 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-18
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: An engine produces 200 hp from fuel containing 744 potential horsepower. Its thermal efficiency is:
questions.expl: Thermal efficiency = HP produced ÷ potential HP in fuel = 200 ÷ 744 = 0.2688 = 26.88%. More than 70% of the fuel's energy is not turned into usable horsepower. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.240 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-19
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Heat always transfers from:
options: Areas of low heat energy to high heat energy
Areas of high heat energy to areas of low heat energy
questions.short: Areas of high heat energy to areas of low heat energy
questions.expl: Heat always flows high → low. The three transfer methods are conduction (direct contact), convection (movement of heated fluid), and radiation (waves).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.241 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-19
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Which heat-transfer method works by electromagnetic waves and requires NO physical contact or fluid?
questions.expl: Radiation transfers heat by radiant energy (waves). Conduction needs direct physical contact; convection needs movement of a heated fluid (gas or liquid).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.241 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 pp.5-19/5-20
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: At standard conditions, pure water BOILS at:
questions.expl: Water boils at 100°C = 212°F = 373 Kelvin = 672 Rankine; it freezes at 0°C = 32°F = 273 K = 492°R.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.244 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-21
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: All molecular activity ceases at absolute zero, which is:
questions.expl: Absolute zero = −273 Celsius = −460 Fahrenheit = 0 Kelvin = 0 Rankine.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.244 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-21
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Convert 20°C to Fahrenheit.
questions.expl: °F = (°C × 1.8) + 32 = (20 × 1.8) + 32 = 36 + 32 = 68°F. ⚠ Exam note: the formula sheet in FAA-H-8083-30B Fig. 3-23 contains a misprint of this conversion — the correct form is °F = (1.8 × °C) + 32, exactly as the AIM slide teaches it.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.245 (AM.I.J.S1, AM.I.J.R4) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-21 (cf. Fig.3-23 misprint)
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: To convert Celsius to Kelvin you:
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.245 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-21
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The formula for linear thermal expansion is:
options: Expansion = coefficient × length × rise in temperature
questions.short: Expansion = coefficient × length × rise in temperature
questions.expl: Expansion = coefficient × length × temperature rise. Slide example: a 9-ft steel rod heated 34°C grows by (11×10⁻⁶) × 9 × 34 ≈ 0.0034 ft. Coefficients are in Fig. 5-32, p.5-23.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.246 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-23 Fig.5-32
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level (59°F) is 14.7 psi, which also equals:
options: 29.92 inches of mercury / 760 mm of mercury / 1013.2 millibars
questions.short: 29.92 inches of mercury / 760 mm of mercury / 1013.2 millibars
questions.expl: Standard sea-level atmosphere: 14.7 psi = 29.92 in Hg = 760 mm Hg = 1013.2 millibars, at 59°F/15°C.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.247-248 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-24
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: PSIA stands for and measures:
options: Pounds per square inch ABSOLUTE — pressure measured from a complete vacuum (zero)
The difference between two pressure sources
Pounds per square inch ATMOSPHERIC — gauge pressure plus 100
Pressure relative to the local field elevation
questions.short: Pounds per square inch ABSOLUTE — pressure measured from a complete vacuum (zero)
questions.expl: PSIG = gauge pressure (above ambient), PSIA = absolute pressure (from vacuum), PSID = differential pressure (difference between two sources).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.249-251 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-25
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Boyle's Law states that the volume of an enclosed dry gas varies ___ with its absolute pressure, temperature constant.
questions.expl: Boyle's Law: volume varies inversely with absolute pressure at constant temperature. V1 × P1 = V2 × P2.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.252 (AM.I.J.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-26
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: 10 ft³ of nitrogen at 500 psia is compressed to 7 ft³ at constant temperature. The new pressure is approximately:
questions.expl: V1P1 = V2P2 → 10 × 500 = 7 × P2 → P2 = 5,000 ÷ 7 = 714.29 psia. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.253 (AM.I.J.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-26
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Charles' Law states that gases expand and contract in direct proportion to the change in ___, pressure constant.
questions.expl: Charles' Law: all gases expand and contract in direct proportion to the change in ABSOLUTE temperature (Kelvin/Rankine), pressure held constant.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.255 (AM.I.J.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-26
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The General Gas Law combines Boyle's and Charles' laws into:
questions.expl: The general gas law lets you solve for pressure, volume and temperature when one or more variables change: P1V1/T1 = P2V2/T2.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.257 (AM.I.J.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-27
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: When two or more gases that do not combine chemically share a container, each gas's reduced pressure is called its ___ (Dalton's Law).
questions.expl: Dalton's Law: each gas expands through the total space and the absolute pressure of each gas drops to a lower value, called its partial pressure.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.258 (AM.I.J.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-27
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Per Avogadro's principle, why does humid air affect aircraft performance?
options: A water molecule (weight 18) is LIGHTER than N₂ (28) or O₂ (32), so humid air is less dense
Water molecules are heavier than air molecules, making humid air denser
Water vapor blocks oxygen from the engine entirely
Humidity increases air pressure, adding drag
questions.short: A water molecule (weight 18) is LIGHTER than N₂ (28) or O₂ (32), so humid air is less dense
questions.expl: Equal volumes of gases hold equal numbers of molecules. Water (H₂O = 18) weighs less than the N₂ (28) and O₂ (32) it displaces, so humid air is less dense — hurting lift and engine performance.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.259 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-27
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Buoyant force equals:
options: Volume of the object × density of the fluid displaced
Weight of the object × specific gravity
questions.short: Volume of the object × density of the fluid displaced
questions.expl: Buoyant force = volume × density of displaced fluid. Water at 4°C is 62.4 lb/ft³, so 0.11 cu ft displaced = 0.11 × 62.4 = 6.864 lbs of buoyant force. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.262 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The pressure a liquid exerts on the bottom of its container (static pressure) is caused solely by:
options: The color and temperature of the fluid
The surface area of the container bottom
The height of the fluid, not the container shape
questions.short: The height of the fluid, not the container shape
questions.expl: Static pressure depends only on fluid height: containers of different shapes each filled to 231 inches all read 8.34 psi at the bottom.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.263 (AM.I.J.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Pascal's Law states that pressure set up in a fluid:
options: Decreases with distance from the pump
questions.expl: Pressure in a closed hydraulic system is equal and constant throughout the entire system. Use the FAP triangle: Force = Pressure × Area; Pressure = F ÷ A; Area = F ÷ P.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.264 (AM.I.J.K13) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: A hydraulic actuator must produce 18,000 lbs of force and holds 48 cubic inches of fluid over an 8-inch stroke. What is the piston area and system pressure?
questions.expl: VAD: Volume = Area × Distance(Length) → A = 48 ÷ 8 = 6 sq in. FAP: P = F ÷ A = 18,000 ÷ 6 = 3,000 psi. (Built from slide p.267 practice problem #1.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.266-267 (AM.I.J.K13, AM.I.J.S4) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-28
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The three elements necessary for the transmission and reception of sound are:
options: A speaker, an amplifier, and a microphone
The source, a medium for carrying the sound, and the detector
questions.short: The source, a medium for carrying the sound, and the detector
questions.expl: Sound transmission/reception requires: the source, a medium to carry it, and the detector.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.270 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-30
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: As a general rule, sound travels FASTEST in:
options: Gases (slower in liquids, slowest in solids)
Solids (slower in liquids, slowest in gases)
questions.short: Solids (slower in liquids, slowest in gases)
questions.expl: Sound travels fastest in solid materials, slower in liquids, and slowest in gases — and not at all in a vacuum (no medium).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.272 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-31
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The velocity of sound in air at 0°C (32°F) is:
questions.expl: Sound in air at 0°C travels 1,087 feet per second, increasing 2 fps per °C of temperature rise (1.1 fps per °F).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.272 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-31
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Mach number is the ratio of:
options: The speed of sound to the aircraft's speed
An aircraft's speed to the speed of sound
questions.short: An aircraft's speed to the speed of sound
questions.expl: Mach number = aircraft speed ÷ speed of sound, named for Austrian scientist Ernst Mach. The speed of sound at sea level on a standard day is 761 mph (666.739 knots).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.273 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-31
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: When a sound source moves toward you, the frequency you sense increases. This is known as:
questions.expl: Forward motion adds to the frequency sensed from the front and subtracts from the frequency sensed behind — the Doppler Effect.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.277 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-32
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Two pieces of matter share the same natural frequency; one vibrates and transfers its wave energy to the other. This is:
questions.expl: All matter has a natural vibration frequency. Energy transfer between two objects with the same natural frequency is resonance — a serious consideration in aircraft structures.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.278 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-32
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The composition of the atmosphere is approximately:
questions.expl: Air is about 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, 0.9% argon, plus traces of CO₂ and other gases.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.283 (AM.I.J.K9) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-33
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The atmospheric layer where we live (0-7 miles average) is the:
questions.expl: From the surface up: troposphere (where we live, 0-7 mi avg), stratosphere (7-31 mi), mesosphere, thermosphere, exosphere.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.283 (AM.I.J.K9) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-33
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Altimeter setting is 29.45 in Hg and field elevation is 5,000 ft. Pressure altitude is:
questions.expl: PA = (29.92 − current setting) × 1,000 + field elevation = (29.92 − 29.45) × 1,000 + 5,000 = 470 + 5,000 = 5,470 ft. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.289 (AM.I.J.R1, AM.I.J.S3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-34
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: The density of a gas varies ___ with pressure and ___ with temperature.
questions.expl: Double the pressure → twice as dense. Raise the temperature → density decreases. So high-altitude air and hot air are both less dense. Density decreases with increased altitude.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.290 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-35
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Density altitude is best described as:
options: Field elevation corrected for runway slope
The height indicated on the altimeter at all times
The altitude where the air is 100% dry
The altitude the airplane 'feels' like it is at — where standard density equals the observed density
questions.short: The altitude the airplane 'feels' like it is at — where standard density equals the observed density
questions.expl: Density altitude is the altitude (relative to standard atmosphere) at which the air density equals the density at the place of observation — if DA is 4,200 ft at sea level, the airplane takes off as if already at 4,200 ft.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.292 (AM.I.J.S2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-35
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Pressure altitude is 5,470 ft, OAT is 4°C, and ISA temp for the altitude is 5°C. Density altitude is:
questions.expl: DA = PA + [120 × (OAT − ISA)] = 5,470 + [120 × (4 − 5)] = 5,470 − 120 = 5,350 ft. (Slide's worked example.) ISA temp rule: 15 − (2 × altitude in thousands).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.293-294 (AM.I.J.R1, AM.I.J.S2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-35
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: RELATIVE humidity is:
options: The ratio of water vapor actually present to the amount present if the air were saturated at that temperature and pressure
The temperature at which air becomes saturated
The weight of water vapor per gallon of fuel
The actual amount of water vapor, in lbs per cubic foot
questions.short: The ratio of water vapor actually present to the amount present if the air were saturated at that temperature and pressure
questions.expl: Absolute humidity = the actual amount of water vapor. Relative humidity = ratio of actual vapor to saturation. Dew point = the temperature to which humid air must be cooled at constant pressure to become saturated.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.296 (AM.I.J.K9) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-36
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Water vapor weighs approximately ___ as much as an equal amount of dry air.
questions.expl: Water vapor weighs about 5/8 as much as an equal amount of dry air — so moist air is lighter (less dense) than dry air, influencing aircraft performance.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.297 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-36
sources: Block 1 Heat, Gas Laws, Sound & Atmosphere — Quiz

questions.q: Inspections are defined as:
options: Visual examinations and manual checks to determine the condition of an aircraft or component
Mandatory engine teardowns at fixed intervals
Paperwork reviews performed only by the FAA
questions.short: Visual examinations and manual checks to determine the condition of an aircraft or component
questions.expl: An inspection is a visual examination and manual check to determine the condition of an aircraft or component.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.405 (AM.I.K.K4/K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-1

questions.q: Before starting an inspection, you should be certain that:
options: The aircraft has been fueled to capacity
The engine has been removed from the airframe
All plates, access doors, fairings, and cowling have been opened or removed and the structure cleaned
All avionics have been powered up
questions.short: All plates, access doors, fairings, and cowling have been opened or removed and the structure cleaned
questions.expl: Open/remove all plates, access doors, fairings and cowling and clean the structure first — and before cleaning, note any oil or other evidence of fluid leakage.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.405 (AM.I.K.K4/K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-1

questions.q: The aircraft logbook is:
options: The same thing as the Type Certificate Data Sheet
The record where all data concerning the aircraft is recorded — condition, inspections, time on airframe, engines, propellers
A pilot-only document not used by mechanics
questions.short: The record where all data concerning the aircraft is recorded — condition, inspections, time on airframe, engines, propellers
questions.expl: Aircraft logs record the history of all significant events for the aircraft, its components and accessories. Make entries legible and clear for any future reader.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.406 (AM.I.K.K4/K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-2

questions.q: At a minimum, an inspection checklist should include information from:
options: 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix D — Scope and Detail of Items
questions.short: 14 CFR Part 43, Appendix D — Scope and Detail of Items
questions.expl: Always use a checklist — your own design, the manufacturer's, or another source — but at minimum it must include the scope-and-detail items of Part 43 Appendix D.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.407 · 14 CFR Part 43 App. D · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-2

questions.q: The slide lists two categories of Airworthiness Directives (ADs):
options: Emergency in nature requiring immediate compliance, and less urgent requiring compliance within a specific time
questions.short: Emergency in nature requiring immediate compliance, and less urgent requiring compliance within a specific time
questions.expl: AD categories per the slide: (1) emergency — immediate compliance; (2) less urgent — compliance within a specified time.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.408 · 14 CFR Part 39 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10

questions.q: Under FAR 43.3, who may perform PREVENTIVE maintenance on an aircraft?
options: A private pilot, on an aircraft he or she owns
questions.short: A private pilot, on an aircraft he or she owns
questions.expl: Per the slide: an aircraft private pilot may do preventive maintenance on his own aircraft under FAR 43.3.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.410 · 14 CFR 43.3 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10

questions.q: An ANNUAL inspection is required every:
questions.expl: Annual inspections (FAR 91.409) are due every 12 calendar months. A calendar month extends from a given day until midnight of the last day of that month.

questions.q: An annual inspection must be performed by:
options: An A&P mechanic holding an Inspection Authorization (IA), a certificated repair station, or the aircraft manufacturer
A private pilot with 100 hours in type
questions.short: An A&P mechanic holding an Inspection Authorization (IA), a certificated repair station, or the aircraft manufacturer
questions.expl: Annual = IA, certificated repair station, or manufacturer. (A 100-hour inspection may be performed by an A&P without IA.)

questions.q: An aircraft's annual inspection has lapsed. To fly it to a base where the inspection can be performed, you need:
options: Nothing, if the flight is under 50 miles
A verbal OK from an IA
A special flight permit (the aircraft must be safe to fly)
questions.short: A special flight permit (the aircraft must be safe to fly)
questions.expl: After the annual lapses the aircraft is no longer airworthy. A special flight permit allows ferrying it to where repairs/inspection can be made, if it is capable of safe flight.

questions.q: A 100-hour inspection is required for aircraft:
questions.expl: Aircraft used commercially must have the complete inspection every 100 hours in addition to the annual. The 100-hour may be performed by an A&P mechanic.

questions.q: The 100-hour limit may be exceeded by how much, and for what purpose?
options: Up to 25 hours, for any reason
Up to 10 hours, while en route to reach a place where the inspection can be done
Up to 50 hours with FSDO approval
questions.short: Up to 10 hours, while en route to reach a place where the inspection can be done
questions.expl: There is a 10-hour grace period while en route to have the inspection accomplished; the excess time counts against the next 100 hours.

questions.q: Under a PROGRESSIVE inspection (FAR 91.409(d)), each segment must be accomplished every:
questions.expl: Progressive inspections divide the complete inspection into segments (detailed + routine) done every 90 days or 25 hours, with the complete aircraft covered within each 12 calendar months. Prior FAA approval is required and the aircraft must be completely inspected before the program starts.

questions.q: The CONTINUOUS inspection program is designed for:
options: Large aircraft of 12,500 lbs gross takeoff weight or more, multi-engine jets or turboprops
questions.short: Large aircraft of 12,500 lbs gross takeoff weight or more, multi-engine jets or turboprops
questions.expl: Continuous inspection programs serve large aircraft (12,500+ lbs GTOW, turbojet/turboprop multiengine); they require a large maintenance organization with extensive personnel, facilities and equipment.

questions.q: ATA iSpec 2200 is:
options: A standardized format for presenting maintenance information in all aircraft maintenance manuals
An FAA inspection checklist for transponders
questions.short: A standardized format for presenting maintenance information in all aircraft maintenance manuals
questions.expl: ATA iSpec 2200 standardizes maintenance manuals into numbered systems (e.g., 21 Air Conditioning, 27 Flight Controls, 28 Fuel, 32 Landing Gear).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.418 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-6 Fig.10-4

questions.q: ATC transponder tests are required every ___, performed by ___.
options: 24 calendar months; the aircraft manufacturer or a repair station with a radio rating
questions.short: 24 calendar months; the aircraft manufacturer or a repair station with a radio rating
questions.expl: Transponder tests: every 24 calendar months, per Part 43 Appendix F, by the manufacturer or a radio-rated repair station.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.419 (AM.I.K.K4/K5) · 14 CFR 91.413, Part 43 App. F · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-7

questions.q: Altimeter and static system checks are required every 24 calendar months for aircraft:
options: Operating in controlled airspace under instrument flight rules (IFR)
questions.short: Operating in controlled airspace under instrument flight rules (IFR)
questions.expl: Altimeter/static tests: every 24 calendar months for IFR in controlled airspace, per Part 43 Appendix E, by the manufacturer or an instrument-rated repair station.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.420 (AM.I.K.K4/K5) · 14 CFR 91.411, Part 43 App. E · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-7

questions.q: The static system LEAK test may be performed by:
options: A mechanic with an Airframe (A) rating
questions.short: A mechanic with an Airframe (A) rating
questions.expl: The static system test and inspection (leak test) may be performed by an airframe-rated mechanic; details are in Part 43 Appendix E (which refers to FAR 23.1325/25.1325 for standards).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.421 · 14 CFR Part 43 App. E · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-7

questions.q: Which of these is listed as a SPECIAL inspection on the slide?
questions.expl: Special inspections: overweight/hard landing, severe turbulence, lightning strike, bird strike, fire damage, flood damage, seaplanes, aerial application aircraft.

questions.q: Which is one of the five purposes for which a special flight permit may be issued under 14 CFR 21.197?
options: Carrying paying passengers on a scheduled route
Evacuating aircraft from areas of impending danger
questions.short: Evacuating aircraft from areas of impending danger
questions.expl: Special flight permit purposes: (1) flight to a repair/storage base, (2) delivering or exporting, (3) production flight testing, (4) evacuating from impending danger, (5) customer demonstration flights in new production aircraft that completed production flight tests.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.423 · 14 CFR 21.197 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10

questions.q: Nondestructive testing (NDT) is defined as:
options: Testing that always requires part removal
Noninvasive techniques to determine the integrity of a material, component or structure — inspect or measure without doing harm
Testing a sample part to destruction to find its limits
questions.short: Noninvasive techniques to determine the integrity of a material, component or structure — inspect or measure without doing harm
