questions.expl: NDT determines integrity or quantitatively measures a characteristic of an object without doing harm.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.469 (AM.I.K.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-17

questions.q: Anyone performing NDT must be qualified and certified to standards such as:
questions.expl: MIL-STD-410 (NDT personnel qualification and certification) and ATA iSpec 105 (guidelines for training and qualifying NDT personnel).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.470 (AM.I.K.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-17

questions.q: The most basic and common inspection method — also the least expensive — is:
questions.expl: Visual inspection requires the least tools: work light/flashlight, fiberscopes, borescopes, magnifying glasses, and mirrors.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.473 (AM.I.K.K3, AM.I.K.S4) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-19

questions.q: Liquid dye penetrant inspection detects discontinuities:
options: Only on the SURFACE of objects (and only on non-porous surfaces)
questions.short: Only on the SURFACE of objects (and only on non-porous surfaces)
questions.expl: Dye penetrant detects surface discontinuities only, on non-porous surfaces, in metals and other materials. There are two penetrant types: visible and fluorescent.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.474-475 (AM.I.K.K3, AM.I.K.S5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-20

questions.q: Put the dye penetrant steps in order:
options: Preparation → penetrant application → penetrant removal → developer application → inspect → remove developer
questions.short: Preparation → penetrant application → penetrant removal → developer application → inspect → remove developer
questions.expl: Six steps: 1) prepare/clean the surface, 2) apply penetrant, 3) remove excess penetrant, 4) apply developer, 5) inspect and record, 6) remove developer and dry.

questions.q: During penetrant PREPARATION, which cleaning method is prohibited?
options: Abrasive blasting, scraping, or heavy brushing
questions.short: Abrasive blasting, scraping, or heavy brushing
questions.expl: Surfaces must be free of grease/dirt/oil and paint removed — but do NOT clean by abrasive blasting, scraping, or heavy brushing (these can smear metal over cracks). Use a volatile petroleum-based solvent, rinse and dry.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.476 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-20

questions.q: Penetrant DWELL TIME is:
options: The amount of time required for the penetrant to cure (work into discontinuities)
The time the developer takes to dry
questions.short: The amount of time required for the penetrant to cure (work into discontinuities)
questions.expl: Dwell time depends on the size and shape of the discontinuities sought — small thin cracks need LONGER dwell. Heating the part decreases dwell time, but too much heat evaporates the penetrant.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.479 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-20

questions.q: Water-soluble penetrants are flushed away with water sprayed at:
questions.expl: Flush at 30-40 psi and spray at 45° so the penetrant is not washed OUT of the cracks. Post-emulsifying penetrants get an emulsifying agent first; solvent penetrants are wiped with solvent-dampened towels.

questions.q: The three kinds of penetrant developer are:
questions.expl: Dry developer (loose powder/powder gun), wet developer (white powder mixed with water, air-dried), and nonaqueous developer (chalk-like powder suspended in solvent, sprayed from a can). Apply developer as soon as possible after penetrant removal.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.482 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-21

questions.q: Eddy current testing works by:
options: Spraying ferromagnetic particles on the part
Applying AC to a coil and watching how defects distort the induced current/resistance display
Beaming X-rays through the part onto film
Tapping the part and listening for a dead response
questions.short: Applying AC to a coil and watching how defects distort the induced current/resistance display
questions.expl: AC in a coil (electromagnet) sends electrons through the component; the electron flow directly influences the coil's resistance, and surface or subsurface defects distort the current display.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.485 (AM.I.K.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-23

questions.q: The three types of ULTRASONIC testing are:
options: Pulse echo, through transmission, and resonance
questions.short: Pulse echo, through transmission, and resonance
questions.expl: Pulse echo, through transmission, and resonance. Through transmission requires access to the BACK of the test piece because it uses a separate transmitter and receiver.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.486 (AM.I.K.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-24

questions.q: In bonded-structure inspection, a TYPE V defect is:
options: A void between layers of laminate
A disbond at the outer skin-to-adhesive interface
questions.expl: Bonded/laminate defect types: I = disbond/void at outer skin-to-adhesive; II = at adhesive-to-core; III = voids between laminate layers; IV = voids in foam adhesive / closure-member disbonds; V = water in the core.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.492 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-26

questions.q: What advantage does ACOUSTIC EMISSION inspection have over other NDI methods?
options: It can detect and locate ALL the flaws in a structure in one test
It needs no sensors or equipment
It works without applying any load
questions.short: It can detect and locate ALL the flaws in a structure in one test
questions.expl: Acoustic sensors are placed on the aircraft and a load/stress is applied; cracks and corrosion emit sounds and stress waves as ultrasonic pulses, locating every flaw in a single test.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.493 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-26

questions.q: Magnetic particle inspection is only effective on:
questions.expl: The part must be magnetized; an indicating medium of ferromagnetic particulate is applied. It detects surface and subsurface discontinuities, but only on ferrous materials.

questions.q: For a discontinuity to be detected by magnetic particle inspection, it must run:
options: Parallel to the lines of flux
Perpendicular to the lines of flux
questions.short: Perpendicular to the lines of flux
questions.expl: Flux travels pole to pole; a discontinuity must interfere with the flux lines (flux leakage) to attract particles. Parallel discontinuities go undetected — so flux must be introduced in multiple directions.

questions.q: CONTINUOUS magnetization (force applied during testing) provides:
options: Greater sensitivity in locating SUBSURFACE discontinuities
Permanent magnetization that needs no removal
questions.short: Greater sensitivity in locating SUBSURFACE discontinuities
questions.expl: Residual magnetism: force removed before testing, used on heat-treated stressed steels. Continuous magnetism: applied during testing, greater subsurface sensitivity.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.497 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-29

questions.q: Magnaglo inspection differs from standard magnetic particle inspection because it:
options: Uses a fluorescent particle solution inspected under black light
Requires no magnetization of the part
questions.short: Uses a fluorescent particle solution inspected under black light
questions.expl: Magnaglo's neon-like glow of defects under black light lets smaller flaw indications be seen.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.500 (AM.I.K.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-30

questions.q: After magnetic particle inspection, a part returning to service must be demagnetized. The basic procedure is:
options: Soak the part in penetrant developer
Subject the part to a reversing and gradually decreasing magnetizing force (e.g., AC solenoid, slowly withdrawing the part)
Strike the part sharply with a mallet
questions.short: Subject the part to a reversing and gradually decreasing magnetizing force (e.g., AC solenoid, slowly withdrawing the part)
questions.expl: Use an AC-energized solenoid coil and slowly move the part out of and away from the field — or pass AC through the part while gradually reducing the current to zero.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.501 (AM.I.K.R1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-30

questions.q: The three steps of radiographic inspection are:
options: Exposure to radiation, processing of the film/recording device, and interpretation of the radiograph
questions.short: Exposure to radiation, processing of the film/recording device, and interpretation of the radiograph
questions.expl: Radiography: (1) exposure, (2) processing, (3) interpretation. Two source types: X-ray and isotope.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.504 (AM.I.K.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-31

questions.q: In a tap test (coin/ring test) on composite or honeycomb structure, a 'thud' or dead knock indicates:
options: Possible delamination (a ringing sound indicates good continuity)
questions.short: Possible delamination (a ringing sound indicates good continuity)
questions.expl: Tap testing locates subsurface discontinuity by sound: ringing/reverberation = good continuity; thud/dead response = possible delamination.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.509 (AM.I.K.K3, AM.I.K.S8) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-36

questions.q: Thermography inspects composites by:
options: Passing eddy currents through the skin
Applying electromagnetic heat and viewing how discontinuities affect heat dissipation with an IR camera
questions.short: Applying electromagnetic heat and viewing how discontinuities affect heat dissipation with an IR camera
questions.expl: Discontinuities change the heat-dissipation pattern, which an infrared camera reveals. Used on composites.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.511 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-37

questions.q: Which list matches the slide's types of welds?
options: Spot, seam, plug, and groove welds
Square, vee, bevel, and J welds
Stick, MIG, TIG, and gas welds
Butt, double butt, lap, fillet, and edge welds
questions.short: Butt, double butt, lap, fillet, and edge welds
questions.expl: Weld types named on the slide: butt weld, double butt weld, lap weld, fillet weld, edge weld.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.513 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-38

questions.q: The MOST important characteristic of a good weld is:
options: Penetration — the depth of fusion in the weld
questions.short: Penetration — the depth of fusion in the weld
questions.expl: Penetration is the depth of fusion and the most important characteristic of a good weld. It depends on material thickness, filler rod size, and welding technique.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.515 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-38

questions.q: A good weld is uniform in width with even ripples, and no oxide should form on the base metal more than:
options: Any oxide at all is acceptable
questions.expl: Good weld: uniform width, even ripples tapering smoothly into the base metal, no burn marks or overheating signs, oxide limited to within 1/2 inch of the weld, and free of gas pockets, porosity, and inclusions.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.517 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-38

questions.q: Bumps along the center and craters along the weld's edge are caused by:
options: Too much acetylene, making the molten metal boil
questions.short: Too much acetylene, making the molten metal boil
questions.expl: Poor welds: too much acetylene → boiling metal, bumps and craters; cold weld → irregular edges and varying penetration; excessive heat → pitting and long pointed ripples; cooled too quickly → cracks adjacent to the weld.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.520 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-39

questions.q: If a weld has ANY defects, the correct repair is to:
options: Weld a second bead over the top
Grind the surface smooth and paint it
Remove ALL of the old weld and re-weld the joint
Drill-stop the ends of the defect
questions.short: Remove ALL of the old weld and re-weld the joint
questions.expl: Correcting bad welds: all of the old weld must be removed and the joint re-welded.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.521 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10 p.10-39

questions.q: A cylinder has a volume of 90 cubic inches with the piston at BDC and 10 cubic inches at TDC. What is the compression ratio?
questions.expl: Compression ratio = BDC volume ÷ TDC volume. Compression ratio = 90 ÷ 10 = 9:1.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.70 (Ratios — compression ratio) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-7

questions.q: A cylinder has 120 cubic inches volume at BDC and 15 cubic inches at TDC. What is the compression ratio?
questions.expl: Compression ratio = BDC volume ÷ TDC volume. Compression ratio = 120 ÷ 15 = 8:1.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.70 (Ratios — compression ratio) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-7

questions.q: An engine has a compression ratio of 7:1. If the TDC volume is 12 cubic inches, what is the BDC volume?
questions.expl: BDC volume = compression ratio × TDC volume. BDC volume = 7 × 12 = 84 cubic inches.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.70 (Ratios — compression ratio) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-7

questions.q: A cylinder has a BDC volume of 100 cubic inches and a clearance volume of 20 cubic inches. What is the compression ratio?
questions.expl: Clearance volume is the TDC volume. Compression ratio = 100 ÷ 20 = 5:1.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.70 (Ratios — compression ratio) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-7

questions.q: A driving gear has 20 teeth and turns a driven gear with 40 teeth. What is the gear ratio?
questions.expl: Gear ratio = driven teeth ÷ driving teeth. Gear ratio = 40 ÷ 20 = 2:1.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73 (Ratios — gear ratio) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: A 20-tooth driving gear turns at 1,000 RPM and drives a 40-tooth gear. What is the driven gear speed?
questions.expl: Driven speed = driving speed × driving teeth ÷ driven teeth. Driven speed = 1,000 × 20 ÷ 40 = 500 RPM.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73-74 (gear/speed ratio worked example) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: A 60-tooth driving gear turns a 20-tooth driven gear. What happens to the driven gear speed?
questions.expl: A larger driving gear turning a smaller driven gear increases speed. The driving gear has 3 times as many teeth as the driven gear, so the driven gear turns 3 times faster.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73-74 (gear/speed ratio worked example) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: A gear train has a driving gear of 30 teeth and a driven gear of 90 teeth. The driving gear turns at 1,800 RPM. What is the driven gear speed?
questions.expl: Driven speed = driving speed × driving teeth ÷ driven teeth. Driven speed = 1,800 × 30 ÷ 90 = 600 RPM.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73-74 (gear/speed ratio worked example) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: A small gear with 25 teeth drives a larger gear with 100 teeth. What is the speed ratio?
questions.expl: Compare driven teeth to driving teeth for the reduction ratio. The driven gear has 100 teeth and the driver has 25 teeth, so 100:25 reduces to 4:1.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73 (speed ratio = reverse of gear ratio) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: A 10-tooth gear drives a 50-tooth gear. If the small gear turns at 2,500 RPM, what is the speed of the large gear?
questions.expl: Driven speed = driving speed × driving teeth ÷ driven teeth. Driven speed = 2,500 × 10 ÷ 50 = 500 RPM.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73-74 (gear/speed ratio worked example) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: A 72-tooth gear drives a 24-tooth gear. If the large gear turns at 900 RPM, what is the speed of the small gear?
questions.expl: The smaller driven gear turns faster. Driven speed = 900 × 72 ÷ 24 = 2,700 RPM.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.73-74 (gear/speed ratio worked example) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: Convert 3/8 to a decimal.
questions.expl: Divide numerator by denominator. 3 ÷ 8 = 0.375.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.67 (AM.I.H.S5) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-7

questions.q: Convert 0.75 to a fraction.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.66 (AM.I.H.S5) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-6

questions.q: A mixture ratio is 4 parts solvent to 1 part cleaner. How many total parts are in the mixture?
questions.expl: Add all parts in the ratio. Total parts = 4 + 1 = 5 parts.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.69 (AM.I.H.K4) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-7

questions.q: A drawing scale is 1 inch = 4 feet. A line on the drawing is 3 inches long. What real length does it represent?
questions.expl: Multiply drawing length by the scale. 3 inches × 4 feet per inch = 12 feet.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.76-77 (AM.I.H.K5, proportion) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.3-8

questions.q: In addition, the answer is called the:
questions.expl: Addition is the process in which the value of one number is added to another. The answer is called the sum.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.37 (AM.I.H.K13) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-2

questions.q: Multiplication is best described as the process of:
questions.expl: Multiplication is repeated addition: 4 x 3 is the same as 4 + 4 + 4. The result is called the product.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.41 (AM.I.H.K13) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-2

questions.q: In a fraction, the DENOMINATOR represents:
options: The parts of the whole being counted
The number of parts that make up the whole
questions.short: The number of parts that make up the whole
questions.expl: A fraction is N/D: N (numerator) is the parts of the whole number being counted; D (denominator) is the number of parts that make up the whole.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.45 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-4

questions.q: What is the FIRST step when adding or subtracting fractions with unlike denominators?
options: Convert each fraction to a percentage
questions.expl: The first step in adding or subtracting fractions is to find the Least Common Denominator (LCD).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.47 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-4

questions.expl: The LCD is 64: 3/32 = 6/64. Then 6/64 + 1/64 = 7/64. (This is the slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.51 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-4
BLK01 slides p.53 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-5

questions.q: Per the slide memory aid KEEP–SWITCH–FLIP, how do you divide 7/8 by 4/3?
questions.expl: Division of fractions: KEEP the first fraction, SWITCH division to multiplication, FLIP the second fraction. 7/8 ÷ 4/3 = 7/8 × 3/4 = 21/32.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.55 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-5

questions.q: 13/16 − 7/16 = 6/16. Expressed in lowest terms, the answer is:
questions.expl: 6/16 is not in lowest terms because 6 and 16 share a common factor of 2: 6÷2 / 16÷2 = 3/8.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.56 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-5

questions.q: A bolt is 3 1/8 inches long and the threaded portion is 1 5/16 inches. What is the grip length?
questions.expl: 3 1/8 − 1 5/16: convert to sixteenths — 3 2/16 − 1 5/16 = 2 18/16 − 1 5/16 = 1 13/16 inches (borrow from the whole number).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.59 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-6

questions.q: The decimal number system is a base-___ system.
questions.expl: The prefix dec is Latin for ten. The decimal system has ten digits (0-9) and is a base 10 system in use for over 5,000 years.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.60 (AM.I.H.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-6

questions.q: An actuator shaft is 2.1938 inches in diameter. Rounded to the nearest thousandth it is:
questions.expl: When the digit immediately to the right of the last retained digit is 5 or greater, round up by 1. The 8 after 2.193 rounds the 3 up to 4.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.65 (AM.I.H.K9, AM.I.H.R3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-7

questions.q: What is the rounding rule taught on the slide?
options: Always round toward zero for safety
Round up only when the digit is greater than 5 (a 5 rounds down)
Always round up when shortening a decimal
If the digit to the right of the last retained digit is 5 or greater, round up; if less than 5, leave it unchanged
questions.short: If the digit to the right of the last retained digit is 5 or greater, round up; if less than 5, leave it unchanged
questions.expl: Degree of accuracy desired determines digits retained. 5 or greater rounds up by 1; less than 5 leaves the retained digit unchanged.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.65 (AM.I.H.K9) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-7

questions.q: A ratio may be expressed in which three ways?
options: As a decimal, a percent, or a power
Only as a fraction or a percent
As a sum, a product, or a quotient
As a fraction, with a colon, or with the word 'to'
questions.short: As a fraction, with a colon, or with the word 'to'
questions.expl: A ratio is the comparison of two numbers or quantities: 5/7, 5:7, or 5 to 7.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.69 (AM.I.H.K4) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-7

questions.q: Compression ratio compares the cylinder volume with the piston at ___ to the volume with the piston at ___.
options: BDC (bottom of stroke); TDC (top of stroke)
questions.short: BDC (bottom of stroke); TDC (top of stroke)
questions.expl: Compression ratio = volume at Bottom Dead Center compared to volume at Top Dead Center. Example: 40 in³ BDC vs 4 in³ TDC = 10:1.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.70 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-7

questions.q: A wing has a span of 42 feet and a mean chord of 4 feet. What is its aspect ratio?
questions.expl: Aspect ratio is the ratio of the span of an airfoil to its mean chord: 42 ÷ 4 = 10.5, written 10.5:1. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.71 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-8

questions.q: Glide ratio is the ratio of:
options: Forward distance traveled to vertical distance descended (without power)
Lift produced to drag produced at altitude
Vertical distance descended to forward distance traveled
questions.short: Forward distance traveled to vertical distance descended (without power)
questions.expl: Glide ratio = horizontal distance ÷ vertical distance when operating without power. The slide example: 10,560 ft forward / 1,000 ft down = 10.56:1.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.72 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-8

questions.q: When two gears mesh, the gear with the HIGHER number of teeth:
questions.expl: As the number of teeth decreases, rotational speed increases. The gear with more teeth turns slower; the gear with fewer teeth rotates faster. The speed ratio is the reverse of the gear ratio.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.73 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-8

questions.q: In the proportion 3:4 = 6:8, the numbers 3 and 8 are called the:
questions.expl: A proportion is a statement of equality between two or more ratios. The outer numbers (3 and 8) are the extremes; the inner numbers (4 and 6) are the means.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.76 (AM.I.H.K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-8

questions.q: Express the fraction 5/8 as a percentage.
questions.expl: Change the fraction to a decimal (5 ÷ 8 = 0.625), then convert the decimal to a percentage: 62.5%.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.79 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-9

questions.q: A small engine rated at 12 horsepower is delivering only 10.75 horsepower. What is the motor efficiency as a percent?
questions.expl: N% × 12 = 10.75, so N% = 10.75 ÷ 12 = 0.8958 = 89.58%. (Slide's worked example, both algebra and proportion methods.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.80-81 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-9

questions.q: Denver is at −6°. The temperature at 15,000 ft is 20° colder. What is the temperature at 15,000 ft?
questions.expl: −6 − (+20) becomes −6 + (−20) = −26. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.85 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-10

questions.q: Any non-zero number raised to the zero power equals:
questions.expl: Power of zero: 4⁰ = 1, 7⁰ = 1, 284⁰ = 1 — any non-zero number to the zero power always equals 1.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.93 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-12

questions.q: When MULTIPLYING numbers with powers that have the same base, you ___ the powers.
questions.expl: Law of exponents: 3² × 3⁴ = 3⁶ = 729 (add when multiplying); 4⁵ ÷ 4³ = 4² = 16 (subtract when dividing).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.95-97 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-12

questions.q: The symbol √ placed over a number is called a:
questions.expl: The square root of 25 = 5; the symbol is the radical sign. Numbers whose square roots are whole numbers are called perfect squares.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.100 (AM.I.H.S1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-12

questions.q: The cube root of 125 is:
questions.expl: 5 cubed = 5 × 5 × 5 = 125, so the cube root of 125 equals 5.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.101 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-13

questions.q: The velocity of light, 186,000 miles per second, written in scientific notation is:
questions.expl: Scientific notation is shorthand for very large or very small numbers — one digit left of the decimal times a power of 10. 186,000 = 1.86 × 10⁵.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.104 (AM.I.H.K8) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-13

questions.q: Algebra is the branch of mathematics that:
options: Studies the angles and sides of triangles
Uses letters or symbols to represent variables in formulas and equations
questions.short: Uses letters or symbols to represent variables in formulas and equations
questions.expl: Algebra uses letters/symbols as variables. Aviation equations normally have an equals sign showing the relationship between variables.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.108 (AM.I.H.K6) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-16

questions.q: Solve for N: 3N = 21
questions.expl: Whatever you do to one side you do to the other: divide both sides by 3, N = 21 ÷ 3 = 7.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.109 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-16

questions.q: In the order of operations (PEMDAS), what is evaluated FIRST?
options: Everything inside parentheses, starting from the innermost
questions.short: Everything inside parentheses, starting from the innermost
questions.expl: Order: Parentheses, Exponents (roots count as exponents), Multiplication and Division left-to-right, then Addition and Subtraction left-to-right.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.111-112 (AM.I.H.R1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.3 p.3-17

questions.q: If a driven gear rotates slower than the driving gear, what usually happens to torque?
questions.expl: Speed reduction usually gives torque multiplication. A gear reduction lowers speed but increases torque, ignoring friction losses.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.197 (the gear) & p.73 · FAA-H-8083-30B pp.5-10/5-11
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A mechanic applies a force of 50 pounds through a distance of 10 feet. How much work is done?
questions.expl: Use work = force × distance. Work = 50 × 10 = 500 foot-pounds.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.188 (AM.I.J.K2, Work = F × D) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-4
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: An engine does 11,000 ft-lb of work in 10 seconds. What is the power in ft-lb/sec?
questions.expl: Power = work ÷ time. Power = 11,000 ÷ 10 = 1,100 ft-lb/sec.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.190 (AM.I.J.K2, P = F×D/T) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-6
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: An engine produces 1,100 ft-lb/sec of power. How many horsepower is this?
questions.expl: 1 horsepower = 550 ft-lb/sec. Horsepower = 1,100 ÷ 550 = 2 hp.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.191 (1 hp = 550 ft-lb/s) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-6
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Which simple machine is used when a ramp helps move equipment to a higher level?
questions.expl: A ramp spreads the work over a longer distance. A ramp is an inclined plane.
questions.cite: Your class bank (verbatim) · BLK01 slides p.197 & 212 (AM.I.J.K3, inclined plane) · FAA-H-8083-30B p.5-11
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Physics studies how matter and energy behave. What does it NOT attempt to determine?
options: How energy relates to physical phenomena
WHY matter and energy behave as they do
How matter behaves in relation to physical phenomena
questions.short: WHY matter and energy behave as they do
questions.expl: Physics does not attempt to determine why matter and energy behave as they do in relation to physical phenomena, but rather HOW they behave.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.170 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-2
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: According to the Law of Conservation, matter:
options: Can be created but never destroyed
Can be destroyed only by chemical means
Is created whenever energy is released
Cannot be created or destroyed, but its physical state can change
questions.short: Cannot be created or destroyed, but its physical state can change
questions.expl: Matter is whatever occupies space, has mass, and is perceptible to the senses. Per the Law of Conservation it cannot be created or destroyed, though its physical state can change.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.171 (AM.I.J.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-2
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: The smallest particle of a COMPOUND is:
questions.expl: Matter is composed of atoms; atoms create molecules. The molecule is the smallest particle of a compound.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.172 (AM.I.J.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-2
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: An ELEMENT is a substance that:
options: Can only exist as a gas
Cannot be reduced to a simpler form by chemical means
Contains two or more kinds of atoms
questions.short: Cannot be reduced to a simpler form by chemical means
questions.expl: An element (iron, gold, silver, copper, oxygen) cannot be reduced to a simpler form by chemical means — beyond that point it ceases to be what it is.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.173 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-2
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Mass equals:
questions.expl: Mass = Weight ÷ Gravity, and Weight = Mass × Gravity. Mass is the amount of matter in an object and is constant anywhere in the universe.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.174 (AM.I.J.K12) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: The acceleration due to gravity used in these formulas is:
questions.expl: Acceleration due to gravity = 32.2 feet per second per second (32.2 ft/sec²).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.174 (AM.I.J.K12) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Density is defined as:
questions.expl: Density = weight per unit volume, expressed in lb/ft³ (U.S.) or g/cm³. Divide weight by volume to find it.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.178 (AM.I.J.K12) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: The standard substance used when comparing the specific gravity of all liquids and solids is:
questions.expl: Specific gravity compares the weight/density of a substance to an equal volume of water at 4°C. (For gases the standard is air.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.179-180 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: The instrument used to check the specific gravity of liquids is the:
questions.expl: A hydrometer checks liquids against the pure-water standard of 1.000.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.181 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: An aircraft lead-acid battery is FULLY CHARGED when its electrolyte specific gravity reads:
questions.expl: Fully charged = 1.275 to 1.310. A reading of 1.150 shows the battery is in a discharged state.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.181 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Energy is defined as:
questions.expl: Energy is the ability to do work, classified as potential (stored) or kinetic (motion).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.183 (AM.I.J.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Which is NOT one of the three groups of potential energy from the slide?
options: Energy due to velocity of a moving body
Energy which produces work through chemical action
Energy due to distortion of an elastic body
questions.short: Energy due to velocity of a moving body
questions.expl: Potential energy groups: position, distortion of an elastic body, and chemical action. Energy of motion is KINETIC energy.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.184 (AM.I.J.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-3
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: The formula for kinetic energy is:
questions.expl: Kinetic energy is energy due to motion: KE = ½MV².
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.186 (AM.I.J.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-4
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Force is defined on the slide as:
options: A push or pull that causes a change in motion (F = Mass × Acceleration)
questions.short: A push or pull that causes a change in motion (F = Mass × Acceleration)
questions.expl: Force = a push or pull causing a change in motion; F = M × A.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.187 (AM.I.J.K2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-4
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Mechanical advantage can be computed as:
options: Force out ÷ Force in (or distance out ÷ distance in)
questions.short: Force out ÷ Force in (or distance out ÷ distance in)
questions.expl: MA = Resistance/Effort = Force Out/Force In = Distance Out/Distance In = Effort Arm/Resistance Arm.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.187 & 196 (AM.I.J.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-7
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: 100 lbs is moved 100 feet. The work done is:
questions.expl: Work = Force × Distance = 100 × 100 = 10,000 ft-lbs. (In metric, 100 N moved 100 m = 10,000 joules.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.188 (AM.I.J.K2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-4
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Which type of friction is always LESS than starting (static) friction?
questions.expl: Sliding friction — the resistance after the object has been set in motion — is always less than starting friction. Rolling friction (wheels/rollers) is less still.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.189 (AM.I.J.K2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-5
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: One horsepower equals:
options: 33,000 ft-lb per minute (550 ft-lb per second)
questions.short: 33,000 ft-lb per minute (550 ft-lb per second)
questions.expl: 1 hp = 550 ft-lb/s = 33,000 ft-lb/min = 746 watts (electrical conversion).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.191 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-6
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Torque differs from work in that torque is:
options: Rotational in nature, expressed in lb-in or lb-ft
questions.short: Rotational in nature, expressed in lb-in or lb-ft
questions.expl: Torque = Force × Distance but is rotational; torque units are lb-in or lb-ft, while work units are in-lb or ft-lb.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.192 (AM.I.J.K2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-6
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A machine can multiply force or multiply speed, but it cannot:
options: Change the direction of a force
Transfer energy from one place to another
Transform energy from one form to another
Multiply force and speed at the same time
questions.short: Multiply force and speed at the same time
questions.expl: To gain one it must lose the other — otherwise more power would come out than goes in, which is not possible.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.196 (AM.I.J.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-7
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Which list contains the six simple machines from the slide?
options: Lever, pulley, wheel and axle, inclined plane, screw, gear
Pulley, wheel, axle, wedge, crank, screw
Lever, wedge, piston, screw, gear, crank
Lever, pulley, ramp, spring, cam, gear
questions.short: Lever, pulley, wheel and axle, inclined plane, screw, gear
questions.expl: There are only six simple machines: the lever, the pulley, the wheel and axle, the inclined plane, the screw, and the gear.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.197 (AM.I.J.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-7
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: For a bolt (a screw), the EFFORT ARM is:
options: The length of the bolt shank
The diameter of the bolt head
questions.expl: On a screw, the circumference is the effort arm and the distance between the threads is the resistance arm.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.197 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-11
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A first-class lever has an effort arm (L) of 8 feet and a resistance arm (I) of 2 feet. What effort lifts a 220-lb weight?
questions.expl: L/I = R/E → 8/2 = 220/E → E = (2 × 220) ÷ 8 = 55 lbs. Mechanical advantage = 8/2 = 4:1. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.198 (AM.I.J.K3, AM.I.J.S5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-8
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Box 1 (300 lbs) sits 32 inches left of the fulcrum. Box 2 sits 160 inches right of the fulcrum. For balance, Box 2 must weigh:
questions.expl: For balance the moments must be equal: 300 × 32 = 9,600 in-lbs. Then 9,600 ÷ 160 = 60 lbs. Weight × Arm = Moment (WAM). (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.199 (AM.I.J.S5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-8
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A wheelbarrow — with the resistance between the fulcrum and the effort — is an example of a:
questions.expl: First class = fulcrum between effort and resistance (see-saw); second class = resistance in the middle (wheelbarrow); third class = effort in the middle (your forearm).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.200 & 202 (AM.I.J.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-8
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: In a THIRD-class lever, such as your forearm, the applied effort is:
options: Greater than the resistance, in order to gain speed/distance
questions.short: Greater than the resistance, in order to gain speed/distance
questions.expl: Slide example: R = 200 lbs, L = 0.5 ft, I = 4 ft → E = 1,600 lbs. Third-class levers trade large effort for speed and distance of the resistance end.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.201-202 (AM.I.J.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-9
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A single FIXED pulley operates as what class of lever, and with what mechanical advantage?
questions.expl: A single fixed pulley = class 1 lever with MA 1:1 — it only changes the direction of the force. A single MOVABLE pulley = class 2 lever with MA 2:1.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.204 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-9
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: What determines the mechanical advantage of a block and tackle?
options: The total number of pulleys, fixed and movable
The number of ropes supporting the weight
questions.short: The number of ropes supporting the weight
questions.expl: MA = number of weight-supporting ropes. Slide example: 4 supporting ropes → MA of 4 → a 200-lb weight needs only a 50-lb effort.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.205 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-10
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A 500-lb engine is moved up a 12-ft ramp onto a platform 4 ft high. The required effort is approximately:
questions.expl: Inclined plane: L/I = R/E → 12/4 = 500/E → E = (4 × 500) ÷ 12 = 166.7 lbs. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.212 (AM.I.J.K3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-11
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: When an external force acts on a body, the internal force that opposes it is known as:
questions.expl: Stress is the internal force opposing an external force, typically measured in pounds per square inch (psi). It manifests in one of five forms.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.214 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-12
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Which list gives the five forms of stress?
questions.expl: External force causes stress to manifest in one of five forms: tension (pulls apart), compression (pushes together), torsion (twist), bending, and shear (slices).
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Torsion stress, applied when a material is twisted, is a combination of:
questions.expl: Torsion is applied when a material is twisted; it is a combination of tension and compression stresses.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.217 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-12
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: In FLIGHT, lift bends the wing upward. The wing skin experiences:
options: Tension on top, compression on the bottom
Compression on top, tension on the bottom
Torsion on top, tension on the bottom
questions.short: Compression on top, tension on the bottom
questions.expl: In flight, lift raising the wing compresses the TOP skin and puts the BOTTOM skin in tension. On the ground it reverses: gravity bends the wing down — top in tension, bottom in compression.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.218-219 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-13
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: A clevis bolt in an aircraft control system is designed to carry which stress?
questions.expl: Shear stress tries to slice a body apart — the clevis bolt is under shear (cutting) stress.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.220 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-13
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: When stress distorts (bends) a body, the resulting distortion is called:
questions.expl: Strain is a consequence of stress: when stress is applied and the body becomes distorted, the result is strain (e.g., the deflecting-beam torque wrench).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.221 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-13
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Velocity differs from speed because velocity is:
options: The rate of change of acceleration
The object's speed in a given direction
questions.short: The object's speed in a given direction
questions.expl: Speed = distance ÷ time. Velocity = speed in a given direction. Acceleration = the rate of change of velocity.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.224 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-14
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Newton's FIRST law — objects at rest stay at rest and objects in motion stay in motion unless acted on by an external force — is known as:
options: The law of action and reaction
questions.expl: First law = inertia. Second law = momentum (rate of change of momentum proportional to applied force). Third law = action and reaction.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.225 (AM.I.J.K6) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-15
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: 'For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction' is Newton's:
questions.expl: Newton's third law — the law of action and reaction. (It is also the basis for jet/rocket propulsion and part of how wings make lift.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.227 (AM.I.J.K6) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-15
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: In circular motion, the force that HOLDS an object within its orbit (acting toward the center) is:
questions.expl: Centripetal force (X to C) holds the object within the orbit; centrifugal force (X to Y) tries to throw the object out of orbit.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.228 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.5 p.5-16
sources: Block 1 Physics: Matter, Energy & Machines — Quiz

questions.q: Which micrometer parts does the measured object sit between?
options: The ratchet stop and the barrel
The frame and the lock nut
questions.expl: Micrometer parts: frame, anvil, spindle, sleeve, thimble, ratchet stop, lock nut. The work sits between the fixed anvil and the moving spindle.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.436 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-17

questions.q: When weighing to determine empty weight, the only fuel weight included should be:
questions.expl: Three acceptable conditions: (1) no fuel at all, (2) tanks drained per the manufacturer leaving only residual fuel, or (3) tanks completely full with the fuel weight calculated and subtracted (hydrometer).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.570 (AM.I.C.K8/S8/S10) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-8

questions.q: For aircraft certificated AFTER 1978 under 14 CFR Part 23, engine oil is treated how during weighing?
options: It is useful load — the tank must be drained
It is part of the EMPTY weight — the oil tank must be serviced FULL before weighing
questions.short: It is part of the EMPTY weight — the oil tank must be serviced FULL before weighing
questions.expl: Post-1978 (14 CFR 23.29): oil is part of empty weight — service it full. Pre-1978 aircraft (certified under CAR Part 3): oil is part of the USEFUL LOAD — drain it or calculate it out.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.571 (AM.I.C.K8/S8) · 14 CFR 23.29 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-9

questions.q: PERMANENT ballast is:
options: Sandbags strapped in the baggage bay
Removable lead shot in canvas bags
Any fuel left in the aft tank
Lead blocks permanently attached to the structure, painted RED, marked 'PERMANENT BALLAST — DO NOT REMOVE'
questions.short: Lead blocks permanently attached to the structure, painted RED, marked 'PERMANENT BALLAST — DO NOT REMOVE'
questions.expl: Permanent ballast corrects an empty-weight CG out-of-limits condition after a repair/alteration; it becomes part of the aircraft's empty weight.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.612 (AM.I.C.K9) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-23

questions.q: 'Basic Empty Weight' differs from 'Licensed Empty Weight' in that basic empty weight includes:
options: Full engine oil (licensed empty weight includes only residual oil)
questions.short: Full engine oil (licensed empty weight includes only residual oil)
questions.expl: Empty weight includes the airframe, powerplant, required/optional equipment, fixed ballast, hydraulic fluid, and residual fuel and oil. Basic empty weight = full oil; licensed empty weight = residual oil only; standard empty weight = a value supplied by the manufacturer.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.544 (AM.I.C.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-4

questions.q: Small hole gauges measure inside diameters of holes from:
questions.expl: The ball end is inserted in the hole and the shaft twisted to expand the ball until it fits; the ball is then measured with a micrometer.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.463 (AM.I.K.K1, AM.I.K.S3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-20

questions.q: In a FORWARD adverse-loaded CG check, which items are loaded?
options: Only items behind the aft limit
All useful-load items FORWARD of the forward CG limit (items behind it left empty); METO fuel if the tank is behind the limit
questions.short: All useful-load items FORWARD of the forward CG limit (items behind it left empty); METO fuel if the tank is behind the limit
questions.expl: Forward extreme: load everything ahead of the forward limit, leave everything aft of it empty; if fuel is located behind the forward CG limit, use METO fuel (engine HP ÷ 2 in lbs).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.599-600 (AM.I.C.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-20

questions.q: TEMPORARY ballast must be marked:
options: 'Ballast XX pounds — Removal Requires Weight and Balance Check'
questions.short: 'Ballast XX pounds — Removal Requires Weight and Balance Check'
questions.expl: Temporary ballast (lead bars, sand or lead shot in heavy canvas bags) adjusts CG for a flight-loaded condition, must be secured against movement, removed before weighing, and marked 'Ballast XX pounds — Removal Requires Weight and Balance Check.'
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.607 (AM.I.C.K9/S3/S15) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-22

questions.q: The FAA requires the weight and balance report to be:
options: Current and accurate, part of the permanent records, and IN the aircraft when it is flown
Kept by the mechanic at the shop
questions.short: Current and accurate, part of the permanent records, and IN the aircraft when it is flown
questions.expl: A current, accurate empty weight and EWCG must be known; the W&B report is part of the aircraft permanent records and must be in the aircraft when it is being flown.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.629 (AM.I.C.S7/S17) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-31

questions.q: The sleeve shows 0.200 plus two small divisions, and the thimble reads 0. What is the reading?
questions.expl: 0.200 + (2 × 0.025) = 0.250 in. When thimble '0' lines up with the reference line, the thimble adds nothing. (Slide's worked example.)

questions.q: The micrometer THIMBLE scale is divided into 25 parts. Each part equals ___ and one full revolution moves ___.
questions.expl: Thimble: 25 divisions × 0.001 in each; one revolution = 0.025 in — exactly one sleeve division.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.444 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: The ballast weight formula is:
options: B = (distance needed to shift CG × aircraft total weight) ÷ distance between ballast location and desired CG
questions.short: B = (distance needed to shift CG × aircraft total weight) ÷ distance between ballast location and desired CG
questions.expl: Ballast = (D × W) ÷ X. Slide example: CG out by 0.6" on a 3,034-lb plane, ballast station at +60 vs desired CG 99: B = (0.6 × 3,034) ÷ (99 − 60) = 1,820.4 ÷ 39 = 46.67 → 47 lbs.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.609 (AM.I.C.K9/S3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-23

questions.q: Scales and rules are used where:
options: Tolerances are under one thousandth of an inch
questions.expl: Scales and rules are non-precision layout/measuring tools. Scales for sheet metal work are graduated in 32nds and 64ths; rulers used in homebuilding are normally in 16ths.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.424 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-14

questions.q: Scales used for SHEET METAL work are graduated in:
questions.expl: Sheet metal scales read in 32nds and 64ths; homebuilding rulers are normally in 16ths. Decimal and metric scales are also used.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.424 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-14

questions.q: On aircraft, protractors are used to measure:
options: Flight control surface travel (angles in degrees)
questions.short: Flight control surface travel (angles in degrees)
questions.expl: Protractors measure angles — on aircraft they measure flight control surface travel (e.g., the blade protractor).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.426 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-15

questions.q: A combination set consists of a 12-inch steel scale and which three removable heads?
options: Stock head, protractor head, and center head
Dial head, vernier head, and depth head
Square head, level head, and clamp head
Anvil head, spindle head, and thimble head
questions.short: Stock head, protractor head, and center head
questions.expl: The combination set's three heads move along the scale and may be used in any combination: stock head (90° and 45° angles), protractor head (degrees + bubble level), and center head (finds the center of round stock).

questions.q: Which head of the combination set is used to find the center of round bar stock?
questions.expl: Center head = centers of round objects. Stock head = 90° and 45° angles. Protractor head = degrees on control surfaces, and it contains a bubble level.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.430 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-15

questions.q: The three types of calipers named on the slide are:
questions.expl: Inside calipers measure the inside diameter of a hole; outside calipers measure outside diameters; hermaphrodite calipers mark distances away from edges.

questions.q: Hermaphrodite calipers are used to:
options: Measure the inside diameter of holes
questions.expl: Hermaphrodite calipers (one bent leg, one scribe leg) lay out lines a set distance from an edge.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.433 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-16

questions.q: Which micrometer type is used to measure the DEPTH of a hole?
questions.expl: Outside micrometer = fit of engine parts / thickness; inside micrometer = inside size of a hole; depth micrometer = depth of a hole.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.435 (AM.I.K.K1, AM.I.K.S2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-17

questions.q: Before use, a micrometer must be calibrated to zero using:
options: A gauge block and a spanner wrench
questions.short: A gauge block and a spanner wrench
questions.expl: Calibrate to zero with a gauge block and spanner wrench — and always turn the thimble using the ratchet stop to apply proper, repeatable pressure.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.437 (AM.I.K.K1, AM.I.K.K2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: On the micrometer SLEEVE scale, each numbered division equals ___ and each small division between numbers equals ___.
questions.expl: The 1-inch sleeve scale is divided into ten numbered parts (tenths, 0.100) and each tenth into four parts of 0.025 inch.

questions.q: Sleeve shows 0.200 + one division (0.025); thimble reads 1. The micrometer reading is:
questions.expl: 0.200 + 0.025 + 0.001 = 0.226 in. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.451 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: Sleeve shows 0.500 + one division; thimble reads 21. The reading is:
questions.expl: 0.500 + 0.025 + 0.021 = 0.546 in. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.452 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: A standard (non-vernier) micrometer can only read dimensions to the nearest:
questions.expl: A regular micrometer reads in thousandths; if a thimble line doesn't align with the reference line you estimate or neglect the remainder. Vernier micrometers add a ten-thousandths (0.0001) scale.

questions.q: A vernier micrometer reads: sleeve 0.300 + three divisions (0.075), thimble 20, vernier 6. The reading is:
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.457 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-19

questions.q: A 5-6 inch micrometer shows sleeve 0.400 + one division and thimble 16. The actual measurement is:
questions.expl: A 5-6 micrometer measures the range 5 to 6 inches, so add 5 inches: 5 + 0.400 + 0.025 + 0.016 = 5.441 in. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.458 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-19

questions.q: Micrometer calipers are used to measure the thickness of objects and to check cylindrical objects for:
questions.expl: Micrometers measure thickness and check cylindrical objects (like engine parts) for out-of-roundness. Types: outside, inside, and depth micrometers.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.435 (AM.I.K.K1, AM.I.K.S2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-17

questions.q: When closing a micrometer on a part, you should always turn the thimble using the:
options: Ratchet stop, to apply the proper pressure
questions.short: Ratchet stop, to apply the proper pressure
questions.expl: Always turn the thimble using the ratchet stop in order to apply the proper, repeatable pressure to the object or gauge block.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.437 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: The protractor head of a combination set measures degrees on control surfaces and also contains a:
questions.expl: The protractor head measures degrees (control surface travel) and contains a bubble level for checking level conditions.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.429 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-15

questions.q: The arrow on the sleeve sits at the .300 mark plus ONE small division. The sleeve reading is:
questions.expl: Each small sleeve division between numbers is 25/1000 (0.025): 0.300 + 0.025 = 0.325. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.440 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: Sleeve shows .300 plus two small divisions; the thimble is at 0. The reading is:
questions.expl: 0.300 + 0.050 = 0.350 in. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.442 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-18

questions.q: Sleeve reads 0.300 + two divisions (0.050) and the thimble reads 15, but the thimble line doesn't align exactly. A standard micrometer reads:
options: 0.365 inch — estimate or neglect any distance beyond the closest .001 line
questions.short: 0.365 inch — estimate or neglect any distance beyond the closest .001 line
questions.expl: 0.300 + 0.050 + 0.015 = 0.365. With a regular micrometer, additional distance between thimble lines must be estimated using the closest line or entirely neglected. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.453 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-19

questions.q: A VERNIER micrometer reads: sleeve 0.200 + one division, thimble 22, vernier 0. The reading is:
questions.expl: 0.200 + 0.025 + 0.022 + 0.0000 = 0.2470 in. The vernier scale adds the fourth decimal (ten-thousandths). (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.456 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-19

questions.q: Dial indicators are used to check shafts for:
questions.expl: Dial indicators measure in thousandths and ten-thousandths of an inch; the base holds the dial in place while the shaft is rotated.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.459 (AM.I.K.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-20

questions.q: Per the slide, torque wrenches must be calibrated:
options: Every five years regardless of use
Annually — or any time the tool is dropped
questions.short: Annually — or any time the tool is dropped
questions.expl: All precision tools carry a calibration decal; calibration intervals vary by tool. Torque wrenches: annually, or when dropped. ALL precision tools must be recalibrated when dropped.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.460 (AM.I.K.K2, AM.I.K.R3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (calibration)

questions.q: Calibration is defined as:
options: Replacing a tool at the end of its service life
Cleaning and lubricating a precision tool
A set of documented operations comparing an instrument's measurements to a standard, to detect, report, or eliminate errors by adjustment
Reading a tool twice and averaging
questions.short: A set of documented operations comparing an instrument's measurements to a standard, to detect, report, or eliminate errors by adjustment
questions.expl: Calibration compares the measurements performed by an instrument against a standard, for the purpose of detecting and reporting — or eliminating by adjustment — errors in the instrument tested.

questions.q: Telescoping gauges accurately measure holes with diameters between:
questions.expl: T-shaped telescoping gauges: spring-loaded arms extend against the hole walls, lock, and are removed; the arm length is then measured with a micrometer.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.461-462 (AM.I.K.K1, AM.I.K.S3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-20

questions.q: How does a telescoping gauge transfer its measurement?
options: It stamps the dimension on the part
It has its own built-in dial readout
The locked arms are removed from the hole and measured with a micrometer
It is read directly off the gauge's vernier scale
questions.short: The locked arms are removed from the hole and measured with a micrometer
questions.expl: Spring pressure extends the arms against the hole walls; the gauge is locked, removed, and the arm length measured with a micrometer.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.462 (AM.I.K.S3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 p.11-20

questions.q: The PRIMARY reason for an aircraft's weight and balance requirements is:
questions.expl: Safety is the primary reason; aircraft efficiency is also a factor. Maximum weight is designed in based on the lift the wings or rotors can produce.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.530 (AM.I.C.K2/K5/K6) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-1

questions.q: The manufacturer is only required to actually WEIGH what fraction of each aircraft model produced?
questions.expl: The manufacturer creates a W&B report for each aircraft but is only required to weigh 10-20% of each model; the rest use computed values.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.533 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-2

questions.q: Which aircraft are required to be weighed and have CG calculated periodically (typically every 3 years)?
options: Part 135 air taxi/charter twins, and airplanes with 20+ passenger seats or 6,000+ lbs payload (Part 125)
questions.short: Part 135 air taxi/charter twins, and airplanes with 20+ passenger seats or 6,000+ lbs payload (Part 125)
questions.expl: Periodic reweigh examples: 14 CFR 135.185(a) multiengine air taxi/charter, and 14 CFR 125.91(b) airplanes with seating for 20+ or max payload 6,000 lbs+.

questions.q: Does a small GA aircraft like a Cessna 172 have an FAA requirement for periodic reweighing?
options: Yes — every annual inspection it must go on scales
No — but the mechanic must ensure the W&B data is correct at each annual inspection
questions.short: No — but the mechanic must ensure the W&B data is correct at each annual inspection
questions.expl: Small private GA aircraft have NO periodic reweigh requirement. But the aircraft must be weighed if the W&B report is lost, or after extensive repair, major alteration, or new equipment installation. Over time almost all aircraft tend to GAIN weight.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.535 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-2

questions.q: Weight and balance data can be found in all of the following EXCEPT:
options: The Aircraft Specification or Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS)
The aircraft weight and balance report
The aircraft flight manual / operating limitations
questions.expl: W&B data sources: TCDS/Aircraft Specification, aircraft operating limitations, aircraft flight manual, and the W&B report.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.536 (AM.I.C.S16) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-3

questions.q: The DATUM is:
options: A removable weight used during weighing
An imaginary vertical plane from which all horizontal measurements are taken with the aircraft in level flight attitude
The physical balance point of the aircraft
The centerline of the main wheels
questions.short: An imaginary vertical plane from which all horizontal measurements are taken with the aircraft in level flight attitude
questions.expl: The manufacturer establishes the datum location (found in the Aircraft Specifications or TCDS). The datum is the 'zero arm' location.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.537 (AM.I.C.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-3

questions.q: The ARM is:
options: The horizontal distance, in inches, that an item is located from the datum (may be + or −)
The vertical height of an item above the floor
Always a positive distance from the nose
The weight of an item times its distance
questions.short: The horizontal distance, in inches, that an item is located from the datum (may be + or −)
questions.expl: Arm = horizontal distance from the datum, always measured in inches; negative ahead of the datum, positive behind it.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.539 (AM.I.C.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-3

questions.q: The MOMENT is the product of:
questions.expl: W × A = M. Also: A = M ÷ W and W = M ÷ A. Moments are expressed in inch-pounds (in-lb).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.540 (AM.I.C.K1/K4) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-3

questions.q: The center of gravity (CG) is the point about which:
options: The wings attach to the fuselage
The nose-heavy and tail-heavy moments are exactly equal
questions.short: The nose-heavy and tail-heavy moments are exactly equal
questions.expl: CG = the balance point. Total Moment ÷ Total Weight = CG (for the empty aircraft, the empty weight CG).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.541 (AM.I.C.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-3

questions.q: All weight and balance calculations — loading, equipment change, adverse checks — BEGIN with:
options: The empty weight and the empty weight center of gravity (EWCG)
questions.short: The empty weight and the empty weight center of gravity (EWCG)
questions.expl: The EWCG is the point where the aircraft balances in its empty weight condition; every other W&B calculation starts there.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.545 (AM.I.C.K1/K6) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-4

questions.q: USEFUL LOAD is the difference between:
options: The empty weight and the maximum weight of the aircraft
The forward and aft CG limits
The ramp weight and the landing weight
questions.short: The empty weight and the maximum weight of the aircraft
questions.expl: Useful load = the amount of weight the aircraft is designed to carry = maximum weight − empty weight.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.546 (AM.I.C.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-4

questions.q: MINIMUM FUEL for weight and balance purposes (METO) is calculated as:
options: Maximum continuous engine horsepower ÷ 2, stated in POUNDS of fuel
Half the tank capacity in gallons
questions.short: Maximum continuous engine horsepower ÷ 2, stated in POUNDS of fuel
questions.expl: METO (Maximum Except Take-Off): minimum fuel = max continuous HP ÷ 2, in pounds. Example: 180 HP ÷ 2 = 90 lbs of fuel.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.547 (AM.I.C.K1) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-4

questions.q: TARE weight is:
options: The weight of items used to weigh the aircraft that are not part of it (chocks, ground locks) — subtracted from the scale readings
The weight of the empty scales
questions.short: The weight of items used to weigh the aircraft that are not part of it (chocks, ground locks) — subtracted from the scale readings
questions.expl: Tare = chocks, ground locks, hoists, etc. on the scales during weighing; their weight must be subtracted from the readings.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.548 (AM.I.C.K1, AM.I.C.S10) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-5

questions.q: The standard weights used for aircraft W&B are:
options: AvGas 6.7 · turbine fuel 6 · oil 8.35 · water 7.5 · people 180
AvGas 8.35 · turbine fuel 7.5 · oil 6 · water 6.7 · people 170
AvGas 6 lbs/gal · turbine fuel 6.7 lbs/gal · oil 7.5 lbs/gal · water 8.35 lbs/gal · crew/passengers 170 lbs each
questions.short: AvGas 6 lbs/gal · turbine fuel 6.7 lbs/gal · oil 7.5 lbs/gal · water 8.35 lbs/gal · crew/passengers 170 lbs each
questions.expl: Aviation weight standards: AvGas 6 lb/gal, turbine fuel (Jet A/B, JP5) 6.7 lb/gal, lubricating oil 7.5 lb/gal, water 8.35 lb/gal, crew and passengers 170 lb per person.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.549 & 580 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-5

questions.q: A plane weighs: right main 755 lbs @ 108", nose 440 lbs @ 42", left main 781 lbs @ 108". The CG is:
questions.expl: Moments: 81,540 + 18,480 + 84,348 = 184,368 in-lb. Total weight 1,976 lbs. CG = 184,368 ÷ 1,976 = 93.30 in. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.555 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-5

questions.q: Which weight and balance information is in the TCDS?
options: CG range, maximum weight, number of seats, baggage/fuel/oil capacity, datum and leveling means, and the oil/fuel included in empty weight
The serial number of every part installed
questions.short: CG range, maximum weight, number of seats, baggage/fuel/oil capacity, datum and leveling means, and the oil/fuel included in empty weight
questions.expl: TCDS W&B items: engine, CG range, max weight, seats, baggage capacity, fuel capacity, oil capacity, datum information, leveling means, oil in empty weight, fuel in empty weight.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.556-558 (AM.I.C.R2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-6

questions.q: Why must an aircraft be weighed INSIDE a hangar?
options: To keep the public from watching
So wind cannot blow over the surfaces and cause fluctuating or false scale readings
So the temperature stays at exactly 59°F
questions.short: So wind cannot blow over the surfaces and cause fluctuating or false scale readings
questions.expl: Weigh inside a hangar — wind over the wings causes lift and false/fluctuating readings.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.568 (AM.I.C.R2/R3) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-8

questions.q: Which is a jacking safety rule from the slide?
options: Jack one wheel at a time for stability
Angle the jacks outward under load
Raise all jacks simultaneously and keep the safety collars screwed down against the jack cylinder
Let crew move around inside during jacking
questions.short: Raise all jacks simultaneously and keep the safety collars screwed down against the jack cylinder
questions.expl: Jacking safety: stress plates if specified, no movement inside the aircraft, jacks straight under the jack pads, raise all jacks simultaneously, and keep safety collars screwed down to prevent tipping if a jack loses pressure.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.574 (AM.I.C.K3/K10) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-10

questions.q: When weighing from the wheels, leveling is normally done by:
options: Shimming the scales with wood blocks
Adjusting the air pressure in the nosewheel shock strut
questions.short: Adjusting the air pressure in the nosewheel shock strut
questions.expl: Reference the TCDS for leveling means. With load cells on jacks, level with the jacks; on wheels, adjust nosewheel/shock strut air pressure. The aircraft must be in level flight attitude so all components are the correct distance from the datum. A spirit level or plumb bob is used to check level.

questions.q: On some aircraft, as loaded weight increases the CG RANGE gets smaller because:
options: The aft limit moves forward while the forward limit stays
The forward limit moves back while the aft limit stays in the same place
questions.short: The forward limit moves back while the aft limit stays in the same place
questions.expl: The CG range (forward limit to aft limit, in inches from the datum, found in the TCDS) can shrink with weight: the forward limit moves aft.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.578 (AM.I.C.K3/K10) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-11

questions.q: In the EWCG formulas, what does L stand for?
options: The distance between the nose wheel (or tail wheel) and the main wheels
The leading edge of the wing
The total length of the aircraft
The lateral arm to the butt line
questions.short: The distance between the nose wheel (or tail wheel) and the main wheels
questions.expl: D = datum to main wheels, F = forward (nose) wheel weight, R = aft (tail) wheel weight, L = distance between nose/tail wheel and main wheels, W = total weight.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.585 (AM.I.C.K8, AM.I.C.S2) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 pp.6-16/6-20

questions.q: Which formula applies to a NOSEWHEEL airplane with the datum FORWARD of the main wheels?
questions.expl: Formula 1: nosewheel, datum forward of mains: CG = D − (F×L/W). Formula 2: nosewheel, datum aft: CG = −(D + F×L/W). Formula 3: tailwheel, datum forward: CG = D + (R×L/W). Formula 4: tailwheel, datum aft: CG = −D + (R×L/W).

questions.q: Nosewheel airplane, datum forward of mains: D = 46", nose wheel weight F = 340 lbs, L = 78", total weight W = 2,006 lbs. The CG is:
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.586 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-18

questions.q: Who is ULTIMATELY responsible for the weight and balance of a loaded airplane before flight?
questions.expl: The mechanic must know how to calculate W&B, but the PIC is ultimately responsible and makes the final decision on whether the airplane is safe to fly.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.595 (AM.I.C.K5) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-19

questions.q: The slide's Piper Seneca loading example totals 4,225 lbs with CG at 96.42". Limits: max takeoff 4,200 lbs, aft CG limit 94.6". The aircraft is:
options: Legal if the pilot files a waiver
NOT safe or legal to fly — both overweight and CG aft of the limit
Safe once the gear is retracted
questions.short: NOT safe or legal to fly — both overweight and CG aft of the limit
questions.expl: Loaded CG 96.42 exceeds the aft limit of 94.6, and 4,225 is 25 lbs over max weight. Both conditions must be corrected before flight. (Slide's worked example.)

questions.q: WHEN must adverse-loaded CG checks be performed?
options: Only when the aircraft is repainted
After any repair or alteration that changes the aircraft's weight and balance
questions.short: After any repair or alteration that changes the aircraft's weight and balance
questions.expl: After a W&B-changing repair/alteration, the mechanic or repairman must ensure no LEGAL loading condition can move the CG outside its limits — performing adverse-loaded CG checks and noting results in the revised W&B sheet.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.598 (AM.I.C.K5/K7/S6) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-20

questions.q: In an AFT adverse-loaded CG check, which occupant is ALWAYS included?
questions.expl: Aft extreme: all useful-load items behind the aft CG limit fully loaded; everything forward of the aft limit left empty EXCEPT the pilot. Fill the fuel tank that sits aft of the limit.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.601 (AM.I.C.K7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-20

questions.q: After an equipment change, the new empty weight and EWCG may be determined mathematically (instead of weighing) ONLY if:
options: The exact weight and arm of all the changes are known
The total change is under 50 lbs
questions.short: The exact weight and arm of all the changes are known
questions.expl: Either reweigh the aircraft or calculate — the math is acceptable only when the exact weight and arm of every change is known.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.603 (AM.I.C.S5/S7) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-21

questions.q: A radio (5.8 lbs) is installed at arm −28". The moment change is:
questions.expl: Added weight × negative arm = negative moment: 5.8 × (−28) = −162.4 in-lb. Removed items get negative weight (e.g., removing 3-lb ADF at −28" gives −3 × −28 = +84 in-lb). (Slide's worked example.)

questions.q: Ballast should be located:
options: As far aft or as far forward as possible, to bring the CG within limits using MINIMUM weight
questions.short: As far aft or as far forward as possible, to bring the CG within limits using MINIMUM weight
questions.expl: Ballast attains the desired CG when it is out of limits or not where the operator wants it; placing it at the extreme gives the longest arm so the least weight is needed.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.606 (AM.I.C.K9) · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-22

questions.q: When the computed ballast weight comes out to 46.67 lbs, you install:
options: 46 lbs — round down to save weight
47 lbs — ALWAYS round ballast UP to the next pound
questions.short: 47 lbs — ALWAYS round ballast UP to the next pound
questions.expl: Always round ballast UP to the next whole pound (46.67 → 47). Rounding down would leave the CG out of limits.

questions.q: Large aircraft (business jets to jumbo jets) state their CG location as:
options: A percentage of MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord)
questions.short: A percentage of MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord)
questions.expl: Large aircraft don't use a simple datum arm for CG; their tapered, swept wings vary in chord, so CG is stated as % of MAC from the leading edge (LEMAC = Leading Edge of the Mean Aerodynamic Chord).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.623 & 625 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-29

questions.q: MAC = 180", LEMAC at station 900. If the CG is at 25% MAC, the CG in inches from the datum is:
questions.expl: CG inches = (%MAC ÷ 100 × MAC) + LEMAC = (25 ÷ 100 × 180) + 900 = 45 + 900 = 945. (Slide's worked example.)
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.627 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-30

questions.q: Compared to airplanes, helicopters typically have:
options: A CG behind the tail rotor
A CG range twice as wide
A much more restricted CG range — sometimes as little as 3 inches
questions.short: A much more restricted CG range — sometimes as little as 3 inches
questions.expl: All W&B terminology is the same, but the helicopter's weight hangs from the main rotor mast like a pendulum; the CG is centered around the mast and the range can be as little as 3 inches.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.617 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-27

questions.q: Helicopter LATERAL arms are measured from the:
options: Butt line (the centerline of the fuselage) — right positive, left negative, facing forward
questions.short: Butt line (the centerline of the fuselage) — right positive, left negative, facing forward
questions.expl: Both longitudinal AND lateral weigh points are needed for helicopter EWCG. Longitudinal arms come from the datum; lateral arms from the butt line — arms right of it positive, left negative (as viewed facing forward / from the pilot seat).
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.619-620 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.6 p.6-28

questions.q: A maintenance logbook entry for repair or replacement of an item (FAR 43.9) must include, in order:
options: Description of work, date completed, type of certificate, certificate number, printed name, and signature
The aircraft's sale price and hours
questions.short: Description of work, date completed, type of certificate, certificate number, printed name, and signature
questions.expl: Per FAR 43.9: specific description of work (so the next mechanic can point to the part), date completed, type of certificate (A&P or IA), certificate number, printed name, and signature — the signature constitutes approval for return to service only for the work performed.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.630 (AM.I.C.S7/S17) · 14 CFR 43.9 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10

questions.q: The AIRWORTHY certification statement after a 100-hour or annual inspection (FAR 43.11) reads:
options: 'I certify that this aircraft has been inspected in accordance with (insert type) inspection and was determined to be in airworthy condition.'
'This aircraft is perfect and needs no further inspections.'
questions.short: 'I certify that this aircraft has been inspected in accordance with (insert type) inspection and was determined to be in airworthy condition.'
questions.expl: For an UNAIRWORTHY result the statement instead certifies a list of discrepancies and unairworthy items, dated, has been provided to the owner or operator.
questions.cite: BLK01 slides p.631 (AM.I.C.S7/S17) · 14 CFR 43.11 · FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.10

questions.q: In aircraft hardware coding, the letters "AN" stand for what standard?
questions.expl: AN = Air Force-Navy. The handbook lists AN (Air Force-Navy) numbers as one of the primary ways threaded fasteners and rivets are identified.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which standards abbreviation means "Air Force-Navy" Standard?
questions.expl: AN = Air Force-Navy. NAS = National Aerospace/Aircraft Standard, MS = Military Standard, SAE = Society of Automotive Engineers.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The abbreviation "NAF" on older aircraft hardware refers to the:
questions.expl: NAF = Naval Aircraft Factory, an early military source of aircraft-parts standards on the AMT 211 standards list.
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which abbreviation stands for "Naval Aircraft Factory"?
questions.expl: NAF = Naval Aircraft Factory. Do not confuse with NAS (National Aerospace Standard) or AF (Air Force Standard).
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: "AF" as a hardware standard designation means:
questions.expl: AF = Air Force Standard on the AMT 211 standards-organizations list.
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which abbreviation stands for "Air Force Standard"?
questions.expl: AF = Air Force Standard. AN = Air Force-Navy; NAF = Naval Aircraft Factory.
