questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The abbreviation "NAS" on aircraft hardware stands for:
questions.expl: NAS. The -30B spells it 'National Aircraft Standard'; the AIM worksheet uses 'National Aerospace Standard.' Both name the same NAS series of close-tolerance/internal-wrenching parts. AIM wording wins on the worksheet.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which abbreviation designates a "National Aerospace Standard" part?
questions.expl: NAS. AIM uses 'National Aerospace Standard'; the FAA handbook says 'National Aircraft Standard' — same NAS series.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The standards organization abbreviated "SAE" is the:
questions.expl: SAE = Society of Automotive Engineers, the body behind many alloy and hardware specifications referenced in aircraft maintenance.
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which abbreviation stands for "Society of Automotive Engineers"?
questions.expl: SAE = Society of Automotive Engineers.
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: "ASA" (American Standards Association) is the predecessor of which present-day body?
questions.expl: The American Standards Association (ASA) was renamed and is known today as ANSI.
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: On the AMT 211 standards list, "ASA" stands for what — and is now known as what?
questions.expl: ASA = American Standards Association, which became ANSI (American National Standards Institute).
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: "MS" in a part number such as MS20004 designates which standard?
questions.expl: MS = Military Standard. Threaded fasteners are identified by AN, NAS, or MS numbers per the Identification section.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which abbreviation stands for "Military Standard"?
questions.expl: MS = Military Standard (e.g., MS20363 self-locking nuts, MS20004 bolts).
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Threaded fasteners and rivets are identified by which three families of numbers?
questions.expl: The handbook states threaded fasteners and rivets are identified by AN (Air Force-Navy), NAS (National Aircraft Standard), or MS (Military Standard) numbers.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The general-purpose hex-head aircraft bolt falls in which AN number range?
questions.expl: The hex-head aircraft bolt (AN-3 through AN-20) is the all-purpose structural bolt for tension or shear where a light drive fit is permissible.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-37 to 7-38 (Aircraft Bolts / General Purpose Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which AN bolt range is the clevis bolt (shear) series?
questions.expl: Clevis bolts (AN21-AN36) are designed for shear loads only and are often used as a mechanical pin in a control system.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The drilled-head bolt (deeper head drilled to receive safety wire) is in which range?
questions.expl: The AN-73 through AN-81 drilled-head bolts are like the standard hex bolt but have a deeper head drilled for safety wire; AN-3 and AN-73 are interchangeable for strength.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-38 (General Purpose / Close Tolerance Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Close-tolerance hex-head bolts, machined more accurately for tight drive-fit holes, are in which range?
questions.expl: Close-tolerance bolts may be hex-headed (AN-173 through AN-186) or 100-degree countersunk (NAS-80 through NAS-86); they require a tight drive fit.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-38 (General Purpose / Close Tolerance Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Bolts numbered AN3 to AN20 are which type?
questions.expl: AN3-AN20 = the general-purpose hex-head structural bolt, the most common bolt on the aircraft.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-37 to 7-38 (Aircraft Bolts / General Purpose Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Bolts numbered AN21 to AN36 are which type?
questions.expl: AN21-AN36 = clevis bolts, used only in shear (as a pin in control systems), driven by a screwdriver slot rather than a wrench.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Bolts numbered AN73 to AN81 are which type?
questions.expl: AN73-AN81 = drilled-head bolts; the head is drilled to receive safety wire. Interchangeable with AN3 for tension/shear.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-38 (General Purpose / Close Tolerance Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Bolts numbered AN173 to AN186 are which type?
questions.expl: AN173-AN186 = close-tolerance hex-head bolts, used in tight drive-fit holes that carry reversing/vibrating loads.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-38 (General Purpose / Close Tolerance Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A close-tolerance bolt is properly seated into its hole only when struck with what tool?
questions.expl: The handbook defines the tight drive fit of a close-tolerance bolt: it moves into position only when struck with a 12- to 14-ounce hammer.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-38 (General Purpose / Close Tolerance Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The AN-3 and AN-73 series bolts are essentially interchangeable in:
questions.expl: The AN-73 is the same as the AN-3 except for the drilled (deeper) head; the two series are interchangeable for tension and shear strengths.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-38 (General Purpose / Close Tolerance Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Clevis bolts are designed to carry which kind of load?
questions.expl: Clevis bolts (AN21-AN36) are made for shear loads only and are not used in tension; they act as a mechanical pin.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A single raised dash on a bolt head identifies the bolt as:
questions.expl: Per Figure 7-19, a single raised dash = AN standard steel bolt. (The running text on p.7-39 has a known self-contradicting typo; the figure and the AIM worksheet agree on single-dash = steel/CRES.)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Two raised dashes on a bolt head indicate the bolt is made of:
questions.expl: Figure 7-19 shows the aluminum-alloy (2024) bolt marked with two raised dashes. Single dash = steel; double dash = aluminum.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A raised or recessed triangle on a bolt head identifies a:
questions.expl: Close-tolerance NAS bolts are marked with either a raised or recessed triangle; the material marking is otherwise the same as AN bolts.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A cross (plus-sign) marking on a bolt head indicates:
questions.expl: AN standard steel bolts are marked with a raised dash, an asterisk, a cross, or a single raised dash (Figure 7-19). The cross identifies a standard AN steel bolt.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: An asterisk (*) on a bolt head is one of the markings used for:
questions.expl: The handbook lists the AN standard steel bolt markings as a raised dash, an asterisk, or a single raised dash (Figure 7-19).
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A bolt head marked with the letter "C" indicates the bolt is:
questions.expl: A 'C' (whether on the head or in the part number) designates corrosion-resistant steel.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: An orange-dyed bolt head indicates the bolt has been:
questions.expl: Figure 7-19 labels an orange-dyed bolt as magnetically inspected; bolts inspected magnetically (Magnaflux) or fluorescently (Zyglo) are identified by colored lacquer or a distinctive head marking. (Answer-key flags color conventions vary by source — defer to instructor if marked otherwise.)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A letter "S" stamped on a bolt head usually identifies a:
questions.expl: Where manufacturers make bolts of different dimensions or greater strength than standard, the letter 'S' stamped on the head usually identifies these special bolts.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-37 to 7-38 (Aircraft Bolts / General Purpose Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which set of head markings ALL identify a standard AN steel bolt?
options: Two raised dashes or a triangle
Single raised dash, asterisk, or cross
Orange dye or a letter A
questions.short: Single raised dash, asterisk, or cross
questions.expl: AN standard steel bolts are marked with a raised dash, an asterisk, a cross, or a single raised dash (Figure 7-19).
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: How is a bolt head marked to show 2024 aluminum-alloy construction?
questions.expl: Figure 7-19: the 2024 aluminum-alloy bolt carries two raised dashes; the single dash/asterisk/cross are steel.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN3DD5A, what does the "AN" indicate?
questions.expl: The handbook's own AN3DD5A breakdown: 'AN' designates an Air Force-Navy standard bolt.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN3DD5A, what does the "3" indicate?
questions.expl: The number right after AN gives diameter in sixteenths of an inch: '3' = 3/16 inch.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN3DD5A, what does the "DD" indicate?
questions.expl: Per the handbook example, 'DD' = 2024 aluminum alloy. A 'C' in its place = corrosion-resistant steel; no letter = cadmium-plated steel.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN3DD5A, what does the "5" indicate?
questions.expl: The number before any trailing letter gives length in eighths of an inch: '5' = 5/8 inch.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN3DD5A, what does the trailing "A" indicate?
options: The shank is undrilled (no cotter-pin hole)
questions.short: The shank is undrilled (no cotter-pin hole)
questions.expl: Per the handbook example, a trailing 'A' means the shank is undrilled. Absence of the 'A' means the shank IS drilled for a cotter pin.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Decode the full part number AN3DD5A. Which reading is entirely correct?
options: AN std · 3/8 dia · steel · 5/16 long · drilled
AN std · 3/16 dia · 2024 aluminum · 5/8 long · undrilled shank
AN std · 3/16 dia · cadmium steel · 5 in long · drilled head
AN std · 3 in dia · aluminum · 5/8 long · undrilled
questions.short: AN std · 3/16 dia · 2024 aluminum · 5/8 long · undrilled shank
questions.expl: AN3DD5A = Air Force-Navy std bolt, 3/16-inch diameter, DD = 2024 aluminum, 5/8-inch length, A = undrilled shank. (Handbook worked example, verbatim.)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN3C5, the letter "C" tells you the bolt is:
questions.expl: A 'C' in place of the 'DD' indicates corrosion-resistant steel.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Decode AN3C5: which reading is correct?
questions.expl: AN3C5 = 3/16-inch diameter, C = corrosion-resistant steel, 5/8-inch length (no trailing A, so shank is drilled).
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN4-7A, what does the "4" indicate?
questions.expl: The first number after AN = diameter in sixteenths. AN4 = 4/16 = 1/4-inch diameter.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN4-7A, what does the "7" indicate?
questions.expl: After the material code (here a dash = cadmium-plated steel), the number = length in eighths. 7 = 7/8 inch.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Decode AN4-7A fully. Which is correct?
options: 4/16 dia · CRES steel · 7 in long · drilled
1/4 dia · cadmium-plated steel · 7/8 long · undrilled shank
questions.short: 1/4 dia · cadmium-plated steel · 7/8 long · undrilled shank
questions.expl: AN4-7A: 4 = 1/4-inch dia; the dash (no C/DD) = cadmium-plated carbon steel; 7 = 7/8-inch length; A = undrilled shank.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN6-20, what does the "6" indicate?
questions.expl: AN6 = 6/16 = 3/8-inch diameter.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the part number AN6-20, the "20" tells you the length is:
questions.expl: Length is in eighths of an inch: 20/8 = 2.5 inches. (And no trailing A means the shank is drilled for a cotter pin.)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In a bolt part number, absence of any material code letter (e.g., AN3-5) means the bolt is:
questions.expl: The handbook states the absence of the material letters indicates cadmium-plated steel.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: If an "H" precedes the length number in a bolt part number (e.g., AN3H5A), the bolt has a:
questions.expl: An 'H' before the length digit means the head is drilled for safetying; a trailing 'A' still means the shank is undrilled.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A bolt part number with a trailing "A" tells the technician the shank is:
questions.expl: Trailing 'A' = undrilled shank. NO trailing letter = the shank IS drilled for a cotter pin.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A bolt part number that ends with NO letter after the length number means the shank is:
questions.expl: The handbook contrasts the trailing A (undrilled) with its absence: no trailing A means the shank is drilled.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: On the AMT 211 key, an "A" placed BETWEEN the diameter and length in a part number is taught to mean:
questions.expl: [verify with instructor] The AMT 211 key teaches a mid-number 'A' = aluminum. The FAA -30B does NOT define this; -30B only defines a TRAILING A = undrilled and DD/C as material codes. The key itself flags this (⚠). Use the AIM answer on the worksheet, but know the FAA handbook differs.
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which nut is castellated (slotted) to be safetied with a cotter pin or lock wire?
questions.expl: The castle nut AN310 is used with drilled-shank AN hex bolts, clevis bolts, eyebolts, drilled-head bolts, or studs; its castellations accommodate a cotter pin or lock wire and it withstands large tensional loads.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A plain hex nut that requires a separate locking device (jam/check nut or lock washer) is the:
questions.expl: The plain hex nut AN315 (and AN335 coarse thread) carries large tension loads but requires an auxiliary locking device such as a check nut or lock washer.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The castellated SHEAR nut (thinner, shallower castellations than the castle nut) is the:
questions.expl: The castellated shear nut AN320 is used with drilled clevis bolts and threaded taper pins where shear stress only is expected; it is not as deep or as strong as the AN310 castle nut.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The plain CHECK nut, used as a locking device for plain nuts, set screws, and threaded rod ends, is the:
questions.expl: The plain check nut AN316 is employed as a locking device for plain nuts, set screws, threaded rod ends, and other devices.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The light hex nut for miscellaneous light-tension requirements (fine AN340 / coarse AN345) must be:
questions.expl: The light hex nut AN340 (fine) and AN345 (coarse) is much lighter than the plain hex nut and must be locked by an auxiliary device; used for light tension.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The wing nut, intended for hand tightening where the assembly is frequently removed, is the:
questions.expl: The wing nut AN350 is used where the desired tightness can be obtained by hand and where the assembly is frequently removed.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: How is a low-temperature, fiber-insert self-locking nut typically identified?
options: Its blue color / blue fiber collar
questions.expl: Self-locking nuts can be identified by the color of the insert; the low-temperature fiber-insert (elastic stop) nut is blue and is limited to about 250 F.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-44 to 7-45 (Self-Locking / Elastic Stop Nut; nut bases)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Elastic stop (fiber-insert) self-locking nuts must NOT be installed where temperatures exceed:
questions.expl: Do not install elastic stop nuts where the temperature is higher than 250 F, because the self-locking action is reduced beyond that point.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-45 (Elastic stop nut 250 F limit; nut part numbers; sheet spring nuts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Above 250 F you should NOT use which nut?
questions.expl: The fiber-collar elastic stop nut loses self-locking effectiveness above 250 F. For high heat, an all-metal locknut is used instead.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-45 (Elastic stop nut 250 F limit; nut part numbers; sheet spring nuts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: What provides the self-locking action in an elastic stop (fiber-insert) nut?
options: A spring washer behind the nut
The bolt threads impressing themselves into the untapped fiber
questions.short: Bolt threads impress into the untapped fiber
questions.expl: The self-locking action of the elastic stop nut results from the bolt threads impressing themselves into the untapped fiber. Do NOT tap the fiber insert.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A self-locking nut that holds in high-temperature areas (where fiber nuts cannot be used) is the:
questions.expl: All-metal self-locking nuts (e.g., Boots, Flexloc, stainless types) carry their locking feature in the metal itself, so they work above the 250 F fiber limit.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Patented self-locking nuts (Boots, Flexloc, fiber locknut, elastic stop nut) carry which MS part-number range?
questions.expl: The patented self-locking types are assigned part numbers ranging from MS20363 through MS20367; the Boots, Flexloc, fiber locknut, elastic stop nut, and self-locking nut belong to this group.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-45 (Elastic stop nut 250 F limit; nut part numbers; sheet spring nuts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Self-locking nuts must NOT be used at joints that subject the nut or bolt to:
questions.expl: Do not use self-locking nuts at joints that subject either the nut or bolt to rotation; they are for tight connections that must not shake loose under vibration.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A self-locking nut stamped out of spring sheet metal for non-structural use (access panels) is a:
questions.expl: Sheet-spring nuts, such as speed nuts, are made of arched spring steel and used with standard and self-tapping screws in non-structural locations.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-45 (Elastic stop nut 250 F limit; nut part numbers; sheet spring nuts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: What gives a speed (sheet-spring) nut its locking action?
options: An arched spring lock that prevents the screw from backing out
questions.expl: Speed nuts are made from spring steel and arched prior to tightening; this arched spring lock prevents the screw from working loose.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-45 (Elastic stop nut 250 F limit; nut part numbers; sheet spring nuts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A nut riveted or welded permanently to the structure so a bolt can be installed from one side is a/an:
questions.expl: Self-locking nut bases (anchor nuts / nut plates) are riveted or welded to aircraft structure, allowing bolts and screws to be installed from one side — common around inspection holes.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-44 to 7-45 (Self-Locking / Elastic Stop Nut; nut bases)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A removable channel-mounted self-locking nut base carries what kind of nut?
options: A floating nut that snaps in/out for easy replacement
questions.expl: The removable channel type carries a floating nut that can be snapped in or out of the channel, making it easy to replace damaged nuts.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-44 to 7-45 (Self-Locking / Elastic Stop Nut; nut bases)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Clinch-type and spline-type self-locking nuts that depend on friction for anchorage are:
options: NOT acceptable for use in aircraft structures
questions.expl: Nuts such as the clinch-type and spline-type depend on friction for their anchorage and are not acceptable for use in aircraft structures.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-44 to 7-45 (Self-Locking / Elastic Stop Nut; nut bases)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: High-strength internal- and external-wrenching nuts (elastic stop / Unbrako types) are:
questions.expl: The internal/external wrenching elastic stop nut and the Unbrako wrenching nut are both self-locking, heat-treated, and carry high-strength bolt tension loads.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the nut code AN310D5R, what does the "D" indicate?
questions.expl: For nut coding the handbook states: B = brass, D = 2017-T aluminum, DD = 2024-T aluminum, C = stainless steel, dash = cadmium-plated carbon steel. So 'D' = 2017-T aluminum.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the nut code AN310D5R, what does the trailing "R" indicate?
questions.expl: In AN310D5R, R = right-hand thread (usually 24 threads per inch).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the nut code AN310D5R, what does the "5" indicate?
questions.expl: In the AN310D5R example, 5 = 5/16-inch diameter (the dash number corresponds to the bolt diameter it fits — a 5 fits an AN5 bolt).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In nut coding, the letter "B" following the part number means the nut material is:
questions.expl: The letter 'B' following the part number indicates the nut material is brass.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In nut coding, a "DD" indicates the nut material is:
questions.expl: For nuts: B = brass, D = 2017-T aluminum, DD = 2024-T aluminum, C = stainless steel, dash = cadmium-plated carbon steel.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: For self-locking nuts whose code ends in three or four digits, the LAST two digits indicate:
questions.expl: Self-locking nut code numbers end in three or four digits: the last two digits = threads per inch, and the preceding one or two digits = nut size in 16ths of an inch.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A screw threaded along its length with no clearly defined grip, used for general repair, is a:
questions.expl: Machine screws (roundhead AN515 coarse / AN520 fine; fillister AN500-AN503; countersunk AN505/507/510) are general-purpose screws with no clearly defined grip length.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The roundhead machine screws AN515 (coarse) and AN520 (fine) differ from each other in:
questions.expl: The AN515 roundhead screw has coarse threads and the AN520 has fine threads; both are slotted or recessed head.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The 100-degree countersunk machine screw is designated:
questions.expl: Countersunk machine screws are AN505 and AN510 for 82 degrees and AN507 for 100 degrees.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The fillister-head general-purpose screw used as a cap screw in light mechanisms is the:
questions.expl: The fillister-head screw AN500-AN503 is a general-purpose cap screw for light mechanisms such as gearbox cover plates; screws larger than No. 6 are drilled in the head for safetying.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A screw with coarse threads and a sharp point, designed to cut its own threads, is a:
questions.expl: Self-tapping screws (machine AN504/AN506; sheet-metal AN530/AN531 Parker-Kalon Z-type) cut their own mating threads.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The AN530 and AN531 self-tapping SHEET-METAL screws are also known by what trade name?
questions.expl: AN530 and AN531 self-tapping sheet-metal screws, such as the Parker-Kalon Z-type, are blunt on the end and used for temporary attachment of metal for riveting and permanent nonstructural assembly.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Self-tapping screws should NOT be used to replace:
options: Standard screws, nuts, bolts, or rivets
questions.short: Standard screws, nuts, bolts, or rivets
questions.expl: The handbook explicitly states self-tapping screws should not be used to replace standard screws, nuts, bolts, or rivets.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Drive screws (AN535), which correspond to the Parker-Kalon U-type, are actually:
questions.expl: Drive screws AN535 are not actually screws but nails; they are driven into metal with a mallet, used for nameplates and sealing drain holes, and are not meant to be removed.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A screw that has a defined grip and the same shear strength as a comparable bolt is a:
questions.expl: Structural screws (AN509, AN525, and the NAS204-NAS235 series) have a definite grip and the same shear strength as a bolt of the same size; threads are National Fine.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The AN509 structural screw is specifically a:
questions.expl: The AN509 (100-degree) flathead screw is used in countersunk holes where a flush surface is necessary.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The AN525 structural screw is a:
questions.expl: The AN525 washer-head structural screw is used where raised heads are not objectionable; the washer head provides a large contact area.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Commonly used screws are classified into how many groups?
questions.expl: Screws are classified in four groups: (1) structural, (2) machine, (3) self-tapping, and (4) drive screws.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-66 (Airloc fasteners; Screws — four groups)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Aircraft screws differ from bolts mainly because screws are:
options: Generally lower-strength material with a loose-fitting thread
questions.expl: Screws are generally made of lower-strength materials than bolts, can be installed with a loose-fitting thread, and have heads shaped to engage a screwdriver or wrench.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-66 (Airloc fasteners; Screws — four groups)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Recessed-head structural screws are driven with which drivers?
questions.expl: Either a Phillips or a Reed & Prince screwdriver drives the recessed-head structural screws.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-67 (Structural / Machine / Self-Tapping / Drive Screws)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which washer provides a smooth bearing surface and acts as a shim to obtain correct grip length?
questions.expl: Plain washers AN960 (and AN970) provide a smooth bearing surface, act as shims for grip length, and position castellated nuts relative to drilled cotter-pin holes.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The plain washer with a GREATER bearing area, used on wooden structures to prevent crushing, is the:
questions.expl: The AN970 steel washer provides a greater bearing area than the AN960 and is used on wooden structures under both the head and nut to prevent crushing the surface.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Which washer's spring action provides friction to keep a nut from loosening under vibration?
questions.expl: The AN935 split lock washer is used with machine screws or bolts where a self-locking/castellated nut is not appropriate; its spring action prevents loosening from vibration.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-46 to 7-47 (Plain & Lock Washers AN960/AN970/AN935/AN936)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The toothed (star) lock washer is designated:
questions.expl: Lock washers AN935 (split) and AN936 (toothed) are used where a self-locking or castellated nut is not appropriate.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-46 to 7-47 (Plain & Lock Washers AN960/AN970/AN935/AN936)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A plain washer should be placed under a lock washer in order to:
options: Prevent damage (galling) to the surface material
questions.short: Prevent damage to the surface material
questions.expl: The handbook directs using plain washers under lock washers to prevent damage to the surface material.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Lock washers should NEVER be used in which situation (per the handbook list)?
options: Where they may be subjected to frequent removal, on soft materials, or with screws to primary structure
questions.short: Frequent removal / soft material / primary structure (per restriction list)
questions.expl: The handbook lists conditions where lock washers should never be used (e.g., where subject to frequent removal, on soft materials, in primary or secondary structure where failure could be hazardous). Follow the manufacturer for the specific joint.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-46 to 7-47 (Plain & Lock Washers AN960/AN970/AN935/AN936)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: An aluminum or aluminum-alloy washer is used under a bolt head or nut on aluminum/magnesium structure to:
questions.expl: Aluminum/aluminum-alloy washers are used under bolt heads or nuts on aluminum-alloy or magnesium structures where dissimilar-metal corrosion is a factor; current then flows between the washer and the steel bolt.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A countersunk washer is used to:
options: Provide a seat for the head of a countersunk (flush) fastener
Replace a plain washer on wood
questions.expl: A countersunk washer provides a bearing seat for the head of a flush/countersunk fastener (e.g., flush bolts/screws). [verify exact AN number with instructor — AIM slide series.]
questions.cite: [class notes] — AMT 211 Review Answer Key (AIM Block 2)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: To repair a worn or oversized HOLE, the technician installs a:
questions.expl: Worn or oversized holes are repaired with replacement (sleeve) bushings — often shrink-fit and made of wear-resistant material, such as spark-plug bushings in cylinder heads; oversize up to 0.040 in 0.005 increments.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: To repair worn-out internal THREADS in a casting, the technician installs a:
questions.expl: Helicoils are precision-formed coils of 18-8 stainless wire (diamond cross-section) used as screw-thread bushings to restore damaged threads in castings/light metals.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A Helicoil insert is made of what material with what cross-section?
questions.expl: Helicoils are precision-formed screw-thread coils of 18-8 stainless steel wire having a diamond-shaped cross section; they form UNC or UNF class 2B and 3B threads.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A Helicoil insert has a small feature that must be removed after installation. It is the:
questions.expl: Each Helicoil insert has a driving tang with a notch to facilitate removal of the tang after the insert is screwed into the tapped hole.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A shrink-fit replacement bushing is installed by:
options: Heating the bushing and freezing the hole
Heating the cylinder/housing and freezing the bushing
questions.short: Heating the housing, freezing the bushing
questions.expl: Original installation and overhaul replacements are shrunk fit: a heated cylinder head and a frozen bushing, so it tightens as temperatures equalize.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Name the three common turnlock (quarter-turn captive) fasteners.
questions.expl: The most commonly used captive/turn-lock fasteners are the Dzus, Camloc, and Airloc; a quarter turn of the stud locks them. They allow quick removal of access panels.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-64 (Captive / Turn Lock Fasteners; Dzus)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A Dzus turnlock fastener consists of which three parts?
questions.expl: The Dzus turnlock fastener consists of a stud, a grommet, and a receptacle; the grommet (aluminum) holds the stud and a spring secures it.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-64 (Captive / Turn Lock Fasteners; Dzus)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: How is a Dzus (and any turnlock) fastener locked?
options: A quarter turn of the stud clockwise
questions.expl: A quarter of a turn of the stud (clockwise) locks the fastener; it is unlocked only by turning the stud counterclockwise. A Dzus key or ground screwdriver is used.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A Camloc fastener consists of which three parts?
questions.expl: The Camloc fastener consists of a stud assembly, a grommet, and a receptacle; receptacles come in rigid and floating types. Used to secure cowlings and fairings.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: An Airloc fastener consists of which three parts?
options: Stud, a cross pin, and a stud receptacle
questions.short: Stud, cross pin, and stud receptacle
questions.expl: The Airloc fastener consists of a stud, a cross pin, and a stud receptacle; the stud is case-hardened steel and the stud hole is reamed for a press fit of the cross pin.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-66 (Airloc fasteners; Screws — four groups)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The Airloc cross pin (chrome-vanadium steel) should:
options: Never be used a second time — replace it once removed
questions.short: Never be reused — replace once removed
questions.expl: The Airloc cross pin is chrome-vanadium steel, heat-treated; it should never be used the second time — once removed from the stud, replace it with a new pin.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-66 (Airloc fasteners; Screws — four groups)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: On an Airloc stud, the total material thickness it locks is stamped on the head in:
questions.expl: The total thickness of material each Airloc stud satisfactorily locks is stamped on the stud head in thousandths of an inch (0.040, 0.070, 0.190, etc.).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-66 (Airloc fasteners; Screws — four groups)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Turnlock (quick-action) fasteners are most valued because they:
options: Permit quick and easy removal of access panels for inspection/servicing
questions.short: Allow quick removal of access panels
questions.expl: The most desirable feature of turnlock fasteners is that they permit quick and easy removal of access panels for inspection and servicing; also called quick-opening or stressed-panel fasteners.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-64 (Captive / Turn Lock Fasteners; Dzus)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A swaged copper sleeve squeezed onto a control cable around a thimble is known by what trade-name process?
questions.expl: The Nicopress process is a patented method using copper sleeves swaged onto cable, usable up to the full rated strength of the cable when looped around a thimble.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148b, p. 7-35 (Nicopress process / copper sleeves)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The Nicopress copper-sleeve process may be used up to what cable strength when the cable is looped around a thimble?
options: The full rated strength of the cable
questions.short: Full rated strength of the cable
questions.expl: A patented process using copper sleeves may be used up to the full rated strength of the cable when the cable is looped around a thimble.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148b, p. 7-35 (Nicopress process / copper sleeves)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: The Nicopress copper-sleeve process can replace the five-tuck splice on cables up to what diameter?
questions.expl: The Nicopress process may be used in place of the five-tuck splice on cables up to and including 3/8-inch diameter.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148b, p. 7-35 (Nicopress process / copper sleeves)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Sleeves used in the Nicopress process must be fabricated of what material (without engineering approval)?
questions.expl: Nicopress sleeves are copper. Sleeves of any material other than copper require engineering approval by the FAA for the specific application.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148b, p. 7-35 (Nicopress process / copper sleeves)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: What tool checks a swaged cable terminal for proper compression?
questions.expl: Using a go no-go gauge (or a micrometer), check the terminal shank diameter after swaging; this verifies proper compression and that the fitting will develop full cable strength.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148a(3)(c), p. 7-35, Fig 7-13 (check swaged fitting, go no-go gauge)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: After swaging a cable terminal, the technician proof-loads the cable to what percent of its rated breaking strength?
questions.expl: Test the swaged cable by proof-loading it to 60 percent of its rated breaking strength.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148a, p. 7-33 (swage-type terminals; go/no-go)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: When checking a compressed Nicopress copper sleeve with the terminal gauge, the compressed center should:
options: Enter the gauge opening with very little clearance
Not touch the gauge at all
Be far larger than the gauge
questions.short: Enter the gauge opening with very little clearance
questions.expl: Hold the gauge on the major axis of the sleeve; the compressed center should enter the gauge opening with very little clearance. If it does not, the tool must be adjusted.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148f, p. 7-38, Fig 7-15 (terminal gauge; sleeve compression)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: What device adjusts the tension of a control cable?
questions.expl: A turnbuckle is the threaded barrel used to adjust control-cable tension; one end has right-hand threads and the other left-hand threads so the barrel tightens both terminals at once.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Ch.7 (Turnbuckles — cable tension adjustment)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A turnbuckle barrel adjusts cable tension because its two ends have:
options: One right-hand and one left-hand thread
Metric threads on one end only
questions.short: One right-hand and one left-hand thread
questions.expl: A turnbuckle has a brass or steel barrel threaded RH on one end and LH on the other; turning the barrel draws both terminals in (or lets them out) to set tension.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Ch.7 (Turnbuckles — cable tension adjustment)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A thimble is used in a cable end to:
options: Protect the cable loop from wear/crushing where the sleeve is swaged
questions.short: Protect the cable loop from wear
questions.expl: The cable is looped around a thimble and the copper Nicopress sleeve is swaged over the loop; the thimble keeps the loop from being crushed or chafed.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148b, pp. 7-35 to 7-38 (Nicopress copper-sleeve splice / thimble)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Never solder cable ends before swaging because:
options: The solder increases the tendency of the cable to pull out/fray
questions.expl: The handbook notes: never solder cable ends to prevent fraying, since the presence of solder greatly increases the tendency of the cable to slip in the terminal.
questions.cite: AC 43.13-1B (2025 AIM ed.) Par 7-148a, p. 7-33 (swage-type terminals; go/no-go)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Aircraft bolts are almost always manufactured in which class of thread fit?
questions.expl: Aircraft bolts are almost always manufactured in Class 3, the medium fit.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Aircraft screws are generally manufactured with which class of thread fit?
questions.expl: Aircraft screws are generally manufactured with a Class 2 thread fit for ease of assembly.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: In the thread-class system, which is the LOOSE fit?
questions.expl: Class 1 = loose, Class 2 = free, Class 3 = medium, Class 4 = close. A Class 1 fit can be turned by hand; a Class 4 needs a wrench.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A Class 4 thread fit is described as:
options: Close (requires a wrench to turn the nut on)
questions.expl: A Class 4 fit is a close fit and requires a wrench to turn the nut onto the bolt, whereas a Class 1 fit turns easily by hand.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Grip length of a bolt is best described as:
options: The unthreaded shank length — the total thickness of material the bolt clamps
questions.short: The unthreaded shank that clamps the material
questions.expl: Grip is the unthreaded portion of the shank; it should equal the combined thickness of the material being bolted so threads do not bear in the hole. Washers are used to fine-tune grip.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B pp. 7-37 to 7-38 (Aircraft Bolts / General Purpose Bolts)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Plain washers are used partly to adjust grip length so that:
options: The bolt is fully threaded in the hole
Castellated nut slots line up with the drilled cotter-pin hole
questions.short: Castle-nut slots align with the cotter-pin hole
questions.expl: Plain washers act as shims to obtain correct grip length and to position castellated nuts so their slots line up with the drilled cotter-pin hole in the bolt.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-46 (Nut coding; Aircraft Washers / AN960 / AN970)
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: A 1/4-28 thread designation means the bolt has a 1/4-inch diameter and:
questions.expl: A 1/4-28 thread indicates a 1/4-inch (4/16) diameter bolt with 28 threads in one inch of its threaded length.
sources: AMT 211 — Aircraft Hardware, AN Bolts & Standards Test

questions.q: Why are piano-type hinges on an aircraft prime spots for corrosion?
options: The grease used on hinges is acidic and slowly attacks the aluminum
Constant flexing of the hinge causes fatigue corrosion in the lugs
The steel hinge pin against the aluminum hinge is dissimilar-metal contact, and the hinge is a natural trap for dirt, salt, and moisture
The hinges are made of magnesium, the most chemically active aircraft metal
questions.short: Dissimilar metals (steel pin in aluminum hinge) plus trapped dirt, salt, and moisture.
questions.expl: Hidden corrosion freezes the joint and the lugs break off when the hinge moves. Inspect, lubricate with WATER-DISPLACING lubricant, and work the hinge through several cycles so the lubricant penetrates.

questions.q: How can you tell a bolt that has been inspected by magnetic particle (Magnaflux) or fluorescent (Zyglo) methods from a regular bolt?
options: By the letter M stamped on the shank
By a blue anodized finish on the threads
By a raised dash or asterisk on the bolt head
By colored lacquer (e.g., orange dye) or a distinctive head marking
questions.short: Colored lacquer (orange dye) or a distinctive head marking.
questions.expl: A regular bolt only carries the standard material/manufacturer head markings. Don't confuse this with the raised dash/asterisk — that marks an AN standard STEEL bolt, not an inspected one.

questions.q: Which statement best describes a schematic diagram?
options: It locates components with respect to each other within the system and shows the principle of operation — not their actual location, size, or shape
It shows each part in its final installed position with locating dimensions
It shows the exact size and shape of every part using multiple views
It shows the object as it appears to the eye, like a photograph
questions.short: Components relative to each other + principle of operation; used for troubleshooting and training.
questions.expl: Schematics do NOT show real location or size. The wrong options describe an installation drawing, an orthographic projection, and a perspective drawing — know all four apart.

questions.q: What is galvanic corrosion?
options: Corrosion that occurs when two dissimilar metals make electrical contact while an electrolyte (moisture) is present
Uniform etching of a metal surface by direct chemical attack, such as spilled battery acid
Corrosion produced by cyclic stress and a corrosive environment acting together
Corrosion that attacks along the grain boundaries of improperly heat-treated alloys
questions.short: Two dissimilar metals in electrical contact + an electrolyte.
questions.expl: The more active metal corrodes. The bigger the activity difference between the two metals, the faster the attack — and if the corroding metal is the smaller piece, it corrodes even faster. The wrong options are intergranular, direct chemical, and fatigue corrosion.

questions.q: Why are plastic surfaces (windshields, windows) washed with soap and water instead of strong cleaners?
options: Strong cleaners make the plastic electrically conductive, creating a shock hazard
Soap leaves a wax film that structurally strengthens the plastic
Harsh chemicals like acetone, benzene, lacquer thinner, and window sprays soften plastic and cause crazing
Plastic must never get wet, so soap is used dry on a cloth
questions.short: Harsh solvents soften plastic and cause crazing — use soap and water, preferably by hand.
questions.expl: Procedure: flush with fresh water first (dissolves salt, floats off dust), wash with soap and water by hand, rinse, dry with a chamois. Never rub a dry plastic surface — it scratches and builds static.

questions.q: Who can make changes to an aircraft drawing?
options: Only someone with proper authority — every revision is recorded in the revision block along with the authority for the change
Anyone in the engineering department; no record of the change is required
Only the original draftsman who created the drawing
Any certificated mechanic, as long as he initials and dates the change
questions.short: Only with proper authority — recorded in the revision block.
questions.expl: The revision block records the identification symbol, date, nature of the revision, the AUTHORITY for the change, and the draftsman's name. All changes to approved drawings must be noted on all existing prints.

questions.q: A bolt head marked with an asterisk is what kind of bolt?
questions.short: AN standard steel bolt — marked with either a raised dash or an asterisk.
questions.expl: Marking set: raised dash or asterisk = AN standard steel; SINGLE raised dash = corrosion-resistant steel; TWO raised dashes = aluminum alloy. Keep this trio straight — it shows up twice on this final (see also the single-raised-dash question).

questions.q: A cube has 8-inch sides. What is its surface area?
questions.short: 384 sq in — surface area of a cube = 6 × s².
questions.expl: 6 × (8 × 8) = 6 × 64 = 384. The trap answer 512 is the VOLUME (8³); 64 is just one face.

questions.q: Pressure is equal to —
questions.expl: Pressure is the amount of force acting on a specific amount of surface area — pounds per square inch. Force × Distance is WORK, a different formula on this same final.

questions.q: Work is equal to —
questions.expl: Work happens only when something is moved some distance against a resisting force. Example: jacking a 150,000-lb airplane 4 ft = 600,000 ft-lb. Mass × Acceleration is Newton's second law (force), not work.

questions.q: What is the aspect ratio of a wing?
options: The ratio of lift to drag at cruise speed
The ratio of the wing's span (tip-to-tip length) to its chord (width)
The ratio of wing area to aircraft gross weight
The ratio of the wing's thickness to its chord
questions.expl: Long span + narrow chord = HIGH aspect ratio = less drag at a given speed (think gliders). A typical airliner is about 7:1.

questions.q: What is useful load?
options: Maximum gross weight minus the payload
Maximum allowable gross weight minus empty weight
Empty weight plus the weight of crew and fuel
The weight of passengers and baggage only, not counting fuel
questions.short: Useful load = max allowable gross weight − empty weight.
questions.expl: It's everything the aircraft can carry: crew, passengers, fuel, baggage. An aircraft certificated in both normal and utility categories may list two useful loads.

questions.q: What inspection is required after a bird strike?
options: Only the windshield and radome need to be inspected
A complete teardown inspection of the entire airframe is always required
No inspection is required unless the pilot reports a flight-control problem
Inspect the external areas around the strike; if structural damage is found, also inspect the internal structure and the hydraulic, pneumatic, and other systems in that area
questions.short: External strike area first; structural damage found → internal structure + systems in the area.
questions.expl: It's one of the FAA's "special inspections." Start local and external; escalate to internal structure and systems only when the initial inspection shows structural damage.

questions.q: How does sound travel?
options: As transverse waves that travel fastest through a vacuum
As longitudinal waves through a medium (matter); its speed is governed by the medium's density and elasticity
As electromagnetic waves, the same as light but slower
Only through air — it cannot travel through liquids or solids
questions.short: Longitudinal waves through matter; speed set by density and elasticity.
questions.expl: Particles vibrate back and forth in the direction the wave travels. Sound travels FASTER in water than air, and it cannot travel at all in a vacuum — the exact opposite of the vacuum trap option.

questions.q: Why is liquid (dye) penetrant inspection used?
options: To measure the thickness of paint or plating on a part
To find subsurface flaws buried deep inside a casting
To find cracks in ferrous metals only, using a magnetic field
To find defects that are OPEN TO THE SURFACE in any nonporous material
questions.short: Finds surface-open defects (cracks, porosity) in any nonporous material.
questions.expl: Works on aluminum, magnesium, brass, stainless, titanium — even ceramics, plastics, and glass. Its big limitation: the defect MUST reach the surface. For magnetic materials, magnetic particle inspection is preferred.

questions.q: What is the difference between potential and kinetic energy?
options: Potential energy is electrical; kinetic energy is mechanical
Potential energy is stored energy (energy at rest); kinetic energy is energy in motion
They are the same quantity expressed in different units
Potential energy is energy of motion; kinetic energy is stored energy
questions.short: Potential = stored / at rest; kinetic = in motion. Both in foot-pounds.
questions.expl: Potential comes from position, distortion of an elastic body, or chemical energy (fuel). Kinetic = an airplane rolling down the runway, a spinning flywheel. The first wrong option is the classic reversal — read carefully on the exam.

questions.q: What is the specific gravity of air?
options: 1.0 — air is the standard for gases
questions.short: 1.0 — air is the reference standard for gases (like water for liquids/solids).
questions.expl: Specific gravity compares a substance's weight to an equal volume of the STANDARD — so the standard itself is always 1. 14.7 is sea-level pressure in psi; 0.075 is air density in lb/ft³ — different facts.

questions.q: On a helicopter, what do the collective and cyclic controls do?
options: Both control engine rpm — collective is coarse adjustment, cyclic is fine
Collective tilts the rotor disc for direction; cyclic raises all blade angles for vertical lift
Collective controls the tail rotor; cyclic controls the engine throttle
Collective raises the blade angle of ALL rotor blades uniformly (vertical lift); cyclic tilts the rotor disc to move the helicopter in the desired direction
questions.short: Collective = all blades evenly (vertical); cyclic = tilts the disc (direction).
questions.expl: The collective is the lever by the pilot's seat (its twist-grip is the throttle); the cyclic is the stick in front, like a yoke. The first wrong option is the straight reversal — the exam's favorite trick.

questions.q: Why are wheel chocks used?
options: To take the aircraft's weight off the tires during long-term parking
To keep a parked aircraft from rolling — placed fore and aft of each wheel
To protect the tires from fuel and oil spills on the ramp
To provide a static-electricity ground path for the airframe
questions.short: Chocks fore and aft of the wheels keep a parked aircraft from rolling.
questions.expl: Chock ahead of AND behind each wheel whenever the aircraft is parked, tied down, or being serviced. (The instructor's shorthand said "chalks" — dictation garble for chocks.)

questions.q: A 10-tooth pinion gear drives a 40-tooth spur gear. The spur gear turns at 160 rpm. How fast does the pinion turn?
questions.short: 640 rpm — fewer teeth = spins faster (inverse tooth ratio).
questions.expl: Teeth(pinion) ÷ Teeth(spur) = Speed(spur) ÷ Speed(pinion). So pinion speed = (40 × 160) ÷ 10 = 640 rpm. The 40-rpm trap comes from dividing instead of multiplying.

questions.q: What are the 3 corrosion-inspection environment categories and how often must each be inspected?
options: Severe — every 7 days; moderate — every 30 days; mild — every 60 days
All environments — every 30 days, regardless of severity
Severe environment — every 15 calendar-days; moderate — every 45 days; mild — every 90 days
Severe — every 30 days; moderate — every 60 days; mild — every 120 days
questions.short: Severe 15 days / moderate 45 / mild 90. Wash the aircraft before any corrosion inspection.
questions.expl: Primary reading of the instructor's shorthand. ALTERNATE reading: the 3 DEPTHS of corrosion inspection — daily/preflight, in-depth (panels removed), annual (AC 43-4B ¶5.3). Memorize 15/45/90 first; confirm with the instructor which he wants.

questions.q: What do centerlines look like on a drawing?
options: A series of short, evenly spaced dashes
Thick lines with arrowheads showing where a cut was taken
questions.short: Alternating long and short dashes; where centerlines cross, the short dashes intersect.
questions.expl: The wrong options are the other line types: evenly spaced short dashes = HIDDEN lines; freehand/zigzag = BREAK lines; thick with arrowheads = CUTTING-PLANE lines. The exam loves swapping these.

questions.q: Why is titanium special as an aircraft metal?
options: It is the heaviest aircraft metal but completely fireproof
It is cheaper and easier to machine than aluminum
It is highly magnetic, which makes it easy to inspect
It is light, strong, and corrosion-resistant — between aluminum and stainless steel in weight and strength
questions.short: Light + strong + corrosion-resistant; sits between aluminum and stainless steel.
questions.expl: A protective oxide film forms on contact with air. About 60% heavier than aluminum but 50% lighter than stainless. It is NONmagnetic, resists stress-corrosion cracking, and handles enough heat for firewalls.

questions.q: What is a perspective drawing?
options: A pictorial view in which all lines stay parallel and dimensions are accurate to scale
A pictorial view showing the object as it appears to the eye, like a photograph — so actual angles and dimensions are NOT accurate
A diagram of components relative to each other, used for troubleshooting
A multi-view drawing showing exact size and shape from six possible views
questions.short: Like a photograph — angles and dimensions are not accurate.
questions.expl: Because of perspective, some lines are not parallel. The 4 pictorial types: perspective, isometric, oblique, exploded view. The accurate-to-scale option describes isometric-style drawing, not perspective.

questions.q: What is the most corrosion-prone metal used on aircraft?
questions.short: Magnesium — the most chemically active aircraft metal and the hardest to protect.
questions.expl: Most active metals = magnesium and aluminum; most noble = gold and silver. Aluminum is the runner-up trap — active, but not THE most.

questions.q: Which list gives factors that affect the rate of corrosion?
options: Only the thickness and color of the paint film
Altitude and airspeed — corrosion happens mostly in flight
Only the age of the aircraft and its total flight hours
Type of metal, heat-treatment and grain direction, dissimilar-metal contact, temperature, electrolytes, oxygen availability, biological organisms, and mechanical stress
questions.short: Metal type, heat-treat/grain, dissimilar metals, anodic/cathodic areas, temperature, electrolytes, oxygen, organisms, stress.
questions.expl: Climate matters too — warm, salty, humid environments speed everything up. Corrosion is mostly a GROUND phenomenon driven by chemistry, not flight time.

questions.q: What is spark testing?
options: Checking electrical bonding straps for resistance
Testing spark plugs for the correct electrode gap
Identifying FERROUS metals by the spark stream they throw when held against a revolving grinding stone
Checking magneto timing with a timing light
questions.short: Ferrous-metal ID by spark stream on a grinding wheel.
questions.expl: Each ferrous metal throws its own spark pattern; most nonferrous metals don't spark at all. Special case worth memorizing: TITANIUM gives a brilliant white trace ending in a brilliant white burst.

questions.q: What is exfoliation corrosion?
options: Deep, isolated pits that form holes in the surface
Uniform surface etching that dulls the metal's finish
Corrosion between two dissimilar metals in electrical contact
An advanced form of intergranular corrosion in which the surface grains are lifted up in layers (flaking) by expanding corrosion products at the grain boundaries below
questions.short: Advanced intergranular corrosion — lifts surface grains like flaking pastry layers.
questions.expl: It is visible proof that intergranular corrosion is happening underneath. The wrong options describe uniform/direct chemical attack, pitting, and galvanic corrosion.

questions.q: What does over-torquing a bolt or nut do?
options: It can fail the bolt or nut by overstressing the threaded areas, and it stretches the bolt
It has no effect as long as a self-locking nut is used
It work-hardens the threads and makes the joint stronger
It makes the nut easier to remove at the next inspection
questions.short: Over-torque can fail the bolt/nut by overstressing the threads; it also stretches bolts.
questions.expl: It can also trap internal stress in the joint, which invites stress-corrosion cracking. Flip side for the exam: UNDER-torque causes unnecessary wear of nuts, bolts, and the parts they hold.

questions.q: How many strands are in a typical aircraft (control) cable?
questions.short: 7 strands — six strands laid around a center strand.
questions.expl: The most common aircraft cables are 7×7 (7 strands of 7 wires each) and 7×19 (7 strands of 19 wires each). 19 is the WIRES-per-strand number in 7×19, not the strand count. Note: the shorthand said "lock cable," which isn't a standard handbook term — 7 strands fits control/flexible cable; if the instructor meant safety cable, ask him.

questions.q: What is orthographic projection?
options: A single pictorial view that shows the object as a photograph would
A multi-view drawing system that shows the exact size and shape of all parts of complex objects — with six possible views
A three-dimensional pictorial view drawn around 30-degree axes
A diagram that shows the principle of operation for troubleshooting
questions.short: Multi-view system for exact size and shape; six possible views.
questions.expl: The six: front, top, bottom, rear, right side, left side — but one-, two-, and three-view drawings are the most common. The wrong options are perspective, schematic, and isometric.

questions.q: Which statement best defines HARDNESS?
options: The ability of a material to resist abrasion, penetration, cutting action, or permanent distortion
The property that allows a metal to be hammered, rolled, or pressed without cracking
The property of breaking or shattering with very little bending
The property that allows a metal to be drawn, bent, or twisted without breaking
questions.short: Hardness = resists abrasion, penetration, cutting, permanent distortion.
questions.expl: The distractors are the definitions of DUCTILITY, MALLEABILITY, and BRITTLENESS — the FAA's favorite property swap. Hardness can be increased by cold-working and, in steel and some aluminum alloys, by heat treatment.

questions.q: What is acoustic emission testing?
options: The inspector taps the part with a coin and listens for a dull thud
Sensors are placed at various locations on the structure and a load or stress is applied — cracks and corroded areas emit sound/stress waves (ultrasonic pulses) the sensors pick up
A transducer sends sound into the part and measures the echo returning from the back wall
A microphone records engine noise during a runup to detect bearing wear
questions.short: Sensors on the structure + applied stress; flaws "cry out" with ultrasonic pulses.
questions.expl: ("Acoustic ems" in the shorthand = acoustic EMISSION.) Don't confuse it with ultrasonic pulse-echo (option about back-wall echo) or coin-tap testing — different NDI methods.

questions.q: Which statement best defines DUCTILITY?
options: The ability to resist abrasion, penetration, cutting action, or permanent distortion
The property that allows a metal to be permanently drawn, bent, or twisted into shapes without breaking — essential for wire and tubing
The property that allows a metal to be hammered, rolled, or pressed into shapes without cracking
The ability of a metal to return to its original shape after the load is removed
questions.short: Ductility = drawn/bent/twisted permanently without breaking (wire, tubing).
questions.expl: Distractors = HARDNESS, MALLEABILITY, and ELASTICITY. Ductile metals are preferred in aircraft for easy forming and resistance to shock-load failure; ductility is similar to (but not the same as) malleability.

questions.q: What are the 3 parts of a Jo-bolt?
options: A stud, a castle nut, and a cotter pin
A rivet shank, a pulling mandrel, and a locking spindle
A pin, a collar, and a locking ring
A threaded steel-alloy bolt, a threaded steel nut, and an expandable stainless-steel sleeve
questions.short: Threaded alloy bolt + threaded steel nut + expandable stainless sleeve (factory preassembled).
questions.expl: It's an internally threaded three-piece blind rivet. Caution from the handbook: don't use it where a loose part could be sucked into an engine intake.

questions.q: Which diagrams are used most extensively in aircraft manuals?
questions.short: Schematic and installation — installation to find/identify components, schematic to troubleshoot.
questions.expl: The four classes a mechanic works with: installation, schematic, block, and wiring. The FAA doesn't crown a single winner — if the AIM slide named exactly one (likely "schematic" or "installation"), use the slide's word on the classroom test.

questions.q: What is the most common type of inspection?
questions.short: Visual — the most widely used inspection technique.
questions.expl: Eyes directly on the surface or at a low angle; flashlight, mirror, and 5×–10× magnifying glass; touch helps find hidden corrosion. The other three are specialized NDI follow-ups, not the everyday first look.

questions.q: What 4 conditions must exist for electrochemical corrosion to occur?
options: Heat, fuel, oxygen, and a chain reaction
Two identical metals, water, sunlight, and time
An anode (metal that corrodes), a cathode (dissimilar conductive material), an electrolyte (conductive liquid path), and electrical contact between anode and cathode
An acid, a base, a salt, and a catalyst
questions.short: Anode + cathode + electrolyte + electrical contact. Remove ANY ONE and corrosion stops.
questions.expl: The heat/fuel/oxygen option is the FIRE tetrahedron — a deliberate trap. Electrical contact is usually metal-to-metal, like rivets or bolts.

questions.q: What is the maximum combined height of a washer stack under a nut or bolt head?
questions.expl: Washers may be added to compensate for grip length, but only up to 1/8 inch combined — that limits you to the next standard grip size.

questions.q: Why must wheel wells be inspected for corrosion?
options: The wheel well takes more punishment than any other area (mud, water, salt, gravel, debris), and its complicated shapes make complete paint coverage almost impossible to maintain
Hydraulic fluid in the wheel well instantly dissolves protective coatings
Wheel wells are pressurized in flight, which accelerates corrosion
The FAA requires wheel-well inspection only after hard landings
questions.short: Most-punished area on the aircraft; hard to keep fully painted.
questions.expl: Many complicated shapes and fittings, plus partially applied preservative can actually HIDE corrosion underneath it.

questions.q: What manual/document is used for corrosion control?
options: The manufacturer's published corrosion-control/inspection program first; if none exists, AC 43-4B, Corrosion Control for Aircraft
questions.short: Manufacturer's program takes precedence; otherwise AC 43-4B.
questions.expl: AC 43-4B is the FAA's acceptable-means document for identifying and treating corrosion when the manufacturer hasn't published a program.

questions.q: Which of the 4 electrochemical-corrosion conditions is the easiest to remove to stop corrosion?
options: The cathode — remove all dissimilar metals from the aircraft
The electrical contact — insulate every rivet and bolt from the skin
The electrolyte — keep the metal clean, dry, and painted so moisture can't reach the surface
The anode — replace the corroding metal with gold or silver
questions.expl: The FAA text says removing ANY one condition stops corrosion; a paint film keeping the electrolyte off the anode and cathode is the FAA's own example, and it's the one you can actually control with cleaning and paint. ("Easiest" is the instructor's framing.)

questions.q: Why are blind rivets used?
options: Because they're cheaper and faster, they're preferred everywhere on the airframe
For places where you can't reach both sides of the structure, or where there's no room to swing a bucking bar
They are required around engine intakes, where loose parts can't be tolerated
Where extra shear strength beyond solid rivets is required in primary structure
questions.short: Only one side accessible / no room for a bucking bar; also many non-structural attachments.
questions.expl: Installed entirely from one side — "blind" to the far side. Used for interior furnishings, flooring, deice boots. The intake option is backwards: the handbook warns AGAINST fasteners that could shed parts into an intake.

questions.q: Why are soft-faced hammers used?
options: For driving hardened steel pins and nails
For driving rivets when a rivet gun is not available
For forming soft metals and striking surfaces that are easily damaged
For striking chisels and punches in tight quarters
questions.short: Forming soft metals; striking easily damaged surfaces.
questions.expl: Faces are wood, brass, lead, rawhide, hard rubber, or plastic. Never use them where the face could be damaged — e.g., striking nails or hardened parts.

questions.q: What is an installation drawing?
options: A drawing of a single part with all dimensions and specifications for making it
A drawing showing the relationship of two or more parts to each other
A diagram showing how a system operates, for troubleshooting
A drawing with all the information needed for a part or assembly in its FINAL INSTALLED POSITION in the aircraft
questions.short: All info for the part in its final installed position (plus locating/reference dimensions).
questions.expl: The wrong options are the DETAIL drawing, the ASSEMBLY drawing, and the SCHEMATIC — the same family the exam keeps shuffling.

questions.q: What are nonmetallic materials used for on aircraft?
options: Nonmetallic materials are not approved for use on certificated aircraft
Only interior items like carpet and seat cushions — never structure
Wood (early/restoration aircraft), transparent plastics (windshields, windows), composites/reinforced plastics (modern structure), and rubber (seals, hoses, vibration dampening, tires)
questions.short: Wood, plastics (windshields), composites, rubber (seals/hoses/tires), fabric, leather.
questions.expl: Composites have replaced much of the aluminum in modern airframes — so "never structure" is flat wrong. Magnesium/titanium/Monel are METALS.

questions.q: Why can't chemical corrosion removers be used on titanium?
options: Chemicals make titanium magnetic, which interferes with compass calibration
Titanium is susceptible to hydrogen embrittlement, which can cause stress corrosion — so chlorinated solvents and chemical corrosion removers are prohibited; remove corrosion mechanically
Titanium dissolves completely in any acid solution within minutes
Chemical removers stain titanium purple, ruining its heat resistance
questions.short: Hydrogen embrittlement → stress corrosion/pitting; chemical removers PROHIBITED on titanium.
questions.expl: (The shorthand said "carrion remover" — dictation garble for CORROSION remover.) Remove corrosion mechanically: stainless-steel wool, abrasive mats, glass-bead blasting, hand polish. Also never use steel wool or steel brushes on titanium.

questions.q: What are calipers used for, and what does a vernier scale add?
options: Calipers check surface hardness; the vernier scale compensates for temperature
Calipers measure torque; the vernier scale converts the reading to metric
Calipers measure inside and outside dimensions; the vernier scale lets you read the fraction of a division directly — down to thousandths of an inch
Calipers measure only hole depth; the vernier scale adds angle measurement
questions.short: Calipers = ID/OD measurement; vernier scale = read thousandths directly.
questions.expl: A slide caliper reads on its scale, a dial, or an LCD — more accurate than a ruler, and some include a depth gauge. If the slide asked you to READ a vernier, practice with Figure 11-39's worked examples.

questions.q: Which is heavier — water vapor or dry air?
options: They weigh exactly the same at the same temperature
Humid air is heavier — the water adds weight to the air
It depends on altitude — humid air is heavier only above 10,000 feet
Dry air is heavier — humid air is LESS dense than dry air
questions.short: Dry air is heavier; humid air is less dense.
questions.expl: The intuitive answer ("humid air is heavier") is WRONG — water vapor displaces heavier air molecules. That's why takeoff gross weight is reduced and engine power drops on humid days: water vapor is incombustible and displaces air. (Shorthand "heavy 65" = dictation garble for "heavier.")

questions.q: What does METO power mean?
options: Maximum Except TakeOff horsepower — the highest power allowed continuously
Minimum En route Takeoff Operations power
questions.short: Maximum Except TakeOff hp — highest continuous power (takeoff power is higher but time-limited).
questions.expl: Weight-and-balance use: minimum fuel = 1/12 gallon per METO hp, or simply METO hp ÷ 2 = minimum fuel in POUNDS (avgas = 6 lb/gal).

questions.q: An oxygen concentration cell is what type of corrosion — and which area corrodes?
options: Filiform corrosion under the paint film
Concentration cell (crevice) corrosion — the LOW-oxygen area becomes the anode and corrodes
Galvanic corrosion between two dissimilar metals
Concentration cell (crevice) corrosion — the HIGH-oxygen area corrodes
questions.short: Concentration cell (crevice) corrosion — attack hits the LOW-oxygen area.
questions.expl: One of the three concentration-cell types (metal-ion, oxygen, active-passive). Oxygen cells form wherever air can't reach — under gaskets, wood, rubber, dirt. Stainless steel is especially susceptible. The "high-oxygen" option is the half-right trap.

questions.q: What is Newton's third law?
options: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
Pressure decreases as the velocity of a fluid increases
A body at rest stays at rest unless acted on by an outside force
questions.short: Action–reaction: every action has an equal and opposite reaction.
questions.expl: Jet exhaust shoots backward → thrust pushes forward. The distractors are Newton's FIRST law, Newton's SECOND law, and Bernoulli's principle.

questions.q: What lines are used on a sectional view to show the parts that were cut?
options: Phantom lines — showing an alternate position of a part
Centerlines — alternating long and short dashes
Hidden lines — short, evenly spaced dashes
questions.short: Section (crosshatch) lines show what the cutting plane sliced through.
questions.expl: Sectional views reveal interior shape and construction; the crosshatching marks the material that was "cut away" at the cutting plane.

questions.q: What is the universal numbering system for drawings?
options: A means of identifying standard drawing SIZES — the first digit of the drawing number (1, 2, 4, or 5) tells the drawing size
The ATA chapter numbering system used in maintenance manuals
A worldwide serial-number registry for every aircraft part
A code identifying the alloy and temper of sheet-metal stock
questions.short: Identifies standard drawing sizes; first digit 1, 2, 4, or 5 = size.
