questions.expl: Each drawing number has 6–7 digits: 1 = 8½″×11″, 2 = 11″×17″, 4 = 17″×22″, 5 = larger. (The "abc" in the shorthand is likely dictation noise; if it was a separate jot, titanium uses an A-B-C alloy classification — 30B p. 7-9 — ask the instructor.)

questions.q: What are a nomogram and a flowchart?
options: A nomogram is a graph of (usually) three sets of data — knowing any two lets you read the third; a flowchart illustrates a sequence or flow of events, step by step
A nomogram is a wiring diagram; a flowchart is a hydraulic-system diagram
Both are types of pictorial perspective drawings
A nomogram shows a step-by-step sequence; a flowchart graphs three variables
questions.short: Nomogram = 3-scale graph (know 2, read the 3rd); flowchart = sequence of events.
questions.expl: ("Nonogram" in the shorthand = NOMOGRAM.) A nomogram is often three parallel scales read with a straightedge. The first wrong option is the straight swap.

questions.q: What are the 3 classes of aircraft drawings?
questions.expl: Detail = one single part with full specs; assembly = relationship of two or more parts; installation = the part in its final installed position. "Schematic/block/wiring" are DIAGRAM types — a different list on this same final.

questions.q: When do you use the standard torque table?
options: Only for bolts larger than 1/2-inch diameter
Always — the standard table overrides the manufacturer's listed torque values
Only when a calibrated torque wrench is unavailable
Whenever specific torque values are NOT called out in the maintenance procedures
questions.short: Use the standard table when no specific torque is given in the maintenance procedures.
questions.expl: Related rules: ft-lb = in-lb ÷ 12; don't lubricate nuts/bolts before torquing unless they're corrosion-resistant or you're specifically instructed to.

questions.q: What does "grounding" mean when it comes to electricity around aircraft?
options: Connecting the battery's positive terminal directly to the airframe
Giving electricity a safe path into the earth so static charge drains away instead of sparking — the aircraft, fuel truck, and nozzle are all connected to ground points before fueling
Removing the aircraft from flight status until maintenance is complete
Disconnecting the battery so no current can flow during maintenance
questions.short: Safe path to earth so static charge drains — ground aircraft, truck, and nozzle before fueling.
questions.expl: Flight and fuel flow build static charge in the airframe; grounding keeps it from sparking and igniting fuel vapor. Connect to bare metal/proper grounding points — never the propeller or exhaust. (If his class meant circuit theory: "ground" = the common return/reference point, often the airframe — confirm which sense the slide used.)

questions.q: What number is found in the title block of a drawing?
options: The drawing (print) number — in a number block in the lower right corner
questions.short: The drawing/print number — lower right corner of the title block.
questions.expl: It identifies the print for filing and prevents mix-ups; it may repeat near the top border or on the reverse so it shows when the print is folded or rolled.

questions.q: Which statement describes a highly MALLEABLE metal?
options: One that shatters or breaks with very little bending
One that can be drawn out into wire or tubing without breaking
One that can be hammered, rolled, or pressed into shapes without cracking or breaking — copper is the handbook's example
One that springs back to its original shape after the load is removed
questions.short: Malleable = hammered/rolled/pressed without cracking; example: copper.
questions.expl: Needed for sheet metal worked into compound curves — cowlings, fairings, wingtips. The distractors are DUCTILITY, BRITTLENESS, and ELASTICITY — same property-swap trap as the hardness/ductility questions.

questions.q: What is "purple plague"?
options: A brittle gold-aluminum compound that forms when gold is bonded to aluminum — its growth can fail microelectronic interconnection bonds
Heat discoloration of titanium exhaust components
Magnesium corrosion that appears as a purple powder
A purple fungus that grows in jet-fuel tanks
questions.short: Brittle gold-aluminum compound; fails microelectronic bonds (avionics-level corrosion).
questions.expl: It shows up where gold bonding meets aluminum in microelectronics — an avionics corrosion problem, not an airframe one.

questions.q: Besides the part number, what is another way to identify rivets?
options: By weighing the rivet on a precision scale
By measuring the shank diameter — all alloys share the same head markings
By markings on the rivet head (raised teat, dimple, raised dashes, raised cross, etc.) for alloy/strength, and by the color of the protective coating for finish
By spark testing the rivet head against a grinding wheel
questions.short: Head markings = alloy; coating color = finish.
questions.expl: ("Rivers" in the shorthand = RIVETS.) Coating colors: zinc chromate = yellow, anodized = pearl gray, metal-sprayed = silvery gray. Head marks: teat, two teats, dimple, pair of raised dashes, raised cross, triangle, single dash, or plain.

questions.q: What is the definition of grip length?
options: The threaded portion of the bolt shank
The distance the nut travels from finger-tight to fully torqued
The full length of the bolt measured from under the head to the tip
The unthreaded portion of the bolt shank — generally, the thickness of the material the fastener is designed to hold
questions.short: Grip = unthreaded shank portion = material thickness being held.
questions.expl: Math version: grip = shank length − threaded portion. If grip is slightly long, washers (up to 1/8-inch stack) make up the difference.

questions.q: What determines how often corrosion inspections are done?
options: The severity of the aircraft's operating environment — mild, moderate, or severe — plus manufacturer recommendations
The age of the paint job
The number of passengers typically carried
questions.expl: Think salty coastal air, industrial pollution, runway de-ice chemicals, humidity. Severe = 15 days, moderate = 45, mild = 90. Infrequently flown aircraft use a CALENDAR-based program, not flight hours — which is why "flight hours only" is wrong.

questions.q: What is aliphatic naphtha used for?
options: Wiping down cleaned surfaces just before painting; it is also safe for cleaning acrylics and rubber
Removing heavy carbon deposits from exhaust stacks
Stripping epoxy primer from aluminum skin
questions.short: Pre-paint wipe-down; safe on acrylics and rubber; flash point about 80 °F.
questions.expl: Never confuse it with AROMATIC naphtha, which is toxic and ATTACKS acrylics and rubber — the exam loves that one-word difference.

questions.q: What is a half-section drawing?
options: The bottom half of an orthographic front view
A sectional view where the cutting plane extends only halfway across the object — half shows the interior, the other half stays an exterior view; used on symmetrical objects
A view drawn at half scale to save space on the print
A full section with crosshatching applied to only half the parts
questions.short: Cutting plane goes halfway — half interior, half exterior; for symmetrical objects.
questions.expl: It lets one view show inside and outside at the same time — only useful when the object is symmetrical.

questions.q: What is case hardening?
options: Hardening the entire part uniformly all the way through
Softening the surface of a part so it can be machined more easily
A heat treatment that produces a hard, wear-resistant SURFACE (the case) over a strong, tough CORE
Encasing the part in a protective plastic shell
questions.short: Hard wear-resistant case over a tough core.
questions.expl: Ideal for parts that must resist surface wear but still take loads without snapping. Best suited to low-carbon and low-alloy steels. "All the way through" hardening is the trap — that's ordinary through-hardening, not case hardening.

questions.q: The cotter pin holes don't line up with the castle nut slots. What do you do?
options: Back the nut off slightly until a slot lines up with the hole
Tighten the nut past the torque limit until the next slot lines up
Never over-torque or loosen the nut to force alignment — remove the hardware, change the washer (or realign the hardware), and torque again within the recommended range
File the castle nut slots wider until the cotter pin fits
questions.short: Never force it — remove, change the washer, re-torque within range.
questions.expl: Start a castle nut at minimum recommended torque plus friction drag. Both "tighten past" and "back off" are wrong — the torque must stay within the recommended range.

questions.q: What happens to improperly heat-treated aluminum?
options: High-strength alloys (2014, 2024, 7075) become more susceptible to INTERGRANULAR corrosion when exposed to a corrosive environment
It becomes magnetic and must be degaussed before use
It gains strength but loses electrical conductivity
It becomes immune to corrosion because of its thicker oxide layer
questions.expl: Slow quenching lets precipitation form along the grain boundaries — exactly where intergranular corrosion attacks. Advanced cases become exfoliation.

questions.q: What is casting?
options: Hammering or pressing hot metal into shape between dies
Machining a part from solid bar stock
Forming a part by melting metal and pouring it into a mold of the desired shape — no plastic deformation, so working doesn't alter the grain
Forcing metal through a die to produce long uniform shapes
questions.short: Melt + pour into a mold; grain set by cooling rate, alloying, and thermal treatment.
questions.expl: The distractors are FORGING, EXTRUSION, and machining. Bonus fact: all metal products start as a casting — wrought metals are worked from cast ingots.

questions.q: What does a single raised dash on a bolt head mean?
questions.expl: Full set: raised dash or asterisk = AN standard steel; SINGLE dash = corrosion-resistant steel; TWO raised dashes = aluminum alloy. Exam trap: the -30B handbook's sentence on p. 7-39 is garbled by an editing error — the AC 43.13-1B wording is the clean, correct version.

questions.q: The AN310 castle nut is used —
options: Only on bolts that have a nylon self-locking insert
Where no safetying device is required
With drilled-shank AN hex bolts, clevis bolts, eyebolts, or studs; it takes large TENSION loads and its slots accept a cotter pin or lockwire
Only for shear loads — it must never carry tension
questions.short: AN310: drilled-shank bolts, large tension loads, safetied by cotter pin or lockwire.
questions.expl: The shear-only option describes the AN320 castellated SHEAR nut (shallower, shear loads only) — the classic AN310/AN320 swap.

questions.q: What is a break line on a drawing?
options: A thick line with arrowheads showing where a sectional cut was taken
Alternating long and short dashes marking the center of a part
A series of short, evenly spaced dashes showing hidden edges
A line indicating that a portion of the object is not shown — short breaks are solid freehand lines; long breaks are solid ruled lines with zigzags
questions.short: Part of the object not shown; short = freehand, long = ruled with zigzags.
questions.expl: Distractors = hidden lines, centerlines, and cutting-plane lines — the same line-type lineup as the centerline question; learn all four together.

questions.q: What does corrosion look like on silver?
options: White, powdery deposits like corroding aluminum
A green patina like weathered copper
Silver tarnishes as a brown-to-black film in the presence of sulfur
questions.expl: Silver doesn't corrode in the ordinary sense — it tarnishes. Bonus: silver plated over copper can cause "red plague" — brown-red powder on exposed copper.

questions.q: How many threads must protrude past the nut on an installed bolt?
options: None — the bolt should end flush with the top of the nut
At least one thread protruding through the nut
questions.short: At least ONE thread through the nut (self-locking or plain nuts).
questions.expl: The -30B adds detail: a chamfered-end bolt should extend at least the full round/chamfer through; a flat-end bolt at least 1/32 inch.

questions.q: What is alodizing?
options: A method of welding aluminum under inert gas
An electrolytic-bath process — the same thing as anodizing
A simple CHEMICAL treatment for aluminum alloys that increases corrosion resistance and improves paint bonding
questions.short: Simple chemical treatment for aluminum: corrosion resistance + paint bonding.
questions.expl: Because it's so simple (dip or spray — NO electric bath needed), it has been rapidly replacing anodizing in aircraft work. "Same as anodizing" is the trap: anodizing IS electrolytic; alodizing is not.

questions.q: What is the "application" block near the title block for?
options: It tells what specific aircraft, assembly, or sub-assembly the part is used on
It lists which personnel are authorized to use the drawing
It gives the scale and tolerance standards for the drawing
It records every revision that has been made to the drawing
questions.short: What aircraft/assembly/sub-assembly the part is used on.
questions.expl: Example: "Bracket Assy., Roll Servo, S-Tec autopilot installation." Revisions live in the REVISION block — a different block, and a different question on this final.

questions.q: Where is the identification of a print found on a drawing?
options: On the back of the print only
In the title block — usually the lower right-hand corner of the print
In the revision block, with the change history
In the upper left corner, next to the scale
questions.short: Title block — lower right-hand corner (sometimes a strip across the bottom).
questions.expl: Every print must have this identification, grouped in a prominent place.

questions.q: An alloy is best defined as a base metal to which what has been added?
options: Small quantities of other metals or elements
An acid that replaces ions during a chemical reaction
questions.expl: A base metal such as iron to which small quantities of other metals or elements are added is called an alloy; the additions change or improve the chemical/physical properties.

questions.q: Which three metals does the notes page list as the common AIRCRAFT metals?
questions.expl: The notes name aluminum, titanium, and steel as the core aircraft metals. Magnesium is noted as basically retired because it ignites easily and is hard to extinguish.

questions.q: The term 'ferrous' comes from the Latin word for what element?
questions.expl: 'Ferrous' applies to the group of metals having iron as their principal element. Steel is ferrous because it contains iron.

questions.q: Ferrous metals share which physical property that non-ferrous metals lack?
questions.expl: Because ferrous metals contain iron, they are magnetic; therefore non-ferrous (non-iron) materials are non-magnetic. A magnet is a quick field check for ferrous vs. non-ferrous.

questions.q: Why has magnesium been largely retired from aircraft structural use?
options: It catches fire easily and is difficult to extinguish
It is magnetic and disrupts compasses
questions.expl: The notes flag magnesium as the most flammable structural metal — it ignites readily and is difficult to put out, so it has basically been retired from new construction.

questions.q: Strength is the ability of a material to resist what?
options: Deformation, and to resist force without breaking
questions.expl: Strength is one of the most important properties of a material — the ability to resist deformation, and the ability to resist force without breaking.

questions.q: Which strength describes the ability to resist a PULLING (tension) load?
questions.expl: Tensile strength resists being pulled apart (tension). Shear resists side-to-side forces; bearing strength resists crushing/compressing.

questions.q: Shear strength is the ability of a material to resist which type of force?
options: Side-to-side (forces trying to slide layers past each other)
questions.expl: Shear strength resists side-to-side forces — the kind that try to slice or slide one layer past another (e.g., the load on a rivet or bolt shank).

questions.q: Yield strength refers to a material's ability to do what?
options: Resist deformation (resist taking a permanent set)
questions.expl: Yield strength is the point at which a material resists permanent deformation; past the yield point it takes a permanent set and won't spring back.

questions.q: Bearing strength is the ability of a material to resist what kind of load?
questions.expl: Bearing strength resists crushing or compressing loads — important at bolt holes and fittings where one part presses against another.

questions.q: Hardness is the property that lets a material resist what?
options: Abrasion, penetration, cutting action, and permanent distortion
questions.expl: Hardness is the ability of a material to resist abrasion, penetration, cutting action, and permanent distortion. It can be increased by cold-working and, for steel and some aluminum alloys, by heat treatment.

questions.q: Hardness in steel and certain aluminum alloys can be increased by what two methods?
questions.expl: Hardness may be increased by cold working the metal and, in the case of steel and certain aluminum alloys, by heat treatment.

questions.q: Density is best defined as which of the following?
options: The weight of a unit volume of a material
The ability to be drawn into wire
The ability to return to original shape
questions.expl: Density is the weight of a unit volume of a material. In aviation it matters because weight is always a design concern.

questions.q: Malleability is the property that allows a metal to be:
options: Hammered, rolled, or pressed into shape without cracking or breaking
Returned to its original shape after a load is removed
Melted by the application of heat
questions.expl: A malleable metal can be hammered, rolled, or pressed into various shapes without cracking, breaking, or leaving some other detrimental effect.

questions.q: Ductility is the property that permits a metal to be permanently:
options: Drawn, bent, or twisted into shapes without breaking
questions.expl: Ductility permits a metal to be permanently drawn, bent, or twisted into various shapes without breaking. It is essential for metals used in wire and tubing.

questions.q: Which property is ESSENTIAL for metals used in making wire and tubing?
questions.expl: Because wire and tubing must be drawn out without breaking, ductility is the essential property for those metals.

questions.q: Elasticity is the property that enables a metal to:
options: Return to its original shape and size after the deforming force is removed
questions.expl: Elasticity is the property that enables a metal to return to its original shape and size when the force that caused the change of shape is removed.

questions.q: Toughness is a desirable aircraft-metal property meaning the material can:
options: Withstand tearing or shearing and be deformed without breaking
questions.expl: A tough material can withstand tearing or shearing and may be stretched or otherwise deformed without breaking — a highly desirable property in aircraft metals.

questions.q: Brittleness is the property of a metal that allows little bending or deformation without:
questions.expl: A brittle metal breaks or cracks with little change of shape — it is apt to break or crack without much bending or deformation. Cast iron, cast aluminum, and very hard steel are brittle.

questions.q: Fusibility is the ability of a metal to:
options: Become liquid by the application of heat
questions.expl: Fusibility is the ability of a metal to become liquid by the application of heat — the property that makes welding possible. Steel fuses around 2,600 °F; aluminum alloys at about 1,100 °F.

questions.q: Conductivity is the property that enables a metal to carry what?
questions.expl: Conductivity is the property that enables a metal to carry heat or electricity. Heat conductivity governs how much heat is needed for proper fusion in welding.

questions.q: On the aircraft, all conductive metal surfaces are electrically BONDED together so that:
options: Static electricity is dissipated at the trailing edges of the wings (static discharge)
questions.expl: Electrical bonding ties all conductive surfaces together so static electricity is dissipated by the static dischargers at the trailing edges of the wings, eliminating radio interference.

questions.q: Thermal expansion refers to which reactions in a metal?
options: Contraction and expansion caused by cooling and heating
questions.expl: Thermal expansion is the contraction and expansion produced in metals as a result of heating or cooling; heat applied to a metal causes it to expand (become larger).

questions.q: What are the THREE methods of metalworking?
questions.expl: There are three methods of metalworking: hot-working, cold-working, and extruding. The method used depends on the metal and the part required.

questions.q: 'Wrought' describes a process used to do what to a metal?
options: Shape, alter, bend, and manufacture various forms (e.g., iron, steel, aluminum)
Pour molten metal into a mold
Coat the metal with pure aluminum
questions.expl: Wrought refers to metal that has been mechanically worked (shaped, altered, bent, manufactured) into forms, as opposed to cast. Wrought iron, steel, and aluminum are examples.

questions.q: Casting is a process in which liquid metal is:
options: Poured into a mold with a hollow cavity, then allowed to cool and solidify
questions.short: Poured into a mold, then solidified.
questions.expl: Casting pours liquid metal into a mold containing a hollow cavity of the desired shape, then lets it cool and solidify into the part.

questions.q: Hot-working means forming metal at what temperature?
options: An elevated temperature (above the critical range)
questions.expl: Hot-working forms metal at an elevated temperature, above the critical range. Pressing is used for large objects; drop forging hammers while hot; hammering is used for small parts (smith forging).

questions.q: Cold-working is performed at what temperature relative to the metal's critical range?
options: Below the critical temperature (room temp) — e.g., cold rolling and cold drawing
questions.expl: Cold-working is mechanical working performed below the metal's critical temperature; the two key processes for a mechanic are cold rolling and cold drawing. It strain-hardens the metal.

questions.q: In extrusion, hot or cold metal is forced through a ___ to produce the desired shape.
questions.expl: Extrusion forces hot or cold metal through a die that produces the desired cross-sectional shape — used mostly on aluminum.

questions.q: Non-ferrous metals are defined by the fact that they:
options: Do not contain iron (iron is not the base/principal constituent)
questions.expl: Non-ferrous refers to all metals that have elements OTHER than iron as their base or principal constituent — aluminum, titanium, copper, magnesium, plus alloys like Monel.

questions.q: Why is PURE aluminum alloyed with other metals for aircraft use?
options: Pure aluminum lacks sufficient strength for most aircraft applications
questions.expl: Commercially pure aluminum lacks sufficient strength for most aircraft applications, so it is alloyed with other metals to increase its strength.

questions.q: In the wrought aluminum index, the 1xxx series designates:
questions.expl: The 1xxx group is 99% or higher pure aluminum — excellent corrosion resistance and conductivity, low mechanical properties.

questions.q: What is the principal alloying element in the 2xxx aluminum series (e.g., 2024)?
questions.expl: Copper is the principal alloying element of the 2xxx series; its best-known alloy is 2024. It has poor corrosion resistance unclad, so it is usually clad.

questions.q: What is the principal alloying element in the 3xxx aluminum series (e.g., 3003)?
questions.expl: Manganese is the principal alloying element of the 3xxx series, such as 3003 — a common non-heat-treatable alloy.

questions.q: What is the principal alloying element in the 4xxx aluminum series?
questions.expl: Silicon is the principal alloying element of the 4xxx series; silicon lowers the melting point and is used in welding/brazing filler.

questions.q: What is the principal alloying element in the 5xxx aluminum series (e.g., 5052)?
questions.expl: Magnesium is the principal alloying element of the 5xxx series, such as 5052 — a common non-heat-treatable alloy with good corrosion resistance.

questions.q: The 6xxx aluminum series is alloyed principally with which TWO elements?
questions.expl: The 6xxx series uses magnesium and silicon together (e.g., 6061) — heat-treatable, good corrosion resistance, widely used.

questions.q: What is the principal alloying element in the 7xxx aluminum series (e.g., 7075)?
questions.expl: Zinc is the principal alloying element of the 7xxx series, such as 7075 — a very high-strength heat-treatable alloy.

questions.q: The 8xxx aluminum series is designated for:
options: Other elements (alloying elements not covered by 2xxx-7xxx)
questions.expl: The 8xxx group covers 'other elements' — alloying constituents that don't fall into the 2xxx through 7xxx categories.

questions.q: Most external aircraft surfaces are made of which aluminum?
questions.expl: Most external surfaces of aircraft are made of Alclad aluminum for its corrosion protection.

questions.q: Alclad is best described as:
options: Pure aluminum coated on the surface of an alloyed-aluminum core to improve corrosion resistance
Aluminum with a paint primer baked on
questions.short: Pure Al clad over alloy core for corrosion resistance.
questions.expl: Alclad (and Pureclad) is a sheet with an aluminum-alloy core coated with a layer of PURE aluminum (~5.5% on each side) that protects the core both as a barrier and electrolytically (sacrificially).

questions.q: When discussing aluminum, the property most concerned with is:
options: Weight (aluminum is valued for its light weight)
questions.expl: Matthew's note: 'Most always think of WEIGHT when discussing aluminum.' Aluminum's light weight is its headline aircraft property.

questions.q: Heat treatment is the process of HEATING and COOLING metals to:
options: Coat the metal with pure aluminum
Gain a desired quality (e.g., hardness, softness, strength)
questions.short: Heating + cooling to gain a desired quality.
questions.expl: Heat treating is the controlled heating and cooling of metals to gain a desired quality such as hardness, strength, or softness.

questions.q: The two heat treatments used on ALUMINUM are:
options: Solution heat treatment and precipitation (age) heat treatment
questions.expl: There are two types of heat treatment applicable to aluminum alloys: solution heat treatment and precipitation (also called age) heat treatment.

questions.q: Solution heat treating involves heating the alloy until the constituents enter solid solution, then:
options: Quenching to hold the constituents in solution
questions.short: Quench to hold constituents in solution.
questions.expl: In solution heat treatment the alloy is heated so the alloying constituent enters solid solution, then quenched to lock it in a supersaturated, unstable state ready to age.

questions.q: Which quenching medium is the FASTEST (strongest), and which is the SLOWEST?
options: Oil is fastest; brine is slowest
Brine is fastest; oil is slowest (water is in between)
Water is fastest; brine is slowest
Air is fastest; water is slowest
questions.expl: Of the three quenching liquids, brine is the strongest (fastest) quenching medium, water is next, and oil is the least (slowest). Brine is a 5-10% salt solution.

questions.q: Brine quench is a salt solution of approximately what concentration?
questions.expl: Brine is a 5 to 10 percent solution of salt in water, used as the fastest quench because the salt speeds heat removal.

questions.q: Precipitation (age) heat treatment occurs when the metal, after solution treatment, is:
options: Quenched a second time in brine
Allowed to age harden — naturally at room temperature or artificially at an elevated temperature
questions.expl: Precipitation hardening (also called age-hardening, precipitation-hardening, or precipitation heat treatment) is when the solution-treated metal hardens by aging — naturally at room temperature, or artificially at an elevated temperature.

questions.q: The temper designation -T2 means:
questions.expl: T2 designates annealed (cast products only). Memorize the full ladder T1-T10.

questions.q: The temper designation -T3 means:
questions.expl: T3 = solution heat-treated and then cold-worked (e.g., 2024-T3 sheet).

questions.q: The temper designation -T4 means:
questions.expl: T4 = solution heat-treated, then naturally aged. The handbook also notes T4 and W indicate solution-treated and quenched but not (fully) aged.

questions.q: The temper designation -T5 means:
questions.expl: T5 = artificially aged only (no separate solution-treat step in the designation).

questions.q: The temper designation -T6 means:
questions.expl: T6 = solution heat-treated and then artificially aged — the hardened, heat-treated condition (e.g., 7075-T6, 6061-T6).

questions.q: The temper designation -T7 means:
questions.expl: T7 = solution heat-treated and then stabilized.

questions.q: The temper designation -T8 means:
options: Solution heat-treated, cold-worked, then artificially aged
questions.expl: T8 = solution heat-treated, cold-worked, and then artificially aged (the notes phrase it 'solution, strained and artificially aged').

questions.q: The temper designation -T9 means:
options: Solution heat-treated, artificially aged, then cold-worked (strain hardened)
questions.expl: T9 = solution heat-treated, artificially aged, and then cold-worked (the notes: 'solution, artificially aged, strain hardened').

questions.q: The temper designation -T10 means:
questions.expl: T10 = artificially aged and then cold-worked (the notes: 'artificially aged, strain hardened').

questions.q: Alclad material is generally limited to no more than how many reheat treatments?
questions.expl: Heat-treatable metals can generally be reheat-treated any number of times, but ALCLAD material is generally limited to no more than three reheat treatments, because repeated treatment lets the alloy diffuse into the clad layer and defeats the cladding.

questions.q: Annealing is the process that softens a metal to do what, and it is the opposite of:
options: Decreases internal stress; it is the opposite of hardening
Removes the clad layer; opposite of cladding
questions.short: Softens / relieves stress; opposite of hardening.
questions.expl: Annealing softens a metal to decrease internal stress; it is the opposite of hardening. The temperature is raised to the proper annealing temperature then cooled to 500 °F at a rate of about 50 °F per hour.

questions.q: Which three are the common NON-heat-treatable aluminum alloys from the notes?
questions.expl: 1100 (pure), 3003 (manganese), and 5052 (magnesium) are non-heat-treatable — they are strengthened by strain hardening (cold work), not by heat treatment.

questions.q: Strain hardening (work hardening) strengthens a metal by:
options: Mechanically working it below the critical temperature
Heating it above the critical range and quenching
questions.expl: Strain hardening (also called cold working or work hardening) mechanically works the metal below its critical temperature to strengthen it. Hardness/temper is shown by a letter designation.

questions.q: The hardness designation 'F' means:
questions.expl: F designates the 'as fabricated' condition — no special control over the temper.

questions.q: The hardness designation 'O' means:
options: Annealed, recrystallized (softest temper of wrought products)
questions.expl: O designates the annealed, recrystallized temper — the softest condition of wrought products.

questions.q: The hardness designation 'H1' means:
questions.expl: H1 (plus digits) = strain hardened ONLY.

questions.q: The hardness designation 'H2' means:
questions.expl: H2 (plus digits) = strain hardened and PARTIALLY annealed.

questions.q: The hardness designation 'H3' means:
questions.expl: H3 (plus digits) = strain hardened and STABILIZED.

questions.q: How is magnesium produced, per the notes?
options: By smelting iron ore with coke
questions.expl: Magnesium is one of the lightest structural metals and is obtained from seawater (electrolysis of sea water), brines, and ores like dolomite/magnesite. Like aluminum it is alloyed with other metals.

questions.q: Titanium is best described as a:
options: Pure metal that needs no alloying
Light-weight, high-strength metal with excellent corrosion resistance
questions.expl: Titanium is a light-weight, high-strength metal with excellent corrosion resistance. It falls between aluminum and stainless steel in density and is about 60% heavier than aluminum, 50% lighter than stainless.

questions.q: The three classifications (A-B-C) of titanium alloys are:
questions.expl: The A-B-C classification names the three titanium families: A = alpha, B = beta, C = alpha-beta (combination). The notes list them as alpha, alpha-beta, and beta.

questions.q: Monel is a high-NICKEL alloy whose approximate composition is:
options: About 68% nickel and 29% copper (plus small iron and manganese)
questions.expl: Monel, the leading high-nickel alloy, is about 68% nickel and 29% copper plus small amounts of iron and manganese. It combines high strength with excellent corrosion resistance and CANNOT be hardened by heat treatment.

questions.q: Inconel is a nickel alloy whose approximate composition (AIM deck figure) is:
options: About 80% nickel and 14% chromium (plus small iron/other elements)
questions.expl: AIM deck / note figure: Inconel ≈ 80% nickel and 14% chromium with small iron and other elements. NOTE the FAA-H-8083-31B describes Inconel generically as a nickel-chromium-iron super alloy (Inconel 625/718) for high-temp powerplant use — exam may phrase it that way. Both Monel and Inconel are frequently used on turbine engines.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p. 7-3 to 7-11; FAA-H-8083-31B (AIM notes p.7)

questions.q: Monel and Inconel are noted as being 'frequently used on' what?
questions.expl: Both nickel alloys (Monel and Inconel) are frequently used on turbine engines because of their high strength and corrosion resistance at elevated temperatures.

questions.q: In aircraft, copper is NOT used in structural areas; its primary use is:
options: Electrical wiring / electrical system (bus bars, bonding, lock wire)
questions.expl: Copper's structural use is limited by its great weight, so it is used primarily in the electrical system — bus bars, bonding, and lock wire — because of its high electrical conductivity.

questions.q: Brass is copper alloyed with which element (plus small amounts of others)?
questions.expl: Brass is a copper alloy containing ZINC (commonly 30-45%) plus small amounts of other metals. (Bronze, by contrast, is copper + tin.)

questions.q: Bronze is a copper alloy containing up to about 25% of which element?
questions.expl: True bronzes are copper alloys containing TIN (up to ~25%, but those under ~11% are most useful for tube fittings). Do not confuse with brass (copper + zinc).

questions.q: Beryllium copper is approximately:
options: 97% copper, 2% beryllium, plus enough nickel for strength/elongation
questions.expl: Beryllium copper contains about 97% copper, 2% beryllium, and enough nickel to increase elongation. Heat treatment can raise its tensile strength from 70,000 psi (annealed) to 200,000 psi — good for diaphragms, bearings, springs.

questions.q: Any alloy metal that has IRON as its chief ingredient is considered:
questions.expl: Any alloy metal with iron as its chief ingredient is ferrous. Steel is iron with carbon added.

questions.q: Steel is iron with what added?
questions.expl: Steel is iron with carbon added (up to ~1%). Carbon steel forms the base of alloy steels made by adding other elements.

questions.q: The density of steel is approximately how many grams per cubic centimeter?
questions.expl: Steel's density is approximately 7.9 grams per cubic centimeter — roughly three times aluminum's 2.7 g/cc, which is why weight is a constant trade-off.

questions.q: Iron in its pure state is cast as:
questions.expl: Known in its pure state as cast iron or pig iron, iron is made by mixing iron ore with coke and limestone and heating. Steel is iron with carbon added.

questions.q: The SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) classifies steel with a code of how many digits?
questions.expl: SAE (with AISI) classifies steel with a four-digit number. The first digit = type/alloy class, the second = percent of the main alloying agent, and the last two = carbon content in hundredths of a percent (points).

questions.q: In the SAE 4-digit steel code, the FIRST number represents:
options: The type (class) of steel / primary alloying agent group
questions.expl: The first digit represents the type of steel (alloy class): 1 = carbon, 2 = nickel, 3 = nickel-chromium, 4 = molybdenum, 5 = chromium, etc.

questions.q: In the SAE 4-digit steel code, the LAST TWO digits represent:
options: The carbon content in hundredths of one percent (points of carbon)
questions.expl: The last two digits give the carbon content in hundredths of one percent. Example: SAE 1095 = plain carbon (1), 0% special alloy (0), 0.95% carbon (95).

questions.q: In SAE 1030, what is the carbon content?
questions.expl: Reading SAE 1030: '10' = plain carbon steel, '30' = 0.30% carbon. Low-carbon steel (0.10-0.30%) is used for safety wire, nuts, rod ends.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of the heat treatments of ferrous metals listed in the notes?
questions.short: Anodizing is NOT a ferrous heat treatment.
questions.expl: The notes' ferrous heat treatments are: hardening, tempering, annealing, normalizing, and case hardening (carburizing & nitriding). Anodizing is an aluminum SURFACE/corrosion treatment, not a ferrous heat treatment.

questions.q: In plain carbon steel, the maximum hardness obtainable by heat treatment depends almost entirely on what?
options: The carbon content of the steel
questions.expl: In plain carbon steel, maximum hardness depends almost entirely on the carbon content. Pure iron, wrought iron, and extremely low-carbon steels cannot be appreciably hardened because they lack a hardening element.

questions.q: Tempering reduces the BRITTLENESS imparted by hardening and:
options: Produces definite physical properties; it always FOLLOWS hardening and softens the steel slightly
questions.expl: Tempering reduces the brittleness imparted by hardening and produces definite physical properties. Tempering ALWAYS follows the hardening operation and softens the steel slightly to relieve stresses.

questions.q: Annealing of steel produces a fine, soft, ductile metal WITHOUT internal stresses; in the annealed state steel has its:
questions.expl: Annealing produces a fine-grained, soft, ductile metal without internal stresses or strains; in the annealed state steel has its lowest strength. Annealing is the opposite of hardening.

questions.q: Normalizing of steel does what to internal stresses set up by heat-treating, welding, casting, forming, or machining?
questions.expl: Normalizing relieves the internal stresses produced by machining, forging, welding, etc. It is done by heating the steel above the upper critical point and cooling in STILL AIR — producing a harder, stronger material than annealing.

questions.q: Case hardening produces what kind of part?
options: A hard, wear-resistant surface (case) over a strong, tough core
A part with a soft surface and hard core
questions.short: Hard case over a tough core.
questions.expl: Case hardening produces a hard, wear-resistant surface over a strong, tough core. It is ideal for parts needing a wear surface but a tough interior. Best suited to LOW-carbon and low-alloy steels.

questions.q: Carburizing is a case-hardening process in which ___ is added to the surface of low-carbon steel.
questions.expl: Carburizing adds CARBON to the surface of low-carbon steel, giving a high-carbon (hardenable) surface over a low-carbon, tough interior. When heat-treated, the case hardens while the core stays soft and tough.

questions.q: Nitriding differs from other case-hardening processes because, before nitriding, the part is:
options: Packed in charcoal and heated to 1,700 °F
Heat-treated AND tempered first, then placed in a special furnace at ~1,000 °F with ammonia gas
questions.expl: In nitriding the part is heat-treated and tempered BEFORE nitriding, then placed in a special nitriding furnace heated to ~1,000 °F while ammonia gas circulates. The ammonia cracks into nitrogen and hydrogen; nitrogen reacts with iron to form a very hard nitride case.

questions.q: Carburizing (pack method) heats the parts to approximately what temperature?
questions.expl: In pack carburizing the steel is packed with charcoal, sealed, and heated to about 1,700 °F and soaked. (Contrast: nitriding runs cooler, ~1,000 °F.)

questions.q: During nitriding, the ammonia gas cracks (breaks down) into which two elements?
questions.expl: At ~1,000 °F the ammonia gas cracks into nitrogen and hydrogen. The nitrogen reacts with the iron at the surface to form the nitride case; the hydrogen and undissociated ammonia pass on.

questions.q: Why have wood, fabric, and magnesium nearly disappeared from aircraft construction since the mid-1950s?
options: They were banned by the FAA
They became too expensive to mine
They were replaced by aluminum/alloys and, increasingly, reinforced plastics and advanced composites
questions.expl: Wood, fabric, and magnesium use nearly disappeared after the mid-1950s; aluminum dropped from ~80% of airframes in 1950 to ~15% today, replaced increasingly by reinforced plastics and advanced composites.

questions.q: Which are the three types of wood used in aircraft structures, per the notes?
questions.expl: The notes list solid (for wing spars), laminated (wing spar made of two or three pieces glued together), and plywood (two or more thin layers glued so the grain crosses at 45° or 90°).

questions.q: In plywood, the thin layers are glued so the grain of adjacent layers crosses at:
questions.expl: Plywood is two or more thin layers glued so that grain crosses at 45 or 90 degrees — the cross-grain construction is what gives plywood its strength in multiple directions.

questions.q: Resin (a plastic material) is used in aircraft for which application?
options: Canopies, windshields, windows, and similar transparent enclosures
questions.expl: Thermoplastic resin materials are used in aircraft canopies, windshields, windows, and other similar transparent enclosures.

questions.q: Transparent plastics divide into two classes; what is the key difference between thermoplastic and thermosetting?
options: Both soften every time they are heated
Thermoplastic cannot be reshaped; thermosetting can
Thermoplastic softens when heated and hardens when cooled (can be reshaped); thermosetting hardens on heating and CANNOT be reshaped
questions.expl: Thermoplastic materials soften when heated and harden when cooled — they can be reheated and reshaped. Thermosetting plastics harden upon heating and reheating has no softening effect; once cured they cannot be reshaped.

questions.q: Which two transparent THERMOPLASTICS are named in the notes?
questions.expl: The two transparent thermoplastics named are cellulose acetate (older) and acrylic (modern). Both soften with heat.

questions.q: Cellulose acetate was used on early aircraft for windshields/side windows but has a tendency to:
options: Shrink and turn yellow with age
questions.expl: Cellulose acetate was used on early aircraft for windshields and side windows but tends to shrink and turn yellow with age, so it was largely replaced by acrylic.

questions.q: Acrylic plastic is stiffer and more transparent than cellulose acetate, and acetone leaves what on it?
options: A white residue (it is identifiable that way)
questions.short: Acetone leaves a white residue on acrylic.
questions.expl: Acrylic is stiffer and more transparent than cellulose acetate; acetone leaves a WHITE residue on acrylic — a quick way to tell the two apart (acetone softens cellulose acetate instead).

questions.q: Which three are common TRADE NAMES for acrylic plastic?
questions.expl: Acrylic plastic is sold under the trade names Lucite, Plexiglas, and Perspex — all the same family of transparent thermoplastic.

questions.q: A composite material is made from how many constituent materials?
options: Two or more, with significantly different properties, that remain separate and distinct
questions.expl: A composite is made from two or more constituent materials with significantly different physical or chemical properties that, combined, produce a material with characteristics different from the individual components — and they remain separate and distinct within the finished structure.

questions.q: In composite construction, fiberglass cloth/mat provides strength but lacks rigidity, so it is impregnated with what to make a structural part?
options: Polyester (or epoxy) resin, then molded to shape
questions.expl: Fiberglass cloth/mat has great strength for its weight but lacks rigidity; to make a structural part it is impregnated with polyester (or epoxy) resin and molded into the desired shape.

questions.q: In composite resin chemistry, the CATALYST does what?
questions.expl: A catalyst (often benzoyl peroxide for polyester) shortens and starts the curing (polymerization) process. By contrast, an INHIBITOR is added to PREVENT hardening before use.

questions.q: An INHIBITOR is added to composite resin in order to:
options: Prevent it from hardening before use
questions.expl: An inhibitor is added to prevent the resin from hardening before it is intended to be used (extends shelf/working life). The catalyst does the opposite — it starts the cure.

questions.q: Epoxy resin can be used in place of polyester resin because it has:
options: No need for any hardener and instant cure
A low percentage of shrinkage and high strength, and it does not need a catalyst (uses a curing agent/hardener)
questions.short: Low shrinkage, high strength; uses a curing agent.
questions.expl: Epoxy resin can replace polyester: it has a low percentage of shrinkage, high strength, and doesn't need a catalyst — instead it needs a curing agent (hardener) to start the hardening process.

questions.q: In composites, the STYRENE monomer is added to the resin to do what?
options: Thin the resin (act as a monomer that helps it flow/cure)
questions.short: Styrene = monomer added to the resin.
questions.expl: Styrene is a monomer added to polyester resin (it helps the resin flow and cross-link during cure). Polyester itself is a thick, complex molecule cured by a chemical reaction.

questions.q: Heat is used to affect the curing process; some resins become more active above what temperature?
options: About 90 °F (and may run off vertical surfaces before they set)
questions.expl: Heat speeds curing; some resins become very active above about 90 °F and may run off inclined or vertical surfaces before they set, so working temperature must be controlled.

questions.q: Thixotropic agents are added to a resin to increase its:
options: Viscosity (thickness) — so it can be made into a paste
questions.expl: Thixotropic agents are added to increase viscosity (thickness). A common type is hollow glass balls (microballoons) used to thicken epoxy or polyester resin into a paste for filling.

questions.q: Fiberglass is glass fibers woven into cloth used for:
options: High strength (it is saturated with resin and used where low cost matters more than strength)
questions.expl: Fiberglass is glass fibers woven into a cloth — used for high strength, saturated with resin. It is used where low cost is more important than maximum strength; newer composites have replaced it in many applications.

questions.q: Ceramic fiber is a form of fiber glass used in HIGH-HEAT applications (up to ~3000 °F). Where is it used?
options: Around engines and exhaust systems (high-temperature areas)
questions.expl: Ceramic fiber is a form of fiber glass used in high-heat applications (up to ~3000 °F) — around engines and exhaust systems where ordinary composites would fail.

questions.q: Kevlar (aramid) is a yellow organic fiber valued for:
options: Impact resistance — extremely light, strong, and tough (used where damage from debris occurs, e.g., landing gear and behind propellers)
questions.expl: Kevlar is a yellow organic fiber with high impact resistance — extremely light, strong, and tough. It is used in areas where damage from sand or debris occurs, such as landing gear and behind propellers. (Identify it by its YELLOW color.)

questions.q: Graphite (carbon) fiber, manufactured by heating and stretching rayon fibers, is valued for being:
options: Extremely light weight, strong, and stiff (rigid)
questions.expl: Graphite (carbon) fiber is made by heating and stretching rayon fibers; it is extremely light, strong, and STIFF (rigid). Stiffness is its defining trait — distinguishing it from impact-tough Kevlar.

questions.q: A laminated structure built with an inner CORE of material is called a:
questions.expl: A composite made with an inner core between two face sheets is a SANDWICH structure. Sandwich (laminate) construction is strong and stiff but light — and laminate is equal in strength yet much lighter (very important for aerospace).

questions.q: Which are the three types of CORES listed in the notes?
questions.expl: The three core types in the notes are metal core, wood core, and cellular core (honeycomb / cellular materials). Each is bonded between face sheets.

questions.q: A METAL core composite uses an extremely thin material in the shape of a honeycomb made of:
options: Aluminum, bonded between sheets of fiberglass/resin
questions.expl: A metal core composite uses extremely thin ALUMINUM in a honeycomb shape, bonded between sheets of fiberglass/resin (or metal).

questions.q: A WOOD core uses end-grain slabs of which wood between face sheets?
options: Balsa wood (between two face sheets of fiberglass or metal)
questions.expl: Wood core uses end-grain slabs of BALSA wood between two face sheets of fiberglass or metal — used for floors, wall panels, and aircraft skin.

questions.q: A CELLULAR core composite uses a resin-impregnated material such as:
options: Kraft paper or glass cloth bonded between two sheets of fiberglass
questions.expl: Cellular core uses resin-impregnated KRAFT paper (or glass cloth) bonded between two sheets of fiberglass to form a lightweight cellular (honeycomb) core.

questions.q: Honeycomb (No-Step / No-Push) materials have high aerodynamic strength but poor PHYSICAL strength, meaning:
options: Pushing or standing on them can cause physical damage to the structure
They cannot carry any flight loads
questions.short: Don't step/push — physical strength is poor.
questions.expl: Honeycomb-cored panels have high aerodynamic strength but poor physical (point-load) strength; standing or pushing on them can crush/damage the structure — hence the 'No-Step' and 'No-Push' placards.

questions.q: Natural rubber is used to prevent the entrance of dirt, water, or air and the loss of fluids; where does the latex come from?
options: The rubber tree (harvested as latex by 'tapping' the bark)
questions.short: From the rubber tree (latex, by tapping).
questions.expl: Natural rubber comes from the rubber tree as latex — a sticky, milky colloid drawn off by making incisions in the bark and collecting it ('tapping'). It is then refined into rubber.

questions.q: Why is natural rubber's use in aircraft LIMITED, and where is it never used?
options: It conducts electricity; never used in tires
It is too heavy; never used in seals
It has inferior resistance to deterioration; it is never used in hoses
It is magnetic; never used in cores
questions.short: Inferior deterioration resistance; never in hoses.
questions.expl: Natural rubber's aircraft use is limited by its inferior resistance to the influences that cause deterioration (fuel, solvents, ozone). It is never used in hoses; synthetic rubbers are used there instead.

questions.q: Shock absorber cord is made from what, encased in a braided cotton cover?
questions.expl: Shock absorber cord is made from natural rubber strands encased in a braided cover of woven cotton cords treated to resist oxidation and wear. Great tension/elongation comes from weaving the jacket while the strands are stretched ~3× their length.

questions.q: What is the difference between Type I and Type II shock absorbing cord?
options: Type I is a straight cord; Type II is a continuous ring known as a 'bungee'
Type I is a ring; Type II is straight
Type I is synthetic; Type II is natural
Type I is for tires; Type II is for seals
questions.short: Type I straight; Type II ring (bungee).
questions.expl: There are two types of elastic shock absorbing cord: Type I is a STRAIGHT cord, and Type II is a continuous RING known as a 'bungee.' The Type II ring is easily/quickly replaced and needs no stretching-and-whipping to secure.

questions.q: Synthetic rubber, created in 1906 by Fritz Hofmann, comes in several types. Which are the four named in the notes?
questions.expl: The notes' four synthetic rubbers are Butyl, Buna-S, Buna-N, and Neoprene. Each is compounded for different properties.

questions.q: Butyl rubber is a hydrocarbon rubber with SUPERIOR resistance to what?
options: Gas permeation (it also resists oxygen, ozone, and weathering)
questions.expl: Butyl is a hydrocarbon rubber with superior resistance to GAS permeation; it also resists oxygen, vegetable oils, animal fats, alkalies, ozone, and weathering. (But it swells in petroleum/coal-tar solvents.) Used with phosphate-ester hydraulic fluids (Skydrol).

questions.q: Buna-S rubber most resembles natural rubber but has POOR resistance to gasoline and oil, so it is normally used for:
options: Tires and tubes (as a substitute for natural rubber)
questions.expl: Buna-S resembles natural rubber and has poor resistance to gasoline, oil, acids, and solvents — so it is normally used for tires and tubes as a substitute for natural rubber.

questions.q: Buna-N is OUTSTANDING in its resistance to hydrocarbons, so it is used as a seal for what?
options: Oil and gasoline (fuel/oil systems — it won't stick to the cylinder wall)
questions.expl: Buna-N is outstanding in resistance to hydrocarbons and other solvents; it is used for oil and gasoline (won't stick to the cylinder wall). It is the go-to seal for fuel/oil systems.

questions.q: Neoprene takes more punishment than natural rubber and has exceptional resistance to ozone, sunlight, heat, and aging — so it is used primarily for:
options: Weather seals, window channels, bumper pads, oil-resistant hose, and carburetor diaphragms
questions.expl: Neoprene resists ozone, sunlight, heat, and aging; it is used primarily for weather seals, window channels, bumper pads, oil-resistant hose, and carburetor diaphragms. (Think 'Neoprene = weather.')

questions.q: Seals are used to:
options: Conduct heat away from the engine
Prevent fluid from passing a certain point (and keep air/dirt out)
questions.short: Prevent fluid from passing a point.
questions.expl: Seals prevent fluid from passing a certain point, and keep air and dirt out of the system. The three main classes are packings, gaskets, and wipers.

questions.q: What are the THREE main types/classes of seals?
questions.expl: The three main classes of seals are packings (running/moving seals), gaskets (static seals), and wipers (which clean and lubricate moving parts such as shafts/pistons).

questions.q: PACKINGS are used as which kind of seal?
options: 'Running seals' — in units that contain MOVING parts (actuating cylinders, pumps, selector valves)
Static seals between two flat surfaces
questions.expl: Packings are 'running seals' used in units that contain moving parts — actuating cylinders, pumps, selector valves, and so forth. They are made as O-rings, V-rings, and U-rings.

questions.q: GASKETS are used as which kind of seal?
options: Static (stationary) seals between two flat surfaces
questions.expl: Gaskets are static (stationary) seals used between two flat surfaces. Common gasket materials are asbestos, copper, cork, and rubber.

questions.q: When O-rings are installed with backup rings, what are those backup rings for?
options: To prevent extrusion of the O-ring at high pressure (above ~1,500 psi)
questions.short: Prevent O-ring extrusion at high pressure.
questions.expl: O-rings are installed with BACKUP RINGS (Teflon, MS28782) to prevent extrusion. At pressures above ~1,500 psi, backup rings are required; with pressure from one side use one backup ring (on the side AWAY from the pressure), with pressure from both sides use two.

questions.q: An O-ring designed as a STATIC seal, if installed on a moving part, will probably:
options: Work better than a dynamic seal
Fail to do the job (static and dynamic O-rings are not interchangeable)
questions.short: It will not do the job.
questions.expl: An O-ring designed specifically as a static (stationary) seal probably will NOT do the job when installed on a moving part such as a hydraulic piston. O-rings look alike but their compounds differ widely — match the application, fluid, and temperature.

questions.q: Metal-head hammers are usually sized according to what?
options: The hardness of the head material
The diameter of the striking face
The weight of the head alone, without the handle
The overall length of the hammer including the handle
questions.short: The weight of the head alone (without the handle).
questions.expl: Metal-head hammers are sized by the weight of the head only; the handle is not counted.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Hammers & Mallets)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Peen hammers are used in commercial sheet-metal work primarily to do what?
questions.expl: Peen hammers are used in commercial sheet-metal work to bend metal; they are NOT intended for driving nails.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Hammers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which peen hammer has a flat face and a ball-shaped end?
questions.expl: The ball-peen hammer has a flat striking face and a rounded (ball) end opposite it.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1, Fig 11-1 (Hammers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The straight-peen hammer has a flat face and a wedge-shaped end oriented how?
options: Set at 45 degrees to the handle
At a right angle to the handle
questions.expl: A straight-peen hammer's wedge end runs parallel (in line) with the handle; the cross-peen's wedge runs at a right angle to it.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1, Fig 11-1 (Hammers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The cross-peen hammer has a flat face and a wedge-shaped end oriented how?
options: At a right angle to the handle
questions.short: At a right angle to the handle.
questions.expl: The cross-peen's wedge is set at a right angle to the handle (the opposite of the straight peen).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1, Fig 11-1 (Hammers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A mallet is a hammer-like tool with a head made of which materials?
questions.expl: A mallet has a soft head (hickory, rawhide, or rubber) so it can shape thin metal without leaving creases or dents from abrupt corners.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Mallets / soft-faced)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A soft-faced hammer is handy for what kind of work?
options: Shaping thin metal parts without creases or dents
questions.short: Shaping thin metal without causing creases or dents.
questions.expl: Soft-faced hammers/mallets shape thin metal and soft aluminum without marring it; a steel hammer used the same way would crease or dent the part.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Mallets / soft-faced)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The two types of soft-faced (plastic-tipped) hammers his notes list are:
options: Soft plastic tipped and hard plastic tipped
questions.short: Soft plastic tipped and hard plastic tipped.
questions.expl: His notes list two soft-faced types: soft plastic tipped and hard plastic tipped. The replaceable tips let a worn or wrong-hardness tip be swapped.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (soft-faced hammers); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The tips on a soft-faced (plastic-tipped) hammer are designed to be:
questions.expl: Soft-faced hammer tips are replaceable, so a worn tip (or the wrong hardness) can be swapped instead of discarding the hammer.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A soft-faced hammer is commonly used to shape what material?
questions.expl: Soft-faced hammers are used to shape soft aluminum alloy without marring or work-hardening the surface.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A screwdriver is classified by which three things?
options: Its shape, type of blade, and blade length
questions.short: Shape, type of blade, and blade length.
questions.expl: The screwdriver is classified by shape, type of blade, and blade length. It has one purpose: loosening/tightening screws or screw-head bolts.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A common (flat-blade) screwdriver is sized in inches by the length of its:
questions.expl: A common screwdriver is sized in inches by the length of the shank (the metal shaft between handle and tip).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Screwdrivers); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A common screwdriver blade must fill at least what percentage of the screw slot?
questions.short: At least 75 percent of the slot.
questions.expl: A common screwdriver must fill at least 75% of the screw slot; the wrong size cuts and burrs the slot, possibly requiring a screw extractor.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2 (Screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: When selecting a common screwdriver, you should pick:
options: The smallest blade that fits in the slot
The largest screwdriver whose blade makes a good fit
A blade exactly one size larger than the slot
questions.short: The largest screwdriver whose blade makes a good fit.
questions.expl: Select the largest screwdriver whose blade makes a good fit in the slot — that gives the most contact and the least chance of slipping or burring.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1/11-2 (Screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: What happens if a common screwdriver is the wrong size for the slot?
options: It cuts and burrs the slot, possibly ruining the screw
questions.short: It cuts and burrs the slot, possibly ruining the screw.
questions.expl: A wrong-size blade cuts and burrs the slot (may need a screw extractor) and can slip and damage adjacent structure.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2 (Screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: An offset screwdriver is used where:
options: There is no clearance for a straight screwdriver
questions.short: Where there is no clearance (tight spots).
questions.expl: Offset screwdrivers reach screws in tight places where there is no clearance for a normal straight screwdriver.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2 (Screwdrivers); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A common (flat-tip) screwdriver is also called a:
questions.expl: The common screwdriver is the flat / straight-slot type, used where slotted-head screws or fasteners are found.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-1 (Screwdrivers); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The two types of recessed-head (crosspoint) screwdrivers are the Phillips and the:
questions.expl: The two recessed-head crosspoint types are the Phillips and the Reed & Prince. They are NOT interchangeable.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2 (Recessed-head screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which recessed-head screwdriver has a SHARP pointed tip?
questions.expl: The Reed & Prince has a sharp pointed tip; the Phillips is blunt. They look similar but are NOT interchangeable.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2, Fig 11-2 (Recessed-head); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The Phillips screwdriver has a blunt tip and is sized by which numbers?
questions.expl: The Phillips has a blunt tip and is sized #1, #2, #3, #4 (his notes). The Reed & Prince, by contrast, has a sharp point.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2 (Recessed-head); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Phillips and Reed & Prince screwdriver tips are:
options: Similar in appearance but NOT interchangeable
The same tool with two names
questions.expl: Although they look alike, the Phillips is not interchangeable with the Reed & Prince — using the wrong one mutilates the screw head.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-2 (Recessed-head screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A replaceable-tip ('10-in-1') screwdriver allows for:
options: More torque than a fixed driver
Quick changing of the tip and economical replacement when worn
questions.short: Quick tip changes and cheap replacement of worn tips.
questions.expl: The replaceable-tip ('10-in-1') screwdriver lets you quickly swap tips and economically replace a tip when it becomes worn.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Replaceable tip screwdrivers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Replaceable screwdriver tips are commonly called:
questions.expl: Replaceable screwdriver tips are commonly called apex bits.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A speed handle with apex bits is best used when:
options: A large number of screws must be removed/installed and no power tools are available or allowed
questions.short: Many screws to remove/install and no power tools available/allowed.
questions.expl: Speed handles (with apex bits) speed up removing or installing a large number of screws by hand when power tools aren't available or allowed.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The pliers used most frequently in aircraft repair work are the:
questions.expl: The pliers used most often in aircraft work are the diagonal, the needle-nose, and the duckbill.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Pliers); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Plier size indicates the tool's overall length, usually ranging from:
questions.expl: The size of pliers indicates their overall length, usually ranging from 5 to 12 inches.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Pliers); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which is one of the two important rules for using pliers?
options: Never use pliers to turn nuts or bolts
Always use pliers to cut hardened steel
Always oil the joint before use
questions.short: Never use pliers to turn nuts or bolts.
questions.expl: Rule: do NOT use pliers to turn nuts — in seconds, pliers can damage a nut more than years of service. Also: don't work pliers beyond their capacity.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Pliers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Needle-nose pliers are used to:
options: Hold objects and make adjustments in tight places
questions.short: Hold objects and make adjustments in tight places.
questions.expl: Needle-nose pliers have half-round jaws of varying lengths; they hold objects and make adjustments in tight spots.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Pliers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Duckbill pliers are used exclusively for:
questions.expl: Duckbill pliers have thin, flat jaws shaped like a duck's bill and are used exclusively for twisting safety wire.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Pliers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Diagonal cutters ('dikes') are used to:
options: Cut wire, rivets, small screws, and cotter pins, and remove safety wire
questions.short: Cut wire, rivets, small screws, cotter pins; remove safety wire.
questions.expl: Diagonal cutters cut wire, rivets, small screws, and cotter pins, and are practically indispensable for removing/installing safety wire.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Pliers / Diagonal)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Punches are used to concentrate the force of what?
questions.expl: Punches concentrate the force of a hammer blow. Different punch types are designed for specific tasks and are identified by their shape.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Punches); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Punches are identified by their:
questions.expl: Punch types are identified by their shape, each designed to accomplish a specific task.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3 (Punches); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A PRICK punch is used to:
options: Place reference marks on metal (e.g., for layout/drilling)
Make a 60-degree starting indentation for a drill
questions.expl: The prick punch has a small sharp point used to place reference marks on metal — e.g., transferring a dimension from a paper pattern to show where to drill.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-3/11-4 (Punches / Prick punch)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A CENTER punch is ground to a point of about what angle?
questions.expl: The center punch has a heavier body than a prick punch and is ground to a point of about 60 deg; it makes the indentation that starts a drill.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches / Center punch)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: What is the main difference between a center punch and a prick punch?
options: The center punch has a heavier body and a wider (~60 deg) point to start drills; the prick punch is finer for layout marks
The prick punch is used to drive out pins
questions.short: Center punch = heavier, ~60 deg, starts drills; prick punch = fine layout marks.
questions.expl: The center punch is heavier and ground to ~60 deg to make a drill-starting indentation; the prick punch is finer and used to lay out reference marks.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A PIN punch (drift punch) is used to:
options: Remove (drive out) rivets, bolts, and pins
questions.short: Remove (drive out) rivets, bolts, and pins.
questions.expl: Pin punches (often called drift punches) drive out rivets, bolts, and pins. They have a straight shank (unlike the tapered drive/starting punch).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches / Pin punch)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A STARTING punch (FAA: drive/tapered punch) is used to:
options: Mark rivet holes from a template
Drive (start) bolts and pins out of their holes
questions.short: Start driving bolts/pins out of their holes.
questions.expl: The starting punch (his notes) is the FAA 'drive punch / tapered punch': used to START driving bolts and pins out of holes. A pin punch then finishes the job. Same tool, two names.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches / Drive punch); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Why must a starting (drive) punch be followed by a pin punch when driving a pin out?
options: The drive punch is too weak
It prevents the hammer from breaking
The pin punch is only for marking
The drive punch tapers to its face, so once its sides touch the hole it can't go further; the straight-shank pin punch finishes
questions.short: Drive punch tapers and binds at the hole; the straight pin punch finishes.
questions.expl: A drive punch's sides taper all the way to the face, so it can only start the pin until its sides touch the hole. A straight-shank pin punch then drives the pin the rest of the way out.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A TRANSFER punch is used to:
options: Mark rivet hole locations from a template/pattern
questions.short: Mark/transfer rivet hole locations from a template.
questions.expl: The transfer punch (~4 in. long) has a point that fits a template's drill-locating hole, transferring the hole location to the work; its tip resembles a prick punch point.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches / Transfer punch)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Why should you NEVER use a prick punch or center punch to remove an object from a hole?
options: The pointed tip spreads the object and makes it bind even more
questions.short: The point spreads the object, making it bind worse.
questions.expl: A prick/center punch point spreads the object inside the hole, causing it to bind even more — use a drive/pin punch instead.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Punches)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: American aircraft hardware uses which wrench sizing system?
questions.expl: American aircraft use English (SAE) wrench sizes.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Wrenches); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Aircraft wrenches are generally made from what steel, with what plating?
questions.expl: Wrenches are made from chrome-vanadium steel and are cadmium- or chrome-plated for corrosion resistance.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Wrenches)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The jaws of a wrench are made parallel to the handle or set at an angle between:
questions.expl: Wrench jaws are parallel to the handle or set at an angle ranging from 15 deg to 90 deg, which lets the wrench reach nuts at different positions.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4 (Wrenches); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A solid, nonadjustable wrench with open parallel jaws on one or both ends is a(n):
questions.expl: Open-end wrenches are solid, nonadjustable wrenches with open parallel jaws on one or both ends.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-4/11-5 (Open-end); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A box-end wrench is made with a closed loop of how many points?
questions.expl: Box-end wrenches have a closed loop with 6 or 12 points; the 12-point allows the wrench to be repositioned in a smaller swing arc.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-5 (Box-end); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A combination wrench has:
options: A ratcheting head on both ends
Two open ends of the same size
One open end and one box end of the same size
Two box ends of different sizes
questions.short: One open end and one box end, same size.
questions.expl: A combination wrench has one open end and one box end, both the same size — the best of both styles in one tool.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-5 (Combination); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A ratcheting wrench is distinguished by having a:
questions.expl: The ratcheting wrench has a ratcheting mechanism so the nut can be turned without lifting the wrench off between strokes.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-5; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Flare-nut wrenches are used on the B-nuts and fittings of:
questions.expl: The flare-nut wrench grips a B-nut on fuel, hydraulic, or oxygen lines; its near-closed box shape slips over the tube yet grips more sides than an open-end.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-6 (Flare nut wrench)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Why is a flare-nut wrench preferred over an open-end wrench on a fluid-line B-nut?
options: Its nearly-closed box grips more flats, reducing the chance of rounding the soft B-nut
questions.short: It grips more flats than an open-end, so it won't round off the soft B-nut.
questions.expl: The flare-nut wrench looks like a box-end with a slot to pass over the tubing; it contacts more flats than an open-end, protecting the soft aluminum B-nut from rounding.
