questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-6 (Flare nut wrench)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A socket wrench is made up of which two basic parts?
questions.expl: A socket wrench consists of two parts: the socket (which fits the nut) and the handle (which drives it).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-5 (Socket wrench); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Sockets are commonly available in which two depths?
questions.expl: Sockets come in standard and deep versions; deep sockets reach nuts on long studs or recessed fasteners.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-5/11-6 (Sockets); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which of these is one of the common socket drive sizes from his notes?
questions.expl: His notes list socket drive sizes 1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4, 1, and 2-1/2 inch. 1/2-inch drive is one of the most common.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-5/11-6; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the 'other types of wrenches' his notes list?
questions.expl: His notes list crowfoot, hook spanner, Allen, strap, and impact wrenches as the 'other types.' Pipe wrench is not on that list.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-6/11-7 (Other wrenches); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: An Allen wrench is shaped like a:
questions.expl: Allen wrenches are six-sided bars in the shape of an L, used to turn recessed (hex socket) setscrews.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Allen wrench)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Snips or shears are used for cutting what kind of metal?
questions.expl: Snips/shears are used to cut thin sheet metal in straight lines or curves.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Hand Snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Tinner's snips are designed for what kind of cut?
questions.expl: Tinner's straight snips cut straight lines (and gentle curves) in thin sheet metal.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Hand Snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Aviation (Dutchman) snips are designed especially for cutting:
options: Heat-treated aluminum alloy and stainless steel
questions.short: Heat-treated aluminum alloy and stainless steel.
questions.expl: Aviation/Dutchman snips have serrated, compound-leverage blades for cutting heat-treated aluminum alloy and stainless steel, and for enlarging small holes.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Aviation snips)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Aviation snip blades are serrated and provide what advantage?
options: Compound leverage so they grip and cut thicker/harder sheet
questions.short: Compound leverage for cutting thicker/harder sheet.
questions.expl: The serrated blades and compound-leverage handles let aviation snips grip and cut thicker, harder sheet (heat-treated aluminum, stainless).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Aviation snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: STRAIGHT-cut aviation snips have what color handles?
questions.expl: Color code: STRAIGHT-cut aviation snips = YELLOW handles.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Aviation snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: RIGHT-hand cutting aviation snips have what color handles?
questions.expl: Color code: RIGHT-hand (cut from left to right, curving right) aviation snips = GREEN handles. Green = Go right.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Aviation snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: LEFT-hand cutting aviation snips have what color handles?
questions.expl: Color code: LEFT-hand cutting aviation snips = RED handles.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Aviation snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which color-to-snip pairing is CORRECT?
questions.expl: Straight = yellow, Right-hand = green, Left-hand = red. Only 'Green = right-hand' is correct here.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7 (Aviation snips); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Hand snips typically range in length from:
questions.expl: His notes give a snip length range of 7 to 12 inches.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7; AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A hacksaw consists of which three parts?
questions.expl: A hacksaw has three parts: the blade, the frame, and the handle.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-7/11-8 (Hacksaws); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Hacksaw blades are made of what kind of steel (per his notes)?
questions.expl: His notes: hacksaw blades are carbon or molybdenum steel. (The handbook also mentions high-grade tool steel / tungsten steel.)
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaws); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The 'pitch' of a hacksaw blade refers to:
options: The number of teeth per inch
questions.short: The number of teeth per inch (TPI).
questions.expl: Blade pitch = the number of teeth per inch. Available pitches: 14, 18, 24, and 32 TPI.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaws)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A 14-TPI hacksaw blade is preferred for cutting:
options: Machine steel, cold-rolled steel, soft/structural material
questions.short: Machine steel / cold-rolled / soft, large-section stock.
questions.expl: 14 TPI is for machine steel, cold-rolled steel, and structural/soft large-section stock — fewer teeth clear chips on thick soft material.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaws)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: An 18-TPI hacksaw blade is preferred for:
options: Solid stock aluminum, bearing metal, tool steel, and cast iron
questions.short: Solid stock aluminum, bearing metal, tool steel, cast iron.
questions.expl: 18 TPI suits solid-stock aluminum, bearing metal, tool steel, and cast iron.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaws)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A 24-TPI hacksaw blade is used for:
options: Thin tubing, copper, brass, channel, and angle iron
questions.short: Thin tubing, copper, brass, channel, angle iron.
questions.expl: 24 TPI cuts thicker-than-thin tubing plus copper, brass, channel, and angle iron.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaws)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A 32-TPI hacksaw blade is used for cutting:
questions.expl: 32 TPI (the finest) is for thin-wall tubing and sheet metal — many fine teeth keep at least 2-3 teeth on the thin work so they don't snag.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaws)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: When cutting thin-wall tubing, at least how many teeth should contact the tube wall?
questions.short: At least 3 teeth on the tube wall.
questions.expl: His notes: when cutting thin tubing, at least 3 teeth should be on the tube wall (the handbook phrases it as 'at least two teeth' minimum at all times). More teeth on the work prevents snagging/stripping.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaw procedures); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A hacksaw cuts on which stroke?
questions.expl: Assemble the blade with teeth pointing away from the handle; cutting is done on the forward stroke, and the blade is lifted on the return stroke.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaw procedures)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A hacksaw blade should be installed so the teeth point:
questions.expl: Mount the blade so the teeth point AWAY from the handle, so the saw cuts on the forward (push) stroke.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Hacksaw procedures)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A chisel can cut and chip any metal that is:
options: The same hardness as the chisel
questions.expl: A chisel is hard steel that can cut/chip any metal SOFTER than the chisel itself — e.g., shearing rivets or splitting seized nuts off bolts.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Chisels)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A flat cold chisel can be used for which job?
options: Shearing rivet heads / splitting seized nuts off bolts
questions.short: Shearing rivets / splitting seized nuts from bolts.
questions.expl: Used in restricted areas for shearing rivets, or splitting seized or damaged nuts from bolts.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Chisels)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The cutting edge of a flat cold chisel is ground to about what angle for general use?
questions.expl: The cutting angle of a flat cold chisel should be 60 deg to 70 deg for general use.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8 (Chisels)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The SIZE of a flat cold chisel is determined by:
options: The width of its cutting edge
questions.short: The width of the cutting edge.
questions.expl: A flat cold chisel's size is set by the width of its cutting edge; lengths are usually 5 to 8 inches.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-8/11-9 (Chisels)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Files are catalogued (known) by which of the following?
questions.expl: Files are catalogued three ways: length, shape (cross section/physical configuration), and cut.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9 (Files)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Files are used to do what?
options: Square ends, file rounded corners, remove burrs/slivers, smooth rough edges
questions.short: Square ends, round corners, remove burrs, smooth edges.
questions.expl: Files square ends, file rounded corners, remove burrs and slivers, straighten uneven edges, smooth rough edges, and file holes/slots.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9 (Files)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The part of a file that fits into the handle is the:
questions.expl: The tang is the pointed end of the file that fits into the handle; it is never included in the file's length measurement.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9, Fig 11-16 (Files)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A flat file is used principally for:
options: Smoothing / general finishing on flat surfaces
Rapid stock removal on soft metal
questions.expl: The flat file is the most common file, slightly tapered toward the point, used for smoothing and general finishing.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9 (Flat files); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A half-round file has which two working surfaces?
options: A knife edge and a flat
One convex (curved) face and one flat face
questions.short: One convex (curved/round) face and one flat face.
questions.expl: The half-round file has a curved (convex) side and a flat side, letting it file both flat surfaces and concave (curved) openings.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9 (Half-round files)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A triangular file is also called a:
questions.expl: The triangular (three-square) file is triangular in cross section and is used to file acute angles and to sharpen tools/cutters.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9 (Triangular / three-square)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which file has a knife-blade cross section, used by tool and die makers on acute angles?
questions.expl: The knife file has a knife-blade section and is used on work having acute angles.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9/11-10 (Knife file)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A round file is circular in cross section and is used principally for:
options: Filing circular openings or concave surfaces
questions.expl: Round files are circular in section and file circular openings or concave surfaces.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-9 (Round files)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A vixen (curved-tooth) file is especially designed for:
options: Rapid filing and producing a smooth finish on soft metals/aluminum
questions.short: Rapid filing / smooth finish on soft metals (aluminum).
questions.expl: Vixen (curved-tooth) files are designed for rapid filing and give excellent results on soft metals like aluminum, lead, and phosphor bronze.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-10 (Vixen files)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: What tool is used to clean a file (clear metal filings from the teeth)?
questions.expl: A file card (a stiff wire-bristle brush) is used to clean filings out of a file's teeth; a dirty file scratches the work and won't cut cleanly.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-10, Fig 11-18 (File card)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: His notes list FIVE types of portable drills used in aviation. Which is one of them?
questions.expl: His notes list five portable drill types: hand, electric, pneumatic, cordless, (and the handwritten 5th). The handbook names four (hand, breast, electric, pneumatic); 'cordless' is the common 5th in AIM teaching.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11 (Portable drills); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Holes 1/4 inch in diameter and under can be drilled with which tool?
questions.expl: Holes 1/4 inch in diameter and under can be drilled with the hand drill, commonly called an 'egg beater.'
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11 (Portable drills)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The hand drill used for small (<=1/4 in.) holes is commonly nicknamed the:
questions.expl: The hand drill for holes 1/4 inch and under is commonly called the 'egg beater.'
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11 (Portable drills)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Why is a breast drill used instead of a hand drill for larger holes?
options: Its breastplate lets you apply body weight to increase cutting power
questions.short: Its breastplate lets you add body weight for more cutting power.
questions.expl: The breast drill holds larger twist drills and has a breastplate so the operator can press body weight against it to increase cutting power.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11 (Breast drill)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Pneumatic (air) drills are preferred over electric drills around flammable materials because:
options: Electric drill sparks are a fire/explosion hazard
They never need a power source
questions.short: Electric drills can spark and ignite flammable materials.
questions.expl: Around flammable materials, pneumatic drills are preferred because sparks from an electric drill are a fire or explosion hazard.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-12 (Drills)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Twist drills are made of which materials?
questions.expl: Twist drills are made of carbon steel or high-speed alloy steel. Carbon-steel drills are fine for general work but dull quickly if overheated.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11 (Twist drills)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: What is the risk of overheating a CARBON steel twist drill?
options: It loses its temper/hardness (becomes dull and useless)
questions.short: It loses its temper/hardness and goes dull.
questions.expl: Overheating a carbon-steel drill draws its temper, ruining the cutting edge. High-speed steel tolerates heat far better.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11 (Twist drills); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A twist drill is made up of which three principal parts?
questions.expl: The three principal parts of a twist drill are the shank, the body, and the point (cutting tip).
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11/11-12, Fig 11-19 (Twist drill)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The spiral grooves running the length of a twist drill body are called:
questions.expl: A twist drill has helical (spiral) flutes that carry chips out of the hole — the spiral cut is why it's called a 'twist' drill.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-11/11-12 (Twist drill)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: For MOST drilling (general materials), the included cutting-point angle should be about:
questions.expl: For most drilling, a 118 deg cutting angle (59 deg on each side of center) is sufficient. This is the FAA-confirmed 'most materials' value.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-12 (Twist drill sharpening)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: When drilling SOFT metals (e.g., lead), which cutting-point angle may be more efficient?
questions.expl: When drilling soft metals, a cutting angle of 90 deg may be more efficient. (FAA-confirmed value.)
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-12 (Twist drill sharpening)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: For HARD materials such as stainless steel, AIM teaches a drill-point angle of about:
questions.short: About 135 degrees (AIM/shop convention — verify with instructor).
questions.expl: DIVERGENCE: AIM slides teach ~135 deg for hard materials/stainless. FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 only states 118 deg (general) and 90 deg (soft) — it does NOT print the 135 deg value, so treat this as AIM/shop convention. [verify with instructor]
questions.cite: AIM Tool Notes (per-material table) — NOT in -30B Ch.11 text; [verify with instructor]
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: For aircraft ALUMINUM alloy, AIM teaches a drill-point angle of about:
questions.short: About 135 degrees (AIM/shop convention — verify with instructor).
questions.expl: DIVERGENCE: AIM's per-material table lists ~135 deg for aircraft aluminum alloy. The FAA handbook text gives only 118 deg general and 90 deg soft — the 135 deg figure is AIM/shop teaching. [verify with instructor]
questions.cite: AIM Tool Notes (per-material table) — NOT in -30B Ch.11 text; [verify with instructor]
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which is one of the methods used to indicate (size) a drill?
options: By the fractional, number, letter, or metric/decimal system
questions.short: Fractional, number, letter, and decimal/metric systems.
questions.expl: Drill sizes are indicated four ways: fractional (e.g., 1/16 in), number (No. 80 to No. 1), letter (A to Z), and decimal/metric equivalents.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-12 (Drill sizing)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: In the drilling procedure, the FIRST safety step his notes list is to:
questions.expl: His notes' drilling procedure starts with: use eye protection. Other steps include lubricating the drill and using the correct speed.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-12 (Drilling); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A countersink cuts what, and for what purpose?
options: A cone-shaped chamfer/depression around a hole so a rivet or screw sets flush
A round hole, to pass a bolt
questions.short: A cone-shaped depression so a flush rivet/screw sits flush.
questions.expl: A countersink cuts a cone-shaped chamfer/depression around a hole so a flush (countersunk) rivet or screw head sits flush with the surface.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Countersink)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The standard countersink shown in the handbook has what included angle?
questions.expl: The angle of the standard countersink is 100 deg, matching the standard 100 deg countersunk rivet/screw head.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Countersink)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: When using a countersink, care must be taken NOT to:
options: Remove an excessive amount of material (which reduces flush-joint strength)
questions.short: Remove too much material (weakens the flush joint).
questions.expl: Removing excessive material with a countersink reduces the strength of flush joints; stop countersinks (some with micrometer depth settings) help control depth.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Countersink)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Reamers are used to:
options: Smooth and enlarge holes to an exact size
questions.short: Smooth and enlarge holes to exact size.
questions.expl: Reamers smooth and enlarge an existing hole to an exact size; the hole is first drilled slightly undersize.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Reamers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: When reaming, you must rotate the reamer:
options: In the cutting direction only — never back it out opposite
Opposite the cutting direction to clear chips
questions.short: In the cutting direction ONLY; never back it out the other way.
questions.expl: Always rotate a reamer in the cutting direction only. NEVER back a reamer out of a hole by turning it the opposite way — that chips/dulls the brittle blades.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Reamers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The hole to be reamed should be drilled how, relative to the reamer size?
questions.expl: Drill the hole slightly undersize (about 0.003-0.007 in.) so the reamer removes a thin, even cut to finish it to exact size.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Reamers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Reamer blades are hardened to the point of being brittle, so you must:
options: Handle them carefully to avoid chipping
Always back them out to cool
questions.short: Handle them carefully to avoid chipping the brittle blades.
questions.expl: Reamer blades are hardened to the point of being brittle and must be handled carefully to avoid chipping them.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-13 (Reamers)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A tap is used to cut threads where?
options: On the face of a flange
On the outside of a rod
On the inside of a hole (internal threads)
questions.short: On the inside of a hole (internal threads).
questions.expl: A tap cuts threads on the INSIDE of a hole (internal threads); a die cuts external threads on round stock.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps and Dies)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Standard taps and dies use National Fine (NF) and which other standard (per his notes)?
questions.expl: His notes pair National Fine (NF) and National Coarse (NC). The FAA handbook lists four total: NC, NF, National Extra Fine (NEF), and National Pipe.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps and Dies); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The three taps used in aviation (in a standard set) are the:
questions.expl: A hand-tap set has three taps of identical diameter differing only in taper: the taper tap, the plug tap, and the bottoming tap.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which tap is used to BEGIN the tapping process?
questions.expl: The taper tap (tapered back ~6-7 threads) is used to start tapping; it cuts a full thread once past the taper and is the only tap needed for through-holes in thin sections.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A taper tap is held and turned with what?
questions.expl: His notes: taper taps are held with a T-handle (tap wrench); some taps are held with a solid handle. The T-handle/adjustable tap wrench turns the tap squarely.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The BOTTOMING tap is used to:
options: Cut full threads to the bottom of a blind hole
questions.short: Cut full threads to the bottom of a blind hole.
questions.expl: The bottoming tap is NOT tapered; it cuts full threads to the bottom of a blind hole. A taper tap must be used FIRST to start the threads.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Before using a bottoming tap, what must be done first?
options: Use a taper tap first to start cutting the threads
questions.short: Use a taper tap first to start the threads.
questions.expl: When using the bottoming tap, a taper tap must first be used to start cutting the threads, because the bottoming tap has no taper to ease it in.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A die is used to cut threads where?
options: On the outside (external) of rods, bolts, and pipes
questions.short: External threads on rods, bolts, and pipes.
questions.expl: A die cuts EXTERNAL threads on the outside of rods, bolts, and pipes; a tap cuts internal threads.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Dies)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A die is round or hexagonal in shape with what feature through the center?
questions.short: A round (threaded) hole through the center.
questions.expl: A die is round or hexagonal in shape with a round hole through the center that does the thread cutting.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Dies); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: The two types of dies his notes list are:
options: Solid (hex rethreading) die and split die
questions.short: Solid (hex rethreading) die and split die.
questions.expl: His notes give two die types: (1) the solid hex rethreading die, and (2) the split die. The handbook also describes adjustable vs. plain round split dies.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Dies); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A solid hex rethreading die is best used to:
options: Cut brand-new threads on raw stock
Clean up / restore (rethread) damaged existing threads
questions.expl: A solid (hex) rethreading die is used to chase/restore damaged external threads. It is solid (not adjustable). The split die can be adjusted slightly for thread fit.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Dies); AIM Tool Notes
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: A split die differs from a solid die in that it:
options: Can be adjusted slightly to control the thread depth/fit
questions.short: It can be adjusted to control thread fit.
questions.expl: The adjustable (split) die has an adjusting screw so the thread fit can be controlled; the solid die is fixed-size.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Dies)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Which tool turns a round split die?
questions.expl: Round split dies are turned with a diestock. Taps, by contrast, are turned with a T-handle or adjustable tap wrench.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B Ch.11 (Hand Tools & Measuring Devices), p. 11-14 (Taps and Dies)
sources: Block 2 Tools & Measuring Devices — Notes Review

questions.q: Metal corrosion is BEST defined as the deterioration of the metal by what?
questions.expl: Metal corrosion is the deterioration of the metal by chemical or electrochemical attack. This damage can take place internally as well as on the surface.

questions.q: The MAIN source of corrosion in aircraft is produced when which combination occurs?
options: Water or water vapor containing salt combined with oxygen in the atmosphere
Heat combined with engine exhaust gas
questions.expl: Water or water vapor containing salt combines with oxygen in the atmosphere to produce the main source of corrosion in aircraft.

questions.q: Which operating environments make an aircraft PARTICULARLY susceptible to corrosive attack?
options: Marine environments or areas with corrosive industrial fumes
questions.expl: Aircraft operating in a marine environment, or in areas where the atmosphere contains corrosive industrial fumes, are particularly susceptible to corrosive attacks.

questions.q: The use of composite airframes has had what effect on the need to monitor for corrosion?
options: It has eliminated the need entirely
It transfers the responsibility to the pilot
It has not eliminated the need, because metal components and accessories within the airframe still corrode
questions.short: Need remains; metal parts still corrode
questions.expl: Composite construction has not eliminated the need for careful corrosion monitoring; the use of metal components and accessories within the airframe means the AMT must stay alert for corrosion when inspecting any aircraft.

questions.q: For more in-depth corrosion information, the handbook refers the technician to which FAA document?
questions.expl: The handbook directs the reader to the latest edition of FAA Advisory Circular (AC) 43-4, Corrosion Control for Aircraft, for in-depth information.

questions.q: On aluminum alloys and magnesium, corrosion typically appears as pitting and etching combined with what?
options: A gray or white powdery deposit
questions.expl: On aluminum alloys and magnesium, corrosion appears as pitting and etching and is often combined with a gray or white powdery deposit.

questions.q: On COPPER and copper alloys, the corrosion product forms what?
questions.expl: On copper and copper alloys, corrosion forms a greenish film; on steel it forms a reddish byproduct called rust.

questions.q: Why are even shallow surface pits a concern on a highly stressed part?
options: They change the color of the metal only
They have no effect on a stressed part
They can become sites for crack development
questions.expl: If surface pits are not too deep they may not significantly alter strength; however, the pits may become sites for crack development, particularly if the part is highly stressed.

questions.q: Which of the following is listed as a factor that influences metal corrosion and its rate?
options: The color of the topcoat paint
The altitude at which the metal was milled
questions.expl: Listed factors include type of metal; heat-treatment and grain direction; presence of a dissimilar, less corrodible metal; anodic/cathodic surface areas; temperature; electrolytes; oxygen availability; biological organisms; mechanical stress; time of exposure; and lead/graphite pencil marks.

questions.q: Which seemingly harmless mark on an aircraft surface metal is specifically listed as a factor that influences corrosion?
questions.expl: Lead/graphite pencil marks on aircraft surface metals are listed among the factors that influence metal corrosion and its rate (graphite is highly cathodic to aluminum).

questions.q: The MOST ACTIVE metals (those that lose electrons easily), such as magnesium and aluminum, behave how?
options: They corrode only at high temperature
They are immune to electrochemical attack
questions.expl: The most active metals (those that lose electrons easily), such as magnesium and aluminum, corrode easily. The most noble metals (gold, silver) do not corrode easily.

questions.q: Most pure metals are used in aircraft construction in what manner?
options: Only in combination with other metals to form alloys
questions.expl: Most pure metals are not suitable for aircraft construction and are used only in combination with other metals to form alloys.

questions.q: The speed of electrochemical attack is INCREASED in what kind of climate?
questions.expl: Temperature considerations are important because the speed of electrochemical attack is increased in a hot, moist climate.

questions.q: The operational environment of an aircraft is categorized in what way with respect to corrosion severity?
questions.expl: The operational environment is categorized as mild, moderate, or severe with respect to the corrosion severity of the operational environment.

questions.q: Suggested cleaning/inspection/lubrication/preservation interval for an aircraft based in a MILD corrosion zone is every how many days?
questions.expl: Suggested intervals are: mild zones every 90 days, moderate zones every 45 days, and severe zones every 15 days.

questions.q: Suggested cleaning/inspection interval for a MODERATE corrosion zone is every how many days?
questions.expl: Suggested intervals are mild 90 days, MODERATE 45 days, and severe 15 days.

questions.q: Suggested cleaning/inspection interval for a SEVERE corrosion zone is every how many days?
questions.expl: Suggested intervals are mild 90 days, moderate 45 days, and SEVERE 15 days.

questions.q: Which of the following is listed as controllable FOREIGN MATERIAL that promotes corrosive attack?
options: Spilled battery acids and caustic cleaning solutions
questions.expl: Foreign material that adheres to metal surfaces includes soil and atmospheric dust; oil, grease, and exhaust residues; salt water and salt condensation; spilled battery acids and caustic cleaning solutions; and welding/brazing flux residues.

questions.q: Slimes, molds, and fungi growing on damp surfaces increase corrosion mainly because they do what?
options: Etch the metal directly with acid
Cause the area to remain damp
questions.expl: Once micro-organisms (slimes, molds, fungi) are established, the area tends to remain damp, increasing the possibility of corrosion.

questions.q: Manufacturing processes such as machining, forming, welding, or heat-treatment can leave what in aircraft parts that may cause cracking in a corrosive environment?
questions.expl: Manufacturing processes can leave residual stresses in aircraft parts; this stress can cause cracking in a corrosive environment when the threshold for stress corrosion is exceeded.

questions.q: The two general classifications of corrosion that cover most specific forms are direct chemical attack and what?
questions.expl: There are two general classifications of corrosion: direct chemical attack and electrochemical attack. In both, the metal is converted into a metallic compound such as an oxide, hydroxide, or sulfate.

questions.q: In the corrosion process, the metal that is attacked or oxidized undergoes which change?
questions.expl: The metal that is attacked or oxidized suffers what is called anodic change, while the corrosive agent is reduced and is considered to undergo cathodic change.

questions.q: In DIRECT chemical attack, the anodic and cathodic changes occur where relative to each other?
options: Only in the presence of salt water
questions.expl: Unlike electrochemical attack (where anodic and cathodic changes occur a measurable distance apart), in direct chemical attack the changes occur simultaneously at the same point.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the three MOST COMMON agents that cause direct chemical attack on aircraft?
options: Spilled battery acid or fumes from batteries
questions.expl: The most common agents causing direct chemical attack are spilled battery acid or fumes from batteries; residual flux deposits from inadequately cleaned welded/brazed/soldered joints; and entrapped caustic cleaning solutions.

questions.q: Flux residues from welding, soldering, or brazing tend to cause severe pitting if not removed, because the residues are described as what?
questions.expl: Flux residues are hygroscopic in nature, absorbing moisture, and unless carefully removed tend to cause severe pitting. Remove residual flux immediately after the joining operation.

questions.q: With the introduction of sealed lead-acid and nickel-cadmium batteries, spilled battery acid has become what?
questions.expl: With sealed lead-acid batteries and nickel-cadmium batteries, spilled battery acid is becoming less of a problem; these closed units lessen the hazards of acid spillage and battery fumes.

questions.q: Where entrapment of a cleaning solution is likely to occur, the handbook says to do what?
options: Use the strongest caustic cleaner available
Use a noncorrosive cleaning agent, even though it is less efficient
questions.expl: Where entrapment of the cleaning solution is likely to occur, use a noncorrosive cleaning agent, even though it is less efficient, because some cleaning solutions are themselves corrosive.

questions.q: How many conditions must exist before electrochemical corrosion can occur?
questions.expl: Four conditions must exist: a metal subject to corrosion (anode), a dissimilar conductive material (cathode), a continuous conductive liquid path (electrolyte), and electrical contact between the anode and cathode.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the four required conditions for electrochemical corrosion?
options: Electrical contact between the anode and cathode
questions.expl: The four conditions are an anode, a cathode (dissimilar conductive material), an electrolyte, and electrical contact between them. UV light is not one. Eliminating any one of the four stops electrochemical corrosion.

questions.q: Eliminating ANY ONE of the four conditions for electrochemical corrosion has what effect?
options: Converts it to direct chemical attack
questions.expl: Elimination of any one of the four conditions stops electrochemical corrosion. This is the basis for effective corrosion control.

questions.q: Because corrosion products occupy more volume than the original metal, painted surfaces must be inspected for what irregularities?
questions.expl: Paint can mask the initial stages of corrosion. Since corrosion products occupy more volume than the original metal, painted surfaces must be inspected often for irregularities such as blisters, flakes, chips, and lumps.

questions.q: A metal's specific electrical potential in a given chemical environment is commonly referred to as the metal's what?
questions.expl: All metals and alloys have a specific electrical potential in a given chemical environment, commonly referred to as the metal's 'nobility.' The less noble a metal is, the more easily it corrodes.

questions.q: In an alloy exposed to a conductive corrosive medium, the MORE ACTIVE metal becomes the ___ and the less active becomes the ___ .
questions.short: More active = anode; less active = cathode
questions.expl: Exposure of the alloy surface to a conductive corrosive medium causes the more active metal to become anodic and the less active metal to become cathodic. These are called local cells.

questions.q: The GREATER the difference in electrical potential between two metals, the what?
options: Greater the severity of the corrosive attack
questions.expl: The greater the difference in electrical potential between the two metals, the greater the severity of a corrosive attack if the proper conditions develop.

questions.q: An electrochemical attack requires a medium (usually water) capable of doing what?
options: Conducting a tiny current of electricity
questions.expl: The reaction in electrochemical attack requires a medium, usually water, that is capable of conducting a tiny current of electricity. Electrochemical attack is responsible for most forms of corrosion on aircraft.

questions.q: In the galvanic (electrochemical) series, magnesium and magnesium alloy are positioned as the metals that are what?
questions.expl: In the galvanic series of metals and alloys, magnesium and magnesium alloy are at the anodic end (most likely to corrode), while gold and platinum are at the cathodic end (least likely to corrode).

questions.q: Which is the MOST COMMON form of corrosion, appearing as general roughening, etching, or pitting often with a powdery deposit?
questions.expl: General surface corrosion (also called uniform etch or uniform attack) is the most common form. It appears as general roughening, etching, or pitting, frequently with a powdery deposit, and may be caused by direct chemical OR electrochemical attack.

questions.q: When surface corrosion spreads under a coating, it may be recognized by the paint or plating being lifted into what?
questions.expl: When surface corrosion spreads under the surface coating, closer inspection reveals the paint or plating is lifted off in small blisters from the pressure of underlying corrosion products.

questions.q: A characteristic WORM-LIKE trace of corrosion products beneath a paint film identifies which form of corrosion?
questions.expl: Filiform corrosion is a special form of oxygen concentration cell on metal with an organic coating, recognized by its characteristic worm-like trace of corrosion products beneath the paint film.

questions.q: Which finishes are especially SUSCEPTIBLE to filiform corrosion?
questions.expl: Polyurethane finishes are especially susceptible to filiform corrosion.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion occurs when the relative humidity of the air is between what range and the surface is slightly acidic?
questions.expl: Filiform occurs when the relative humidity is between 78-90 percent and the surface is slightly acidic.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion can be PREVENTED by storing the aircraft in an environment with a relative humidity below what value?
questions.expl: Filiform corrosion can be prevented by storing aircraft in an environment with a relative humidity below 70 percent, using low-oxygen-diffusion coatings, and washing to remove acidic contaminants.

questions.q: On which metal do filiform traces CROSS UNDER one another, making the damage deeper and more severe?
questions.expl: The filiform traces never cross on steel, but they cross under one another on aluminum, making the damage deeper and more severe for aluminum.

questions.q: One of the MOST destructive and intense forms of corrosion, most common on metals that form protective oxide films (aluminum, magnesium), and first noticed as a white/gray powdery deposit, is:
questions.expl: Pitting corrosion is one of the most destructive and intense forms. Most common on metals that form protective oxide films (aluminum and magnesium alloys), it first appears as a white or gray powdery deposit; cleaning it away reveals tiny pits that may penetrate deeply.

questions.q: Pitting damage is dangerous mainly because the tiny surface holes can do what?
options: Penetrate deeply into structural members, causing damage out of proportion to the surface appearance
Be easily polished out in all cases
questions.short: Penetrate deep; damage out of proportion
questions.expl: The small surface openings of pitting may penetrate deeply into structural members and cause damage completely out of proportion to its surface appearance.

questions.q: DISSIMILAR metal (galvanic) corrosion is especially serious because:
options: It always produces a visible white powder
The action often takes place out of sight, detectable only by disassembly and inspection
It can only be found with x-ray
questions.short: Acts out of sight; needs disassembly to find
questions.expl: Dissimilar metal corrosion (a galvanic action at contact points where insulation has broken down) can be very serious because the action often takes place out of sight; the only way to detect it prior to structural failure is by disassembly and inspection.

questions.q: Using steel wool or a steel wire brush on aluminum or magnesium can INDUCE dissimilar metal corrosion because it does what?
options: Forces small pieces of steel into the metal being cleaned
questions.short: Embeds steel particles in the metal
questions.expl: Improper use of steel wool or a steel wire brush on aluminum or magnesium can force small pieces of steel into the metal being cleaned, causing corrosion. A pad used on one type of metal must not be reused on a different metal.

questions.q: Concentration cell corrosion is also known by what name?
questions.expl: Concentration cell corrosion is also known as crevice corrosion. Its three general types are metal ion concentration cells, oxygen concentration cells, and active-passive cells.

questions.q: What are the THREE general types of concentration cell corrosion?
options: Metal ion, oxygen, and active-passive cells
questions.expl: The three general types of concentration cell corrosion are metal ion concentration cells, oxygen concentration cells, and active-passive cells.

questions.q: In a METAL ION concentration cell, which area is anodic and corrodes?
options: The area in contact with the HIGH metal-ion concentration
The area in contact with the LOW metal-ion concentration
questions.expl: In a metal ion concentration cell, the area of metal in contact with the LOW concentration of metal ions is anodic and corrodes; the area in contact with the high metal-ion concentration is cathodic and does not show corrosion.

questions.q: In an OXYGEN concentration cell, corrosion occurs at the area of:
questions.expl: In an oxygen concentration cell, corrosion occurs at the area of low oxygen concentration (anode). Typical locations are under gaskets, wood, rubber, etc. Stainless steel is particularly susceptible to this crevice corrosion.

questions.q: Which alloy is noted as particularly susceptible to OXYGEN concentration (crevice) cell corrosion?
questions.expl: Alloys such as stainless steel are particularly susceptible to oxygen concentration cell (crevice) corrosion.

questions.q: Metals that depend on a tightly adhering passive film (usually an oxide) for protection are prone to rapid attack by which cell, typically starting as an oxygen concentration cell?
questions.expl: Active-passive cells rapidly attack metals that depend on a passive oxide film. The action usually starts as an oxygen concentration cell; the passive film breaks beneath a dirt particle, exposing active metal, and a potential develops between the large passive area and small active area, causing rapid pitting.

questions.q: An attack along the GRAIN BOUNDARIES of an alloy, resulting from a lack of uniformity in the alloy structure, is called:
questions.expl: Intergranular corrosion is an attack along the grain boundaries of an alloy, commonly resulting from a lack of uniformity in the alloy structure caused by heating/cooling during manufacture. It may exist without visible surface evidence.

questions.q: High-strength aluminum alloys such as 2014 and 7075 are MORE susceptible to intergranular corrosion under what condition?
options: When improperly heat-treated and then exposed to a corrosive environment
questions.expl: High-strength aluminum alloys such as 2014 and 7075 are more susceptible to intergranular corrosion if they have been improperly heat-treated and then exposed to a corrosive environment.

questions.q: An ADVANCED form of intergranular corrosion that lifts up the surface grains of a metal, most often seen on extruded sections such as spars, is:
questions.expl: Exfoliation is an advanced form of intergranular corrosion that lifts surface grains by the force of expanding corrosion products at grain boundaries. It is most often seen on extruded sections (like spars) where grain thickness is less than in rolled forms.

questions.q: Which NDI methods are noted as being used with great success to detect exfoliation corrosion?
questions.expl: Exfoliation is difficult to detect in its initial stage, but ultrasonic and eddy current inspection methods are being used with a great deal of success.

questions.q: Stress-corrosion cracking involves a constant or cyclic stress acting together with what?
questions.expl: Stress-corrosion cracking involves a constant or cyclic stress acting in conjunction with a damaging chemical environment. Stress may be internal (trapped during manufacturing) or external (riveting, welding, over-torque, press fit).

questions.q: Which environment causes stress corrosion cracking of HIGH-STRENGTH, heat-treated steel and aluminum alloys?
questions.expl: Salt solutions and sea water cause stress corrosion cracking of high-strength, heat-treated steel and aluminum alloys.

questions.q: Which environment causes stress corrosion cracking of some TITANIUM alloys?
questions.expl: Methyl alcohol-hydrochloric acid solutions cause stress corrosion cracking of some titanium alloys.

questions.q: MAGNESIUM alloys may stress corrode in what environment?
questions.expl: Magnesium alloys may stress corrode in moist air.

questions.q: Shot peening increases resistance to stress corrosion cracking by creating what on the surface?
questions.expl: Shot peening a metal surface increases resistance to stress corrosion cracking by creating compressive stresses on the surface that must be overcome by applied tensile stress before the surface sees any tension load, raising the threshold stress level.

questions.q: A particularly damaging corrosion that occurs when two mating surfaces normally at rest undergo slight relative motion is called:
questions.expl: Fretting corrosion occurs when two mating surfaces, normally at rest with respect to one another, are subject to slight relative motion. It is characterized by surface pitting and finely divided debris that cannot escape.

questions.q: Because fretting can wear deep grooves resembling pressure indentations, it has also been called what?
questions.expl: When the fretting contact areas are small and sharp, deep grooves resembling brinell markings may be worn, so this type of corrosion on bearing surfaces has also been called false brinelling.

questions.q: The most common example of fretting corrosion, identified by a black ring around the rivet, is the:
options: Smoking rivet on engine cowling and wing skins
Green stain at a battery vent
White whisker on a magnesium casting
questions.expl: The most common example of fretting corrosion is the smoking rivet found on engine cowling and wing skins, identified by a black ring around the rivet.

questions.q: Fretting corrosion is unusual because it is one corrosion reaction that is NOT driven by an electrolyte; in fact, moisture does what?
questions.expl: The smoking-rivet (fretting) reaction is not driven by an electrolyte, and in fact moisture may inhibit the reaction. (Note: water VAPOR generally increases fretting deterioration, but this specific reaction can be inhibited by moisture.)

questions.q: Fatigue corrosion involves cyclic stress plus a corrosive environment. Failure from fatigue corrosion generally occurs at a stress level that is:
options: Far below the fatigue limit of an uncorroded part
Independent of the number of cycles
questions.short: Far below the uncorroded fatigue limit
questions.expl: Fracture due to fatigue corrosion generally occurs at a stress level far below the fatigue limit of an uncorroded part, even though the amount of corrosion is relatively small.

questions.q: Fatigue corrosion failure occurs in how many stages?
questions.expl: Fatigue corrosion failure occurs in two stages: first, combined corrosion and cyclic stress pit and crack the metal; second, a fatigue stage where the crack propagates (often from a corrosion pit) controlled by stress concentration and the metal's properties.

questions.q: GALVANIC corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals make electrical contact in the presence of what?
questions.expl: Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals make electrical contact in the presence of an electrolyte. The greater the difference in activity, the faster corrosion occurs.

questions.q: Galvanic corrosion is RAPID and SEVERE when the surface area of the corroding (anodic) metal is:
options: Larger than the less active metal
Smaller than the less active metal
Exactly equal to the less active metal
questions.expl: If the surface area of the corroding metal is SMALLER than the surface area of the less active metal, corrosion is rapid and severe. When the corroding metal is larger, corrosion is slow and superficial.

questions.q: Galvanic corrosion is SLOW and SUPERFICIAL when the corroding metal is:
options: Smaller than the less active metal
Larger than the less active metal
questions.expl: When the corroding metal is larger than the less active metal, corrosion is slow and superficial.

questions.q: The MOST common corrosive agents are described as acids, alkalies, and what?
questions.expl: The most common corrosive agents are acids, alkalies, and salts. The atmosphere and water, the two most common media for these agents, may also act as corrosive agents.

questions.q: Which acid is the SINGLE most familiar example given as 'battery acid' that severely corrodes airframe alloys?
questions.expl: The most destructive acids include sulfuric acid (battery acid), halogen acids (hydrochloric, hydrofluoric, hydrobromic), nitrous oxide compounds, and organic acids from human and animal wastes.

questions.q: As a GROUP, alkalies are described as how corrosive compared with acids, except toward aluminum and magnesium?
questions.expl: As a group, alkalies are not as corrosive as acids. However, aluminum and magnesium alloys are exceedingly prone to corrosive attack by many alkaline solutions unless an inhibitor is present.

questions.q: Which alkali is an EXCEPTION because aluminum alloys are highly resistant to it?
questions.expl: Substances particularly corrosive to aluminum are washing soda, potash (wood ashes), and lime (cement dust). Ammonia, an alkali, is an exception because aluminum alloys are highly resistant to it.

questions.q: Most salt solutions promote corrosion because they are good what?
questions.expl: Most salt solutions are good electrolytes and can promote corrosive attack. Some stainless steels resist salt solutions, but aluminum alloy, magnesium alloys, and other steels are extremely vulnerable.

questions.q: The two MAJOR atmospheric corrosive agents are:
questions.expl: The major atmospheric corrosive agents are oxygen and airborne moisture. Additional moisture often accelerates corrosive attack, particularly on ferrous alloys. The atmosphere may also carry industrial and marine salt spray.

questions.q: One characteristic of WATER that determines its corrosiveness is its conductivity, i.e., its ability to do what?
options: Act as an electrolyte and conduct a current
questions.expl: The corrosiveness of water depends on dissolved impurities and gases (especially oxygen). One characteristic that determines its corrosiveness is conductivity -- the ability to act as an electrolyte and conduct a current. Temperature and velocity also matter.

questions.q: During any corrosion control maintenance, the handbook says to consult which document for information on chemicals used?
questions.expl: During any corrosion control maintenance, consult the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for information on any chemicals used in the process.

questions.q: Which of the following is listed as a specific corrosion preventive-maintenance function?
options: Daily draining of fuel cell sumps
questions.short: Daily draining of fuel cell sumps
questions.expl: Specific preventive functions include adequate cleaning, periodic lubrication, detailed inspection, prompt corrosion treatment and paint touch-up, record keeping, keeping drain holes free of obstructions, DAILY draining of fuel cell sumps, daily wipe-down of exposed critical areas, sealing against water, replacing deteriorated gaskets/sealants, and using protective covers.

questions.q: Keeping drain holes free of obstructions is important because clogged low-point drains may do what?
options: Allow water to collect and become entrapped
questions.expl: Design specifications require drains where water may collect; keeping drain holes free of obstructions and daily inspection of low-point drains prevents water entrapment that leads to corrosion.

questions.q: All corrosion inspections START with what?
options: A thorough cleaning of the area to be inspected
Removal of the part from the aircraft
questions.short: A thorough cleaning of the area
questions.expl: All corrosion inspections start with a thorough cleaning of the area to be inspected, followed by a general visual inspection using a flashlight, inspection mirror, and a 5-10X magnifying glass.

questions.q: The general visual corrosion inspection uses a flashlight, inspection mirror, and a magnifying glass of what power?
questions.expl: A general visual inspection follows cleaning, using a flashlight, inspection mirror, and a 5-10X magnifying glass to look for obvious defects and suspected areas.

questions.q: Which technique is described as the MOST widely used and effective method for detecting and evaluating corrosion?
questions.expl: Visual inspection is the most widely used technique and an effective method for the detection and evaluation of corrosion. Using the sense of touch is also effective for hidden, well-developed corrosion.

questions.q: NDI methods such as eddy current, x-ray, and ultrasonic must be performed only by whom?
questions.expl: NDI methods (liquid penetrant, magnetic particle, eddy current, x-ray, ultrasonic, acoustic emission) have limitations and must be performed only by qualified and certified NDI personnel. Eddy current, x-ray, and ultrasonic require calibrated equipment and a reference standard.

questions.q: In addition to routine maintenance inspections, amphibians or seaplanes must be checked how often, with critical areas cleaned or treated as necessary?
questions.expl: In addition to routine maintenance inspections, amphibians or seaplanes must be checked daily and critical areas cleaned or treated as necessary.

questions.q: Exhaust deposits from both jet and reciprocating engines give particular trouble at gaps, seams, hinges, and fairings located where?
options: Downstream from the exhaust pipes or nozzles
On the upper wing surface only
questions.expl: Exhaust deposits are very corrosive and give particular trouble where gaps, seams, hinges, and fairings are located downstream from the exhaust pipes or nozzles. Don't overlook buildup in remote areas such as the empennage.

questions.q: Battery compartments remain corrosion prone largely because of what?
options: Constant water spray from the runway
Fumes from overheated electrolyte that spread to adjacent cavities
questions.expl: Despite improved finishes and venting, battery compartments stay corrosion prone because fumes from overheated electrolyte are difficult to contain and spread to adjacent cavities, causing rapid corrosive attack on unprotected metal. Battery vent openings on the skin must also be inspected.

questions.q: In BILGE areas, what often masks small quantities of water that settle to the bottom and set up a hidden chemical cell?
questions.expl: Bilge areas are natural collection points for waste fluids and debris; residual oil quite often masks small quantities of water that settle to the bottom and set up a hidden chemical cell.

questions.q: Which area 'probably receives more punishment due to mud, water, salt, gravel, and other flying debris' than any other?
options: The wheel well and landing gear area
questions.expl: More than any other area, the wheel well and landing gear area receives the most punishment from mud, water, salt, gravel, and debris. Complete paint-film coverage is difficult there.

questions.q: Why can preservatives NOT be used on some main landing gear wheels?
questions.expl: Due to heat generated by braking action, preservatives cannot be used on some main landing gear wheels. A partially applied preservative tends to mask corrosion rather than prevent it.

questions.q: When inspecting the wheel well, magnesium wheels should be checked especially around bolt heads, lugs, and web areas for what?
questions.expl: During wheel-well inspection, pay particular attention to magnesium wheels (around bolt heads, lugs, and wheel web areas) for the presence of entrapped water or its effects.

questions.q: Aircraft design requires drains in all areas where water may collect; daily inspection of which is a standard requirement?
questions.expl: Design specifications require drains in all areas where water may collect. Daily inspection of low-point drains is a standard requirement, since neglect lets drains clog with debris, grease, or sealants.

questions.q: Cores of radiator coolers and reciprocating-engine cylinder fins in engine frontal areas may NOT be painted because of what requirement?
questions.expl: In engine frontal areas, cores of radiator coolers, reciprocating-engine cylinder fins, etc., may not be painted due to the requirement for heat dissipation, leaving them corrosion prone.

questions.q: Wing flap and spoiler recesses are potential corrosion problem areas and should be inspected in what position?
questions.expl: Dirt and water collect in flap and spoiler recesses unnoticed because they are normally retracted; inspect these areas with the spoilers and flaps in the fully deployed position.

questions.q: PIANO-type hinges are prime corrosion spots primarily because of what?
options: Dissimilar metal contact between the steel pin and aluminum hinge
questions.short: Steel pin / aluminum hinge dissimilar contact
questions.expl: Piano hinges are prime corrosion spots because of the dissimilar metal contact between the steel pin and aluminum hinge, and they trap dirt, salt, and moisture. Use water-displacing lubricants when servicing them.

questions.q: When servicing piano hinges, the inspection must include lubrication and actuation through several cycles, and what type of lubricant is specified?
questions.expl: Inspection of hinges must include lubrication and actuation through several cycles to ensure complete penetration. Use water-displacing lubricants when servicing piano hinges.

questions.q: Corrosion of metal skins joined by spot welding results from what?
options: Entrance and entrapment of corrosive agents between the layers of metal
Heat from the welding process only
questions.expl: Corrosion of spot-welded metal skins is the result of entrance and entrapment of corrosive agents between the metal layers, evidenced by corrosion products at the crevices. Advanced attack causes skin buckling and spot-weld fracture.

questions.q: How are CONTROL CABLES inspected for internal corrosion if external corrosion is evident?
options: Cut the cable to inspect the core
Relieve tension and check the cable for internal corrosion
Soak the entire cable in solvent
questions.expl: If external corrosion is evident on a control cable, relieve tension and check the cable for internal corrosion. Replace cables with internal corrosion; remove light external corrosion with an oil-soaked nonwoven abrasive pad or steel wire brush, then recoat with preservative.

questions.q: Helicopter rotor heads and gearboxes are corrosion prone because, besides constant exposure, they contain what?
options: Bare steel surfaces, many external working parts, and dissimilar metal contacts
questions.short: Bare steel, working parts, dissimilar metals
questions.expl: Helicopter rotor heads and gearboxes contain bare steel surfaces, many external working parts, and dissimilar metal contacts, and are constantly exposed to the elements; inspect them frequently.

questions.q: The two methods normally used to REMOVE corrosion are:
questions.expl: The methods normally used to remove corrosion are mechanical and chemical. Mechanical includes hand and powered sanding, grinding, and buffing using abrasive mats, papers, wheels, discs, and metal wool. The method depends on the metal and the degree of corrosion.

questions.q: When corrosion damage is SEVERE and exceeds the damage limits set by the manufacturer, the technician must:
options: Apply extra primer and return to service
questions.expl: Repair of corrosion damage includes removal of all corrosion products. When the damage is severe and exceeds the damage limits set by the aircraft or parts manufacturer, the part must be replaced.

questions.q: Before stripping compound is applied, the area must first be cleaned of grease, oil, dirt, or preservatives in order to do what?
options: Assure maximum efficiency of the stripping compound and reveal the extent of corrosion spread
questions.expl: To assure maximum efficiency of the stripping compound, the area must be cleaned of grease, oil, dirt, or preservatives. This preliminary cleaning also helps determine the extent of the corrosion spread.

questions.q: Modern environmental standards encourage the use of which cleaning compounds whenever possible?
questions.expl: Modern environmental standards encourage the use of water-based, non-toxic cleaning compounds whenever possible. Some jurisdictions prohibit solvents containing volatile organic compounds (VOCs).

questions.q: Chemical paint removal from a large area should be accomplished where, wherever practicable?
options: Outside (in open air), preferably in shaded areas
questions.short: Outside, in open air / shaded areas
questions.expl: Wherever practicable, chemical paint removal from any large area is to be accomplished outside (in open air) and preferably in shaded areas. If inside, adequate ventilation must be assured.

questions.q: When applying paint stripper, to what depth is the entire area brushed with stripper?
questions.expl: Brush the entire area to be stripped with a cover of stripper to a depth of 1/32 inch to 1/16 inch. A paintbrush exposed to paint remover must not be reused for other purposes.

questions.q: When assisting paint removal, only which type of scraper is permitted?
questions.expl: Only nonmetallic scrapers are to be used to assist in removing persistent paint finishes. Nonwoven abrasive pads intended for paint stripping may also be useful.

questions.q: All dish-outs from corrosion rework must be elliptically shaped with the major axis running which way on a WING?
questions.expl: All dish-outs must be elliptically shaped with the major axis running spanwise on wings and horizontal stabilizers, longitudinally on fuselages, and vertically on vertical stabilizers.

questions.q: Corrosion rework depressions are blended into smoothly faired dish-outs using what ratio of LENGTH to DEPTH?
questions.expl: Rework depressions by forming smoothly blended dish-outs using a ratio of 20:1, length to depth.

questions.q: On the blend-out diagram, the WIDTH of the cleaned-up area is a minimum of how many times the depth?
questions.expl: On the blending diagram, the length of the cleaned-up area is 20 times the depth minimum, and the WIDTH of the cleaned-up area is 10 times the depth minimum.

questions.q: Why must steel nut-plates and steel fasteners be REMOVED before blending corrosion out of an aluminum structure?
options: They interfere with the paint finish
Steel or copper particles embedded in aluminum can become a point of future corrosion
They must be replaced at every inspection
questions.expl: Steel nut-plates and steel fasteners are to be removed before blending corrosion out of aluminum structure, because steel or copper particles embedded in aluminum can become a point of future corrosion.

questions.q: In CRITICAL and highly stressed areas, all pits remaining after corrosion removal must be blended out to prevent what?
options: Stress risers that may cause stress corrosion cracking
questions.expl: In critical and highly stressed areas, all pits remaining after removal of corrosion products must be blended out to prevent stress risers that may cause stress corrosion cracking. On non-critical structure, blending out every pit is unnecessary.

questions.q: One of the most familiar types of corrosion, ferrous oxide (rust), is described as what kind of coating?
options: NOT a protective coating; it promotes additional attack
A protective coating that seals the base metal
An anodic film that resists moisture
questions.short: Rust is NOT protective; promotes attack
questions.expl: Rust (ferrous oxide) is not a protective coating in any sense; it actually promotes additional attack by attracting moisture from the air and acting as a catalyst for further corrosion. All rust must be removed from steel surfaces.

questions.q: The MOST practicable means of controlling corrosion of steel is what?
options: Complete mechanical removal of corrosion products and restoring corrosion-preventive coatings
questions.expl: The most practicable means of controlling steel corrosion is the complete removal of corrosion products by mechanical means and restoring corrosion-preventive coatings. Except on highly stressed surfaces, abrasive papers, power buffers, wire brushing, or steel wool are acceptable.

questions.q: A drawback of abrasive/polishing removal of rust is that residual rust usually remains where, so the part corrodes more easily next time?
options: In the bottom of small pits and crevices
questions.expl: In any use of abrasives, residual rust usually remains in the bottom of small pits and crevices. It is practically impossible to remove all corrosion products by abrasive or polishing methods alone, so a once-rusted part usually corrodes again more easily.

questions.q: The use of chemical corrosion removers on HIGHLY STRESSED steel parts is prohibited without engineering authorization because these parts are subject to what?
questions.expl: The use of chemical corrosion removers is prohibited without engineering authorization because high-strength steel parts are subject to hydrogen embrittlement.

questions.q: Corrosion products on highly stressed steel parts must be removed with which materials?
options: Mild abrasive papers (rouge or fine grit aluminum oxide) or fine buffing compounds
Coarse carborundum and steel wire wheels
questions.expl: Corrosion on highly stressed steel parts must be removed by careful processing using mild abrasive papers such as rouge or fine-grit aluminum oxide, or fine buffing compounds on cloth wheels; nonwoven abrasive pads can also be used. Do not overheat the steel; reapply protective finish immediately.

questions.q: Why is the use of chemical INHIBITORS on installed steel parts considered not only undesirable but very dangerous?
options: Danger of entrapment of corrosive solutions and resulting uncontrolled attack
They damage the paint shop equipment
questions.expl: Chemical inhibitors on installed steel parts are undesirable and dangerous because of the danger of entrapment of corrosive solutions and the resulting uncontrolled attack that could occur under field conditions.

questions.q: A thin coating of relatively PURE aluminum applied over a base aluminum alloy for corrosion protection is commonly called:
questions.expl: A thin coating of relatively pure aluminum applied over the base alloy is commonly called 'Alclad.' Avoid unnecessary mechanical removal of the protective Alclad layer.

questions.q: Aluminum alloys commonly form a smooth surface oxidation of what thickness that is NOT considered detrimental?
questions.expl: Aluminum alloys commonly form a smooth surface oxidation from 0.001 inch to 0.0025 inch thick. This is not considered detrimental; it provides a hard-shell barrier. It must not be confused with severe corrosion.

questions.q: Corrosion on aluminum surfaces is usually quite OBVIOUS because the corrosion products are what?
options: White and more voluminous than the original base metal
questions.expl: Corrosion on aluminum is usually quite obvious since the products of corrosion are white and generally more voluminous than the original base metal, showing as etching, pitting, or roughness even in early stages.

questions.q: At least three forms of attack on aluminum alloys are particularly serious: penetrating pit-type corrosion of tubing walls, stress-corrosion cracking, and what?
questions.expl: Three particularly serious forms of attack on aluminum alloys are penetrating pit-type corrosion through tubing walls, stress-corrosion cracking under sustained stress, and intergranular corrosion (characteristic of certain improperly heat-treated alloys).

questions.q: When choosing a cleaner for aluminum, why must you select a NEUTRAL pH product?
options: Many consumer products are caustic enough to induce corrosion if trapped between aluminum lap joints
Only neutral products bond to paint
questions.short: Caustic cleaners trapped in lap joints corrode
questions.expl: When choosing an aluminum cleaner, use caution: many commercial consumer products are actually caustic enough to induce corrosion if trapped between aluminum lap joints. Choose a neutral-pH product.

questions.q: Metal polish intended for clad aluminum aircraft surfaces must NOT be used on what, because it would remove the protective film?
questions.expl: Metal polish intended for clad aluminum surfaces must not be used on anodized aluminum, since it is abrasive enough to remove the protective anodized film. It is effective on unpainted Alclad.

questions.q: When treating superficially corroded aluminum to be PAINTED, a 10 percent solution of which acid (activated by sulfuric acid) is applied?
questions.expl: Treat superficially corroded aluminum to be painted with a 10 percent solution of chromic acid and sulfuric acid, applied by swab or brush and scrubbed while damp. Allow chromic acid to remain at least 5 minutes, then flush with water.

questions.q: An inhibitive sodium dichromate / chromium trioxide solution is allowed to remain on a corroded UNPAINTED aluminum area for how long before rinsing?
questions.expl: For treating superficial corrosion on unpainted aluminum, allow the sodium dichromate / chromium trioxide solution to remain on the corroded area for 5 to 20 minutes, then remove excess by rinsing and wiping dry.

questions.q: After any corrosion surface treatment, restoration of paint coatings must be done when?
options: Immediately / the same day as the surface treatment
questions.expl: Restoration of paint coatings must immediately follow any surface treatment. In any case, make sure corrosion treatment is accomplished or reapplied on the SAME DAY that paint refinishing is scheduled.

questions.q: When an ANODIZED coating is damaged in service, it can be restored in what way?
options: Fully restored by re-anodizing in the field
It self-heals and needs no action
Only partially restored by chemical surface treatment
questions.expl: When an anodized coating is damaged in service, it can only be partially restored by chemical surface treatment. Avoid destroying the oxide film in unaffected areas; do not use steel wool, steel wire brushes, or severe abrasives.

questions.q: When INTERGRANULAR corrosion (including exfoliation) is present in heat-treated aluminum, what cleaning approach is required?
options: More severe cleaning -- mechanical removal of all corrosion products and visible delaminated layers
Chemical inhibitor with no mechanical work
questions.expl: More severe cleaning is a must when intergranular corrosion is present. Mechanical removal of all corrosion products and visible delaminated metal layers must be done to determine the extent of destruction and evaluate remaining strength. A DER may assess damage if manufacturer limits are inadequate.

questions.q: Which metal is the MOST chemically active of the metals used in aircraft construction and the most difficult to protect?
questions.expl: Magnesium is the most chemically active of the metals used in aircraft construction and is the most difficult to protect.

questions.q: Magnesium attack is among the EASIEST corrosion to detect early because its corrosion products do what?
options: Occupy several times the volume of the original metal
questions.expl: Magnesium attack is probably the easiest type to detect early, since magnesium corrosion products occupy several times the volume of the original metal. Attack shows as a lifting of the paint film and white spots that develop into snow-like mounds or 'white whiskers.'

questions.q: When mechanically cleaning corroded MAGNESIUM, cleaning must be LIMITED to what tools?
options: Stiff hog-bristle brushes and nonmetallic tools (including nonwoven abrasive pads)
questions.expl: Limit mechanical cleaning of magnesium to stiff hog-bristle brushes and similar nonmetallic tools (including nonwoven abrasive pads). Steel or aluminum tools, steel/bronze/aluminum wool, or pads used on other metals must NOT be used.
