questions.q: On magnesium, which of the following must NEVER be used, because entrapped particles can cause more trouble than the initial attack?
options: Steel wire brushes, carborundum abrasives, or steel cutting tools
questions.short: Steel wire brushes / carborundum / steel tools
questions.expl: On magnesium, steel wire brushes, carborundum abrasives, or steel cutting tools must not be used. Entrapment of steel particles can cause more trouble than the initial corrosive attack.

questions.q: Corroded magnesium is treated liberally with what solution, worked into pits with a NONMETALLIC brush?
options: A chromic acid solution with sulfuric acid added
questions.expl: Treat the corroded magnesium area liberally with a chromic acid solution to which sulfuric acid has been added, working it into pits/crevices with a nonmetallic brush. Allow it to remain 5 to 20 minutes, then wipe up the excess with a clean damp cloth; do not let it dry on the surface (it causes paint lifting).

questions.q: Magnesium CASTINGS, compared with wrought magnesium skins, are generally what?
options: More porous and prone to penetrating attack
questions.short: More porous; prone to penetrating attack
questions.expl: Magnesium castings are generally more porous and prone to penetrating attack than wrought magnesium skins. Engine cases submerged in saltwater overnight can be completely penetrated. Treatment is otherwise the same as for magnesium skin.

questions.q: Titanium is by nature highly corrosion resistant, but may show deterioration from the presence of what, particularly at high temperatures?
questions.expl: Titanium is highly corrosion resistant by nature but may show deterioration from salt deposits and metal impurities, particularly at high temperatures.

questions.q: For cleaning or removing corrosion from TITANIUM, which of the following is PROHIBITED?
options: Steel wool, iron scrapers, or steel brushes
questions.expl: The use of steel wool, iron scrapers, or steel brushes for cleaning or removing corrosion from titanium is prohibited. Hand polishing with aluminum polish or a mild abrasive using fiber brushes only is permissible.

questions.q: After cleaning titanium and treating with a sodium dichromate solution, the surface is wiped with dry cloths and then what?
questions.short: Do NOT use a water rinse
questions.expl: If titanium requires cleaning, hand polish with aluminum polish or a mild abrasive (fiber brushes only), then treat with a suitable solution of sodium dichromate. Wipe the treated surface with dry cloths to remove excess solution, but do NOT use a water rinse.

questions.q: Dissimilar metal contact creates electrolytic action when moisture is present; this action is ACCELERATED when the moisture is what?
questions.expl: Contact of different bare metals creates electrolytic action when moisture is present. If the moisture is salt water, the electrolytic action is accelerated, resulting in oxidation of one or both metals.

questions.q: Dissimilar joints NOT involving magnesium are protected with a minimum of two coats of what?
options: Zinc chromate or, preferably, epoxy primer
questions.short: 2 coats zinc chromate or epoxy primer
questions.expl: All dissimilar joints not involving magnesium are protected by a minimum of two coats of zinc chromate or, preferably, epoxy primer in addition to normal primer, allowed to air dry 6 hours between coats.

questions.q: Primer applied to dissimilar metal joints is allowed to air dry how long between coats?
questions.expl: Primer (zinc chromate or epoxy) for dissimilar metal joints is applied by brush or spray and allowed to air dry 6 hours between coats.

questions.q: For dissimilar joints INVOLVING MAGNESIUM, after at least two coats of primer, what is added?
options: A layer of pressure-sensitive vinyl tape 0.003" thick
questions.expl: For dissimilar joints involving magnesium, at least two coats of zinc chromate or epoxy primer are applied to each surface, then a layer of pressure-sensitive vinyl tape 0.003 inch thick is applied smoothly and firmly. The tape is not stretched (to avoid creep-back).

questions.q: When tape thickness interferes with assembly, relative motion exists, or service temperature exceeds 250 degrees F, the tape is eliminated and replaced with what?
options: A single thick coat of primer
Extra coats (minimum of three) of primer
questions.short: Minimum of three coats of primer
questions.expl: When tape thickness interferes with assembly, relative motion exists between parts, or service temperatures above 250 degrees F are anticipated, the vinyl tape is eliminated and extra coats (minimum of three) of primer are applied.

questions.q: Corrosion damage is classified under how many standard damage types?
questions.expl: Corrosion damage is classified under the four standard damage types: negligible damage, damage repairable by patching, damage repairable by insertion, and damage necessitating replacement of parts.

questions.q: The term 'negligible' corrosion damage means what?
options: The surface must still be cleaned, treated, and painted as appropriate
An engineer must approve any action
questions.short: Still must clean, treat, and paint
questions.expl: The term 'negligible' does not imply that little or nothing is to be done. The corroded surface must be cleaned, treated, and painted as appropriate. Negligible damage is corrosion that has scarred or eaten the protective coats and begun to etch the metal.

questions.q: When corrosion damage exceeds the limits to the extent that repair is NOT possible, the technician must:
options: Apply more primer and continue use
questions.expl: When corrosion damage exceeds the damage limits to the extent that repair is not possible, the component or structure must be replaced.

questions.q: The MAIN purpose of the surface finishes applied to aircraft parts by the manufacturer is to provide what?
questions.expl: Aircraft parts are almost always given a surface finish by the manufacturer; the main purpose is to provide corrosion resistance, though finishes may also increase wear resistance or provide a paint base.

questions.q: Between mechanical and chemical cleaning for surface preparation, which is PREFERRED, because none of the base metal is removed?
questions.expl: Chemical cleaning is preferred over mechanical since none of the base metal is removed by cleaning. The type used depends on the material and the foreign matter being removed.

questions.q: Steel parts are PICKLED (before plating) using which acid solutions?
questions.expl: Steel parts are pickled to remove scale, rust, or foreign matter before plating. The pickling solution can be muriatic (hydrochloric) or sulfuric acid. Sulfuric is cheaper; muriatic is more effective on certain scale. Non-plated parts are then neutralized in a lime bath.

questions.q: Electrocleaning removes grease, oil, or organic matter by suspending the metal in a hot alkaline solution and doing what?
options: Passing an electric current through the solution
Dipping it in a lime bath
questions.expl: Electrocleaning suspends the metal in a hot alkaline solution with wetting agents, inhibitors, and conductivity materials, then passes an electric current through the solution in a manner similar to electroplating.

questions.q: Blast cleaning using abrasive media is NOT applicable to which material?
questions.expl: Blast cleaning using abrasive media is not applicable to thin aluminum sheets, particularly Alclad. Steel grits are not used on aluminum or corrosion-resistant metals.

questions.q: ANODIZING is the most common surface treatment of which surfaces?
questions.expl: Anodizing is the most common surface treatment of nonclad aluminum alloy surfaces. The aluminum is the positive pole in an electrolytic bath where chromic acid or another oxidizing agent produces an aluminum oxide film.

questions.q: In the anodizing process, the aluminum alloy part serves as which pole in the electrolytic bath?
questions.expl: In anodizing, the aluminum alloy sheet or casting is the positive pole in an electrolytic bath in which chromic acid or other oxidizing agent produces an aluminum oxide film. Anodizing increases the thickness and density of the natural oxide film.

questions.q: The anodized coating provides excellent corrosion resistance, but the coating itself is described as what?
questions.expl: The anodic coating provides excellent corrosion resistance, but the coating is soft and easily scratched, requiring extreme caution when handling prior to priming. It is also a poor conductor, so it is removed where bonding wires attach.

questions.q: Which approved tools may be used to clean ANODIZED surfaces?
options: Steel wool and steel wire brushes
Aluminum wool, aluminum-oxide-impregnated nylon webbing, fine nonwoven abrasive pads, or fiber bristle brushes
questions.short: Aluminum wool / nylon webbing / fine pads / fiber brushes
questions.expl: Approved tools for cleaning anodized surfaces are aluminum wool, nylon webbing impregnated with aluminum oxide abrasive, fine-grade nonwoven abrasive pads, or fiber bristle brushes. Steel wool, steel wire brushes, and harsh abrasives are prohibited on any aluminum surface.

questions.q: A simple chemical treatment for ALL aluminum alloys that increases corrosion resistance and improves paint bonding, and is rapidly REPLACING anodizing in aircraft work, is:
questions.expl: Alodizing is a simple chemical treatment for all aluminum alloys to increase corrosion resistance and improve paint bonding. Because of its simplicity, it is rapidly replacing anodizing in aircraft work.

questions.q: In the alodizing process, after precleaning, the parts are rinsed with fresh water under pressure for how long?
questions.expl: In alodizing, after precleaning with an acidic or alkaline cleaner, the parts are rinsed with fresh water under pressure for 10 to 15 seconds before Bonderite is applied.

questions.q: A chromic acid inhibitor effective for treating exposed or corroded aluminum is what strength, activated by a small amount of sulfuric acid?
questions.expl: A 10 percent solution by weight of chromic acid, activated by a small amount of sulfuric acid, is particularly effective for treating exposed or corroded aluminum (and may also treat magnesium). Follow with regular paint finishes the same day.

questions.q: Chromium trioxide flake, used in chromic acid treatment, is a powerful oxidizing agent and must be stored how?
options: Next to paint thinners for convenience
Away from organic solvents and other combustibles
questions.expl: Chromium trioxide flake is a powerful oxidizing agent and fairly strong acid; it must be stored away from organic solvents and other combustibles. Wiping cloths used in chromic acid pickup must be thoroughly rinsed or disposed of.

questions.q: A LESS ACTIVE chemical mixture for surface treatment of aluminum, whose entrapped solutions are less likely to corrode the metal than chromic acid inhibitor, is:
options: Sodium dichromate and chromic acid solution
questions.expl: A less active mixture for surface treatment of aluminum is a solution of sodium dichromate and chromic acid. Entrapped solutions of this mixture are less likely to corrode metal surfaces than chromic acid inhibitor solutions.

questions.q: The handbook states that the MOST effective barrier between metal surfaces and corrosive media is:
questions.expl: A good, intact paint finish is the most effective barrier between metal surfaces and corrosive media. Common finishes include catalyzed polyurethane enamel, waterborne polyurethane enamel, and two-part epoxy paint.

questions.q: Which paint systems have INCREASED in popularity as VOC-emission regulations have taken effect?
questions.expl: As new VOC-emission regulations take effect, the use of waterborne paint systems has increased in popularity. Nitrate and butyrate dope finishes remain available for fabric-covered aircraft.

questions.q: Which is a reason the handbook gives that keeping an aircraft clean matters?
options: It is required by Part 91 before every flight
Dirt can hide cracks in the skin, and a film of dirt reduces flying speed and adds weight
questions.short: Dirt hides cracks; reduces speed; adds weight
questions.expl: Keeping the aircraft clean means more accurate inspections: a cracked landing gear fitting covered with mud may be overlooked, dirt can hide cracks in the skin, and a film of dirt reduces flying speed and adds extra weight.

questions.q: The FOUR general types of cleaning agents used on aircraft are solvents, emulsion cleaners, soaps, and what?
questions.expl: The types of cleaning agents used on aircraft are solvents, emulsion cleaners, soaps, and synthetic detergents.

questions.q: Which cleaners are classed as LIGHT-duty, and must be used whenever possible because they are nontoxic and nonflammable?
questions.expl: Soap and synthetic detergent-type cleaners are light-duty; solvent and emulsion-type cleaners are heavy-duty. The light-duty cleaners that are nontoxic and nonflammable must be used whenever possible.

questions.q: An alkaline cleaner that is not rinsed and neutralized may cause corrosion where on riveted or spot-welded sheet metal?
questions.expl: Cleaners that can be effectively rinsed and neutralized must be used, or an alkaline cleaner may cause corrosion within the lap joints of riveted or spot-welded sheet metal components.

questions.q: What are the THREE methods of cleaning the aircraft EXTERIOR?
options: Wet wash, dry wash, and polishing
questions.expl: The three methods of cleaning the aircraft exterior are wet wash, dry wash, and polishing. Polishing can be further broken into hand polishing and mechanical polishing.

questions.q: WET WASH removes oil, grease, carbon deposits, and most soils EXCEPT what?
questions.expl: Wet wash removes oil, grease, carbon deposits, and most soils, with the exception of corrosion and oxide films. Cleaning compounds are applied by spray or mop, then rinsed with high-pressure running water (alkaline or emulsion cleaners).

questions.q: DRY WASH is used to remove airport film, dust, and small dirt accumulations when liquids are not desirable; it is NOT suitable for what?
options: Removing heavy carbon, grease, or oil deposits, especially in exhaust areas
questions.expl: Dry wash removes airport film, dust, and small accumulations when liquids are neither desirable nor practical, but is NOT suitable for heavy deposits of carbon, grease, or oil, especially in engine exhaust areas.

questions.q: Besides restoring luster, POLISHING is also used to remove what?
questions.expl: Polishing restores the luster to painted and unpainted surfaces and is also used to remove oxidation and corrosion. Polishing materials come in various forms and degrees of abrasiveness; follow the manufacturer's instructions.

questions.q: Washing of aircraft should be performed where whenever possible, to prevent compounds from streaking the surface?
questions.expl: Washing should be performed in the shade whenever possible, because cleaning compounds tend to streak the surface if applied to hot metal or allowed to dry on the area.

questions.q: During washing, particular attention must be paid to protecting which components from water/cleaner entry?
options: Instrument system components such as pitot-static fittings and ports
questions.expl: Install covers over all openings where water or cleaners might enter and cause damage. Pay particular attention to instrument system components, such as pitot-static fittings and ports.

questions.q: Flat-finish painted areas (such as those housing radar or forward of the flight deck) must be cleaned how?
options: Not cleaned more than necessary, and never scrubbed with stiff brushes or coarse rags
questions.expl: Flat-finish painted areas must not be cleaned more than necessary and never scrubbed with stiff brushes or coarse rags. Use a soft sponge or cheesecloth with minimal manual rubbing; rinse immediately.

questions.q: Before applying soap and water to PLASTIC surfaces, the surfaces should first be flushed with what?
questions.expl: Before applying soap and water to plastic surfaces, flush them with fresh water to dissolve salt deposits and wash away dust particles. Then wash with soap and water, preferably by hand.

questions.q: Why should plastic windows NOT be rubbed with a DRY cloth?
options: It can cause scratches and builds an electrostatic charge that attracts dust
questions.expl: In view of the soft surface, do not rub plastic with a dry cloth, since it is likely to cause scratches and also builds an electrostatic charge that attracts dust. Remove the charge and dust by gently blotting with a clean damp chamois.

questions.q: Which materials must NOT be used on aircraft PLASTIC surfaces because they soften the plastic and cause crazing?
options: Acetone, benzene, gasoline, or lacquer thinners
questions.expl: Do not use acetone, benzene, carbon tetrachloride, lacquer thinners, window cleaning sprays, gasoline, fire extinguisher fluid, or deicer fluid on plastics, because they soften the plastic and cause crazing.

questions.q: Surface oil, grease, or fuel can be removed from aircraft TIRES by washing with what?
questions.expl: Surface oil, hydraulic fluid, grease, or fuel can be removed from aircraft tires by washing with a mild soap solution. After cleaning, lubricate grease fittings and hinges where grease may have been removed or diluted.

questions.q: Corrosion can establish itself on the INSIDE structure to a greater degree because:
options: It is difficult to reach some areas for cleaning
questions.short: Hard to reach areas for cleaning
questions.expl: Keeping the interior clean is just as important as the exterior. Corrosion can establish itself on the inside structure to a greater degree because it is difficult to reach some areas for cleaning. Dropped nuts, bolts, and wire combined with moisture cause electrolytic corrosion.

questions.q: When cleaning aircraft cabins, the principal hazard from indiscriminate use of flammable cleaning agents is:
options: Restricted ventilation and quick buildup of flammable vapor/air mixtures
questions.short: Flammable vapor buildup in small enclosure
questions.expl: Aircraft cabins are relatively small enclosures; restricted ventilation and quick buildup of flammable vapor/air mixtures can occur with any indiscriminate use of flammable cleaning agents or solvents, with possible ignition sources nearby. Use nonflammable agents wherever possible.

questions.q: When cleaning seat materials, why must only the necessary amount of water be used?
options: Water laced with leached fire-retardant salts can induce corrosion of aluminum seat frames and rails
questions.short: Leached fire-retardant salts corrode Al frames
questions.expl: Care is needed to prevent leaching of water-soluble fire-retardant salts. Allowing water laced with fire-retardant salts to contact the aluminum framework of seats and seat rails can induce corrosion, so use only the necessary amount of water.

questions.q: Which are examples of NONFLAMMABLE dry cleaning agents that still carry a toxicity hazard?
questions.expl: Perchlorethylene and trichlorethylene used at ambient temperatures are examples of nonflammable dry cleaning agents. They do have a toxicity hazard requiring care, and their use may be restricted by environmental laws.

questions.q: Accumulation of flammable vapors above what percent of the lower flammability limit (measured 5 feet from the point of use) requires emergency revision of operations?
questions.expl: The accumulation of flammable vapors above 25 percent of the lower flammability limit of the vapor being used, measured at a point 5 feet from the location of use, shall result in emergency revisions of operations in progress.

questions.q: The flammable-vapor 25 percent threshold during cabin cleaning is measured at a point how far from the location of use?
questions.expl: The 25 percent of lower flammability limit threshold is measured at a point 5 feet from the location of use.

questions.q: Hand-portable electrical equipment used within an aircraft cabin during cleaning must be approved for use in which hazardous location class/group?
questions.expl: Electrical equipment of a hand-portable nature used within an aircraft cabin shall be of the type approved for use in Class I, Group D, Hazardous Locations as defined by the National Electrical Code.

questions.q: For cleaning OUTSIDE the hangar, portable fire extinguishers at cabin entrances must have a minimum rating of what?
questions.expl: During cleaning/refurbishing of an aircraft outside the hangar, portable fire extinguishers at cabin entrances must have a minimum rating of 20-B. An alternate is a Class A extinguisher rated 4-A or a Class B rated 20-B at cabin doors.

questions.q: Why are all-purpose ABC (dry chemical) fire extinguishers NOT to be used where aluminum corrosion is a problem?
options: They are ineffective on Class B fires
The dry-chemical residue is corrosive to aluminum if the extinguisher is used
They are too heavy to carry
questions.expl: All-purpose ABC (dry chemical) type extinguishers are not to be used in situations where aluminum corrosion is a problem, because of the corrosive residue left if the extinguisher is used.

questions.q: Why is cleaning the powerplant important regarding cooling?
options: Grease and dirt accumulations insulate an air-cooled engine against the cooling effect of airflow
questions.expl: Grease and dirt accumulations on an air-cooled engine provide effective insulation against the cooling effect of air flowing over it, and can also cover up cracks or other defects.

questions.q: When cleaning an engine, begin at the top and wash down the engine and accessories with a fine spray of what?
questions.short: Fine spray of kerosene or solvent
questions.expl: When cleaning an engine, open or remove the cowling, and beginning with the top, wash down the engine and accessories with a fine spray of kerosene or solvent. A bristle brush may help clean some surfaces.

questions.q: Except in the process of etching, what type of material must NOT be used on a PROPELLER?
questions.expl: Fresh water, soap, and approved cleaning solvents may be used on propeller and rotor blades. Except in the process of etching, caustic material must not be used on a propeller. Scrapers, power buffers, steel brushes, or any tool that mars or scratches must not be used on propeller blades.

questions.q: STEEL propeller blades compared with aluminum alloy blades have what abrasion/corrosion characteristic?
options: Less resistance to abrasion and corrosion
More resistance to abrasion and corrosion
questions.expl: Steel propeller blades have more resistance to abrasion and corrosion than aluminum alloy blades. Steel blades, if rubbed down with oil after each flight, retain a smooth surface for a long time.

questions.q: Why is keeping a propeller blade surface wiped with OIL considered a safety feature?
options: It prevents the blade from overheating
Cracks become filled with oil that oxidizes, helping make cracks more obvious
questions.short: Oil-filled cracks show up on inspection
questions.expl: Cracks in steel or aluminum alloy blades can become filled with oil that oxidizes and is readily seen on inspection. Keeping the surface wiped with oil serves as a safety feature by helping make cracks more obvious.

questions.q: After cleaning and polishing propeller parts, the blades must be coated with what?
questions.expl: After rinsing all cleaning substances from propeller parts, all surfaces must be dried and coated with clean engine oil. Steel hubs are cleaned with soap and fresh water or approved solvent, avoiding tools that scratch the plating.

questions.q: HIGH flash point solvents (such as Stoddard solvent) are stable products having a flash point in what range?
questions.expl: High flash point solvents are stable products having a flash point from 100 degrees F to 140 degrees F, with a comparatively low degree of toxicity.

questions.q: LOW flash point Class I flammable liquids have a flash point BELOW what temperature, and are NOT to be used for aircraft cleaning?
questions.expl: Class I flammable liquids (flash point below 100 degrees F) are not to be used for aircraft cleaning or refurbishing. If a flammable liquid is absolutely necessary, use high flash point liquids (100 degrees F or more).

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a Class I (low flash point) flammable liquid that must NOT be used for aircraft cleaning?
questions.expl: Common Class I materials include acetone, aviation gasoline (AVGAS), methyl ethyl ketone, naphtha, and toluol -- all flash below 100 degrees F and are not to be used for aircraft cleaning.

questions.q: In general, solvent cleaners used in aircraft cleaning must have a flashpoint of NOT LESS than what, if explosion-proofing and special precautions are to be avoided?
questions.expl: In general, solvent cleaners used in aircraft cleaning must have a flashpoint of not less than 105 degrees F if explosion proofing of equipment and special precautions are to be avoided. Chlorinated solvents meet the nonflammable requirement but are toxic.

questions.q: Which solvent is the MOST common petroleum-base solvent used in aircraft cleaning, with a flashpoint slightly above 105 degrees F?
questions.expl: Stoddard solvent is the most common petroleum-base solvent used in aircraft cleaning. Its flashpoint is slightly above 105 degrees F; it removes grease, oils, or light soils and is preferable to kerosene, though it leaves a slight residue on evaporation.

questions.q: At what approximate temperature does ALIPHATIC NAPHTHA flash, requiring it to be used with care?
questions.expl: Aliphatic naphtha (recommended for wipe-down of cleaned surfaces just before painting and usable for cleaning acrylics and rubber) flashes at approximately 80 degrees F and must be used with care.

questions.q: AROMATIC naphtha differs from aliphatic naphtha in that aromatic naphtha is:
options: Toxic and attacks acrylics and rubber products
questions.expl: Aromatic naphtha must not be confused with the aliphatic material. It is toxic, attacks acrylics and rubber products, and must be used with adequate controls.

questions.q: What is the approximate flashpoint of Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK)?
questions.expl: MEK is a very active solvent and metal cleaner with a flashpoint of about 24 degrees F. It is toxic when inhaled and has largely been replaced by safer, more environmentally friendly solvents.

questions.q: A very active solvent and paint stripper for small areas, with a flashpoint of about 24 degrees F and toxic when inhaled, is:
questions.expl: MEK has a flashpoint of about 24 degrees F and is a very active solvent, metal cleaner, and paint stripper for small areas. This is the reverse-direction version of the MEK flashpoint fact.

questions.q: Safety solvent, trichloroethane (methyl chloroform), is used for general cleaning and grease removal as a replacement for which material?
questions.expl: Safety solvent, trichloroethane (methyl chloroform), is used for general cleaning and grease removal. It is nonflammable under ordinary circumstances and is used as a replacement for carbon tetrachloride. Prolonged use can cause dermatitis.

questions.q: KEROSENE is mixed with solvent emulsion-type cleaners for what purpose?
questions.expl: Kerosene is mixed with solvent emulsion-type cleaners for softening heavy preservative coatings. It does not evaporate as fast as dry cleaning solvent and leaves an appreciable, possibly corrosive film, so its use must be followed by another protective coating or rinse.

questions.q: Which solvent's use is specifically to be AVOIDED among chlorinated solvents because of its toxicity?
questions.expl: Chlorinated solvents of all types meet the nonflammable requirement but are toxic; safety precautions must be observed, and the use of carbon tetrachloride is to be avoided.

questions.q: Which cleaning compounds are approved for use in the OXYGEN system?
options: Anhydrous (waterless) ethyl alcohol or isopropyl alcohol
questions.expl: Cleaning compounds for the oxygen system are anhydrous (waterless) ethyl alcohol or isopropyl (anti-icing fluid) alcohol, used to clean accessible components such as crew masks and lines.

questions.q: When cleaning oxygen equipment, you must NOT use any cleaning compound that may do what?
questions.expl: Do not use any cleaning compounds that may leave an oily film when cleaning oxygen equipment. Fluids must not be put into tanks or regulators. Follow the manufacturer's instructions at all times.

questions.q: Solvent emulsion compounds are particularly useful for removing what?
options: Heavy deposits such as carbon, grease, oil, or tar
questions.expl: Solvent and water emulsion compounds are used in general aircraft cleaning. Solvent emulsions are particularly useful for removing heavy deposits such as carbon, grease, oil, or tar; used per instructions they do not affect good paint coatings.

questions.q: Which solvent emulsion cleaner is MORE effective for heavy-duty application but also tends to soften paint and requires care around rubber and plastics?
questions.expl: The phenolic-base solvent emulsion cleaner is more effective for heavy-duty application but tends to soften paint coatings and must be used with care around rubber, plastics, or other nonmetallic materials. Wear rubber gloves and goggles.

questions.q: The water emulsion cleaning compound under Specification MIL-C-22543A is safe for use on what?
options: Both painted and unpainted surfaces, including fluorescent-painted surfaces and acrylics
questions.expl: MIL-C-22543A water emulsion cleaner is intended for both painted and unpainted aircraft surfaces, is acceptable for fluorescent-painted surfaces, and is safe on acrylics, though properties vary so a sample application must be checked first.

questions.q: An OIL-SOLUBLE nonionic detergent cleaner is effective at what concentration in dry cleaning solvent for softening heavy preservative coatings?
questions.short: 3 to 5 percent in dry cleaning solvent
questions.expl: The oil-soluble nonionic detergent cleaner is effective in a 3 to 5 percent solution in dry cleaning solvent for softening and removing heavy preservative coatings, with performance similar to emulsion cleaners.

questions.q: The introduction of nonwoven abrasive pads gave the AMT a clean, inexpensive material for what?
options: Removal of corrosion products and other light abrasive needs
questions.expl: Nonwoven abrasive pads give a clean, inexpensive material for removing corrosion products and other light abrasive needs. They can be used on most metals (but the same pad must not be used on different metals) and are generally the first choice.

questions.q: Aluminum metal polish is used to produce a high luster on unpainted aluminum clad surfaces but must NOT be used on what?
questions.expl: Aluminum metal polish produces a high, long-lasting luster on unpainted aluminum clad surfaces but must not be used on anodized surfaces, because it removes the oxide coat.

questions.q: Powdered pumice is used for what?
questions.expl: Powdered pumice can be used for cleaning corroded aluminum surfaces; similar mild abrasives may also be used. Impregnated cotton wadding is used to remove exhaust gas stains and polish corroded aluminum.

questions.q: Which abrasive paper, 300 grit or finer, is described as safe to use on most surfaces?
questions.short: Aluminum oxide paper, 300 grit or finer
questions.expl: Aluminum oxide paper, 300 grit or finer, is available in several forms and is safe to use on most surfaces. Abrasive papers must not contain sharp or needle-like abrasives that imbed in the base metal.

questions.q: Why must CARBORUNDUM (silicon carbide) paper be avoided, particularly on aluminum or magnesium?
options: It is too soft to cut
Its sharp, hard grains penetrate and bury themselves, even in steel surfaces
questions.short: Sharp hard grains embed in the metal
questions.expl: Avoid carborundum (silicon carbide) papers, particularly on aluminum or magnesium; the grain structure is sharp and so hard that individual grains penetrate and bury themselves, even in steel surfaces.

questions.q: The use of EMERY paper or CROCUS cloth on aluminum or magnesium can cause serious corrosion because of what?
options: The chromium content of the abrasive
questions.expl: The use of emery paper or crocus cloth on aluminum or magnesium can cause serious corrosion of the metal by imbedded iron oxide.

questions.q: Impregnated nylon webbing material is PREFERRED over aluminum wool for what?
options: Removing corrosion products and stubborn paint films, and scuffing existing paint before touchup
questions.short: Removing corrosion / scuffing paint for touchup
questions.expl: Three grades of aluminum wool are used for general cleaning of aluminum, but impregnated nylon webbing material is preferred over aluminum wool for removing corrosion products and stubborn paint films and for scuffing existing paint finishes prior to touchup.

questions.q: Why must chemical cleaners be used with great care on ASSEMBLED aircraft?
options: Danger of entrapping corrosive materials in faying surfaces and crevices
questions.expl: Chemical cleaners must be used with great care on assembled aircraft because of the danger of entrapping corrosive materials in faying surfaces and crevices. Materials used must be relatively neutral and easy to remove, and all residues must be removed.

questions.q: Soluble salts from chemical surface treatments (such as chromic acid or dichromate) can cause what problem if not fully removed?
options: Liquefaction that promotes blistering in the paint coatings
questions.expl: Soluble salts from chemical surface treatments, such as chromic acid or dichromate treatment, liquefy and promote blistering in the paint coatings; all residues must be removed.

questions.q: Acid burns from a phosphoric-citric acid cleaner are neutralized by copious water washing followed by treatment with a diluted solution of what?
questions.expl: With phosphoric-citric acid cleaner, wear rubber gloves and goggles. Any acid burns are neutralized by copious water washing followed by treatment with a diluted solution of baking soda (sodium bicarbonate).

questions.q: Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) may be used to neutralize acid deposits in which battery compartment, and to treat acid burns?
questions.expl: Baking soda may be used to neutralize acid deposits in lead-acid battery compartments and to treat acid burns from chemical cleaners and inhibitors. (For ni-cad/alkaline battery spills, an acid such as boric is used instead -- baking soda is for ACID deposits.)

questions.q: Corrosion is best defined as the deterioration of metal by:
questions.expl: Corrosion is the natural deterioration of a refined metal by chemical or electrochemical attack with its environment.

questions.q: Corrosion converts a refined metal back into a more ___ form, such as an oxide, hydroxide, or sulfide.
questions.expl: Corrosion returns the metal to a more chemically stable (natural) state — a metallic compound such as an oxide, hydroxide, or sulfide.

questions.q: The MAIN source of corrosion in aircraft is water/water vapor containing salt combined with:
questions.expl: Water or water vapor containing salt, combined with oxygen in the atmosphere, is the main source of corrosion.

questions.q: On ALUMINUM and MAGNESIUM, corrosion typically appears as:
options: Pitting and etching with a gray or white powdery deposit
questions.short: Gray/white powdery deposit, with pitting and etching.
questions.expl: Aluminum and magnesium corrode to a gray or white powdery deposit with pitting and etching of the surface.

questions.q: On COPPER and copper alloys, corrosion appears as a:
questions.expl: Copper and copper alloys form a greenish corrosion film.

questions.q: On STEEL, corrosion appears as a:
questions.expl: Steel corrodes to a reddish corrosion byproduct commonly called rust (iron oxide).

questions.q: Aluminum corrosion products are usually easy to spot because they are:
options: White and more voluminous than the original base metal
questions.short: White and more voluminous than the base metal.
questions.expl: Aluminum corrosion products are white and generally more voluminous than the original base metal.

questions.q: The two general classifications that cover most corrosion are:
options: Direct chemical attack and electrochemical attack
questions.short: Direct chemical attack and electrochemical attack.
questions.expl: The handbook divides corrosion into two general classifications: direct chemical attack and electrochemical attack.

questions.q: In DIRECT chemical attack, the anodic and cathodic changes occur:
questions.expl: In direct (pure) chemical attack, the metal is converted to a metallic compound and the anodic and cathodic changes take place at the same point.

questions.q: In ELECTROCHEMICAL attack, the anodic and cathodic changes take place:
questions.expl: In electrochemical attack, the anodic and cathodic changes occur a measurable distance apart, requiring an electrolyte.

questions.q: Direct chemical attack is an attack resulting from direct exposure of a bare surface to caustic liquid or gaseous agents. On aluminum the visible product (white/gray powdery oxide) is formed by:
questions.expl: Oxidation is a chemical reaction with oxygen; on aluminum it produces the white/gray powdery oxide that is the visible corrosion product.

questions.q: In the notes, Iron Oxide is defined as:
options: The reddish-brown corrosion byproduct of steel (rust)
questions.expl: Iron oxide is the reddish-brown corrosion byproduct of steel, i.e., rust.

questions.q: Amalgamation is a chemical reaction of aluminum with ___; if spilled on aluminum it must be removed promptly and its vapors are poisonous.
questions.expl: Amalgamation is a reaction of mercury with aluminum; spilled mercury must be removed promptly and mercury vapors are poisonous. [class notes] — amalgamation/mercury is AIM/AC 43-4 wording, not spelled out verbatim in -30B Ch.8.

questions.q: Electrochemical attack may be likened chemically to the reaction in:
options: Electroplating, anodizing, or a dry-cell battery
questions.short: Electroplating, anodizing, or a dry-cell battery.
questions.expl: Electrochemical attack is chemically similar to the reaction in electroplating, anodizing, or a dry-cell battery.

questions.q: If two dissimilar materials are placed in an ___ solution, a chemical reaction will occur.
questions.expl: Two dissimilar materials placed in an electrolyte (electrolytic) solution will set up a chemical reaction — the basis of electrochemical corrosion.

questions.q: In electrochemical attack, electrons are removed from one metal and deposited on the other. This requires a medium, usually ___, to conduct a tiny current of electricity.
questions.expl: The reaction needs a conductive medium — usually water — to carry the tiny current of electricity between the metals.

questions.q: Condition #1 for electrochemical corrosion — a metal subject to corrosion — is the:
questions.expl: The anode is condition #1: the metal that is subject to corrosion.

questions.q: Condition #2 — presence of a dissimilar conductive metal with LESS tendency to corrode — is the:
questions.expl: The cathode is a dissimilar conductive metal that has less tendency to corrode.

questions.q: Condition #3 — a conductive fluid forming the path for electrons between anode and cathode — is the:
questions.expl: The electrolyte is the conductive fluid that provides the path for electrons between the anode and cathode.

questions.q: Condition #4 — electrical ___ between anode and cathode (usually metal-to-metal, e.g., rivets/bolts) — completes the cell.
questions.expl: Electrical contact between anode and cathode — usually metal-to-metal contact such as rivets, bolts, or corrosion — completes the path for the returning electrons.

questions.q: Elimination of ___ of the four conditions will stop electrochemical corrosion.
questions.expl: Eliminating any ONE of the four conditions stops electrochemical corrosion — the basis of corrosion control (most often removing the electrolyte by cleaning).

questions.q: Why does eliminating the ELECTROLYTE stop corrosion?
options: It breaks the conductive liquid path so the cell's current cannot flow
questions.short: It breaks the conductive path so current can't flow.
questions.expl: Removing the electrolyte breaks the conductive liquid path; without it the minute corrosion current cannot flow, so the cell cannot operate.

questions.q: Every metal's specific electrical potential in a given environment is called its:
questions.expl: All metals and alloys have a specific electrical potential in a chemical environment; that potential is called the metal's nobility.

questions.q: The LESS noble a metal is, the:
questions.expl: The less noble a metal, the more easily it is corroded; exposure makes the more active metal anodic and the less active metal cathodic.

questions.q: Small corrosion cells set up on a single alloy surface by these potential differences are called:
questions.expl: The differences in potential establish small anode/cathode areas on the surface called local cells.

questions.q: The electrochemical (galvanic) series is a list of metals/alloys ranked from:
options: Most likely to corrode (anodic) to least likely (cathodic)
questions.short: Most anodic (corrodes) to most cathodic (least likely).
questions.expl: The galvanic/electrochemical series ranks metals from most anodic (most likely to corrode) to most cathodic (least likely).

questions.q: Metals are grouped from the most ___ to the most ___ in the series.
questions.expl: Metals are grouped from most anodic (most likely to corrode) to most cathodic (least likely to corrode).

questions.q: TERM — 'Anode' is defined as the metal that is:
options: Most noble and does not corrode
Most active / least noble — oxidized and corrodes
questions.short: Most active / least noble — it corrodes.
questions.expl: The anode is the metal most likely to corrode (most active / less noble); it is oxidized and corrodes.

questions.q: TERM — 'Cathode' is defined as the metal that is:
options: Least likely to corrode (most noble); does NOT corrode
questions.short: Most noble — it does not corrode.
questions.expl: The cathode is the metal least likely to corrode (most noble / less active); it does not corrode.

questions.q: TERM — 'Electrolyte' is defined as:
options: A continuous conductive liquid path (usually water) able to conduct a tiny current
questions.short: A continuous conductive liquid path, usually water.
questions.expl: The electrolyte is a continuous, conductive liquid path (usually water) capable of conducting a tiny current of electricity.

questions.q: TERM — 'Ions' are best described as:
options: Electrically charged atoms; at the anode metal ions go into solution
questions.short: Electrically charged atoms; metal ions leave at the anode.
questions.expl: Ions are electrically charged atoms; metal ions go into solution at the anode (the metal that loses electrons and corrodes). [class notes] — 'ions' is defined contextually; -30B has no standalone glossary line.

questions.q: TERM — 'Chemical reaction' (corrosion): the metal is converted into a metallic compound — the metal is oxidized (anodic change) and the corrosive agent is:
questions.expl: In the corrosion chemical reaction the metal is oxidized (anodic change) and the corrosive agent is reduced (cathodic change).

questions.q: TERM — 'Electrochemical' corrosion specifically requires an electrolyte because the anodic and cathodic changes are:
questions.expl: Electrochemical corrosion has anodic and cathodic changes a measurable distance apart, which requires an electrolyte (current path).

questions.q: The most common corrosive agents are:
questions.expl: The most common corrosive agents are acids, alkalies, and salts (the atmosphere and water also act as agents/media).

questions.q: Among corrosive agents, the MOST destructive are the:
questions.expl: Acids are the most destructive corrosive agents — sulfuric (battery) acid, halogen acids (hydrochloric, hydrofluoric, hydrobromic), nitrous compounds, and organic acids.

questions.q: Which group is particularly corrosive to ALUMINUM (washing soda, potash, lime)?
questions.expl: Alkalies such as washing soda, potash (wood ashes), and lime (cement dust) are particularly corrosive to aluminum.

questions.q: Which alkali is the EXCEPTION that aluminum alloys are highly resistant to?
questions.expl: Aluminum alloys are highly resistant to ammonia, even though most alkalies attack aluminum.

questions.q: Most salt solutions are corrosive agents mainly because they are:
options: Good electrolytes that promote corrosive attack
questions.expl: Most salt solutions are good electrolytes and therefore promote corrosive attack.

questions.q: The major ATMOSPHERIC corrosive agents are:
questions.expl: The major atmospheric corrosive agents are oxygen and airborne moisture.

questions.q: The corrosiveness of WATER depends mainly on its:
options: Dissolved minerals/gases and conductivity (ability to act as an electrolyte)
questions.expl: Water's corrosiveness depends on dissolved minerals and gases that govern its conductivity — its ability to act as an electrolyte.

questions.q: Aircraft operated near ___ must be cleaned more often to remove salt residue.
questions.expl: Aircraft near salt water / a marine (seacoast) environment need more frequent cleaning to remove corrosive salt residue.

questions.q: Chemical (direct) corrosion tends to occur under ___ conditions over a long period of time.
questions.expl: Direct chemical attack from constant exposure occurs under dry conditions over a long period. [class notes] — phrasing is AIM slide-style; not verbatim in -30B.

questions.q: Electrochemical corrosion occurs under ___ conditions and proceeds ___.
questions.expl: Electrochemical corrosion needs an electrolyte, so it occurs under wet/moist conditions and proceeds rapidly (speed increases in hot, moist climates). [class notes] — AIM-style phrasing.

questions.q: Type of corrosion #1 listed in the 8083 (most common/general form) is:
questions.expl: The handbook lists surface corrosion as the first (most common) of the five types.

questions.q: Type of corrosion #2 — caused by contact of two unlike metals plus an electrolyte — is:
questions.expl: Dissimilar metal corrosion (galvanic) is the second type listed.

questions.q: Type of corrosion #3 — an attack along the grain boundaries of an alloy — is:
questions.expl: Intergranular corrosion is the third type — attack along an alloy's grain boundaries.

questions.q: Type of corrosion #4 — caused by sustained tensile stress plus a corrosive environment — is:
questions.expl: Stress corrosion / stress-corrosion cracking is the fourth type.

questions.q: Type of corrosion #5 — between two surfaces at rest with slight relative motion — is:
questions.expl: Fretting corrosion is the fifth type listed.

questions.q: The notes state additional types of corrosion are listed in which documents?
questions.expl: The worksheet cites AC 43.13 and AC 43-4 for additional/in-depth corrosion types; -30B p.8-1 points to AC 43-4 (Corrosion Control for Aircraft). [class notes]

questions.q: Surface corrosion appears as a general roughening, etching, or pitting, often with a powdery deposit. Under a coating it may first show as:
options: Small blisters where the paint/plating lifts off
questions.expl: Hidden surface corrosion lifts the surface coating (paint or plating) into small blisters from the pressure of corrosion products.

questions.q: Uniform etch corrosion results from a direct chemical or electrochemical attack; on a polished surface it first shows as:
options: A general dulling, then frosted/etched/pitted surface
questions.short: General dulling, then a frosted/etched/pitted surface.
questions.expl: Uniform etch first appears as a general dulling of the surface; if allowed to continue the surface becomes rough and frosted (etched/pitted).

questions.q: The general dulling/discoloration of metal from exposure to elevated temperatures is ___ — and is NOT uniform etch corrosion.
questions.expl: Discoloration from heat is oxidation/heat discoloration, not uniform etch corrosion; colored surface oxides can form on some alloys above 700 °F. [class notes] — AIM-style distractor line.

questions.q: Pitting and filiform corrosion are forms of ___ on a metal surface.
options: Concentration cell corrosion (surface attack on coated metal)
questions.short: Concentration cell / surface attack on coated metal.
questions.expl: Pitting and filiform are forms of concentration cell corrosion — surface attack on coated metal.

questions.q: The most common effect of corrosion on aluminum and magnesium alloys, resembling dust blotching the surface, is:
questions.expl: Pitting is the most common corrosion effect on aluminum and magnesium — a white/gray powdery deposit like dust that blotches the surface.

questions.q: When the pitting deposit is cleaned away, you can see tiny ___ in the surface.
questions.expl: Cleaning the powdery deposit reveals tiny holes or pits in the surface — the signature of pitting corrosion.

questions.q: Pitting can be severe in other alloys when ___ absorb moisture on the surface.
questions.expl: Hygroscopic flux residues absorb moisture and tend to cause severe pitting.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion is a special form of ___ corrosion that occurs under organic coatings.
questions.expl: Filiform is a special form of oxygen concentration cell corrosion that occurs under organic coatings (paint).

questions.q: Filiform corrosion is recognized by its characteristic:
options: Worm-like trace (series of small worms) under the paint
questions.expl: Filiform shows as a series of small worm-like traces beneath the paint film.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion is often seen on surfaces that were improperly ___ prior to painting (slightly acidic surface).
questions.expl: Filiform appears where the surface was improperly cleaned/prepared (left slightly acidic) before painting.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion typically occurs when relative humidity is between:
questions.expl: Filiform occurs when relative humidity is 78–90 percent and the surface is slightly acidic; storing below ~70% RH helps prevent it.

questions.q: The 78–90% range that drives filiform corrosion is a value of:
questions.expl: 78–90 percent is the relative-humidity band in which filiform corrosion develops.

questions.q: If filiform corrosion is left untreated, it can lead to ___, especially around fasteners and seams.
questions.expl: Untreated filiform corrosion can progress to intergranular corrosion, particularly around fasteners and at seams.

questions.q: Dissimilar metal corrosion is also called:
questions.expl: Dissimilar metal corrosion = galvanic (electrolytic) corrosion.

questions.q: Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals make ___ contact in the presence of an electrolyte.
questions.expl: Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals make electrical (metal-to-metal) contact in the presence of an electrolyte.

questions.q: For an aluminum skin next to a steel screw, the rate of galvanic corrosion depends on the difference in ___ between the metals.
questions.expl: The rate depends on the difference in activity — their separation in the galvanic/electrochemical series. Greater difference = faster corrosion.

questions.q: Because of activity differences, materials are grouped together so that similar metals are used together to:
options: Eliminate the possibility of galvanic corrosion
questions.expl: Grouping similar metals (close in the series) minimizes the potential difference and helps eliminate galvanic corrosion.

questions.q: In the galvanic series, the pairing with the GREATEST activity difference (most severe couple) is:
questions.expl: Magnesium is most anodic and gold/graphite most cathodic — the largest potential difference and most severe galvanic couple.

questions.q: Forcing small steel particles into aluminum with a steel wire brush or steel wool can cause:
questions.expl: Embedding steel particles in aluminum creates dissimilar-metal corrosion sites; never use steel wool/steel brushes on aluminum or magnesium.

questions.q: If dissimilar metals (steel screw in aluminum skin) are in contact with a corrosive agent, dissimilar-metal corrosion can then continue as:
questions.expl: Galvanic (dissimilar-metal) corrosion at a steel-screw/aluminum-skin joint can progress into intergranular corrosion.

questions.q: Intergranular corrosion is an attack along ___ of an alloy, commonly from a lack of uniformity in the alloy structure.
questions.expl: Intergranular corrosion attacks along grain boundaries and commonly results from a lack of uniformity (e.g., improper heat treatment) in the alloy.

questions.q: Intergranular corrosion can form between the grain boundaries of areas that are:
questions.expl: Intergranular corrosion can form at the grain boundaries of spot-welded seams.

questions.q: Intergranular corrosion (along grain boundaries) is detected with ___ equipment.
questions.expl: Subsurface intergranular/exfoliation corrosion is detected with ultrasonic and eddy current inspection equipment.

questions.q: Damage from intergranular corrosion is repaired ONLY by:
options: Mechanical removal of all corrosion products and delaminated layers
questions.short: Mechanical removal of all corrosion/delaminated layers.
questions.expl: Intergranular damage is repaired only by mechanical removal of all corrosion products and delaminated metal; then evaluate remaining strength (DER if limits are exceeded).

questions.q: Exfoliation corrosion is best described as a/an:
options: Advanced form of intergranular corrosion that lifts surface grains in layers
questions.short: Advanced intergranular corrosion that lifts grains in layers.
questions.expl: Exfoliation is an advanced form of intergranular corrosion; expanding corrosion products at grain boundaries lift the surface grains in layers.

questions.q: Exfoliation forms most often in ___ such as spars (where grain thickness is less).
questions.expl: Exfoliation is most often seen in extruded sections like spars, where grain thickness is less than in rolled forms.

questions.q: Exfoliation forces the metal to:
questions.expl: Expanding corrosion products at the grain boundaries force the metal to separate (lift) in layers — delamination.

questions.q: Exfoliation corrosion is typically detected by ___ inspection.
questions.expl: Exfoliation (a subsurface corrosion) is detected by ultrasonic inspection.

questions.q: Stress corrosion occurs from the combined effect of sustained ___ and a damaging chemical environment.
questions.expl: Stress corrosion results from sustained tensile stress (constant or cyclic) acting together with a damaging/corrosive chemical environment.

questions.q: Stress-corrosion cracking occurs along lines of external loading (surface) or ___ stress (under the surface).
questions.expl: Cracking occurs along lines of external loading on the surface or internal (trapped/residual) stress under the surface.

questions.q: Internal stresses are produced during ___ (e.g., cold-working or unequal cooling).
questions.expl: Internal (residual) stresses are produced during manufacturing — cold-working, unequal cooling, etc.

questions.q: Stress-corrosion cracking is defined as a ___ of the metal caused by sustained tensile stress and a corrosive environment.
questions.expl: Stress-corrosion cracking is a failure (cracking) of the metal that progresses along grain boundaries under constant tensile stress in a corrosive environment.

questions.q: Fretting corrosion occurs between two mating surfaces, normally at rest, that are subject to slight:
questions.expl: Fretting occurs when two surfaces normally at rest undergo slight relative motion (vibration).

questions.q: Fretting corrosion is characterized by ___ of the surfaces and generation of considerable finely divided debris.
questions.expl: Fretting is characterized by pitting of the surfaces and the generation of considerable quantities of finely divided debris.

questions.q: Deep grooves resembling brinell marks worn into a bearing surface by fretting are called:
questions.expl: Where contact areas are small and sharp, fretting wears grooves resembling brinell marks — called false brinelling.

questions.q: The classic indication of fretting corrosion on an aircraft rivet is a:
options: Black ring (smoking rivet) trailing toward the tail
questions.expl: Fretting on rivets shows as a black ring — the 'smoking rivet' — typically trailing toward the tail after flight.

questions.q: Regarding moisture and fretting, the notes state that parts must be ___ when fretting occurs (water vapor increases the reaction).
questions.expl: To control fretting, parts must be lubricated and fasteners properly torqued; water vapor increases the reaction. [class notes] — 'parts must be ___' phrasing is AIM-style.

questions.q: Corrosion Detection Method #1 — the most widely used — uses magnifying glasses, mirrors, flashlight, and borescopes. It is:
questions.expl: Visual inspection is the most widely used method, aided by a 5–10X magnifier, flashlight, inspection mirror, and borescope.

questions.q: Visual corrosion inspection commonly uses a magnifier of about:
questions.expl: A 5–10X magnifying glass is used with a flashlight and inspection mirror for visual corrosion inspection.

questions.q: Detection Method #2 — induced electrical currents detect surface and subsurface cracks/corrosion — is:
questions.expl: Eddy current uses induced electrical currents to detect surface and subsurface cracks/corrosion; it requires calibrated equipment and a reference standard.

questions.q: Detection Method #3 — high-frequency sound waves detect subsurface corrosion/thinning (e.g., exfoliation) — is:
questions.expl: Ultrasonic uses high-frequency sound waves to detect subsurface corrosion and thinning such as exfoliation; it requires calibration and a reference standard.

questions.q: Detection Method #4 — penetrating radiation reveals hidden/internal corrosion — is:
questions.expl: Radiological (X-ray) uses penetrating radiation to reveal hidden/internal corrosion; it requires calibrated equipment and certified NDI personnel.

questions.q: Which detection methods require properly calibrated equipment and a reference standard each time used?
questions.expl: Eddy current, ultrasonic, and X-ray each require calibrated equipment and a controlling reference standard for reliable results.

questions.q: Battery compartments stay corrosion-prone mainly because:
options: Fumes from overheated electrolyte spread to adjacent cavities
questions.short: Electrolyte fumes spread to adjacent cavities.
questions.expl: Fumes from overheated electrolyte are hard to contain and spread to adjacent cavities, attacking unprotected metal; check the sump jar and test for acid/alkali with litmus paper.

questions.q: Battery boxes are vented to outside air through a vent containing a porous pad moistened with:
questions.short: A neutralizing agent — bicarbonate of soda.
questions.expl: Battery vent systems use a porous pad moistened with a neutralizing agent (bicarbonate of soda) to neutralize acid fumes.

questions.q: Engine exhaust trail areas are corrosion-prone because exhaust deposits are:
options: Very corrosive, collecting at gaps, seams, hinges, and fairings downstream
questions.expl: Both reciprocating and turbine exhaust deposits are very corrosive and give trouble at gaps, seams, hinges, and fairings downstream of the exhaust.

questions.q: Lavatory and food-service areas are corrosion-prone because:
options: Spilled food/waste attracts and retains moisture causing corrosive attack
questions.expl: Spilled food and waste attract and retain moisture, causing corrosive attack; clean these areas frequently and maintain sealant and paint.

questions.q: Which area 'receives more punishment' from mud, water, salt, gravel, and debris than any other?
options: The wheel well and landing gear
questions.expl: The wheel well and landing gear receive more punishment from mud, water, salt, gravel, and debris than any other area; their complex shapes are hard to coat (check magnesium wheels, B-nuts, struts).

questions.q: Abrasion by high-velocity air removes protective coatings on:
options: Engine frontal areas and cooling-air vents (some have abrasion strips)
questions.expl: High-velocity air abrades protective coatings on engine frontal areas and cooling-air vents (some aircraft use abrasion strips).

questions.q: Corrosion that 'first appears along seams and lap joints,' starting in spot-welded seams (checked with a straight-edge ruler for waviness), is:
questions.expl: Concentration-cell / crevice corrosion appears along seams and lap joints, starting in spot-welded seams; waviness is checked with a straight-edge ruler.

questions.q: Organic growth that grows into ___ around rivet heads and seams in the exhaust trail attaches to aluminum skin and causes oxygen-concentration-cell corrosion.
questions.expl: Organic growth (micro-organisms) forms scum/slime that attaches to aluminum skin, causing oxygen-concentration-cell corrosion; water in fuel can seep through sealers causing similar growth.

questions.q: Piano-type hinges are prime corrosion spots mainly because:
options: The steel pin contacts the aluminum hinge (dissimilar metals) and traps moisture (electrolyte)
questions.short: Steel pin + aluminum hinge + trapped moisture.
questions.expl: Piano hinges combine a steel pin with an aluminum hinge (dissimilar metals) and trap moisture that acts as the electrolyte.

questions.q: Areas where movable surfaces (flaps, spoilers) move back into the structure and trap dirt/water are the:
options: Wing flap and spoiler recesses (inspect deployed)
questions.expl: Flap and spoiler recesses collect dirt and water unnoticed while retracted; inspect them with the surfaces fully deployed.

questions.q: Low-point areas where debris and liquids accumulate, with drain holes that may become clogged, are the:
questions.expl: Water entrapment areas collect debris and liquids; drain holes are provided but may clog and must be kept clear.

questions.q: Control cables are inspected for corrosion by:
options: Random cleaning of short sections with solvent-soaked cloths
questions.short: Random cleaning of short sections with solvent cloths.
questions.expl: Inspect cables by random cleaning of short sections with solvent-soaked cloths; water between cables causes corrosion if left unprotected. Replace cables with internal corrosion.

questions.q: Bilge areas and engine mounts (tubular steel structure) are corrosion-prone because starter current returns through the mounts and waste fluids collect. Welded steel tubular elements are periodically protected with:
questions.expl: Welded steel tube structures are filled with hot linseed oil and drained to prevent internal corrosion. [class notes] — the hot-linseed-oil tube treatment is AC 43.13-1B welded-tubular-steel practice, not -30B Ch.8 verbatim.

questions.q: Bilge areas specifically are corrosion-prone because:
options: Waste fluids, water, and debris collect and residual oil masks hidden water cells
questions.short: Waste fluids/water collect; oil masks hidden water.
questions.expl: Bilge areas collect waste hydraulic fluid, water, dirt, and debris; residual oil masks small water pools that set up hidden corrosion cells.

questions.q: A complete corrosion treatment, step 1, is:
options: Cleaning and stripping of the corroded area
questions.expl: Step 1 of a complete corrosion treatment is cleaning and stripping (removing the finish from) the corroded area.

questions.q: Complete corrosion treatment, step 2, is to ___ any residual materials remaining in pits and crevices.
questions.expl: Step 2 is neutralizing residual corrosive materials in pits and crevices.

questions.q: Complete corrosion treatment, step 3, is to ___ protective surface films.
questions.expl: Step 3 is restoring protective surface films that were removed.

questions.q: Complete corrosion treatment, final step, is:
options: Applying temporary or permanent coatings or paint finishes
questions.expl: The final step is applying temporary or permanent coatings or paint finishes.

questions.q: Corrosion under paint cannot be inspected until the paint is:
questions.expl: Corrosion under paint can't be assessed until the finish is stripped; mask/contain the area and protect rubber, acrylics, plastics, and sealants from the stripper.

questions.q: When stripping/cleaning corrosion, you must NOT use steel wool or a steel wire brush on:
questions.expl: Never use steel wool or a steel wire brush on aluminum or magnesium — embedded steel particles cause dissimilar-metal corrosion.

questions.q: If corrosion is severe, it may be easier to ___ than to treat it; some parts must be replaced due to the extent of corrosion.
questions.expl: Where corrosion is extensive, replacing the part can be easier than treating it; some parts must be replaced.

questions.q: When reworking corrosion depressions, clean an area around the pits to a width about 10× the depth and a length of about:
questions.expl: Rework depressions as smoothly blended dish-outs using a 20:1 length-to-depth ratio (width ~10× the depth).

questions.q: The '20:1' figure in corrosion rework refers to the:
options: Length-to-depth ratio of the blended dish-out
questions.expl: 20:1 is the length-to-depth ratio used when blending a corrosion dish-out.

questions.q: In treating ferrous materials, iron oxide (rust) is a problem because it:
options: Causes alloys to corrode and must have all traces removed
questions.short: It causes further corrosion; remove all traces.
questions.expl: Iron oxide (rust) causes alloys to corrode; all traces must be removed (mechanically) because rust is not protective and promotes more attack.

questions.q: On highly stressed steel parts, corrosion is removed with fine stone or fine abrasive paper — NOT brushes — because:
options: Wire brushing/blasting can damage or notch high-stress steel
questions.expl: On highly stressed steel parts use fine stone or fine abrasive paper (not brushes/blasting) to avoid creating stress risers; this removes corrosion safely.

questions.q: Mechanical corrosion removal methods include wire brushes, abrasive paper, abrasive blasting (sand, aluminum oxide, glass beads), and:
options: Mild abrasive (rouge, fine-grit aluminum oxide) / nonwoven abrasive pad
questions.short: Mild abrasive (rouge, fine aluminum oxide) / nonwoven pad.
questions.expl: Methods also include mild abrasives (rouge, fine-grit aluminum oxide, buffing compound on cloth wheels) and nonwoven abrasive pads.

questions.q: Hydrogen embrittlement is a chemical reaction causing ___ to be absorbed into metals, often from non-approved or commercial caustic/acid cleaning compounds.
questions.expl: Hydrogen embrittlement is the absorption of hydrogen into metals, often caused by non-approved/commercial caustic or acid cleaning compounds.

questions.q: Chemical rust removers are restricted on high-strength steel parts (without engineering authorization) because they can cause:
questions.expl: High-strength steel is subject to hydrogen embrittlement, so chemical corrosion removers require engineering authorization.

questions.q: Plating (electroplating) is best described as:
options: A thin coating of one metal electrolytically deposited onto another
questions.short: One metal electrolytically deposited on another.
questions.expl: Plating/electroplating is a thin coating of one metal electrolytically deposited on another (e.g., cadmium on steel).

questions.q: In an electroplating setup from the notes, the part to be plated is connected to the ___ terminal and the coating metal to the ___ terminal, both in an electrolyte.
questions.expl: In plating the steel part is the cathode (connected negative) and the cadmium/chromium coating metal is the anode (connected positive), both immersed in an electrolyte.

questions.q: A SACRIFICIAL coating (such as cadmium or zinc on steel) is the ___; it corrodes rather than the base metal.
questions.expl: A sacrificial coating is anodic to the base metal — it becomes the anode and corrodes preferentially, protecting the base metal.

questions.q: Cadmium is used as a sacrificial plating on steel because cadmium is:
options: Anodic to steel and corrodes preferentially to protect it
questions.short: Anodic to steel — corrodes to protect it.
questions.expl: Cadmium is anodic to steel, so it corrodes sacrificially (uniform surface corrosion of the plating) and protects the steel base.

questions.q: In organic-coating (painting) surface prep, the surface is first prepared by:
questions.expl: Organic-coating surface prep begins with dry abrasive blasting to clean and roughen the surface.

questions.q: Before primer adheres, cadmium-plated parts must be etched with ___ solution.
options: Chromic acid (10% chromic acid activated with sulfuric acid)
questions.expl: Cadmium-plated parts are etched with chromic acid (10% chromic acid activated with sulfuric acid) so the primer will adhere.

questions.q: In the painting sequence, after surface prep you coat with ___ before topcoat.
questions.expl: After prep you apply primer (zinc chromate or epoxy primer); allow it to dry at least one hour before painting.

questions.q: After priming, you must allow the primer to dry at least ___ before painting.
questions.expl: Allow the primer to dry at least one hour before painting (6 hours air-dry between primer coats on dissimilar-metal joints).

questions.q: The most effective barrier between metal surfaces and a corrosive environment is:
options: A good, intact protective paint finish
questions.expl: A good, intact protective paint finish is the most effective barrier against corrosion.

questions.q: The notes give one-word coating DEFINITIONS. 'Tough decorative coating' = :
questions.expl: Enamel = a tough decorative coating. [class notes] — these one-word coating definitions are AIM/AC 43.13 list-style; -30B names polyurethane enamel/epoxy/dope but not each as a single matched term.

questions.q: Coating definition — 'protective glossy finish' = :
questions.expl: Lacquer (polyurethane enamel) = a protective glossy finish. [class notes]

questions.q: Coating definition — 'chemical-resistant finishing material' = :
questions.expl: Epoxy (two-part epoxy paint) = a chemical-resistant finishing material. [class notes]

questions.q: Coating definition — material 'used to shrink cotton/linen fabric' = :
questions.expl: Dope (nitrate dope then butyrate dope) is used to shrink and protect cotton/linen fabric. [class notes]

questions.q: Which dope 'will not cause lifting or softening' of an existing finish?
questions.expl: Butyrate dope does not cause lifting or softening (unlike nitrate dope over some finishes). [class notes]

questions.q: A 'compatible finish system' is best described as a:
options: Group of chemically compatible materials (primer + topcoat that bond together)
questions.expl: A compatible finish system is a group of chemically compatible materials — primer plus topcoat that bond together. [class notes]

questions.q: ___ dissolves oil/grease, does not harm rubber/acrylic, and is used to wipe surfaces clean before painting.
questions.expl: Aliphatic naphtha dissolves oil and grease without harming rubber or acrylics and is used to wipe surfaces clean before painting.

questions.q: Among aircraft construction metals, MAGNESIUM is:
options: First in the series — the most chemically active (most anodic) and hardest to protect
questions.short: Most chemically active / most anodic — hardest to protect.
questions.expl: Magnesium is first in the electrochemical series — the most chemically active (most anodic) metal in aircraft and the most difficult to protect.

questions.q: Magnesium may experience stress-corrosion cracking in:
questions.expl: Magnesium alloys may stress-corrode in moist air (salt water attacks steel/aluminum; methyl-alcohol/HCl attacks titanium).

questions.q: Mechanical corrosion removal on non-ferrous metals (magnesium/aluminum) uses non-metallic (hog-bristle) brushes and:
questions.expl: Non-ferrous removal uses non-metallic (hog-bristle) brushes and carbide-tipped cutting tools — never steel wool, steel wire brushes, or steel tools on magnesium/aluminum.

questions.q: When cleaning corroded magnesium you must NOT use:
options: Steel wire brushes or steel cutting tools
questions.short: Steel wire brushes / steel cutting tools.
questions.expl: Steel wire brushes, carborundum abrasives, and steel tools must never be used on magnesium; use stiff hog-bristle/nonmetallic tools.

questions.q: Pure aluminum forms a dense ___ so air cannot penetrate, preventing further corrosion — but it is not strong enough for aircraft structures.
questions.expl: Pure aluminum forms a dense aluminum oxide film that blocks air and prevents further corrosion, but pure aluminum is too weak for structures.

questions.q: ___ is pure aluminum rolled/clad over a stronger aluminum alloy; the pure cladding is sacrificial (anodic).
questions.expl: Alclad is relatively pure aluminum clad over a stronger alloy; the pure cladding is anodic (sacrificial) and protects the alloy core. Avoid mechanically removing the Alclad layer.

questions.q: In Alclad, the sacrificial (anodic) layer is the:
questions.expl: The pure-aluminum cladding is anodic to (and sacrificially protects) the stronger alloy core.

questions.q: ANODIZING is best described as a/an:
options: Electrolytic factory process — part bathed in a chromic-acid (lead vat) bath with the part as the positive pole
questions.expl: Anodizing is an electrolytic factory process; the part is bathed in chromic acid and water (lead vat) with the aluminum as the positive pole, thickening the protective oxide film.

questions.q: Anodizing causes the surface to take on a light/dark gray color and acts as a/an:
questions.expl: The anodic (oxide) coating is a poor conductor — an electric insulator — so it must be removed where a bonding wire attaches.

questions.q: ALODIZING (Alodine) is best described as a/an:
options: Simple non-electrolytic (chemical conversion) coating for all aluminum alloys
questions.expl: Alodizing is a simple non-electrolytic chemical conversion process forming an oxide film with chromic acid and water (MIL-C-5541 / Alodine 1201); it is replacing anodizing in field work.

questions.q: The military spec / brand associated with the alodine chemical conversion coating is:
questions.expl: Alodizing uses MIL-C-5541 / Alodine 1201 to increase corrosion resistance and paint-bonding qualities.

questions.q: Before alodizing, all corrosion must be removed and the cleaned surface must support an unbroken:
questions.expl: All corrosion must be removed before alodizing, and the surface must support an unbroken (water-break-free) rinse film, confirming cleanliness.

questions.q: For alodizing, the chemical conversion solution is applied and kept ___ for about 2–5 minutes, then rinsed.
questions.expl: Apply the alodine chemical 2–5 minutes, keeping the surface wet, then rinse.

questions.q: Alodine improves corrosion resistance AND:
questions.expl: Alodine improves paint adhesion (paint-bonding qualities) along with corrosion resistance.

questions.q: Which statement about anodize vs. alodine is correct?
options: Anodize is electrolytic; alodine is a non-electrolytic chemical conversion
Alodine is electrolytic; anodize is chemical
questions.expl: Anodizing uses electric current (electrolytic); alodizing/Alodine is a non-electrolytic chemical conversion. Both improve corrosion resistance and paint bond.

questions.q: A thin natural aluminum oxide film (about 0.001"–0.0025" thick) is:
options: Not detrimental — a protective hard-shell barrier
questions.expl: This thin natural oxide (0.001–0.0025") is not detrimental; it forms a protective hard-shell barrier and is not the severe corrosion of concern.

questions.q: A 10% chromic acid solution activated with sulfuric acid is used mainly to:
options: Treat/inhibit corroded aluminum (and magnesium), restoring the protective oxide film
questions.short: Treat corroded aluminum/magnesium and restore the oxide film.
questions.expl: A 10% chromic acid solution activated by sulfuric acid treats corroded aluminum (and magnesium), restoring the protective oxide film.

questions.q: Zinc chromate primer is specified under:
questions.expl: Zinc chromate primer is specified under MIL-P-8585A. [class notes] — the specific MIL number is AIM/AC 43.13 material; -30B confirms zinc chromate/epoxy primer for dissimilar-metal joints.

questions.q: Zinc chromate primer protects partly because its porous film:
options: Releases chromate ions when wetted, inhibiting electrolytic action
questions.short: Releases chromate ions to inhibit electrolytic action.
questions.expl: Zinc chromate primer is a porous film; when wetted it releases chromate ions that inhibit electrolytic (corrosion) action.

questions.q: The color of zinc chromate primer is:
questions.expl: Zinc chromate primer is yellow-green or dark green in color.

questions.q: Zinc chromate primer is thinned with ___ (a less-flammable solvent), and applied using a filter mask or respirator.
questions.expl: Zinc chromate is thinned with toluol/toluene (the approved, less-flammable thinner); use a filter mask or respirator. [class notes] — thinner detail is AIM/AC 43.13 material.

questions.q: Corrosion prevention works by eliminating one or more of the four cell conditions. The four are: anode, cathode, electrolyte, and:
options: Electrical contact (current path) between anode and cathode
questions.expl: Prevention eliminates one or more of: the anode (corroding metal), the cathode (dissimilar metal), the electrolyte (conductive liquid), or the electrical contact (current path) between anode and cathode.

questions.q: Engine oil contributes to corrosion prevention because changing it (or replacing with preservative oil for storage):
options: Removes absorbed contaminants / keeps moisture and contaminants out
questions.expl: Engine oil absorbs contaminants; changing oil — or using preservative oil for storage — keeps moisture/contaminants out and prevents internal corrosion.

questions.q: Steel propeller blades are preserved against corrosion by:
options: Wiping with clean engine oil after each flight
questions.short: Wiping with clean engine oil after each flight.
questions.expl: Steel propeller blades retain a smooth, corrosion-resistant surface if wiped with clean engine oil after each flight.

questions.q: Dissimilar metals should always be ___ on the structure and from contact with water.
questions.expl: Dissimilar metals must be insulated/separated on the structure (and protected from water) to prevent galvanic corrosion.

questions.q: Which is the FIRST listed dissimilar-metal insulation method in the notes?
questions.expl: The notes list insulation methods starting with non-porous dielectric material, then primer, vinyl tape, aluminum tape, washers, and grease/sealant.

questions.q: Dissimilar-metal joints get at least ___ of zinc-chromate (or epoxy) primer.
questions.expl: Dissimilar-metal joints are protected with at least two coats of zinc-chromate or epoxy primer.

questions.q: Pressure-sensitive vinyl tape used to insulate dissimilar-metal joints is:
