questions.expl: Dissimilar-metal joints use at least two coats of primer plus 0.003-inch pressure-sensitive vinyl tape applied smoothly without stretching.

questions.q: Which is a complete list of approved dissimilar-metal separators from the notes?
options: Bare metal contact and a coat of rust
Non-porous dielectric, 2 coats zinc-chromate/epoxy primer, 0.003" vinyl tape, aluminum tape, washers, grease/sealant
Only one layer of vinyl tape
questions.short: Dielectric, primer, vinyl tape, aluminum tape, washers, grease/sealant.
questions.expl: Six valid separators: non-porous dielectric material; at least 2 coats zinc-chromate/epoxy primer; 0.003-inch vinyl tape; aluminum tape; insulating washers; and grease or sealant.

questions.q: Steel fasteners in aluminum structure (countersunk holes treated and primed) should be installed:
options: Wet (coated with primer/sealant while still wet)
questions.short: Wet — with primer/sealant while still wet.
questions.expl: Steel fasteners in aluminum are coated with primer and installed wet (sealant/primer still wet) to insulate the dissimilar-metal contact. [class notes] — 'installed wet' is standard AC 43.13-1B practice; -30B requires primer/insulation on the contact.

questions.q: When dissimilar metals make contact in the presence of moisture, the result is:
options: Oxidation (decomposition) of one or both metals
questions.short: Oxidation/decomposition of one or both metals.
questions.expl: Dissimilar-metal contact with moisture creates electrolytic action that oxidizes (decomposes) one or both metals — accelerated by salt water.

questions.q: Non-destructive testing (NDT/NDI/NDE) is best described as inspection that:
options: Determines material/structural integrity WITHOUT damaging or destroying the item
Always destroys the part to reveal hidden flaws
Replaces every form of visual inspection
Can only ever be done in a sealed laboratory
questions.short: Determines integrity WITHOUT damaging the item
questions.expl: NDT/NDI/NDE evaluates the material or structural integrity of a part without damaging or destroying it, so the part can stay in service.

questions.q: The six common NDT methods covered in the notes are:
options: Visual, magnetic, sonar, radar, infrared, laser
Visual, dye penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, eddy current, X-ray (radiographic)
Hardness, tensile, fatigue, bend, torsion, shear
Penetrant, paint, polish, prime, plate, passivate
questions.short: Visual, dye penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, eddy current, X-ray
questions.expl: The six common methods are visual, dye (liquid) penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, eddy current, and radiographic (X-ray).

questions.q: Which inspection method is the most basic and most commonly used?
questions.expl: Visual inspection is the most basic and most common NDT method - fast, cheap, portable, and needing minimum training.

questions.q: Which three NDT methods may an A&P mechanic typically perform?
questions.expl: Per the notes an A&P can perform visual, dye penetrant, and magnetic particle inspections.

questions.q: Advanced NDI such as ultrasonic and eddy current is usually performed by:
options: Anyone holding an A&P, no restriction
questions.expl: Ultrasonic and eddy current NDI is normally done by quality-control inspectors or specially trained technicians, not every A&P.

questions.q: Radiographic (X-ray) inspection on aircraft is performed by:
questions.expl: X-ray is performed only by certified organizations/contractors specializing in radiographic testing; A&Ps do not perform it.

questions.q: Visual inspections are commonly carried out at which points relative to flight?
questions.expl: Routine visual checks are made pre-flight, thru-flight (turnaround), and post-flight.

questions.q: Visual inspection is used to detect everyday defects such as:
options: Worn tires, clogged tubes/lines, and fluid leaks
questions.short: Worn tires, clogged tubes, and fluid leaks
questions.expl: Visual inspection catches obvious surface conditions - worn tires, clogged tubes/lines, and fluid leaks.

questions.q: Which set lists basic visual-inspection tools from the notes?
questions.expl: Basic visual tools are a flashlight, mirror, magnifying glass, and borescope.

questions.q: According to the notes, inspection lights should be:
options: Dim and colored to reduce glare
Bright - adequate white light, strong enough to clearly see surface defects
questions.expl: Inspection lights should be bright - adequate white light strong enough to clearly reveal surface defects.

questions.q: In the notes light-type list, which bulb gives about 70% more light than a standard incandescent?
questions.expl: The notes order bulbs standard incandescent -> halogen (~70% more light) -> xenon/HID (~100% more) -> LED (over 100% more).

questions.q: Which bulb type does the notes list as giving about 100% more light than a standard bulb?
questions.expl: Xenon/HID is listed at about 100% more light than a standard incandescent bulb.

questions.q: Which bulb type does the notes list as giving OVER 100% more light than standard bulbs?
questions.expl: LED is listed as providing over 100% more light than standard bulbs (and the longest service life of the group).

questions.q: To inspect the INTERIOR areas of objects, which two tools are used?
questions.expl: Interior areas (e.g., engine internals) are inspected with a borescope and a fiber-optic scope.

questions.q: A fiber-optic scope is advantageous because it:
options: Has a flexible probe that can bend around corners (length up to ~4 ft), often with a camera attached
Requires X-ray film to form an image
questions.short: Flexible probe that bends around corners (~4 ft, camera)
questions.expl: A fiber-optic scope has a flexible probe that bends around corners, reaches up to about 4 ft, and may have a camera attached.

questions.q: Per the notes, the approximate purchase cost of a borescope is around:
questions.expl: The notes give a borescope cost around $50,000 (with repair costs around $7,000), which is why special training is required.

questions.q: Per the notes, the approximate REPAIR cost figure tied to the borescope is around:
questions.expl: The notes list a borescope repair cost around $7,000 (versus ~$50,000 to purchase).

questions.q: Why does borescope use require special training?
options: It is expensive equipment that is costly to repair if mishandled
It only works on ferrous metal
questions.expl: The borescope is costly (around $50,000) and expensive to repair (around $7,000), so special training is required to use it.

questions.q: Dye (liquid) penetrant inspection locates cracks, porosity, and other faults that are:
options: Visible to the naked eye already
questions.expl: Penetrant finds only discontinuities OPEN to the surface; subsurface flaws require a different method.

questions.q: Dye penetrant inspection can be used on which materials?
options: Ferrous metals, non-ferrous metals, and non-metallic (plastics)
questions.expl: Penetrant works on ferrous and non-ferrous metals and on non-metallic materials such as plastics (nonporous surfaces).

questions.q: The dye penetrates which kind of fault, and is then made visible by what?
options: Surface faults; made visible by the developer
Subsurface faults; made visible by X-ray film
Internal voids; made visible by a magnet
Magnetic faults; made visible by iron powder
questions.short: Surface faults; made visible by developer
questions.expl: The dye penetrates surface (surface-breaking) faults and is then made visible against the developer.

questions.q: The two types of dye penetrant used on aircraft are:
questions.expl: Aircraft use two penetrant types: visible (red, color-contrast) read in white light, and fluorescent, read under UV/black light.

questions.q: The VISIBLE dye penetrant type is seen how?
options: As black shadows on X-ray film
As red indications against a white developer in normal white light
questions.short: Red against white developer in white light
questions.expl: Visible (color-contrast) penetrant shows red indications against the white developer, viewed in ordinary visible white light.

questions.q: The FLUORESCENT dye penetrant type requires what to read it?
questions.expl: Fluorescent penetrant glows and must be read under ultraviolet (black) light.

questions.q: The physical action that draws penetrant INTO a surface-breaking flaw is:
questions.expl: Capillary attraction (capillary action) draws the liquid penetrant into any fault that reaches the surface.

questions.q: "Dwell time" in penetrant inspection is the time required for the:
options: Penetrant to soak into and fill the discontinuity
questions.short: Penetrant to soak into the discontinuity
questions.expl: Dwell (penetration) time lets the penetrant enter and fill the flaw before excess is washed off.

questions.q: After sufficient dwell time, the next steps are to:
options: Magnetize the part, then apply iron powder
X-ray the part, then degauss it
Wash off the excess penetrant, then apply developer
Heat the part, then quench it
questions.short: Wash off excess penetrant, then apply developer
questions.expl: After dwell, the excess penetrant is washed off and developer is applied to draw the trapped penetrant back out.

questions.q: Tight or narrow cracks generally require what change to dwell time?
questions.expl: Small/tight cracks need a longer dwell so penetrant has time to enter; but excess heat evaporates the penetrant.

questions.q: Heating the part to speed penetration is risky because:
questions.expl: Heat decreases needed dwell time but too much heat evaporates the penetrant before it can enter the flaw.

questions.q: The developer reveals flaws by what action?
options: Forward capillary action drawing penetrant deeper
Reverse capillary action ("bleed-out") drawing penetrant back out of the discontinuity
questions.expl: The developer is a blotter producing reverse capillary action (bleed-out), drawing trapped penetrant back out to form visible indications.

questions.q: If a part is NOT thoroughly washed before the developer, the indication becomes:
options: Fuzzy / blurred from excess background penetrant
questions.expl: Inadequate washing leaves background penetrant that makes the indication fuzzy/blurred instead of sharp.

questions.q: With RED (visible) dye penetrant, fault types appear as:
options: Red lines against the white developer in visible white light
questions.short: Red lines against white developer (white light)
questions.expl: Visible penetrant produces red lines against the white developer, read in ordinary white light.

questions.q: With FLUORESCENT penetrant, fault types appear as:
options: Glowing (greenish-yellow) fluorescent lines under black/UV light
questions.expl: Fluorescent penetrant produces glowing greenish-yellow lines visible under black (UV) light.

questions.q: Before penetrant inspection you must NOT clean the part with which methods?
options: Abrasive blasting, scraping, or heavy wire brushing
questions.expl: Do NOT use mechanical methods (abrasive blasting, scraping, wire brushing) - they smear/peen metal over the flaw and hide it.

questions.q: Mechanical cleaning is avoided before penetrant inspection because it:
options: Smears/peens metal over the flaw, sealing or reducing the opening so penetrant cannot enter
questions.short: Smears metal over the flaw, sealing the opening
questions.expl: Mechanical methods can peen/smear metal across the defect, sealing or narrowing the opening so penetrant cannot enter; etching reopens such smeared openings.

questions.q: Acceptable methods to PREPARE a part for penetrant (remove grease/dirt/oil) include:
options: Heating until the oil burns off
A petroleum-based solvent or detergent, then rinse and dry
questions.short: Petroleum solvent/detergent, then rinse and dry
questions.expl: Preparation removes grease/dirt/oil with a petroleum-based solvent or detergent, then the part is rinsed and dried - no mechanical methods.

questions.q: Why must petroleum-based cleaning products NOT be used on oxygen equipment?
options: Petroleum and oxygen can cause an explosion
questions.expl: WARNING: petroleum products must not contact oxygen equipment because petroleum and oxygen can cause an explosion.

questions.q: Penetrant is APPLIED to the part by:
options: Immersing, swabbing, or spraying so the surface is fully covered
questions.expl: Penetrant is applied by immersing, swabbing, or spraying, making sure the surface is fully covered.

questions.q: When spraying to wash off water-soluble penetrant, the notes specify roughly:
questions.expl: Water-soluble penetrant is flushed off using about 30-40 psi at roughly a 45-degree spray angle.

questions.q: Which psi range does the notes give for the penetrant water flush?
questions.expl: The notes specify a 30-40 psi water flush (at about 45 degrees).

questions.q: Which spray ANGLE does the notes give for the penetrant water flush?
questions.expl: The notes specify about a 45-degree spray angle for the 30-40 psi penetrant flush.

questions.q: With POST-EMULSIFYING penetrant, what extra step is required before washing?
options: Apply an emulsifier so the penetrant can be water-washed
Heat the part to 100 deg F
questions.expl: Post-emulsifying penetrant is not directly water-washable; an emulsifier is applied first so the excess can then be washed off.

questions.q: To remove SOLVENT-removable penetrant, you should:
options: Spray solvent directly into the flaw
Wipe with an absorbent towel, then wipe again with a solvent-dampened towel
Submerge the part in solvent for an hour
questions.short: Wipe with absorbent towel, then solvent towel
questions.expl: Solvent-removable excess is wiped off with an absorbent towel first, then a towel dampened with solvent - never flooding solvent over the surface.

questions.q: The three kinds of developer used in penetrant inspection are:
questions.expl: Developers are dry, wet, and non-aqueous.

questions.q: A DRY developer is applied how, and excess removed how?
options: As loose powder (bin dip for small parts, brush/powder gun for large parts); excess removed with low-pressure air
Sprayed from a solvent can only
Painted on with a brush dipped in oil
questions.short: Loose powder; excess blown off with low-pressure air
questions.expl: Dry developer is a loose powder - small parts dipped in a bin or powder-gunned; large parts brushed or powder-gunned; excess removed with low-pressure air.

questions.q: A WET developer is:
options: A chalk powder suspended in solvent in a spray can
A white powder mixed with water, then air-dried on the part
A loose dry powder blown on
questions.short: White powder mixed with water, air-dried
questions.expl: Wet developer is a white powder mixed with water; it is applied and then air-dried on the part.

questions.q: A NON-AQUEOUS developer is:
options: A dry powder dipped from a bin
A powder mixed only with water
A chalk powder suspended in a solvent, applied from a spray can
questions.short: Chalk powder in solvent, from a spray can
questions.expl: Non-aqueous developer is a chalk powder suspended in a solvent and applied from a spray can.

questions.q: After interpreting the penetrant indications, the developer is removed by:
options: Water rinse / detergent / mechanical means, then drying the part
questions.expl: After inspection the developer is removed by water rinse, detergent, or mechanical means, and the part is dried.

questions.q: If NO defect is found during penetrant inspection, the part is:
questions.expl: A part with no defect is returned to service or reinstalled (with proper documentation/part tag).

questions.q: When a DEFECT is found, the part is handled by:
options: Attaching a part tag (different tags identify good vs. bad parts)
questions.short: Attaching a part tag (good vs. bad)
questions.expl: A defective part gets a part tag; different tags distinguish good (serviceable) from bad (rejected) parts.

questions.q: The Group 1 VISIBLE penetrant chemicals named in the notes are:
options: Zyglo ZL-27A penetrant and ZP-9F developer
Spotcheck SKC-S cleaner, SKL-SP1 penetrant, SKD-S2 developer
questions.short: SKC-S cleaner, SKL-SP1 penetrant, SKD-S2 developer
questions.expl: Group 1 (visible) set: Spotcheck SKC-S cleaner, SKL-SP1 penetrant, and SKD-S2 developer.

questions.q: The Group 2 FLUORESCENT penetrant chemicals named in the notes are:
options: Spotcheck SKC-S cleaner, Zyglo ZL-27A penetrant, Zyglo ZP-9F developer
questions.short: SKC-S cleaner, Zyglo ZL-27A, Zyglo ZP-9F
questions.expl: Group 2 (fluorescent) set: Spotcheck SKC-S cleaner, Zyglo ZL-27A penetrant, and Zyglo ZP-9F developer.

questions.q: If chemicals from DIFFERENT penetrant groups (Group 1 and Group 2) are mixed, the result is:
questions.expl: Mixing chemicals across groups gives false, unreliable results; keep the cleaner/penetrant/developer system matched.

questions.q: Fluorescent penetrant inspection requires what environment?
options: A UV (black) light and a darkened room
questions.short: UV (black) light and a dark room
questions.expl: Fluorescent penetrant must be read under a UV/black light in a darkened area so the glow is visible.

questions.q: The notes give the black-light UV lamp rating as approximately:
options: 28-100 V UV lamp operating on 115 V AC
questions.short: 28-100 V UV lamp on 115 V AC
questions.expl: The notes give a UV lamp of about 28-100 V operating from 115 V AC.

questions.q: How long must the black light warm up before use, per the notes?
questions.expl: The notes specify a black-light warm-up of about 5-10 minutes.

questions.q: A black light must have which condition to work properly?
options: A blocked filter port for safety
No unblocked filter port at all
questions.expl: The black-light filter/port must not be blocked; an obstructed port reduces output and gives poor indications.

questions.q: If a black light is turned OFF, what must happen before relighting it?
options: It must cool down before relighting
It must be recharged for an hour
questions.expl: A black light that is turned off must be allowed to cool before it can be relit.

questions.q: In the fluorescent (Zyglo) sequence, the penetrant used and its dwell are:
options: Zyglo ZL-27A penetrant, about a 10-minute dwell
questions.expl: The fluorescent set uses Zyglo ZL-27A penetrant with about a 10-minute dwell.

questions.q: In the fluorescent process, excess penetrant should be removed with:
questions.short: A clean cloth, NOT automotive rags
questions.expl: Excess fluorescent penetrant is wiped with a clean cloth - not automotive rags, which can leave contaminants/fluorescing residue.

questions.q: Bright greenish-yellow fluorescent INDICATIONS most directly reveal:
options: Cracks, laps, forging bursts, and cold shuts
questions.short: Cracks, laps, forging bursts, cold shuts
questions.expl: Bright greenish-yellow lines indicate cracks, laps, forging bursts, and cold shuts.

questions.q: In fluorescent penetrant, porosity, shrinkage, lack of bond, and leaks appear as:
options: Dots / bright (rounded) areas rather than lines
questions.expl: Porosity, shrinkage, lack of bond, and leaks show up as dots or bright areas rather than line-type indications.

questions.q: Compared with the red (visible) process, the fluorescent process adds which requirement?
questions.expl: The fluorescent steps are essentially the same as the red process except that a black (UV) light is required to read the indications.

questions.q: NDT electronic inspection methods are used to locate faults on the:
questions.expl: Electronic inspection (eddy current, ultrasonic, radiographic) locates faults on the interior of a part.

questions.q: The three NDT electronic-inspection methods are:
questions.expl: The electronic-inspection methods are eddy current, ultrasonic, and radiographic.

questions.q: By induction theory, moving a magnet inside a coil will:
questions.expl: A magnet moved inside a coil induces electricity (a current) in that coil - the basis of induction.

questions.q: Passing AC through a coil produces:
questions.expl: AC through a coil creates a moving/changing magnetic field.

questions.q: When that changing magnetic field crosses another coil/conductor, it causes:
options: Electrical current to flow in that conductor
questions.short: Current to flow in the other conductor
questions.expl: A field crossing another coil/conductor induces an electrical current to flow in it.

questions.q: Eddy currents are best described as:
options: Loops of induced current flowing in closed loops, perpendicular to the magnetic field
Iron particles gathered at a flaw
questions.short: Closed loops of induced current, perpendicular to the field
questions.expl: Eddy currents are loops of induced current flowing in closed loops, oriented perpendicular to the magnetic field that produced them.

questions.q: Eddy current testing is based on:
questions.expl: Eddy current testing is based on electromagnetic induction - an AC probe induces eddy currents whose changes reveal flaws.

questions.q: Eddy current inspection can examine:
options: Only the inside of castings via film
Both surface AND subsurface areas, very quickly
questions.expl: Eddy current can examine surface and subsurface areas very quickly.

questions.q: An advantage of eddy current is that it can inspect a part:
options: Only after the part is X-rayed
Without removing surface coatings such as primer, paint, or anodize
questions.expl: Eddy current can often inspect through surface coatings (primer/paint/anodized) without removing them.

questions.q: Eddy current is LIMITED to materials that:
options: Conduct electricity (it cannot be used on plastics)
questions.expl: Eddy current works only on electrically conductive materials; it cannot inspect plastics/non-conductors.

questions.q: Which list correctly gives the properties that affect eddy current response?
options: Conductivity, permeability, mass, and presence of voids/faults
Capillary action, dwell, developer, and wash
questions.expl: Eddy response is affected by conductivity, permeability, mass, and the presence of voids/faults.

questions.q: In an eddy current probe, the AC produces what at the probe tip?
questions.expl: The probe AC generates an oscillating magnetic field; mutual inductance with the part lets flaws alter the impedance read by the instrument.

questions.q: Defects or thickness changes are sensed in eddy current because they alter the:
options: Impedance seen by the probe coil
questions.expl: Thickness changes and defects alter the impedance of the probe coil, which the instrument reads as a change.

questions.q: Conductivity (as used in eddy current) is the ability of a material to:
questions.expl: Conductivity is the ability to conduct current; it helps differentiate alloy, grain, heat-treat, and tensile condition, set against a null reference.

questions.q: Conductivity differences let eddy current differentiate among:
options: Alloy, grain, heat-treat condition, and tensile strength
questions.expl: Because conductivity changes with them, eddy current can differentiate alloy, grain, heat-treat condition, and tensile strength against a null reference.

questions.q: Permeability, as it affects eddy current, is the ability of a material to:
questions.expl: Permeability is the ability of a material to accept magnetic lines of flux.

questions.q: Mass, as it affects eddy current, refers to:
options: The weight of the probe handle
The amount of material the probe moves over (more mass changes the reading; corrosion/thickness loss shows up)
questions.short: Amount of material the probe passes over
questions.expl: More mass the probe moves over changes the reading, so corrosion and thickness changes (mass loss) are detectable.

questions.q: The ABSOLUTE eddy current method works by:
options: Sending current to induce a given amount, zeroed by a bridge at a reference
questions.short: Induced amount zeroed by a bridge at a reference
questions.expl: The absolute method induces a set amount of current and is zeroed by a bridge circuit at a reference standard.

questions.q: The COMPARISON eddy current method works by:
options: Using a single coil with no reference
Using a double-coil probe that compares the part to a known reference
questions.short: Double-coil probe compares to a reference
questions.expl: The comparison method uses a double-coil probe that compares the test part against a known reference standard.

questions.q: Ultrasonic inspection introduces what into the material to find flaws?
questions.expl: Ultrasonic testers use high-frequency sound waves; reflections from surfaces and flaws reveal discontinuities.

questions.q: Per the notes, ultrasonic is the ONLY NDT method usable on which group?
options: Plastics, ceramics, composites, and most metals
questions.expl: The notes state ultrasonic is the only NDT method usable on plastics, ceramics, composites, and most metals.

questions.q: Ultrasonic is valued for detecting:
options: Minute cracks and voids too small for X-ray
questions.short: Minute cracks/voids too small for X-ray
questions.expl: Ultrasonic detects minute cracks and voids that are too small for radiographic (X-ray) detection.

questions.q: Of the two basic ultrasonic methods, which is done in shops and which can be done anywhere?
options: Immersion = done in shops; Contact = can be done anywhere
questions.expl: Immersion testing (part submerged in a tank) is a shop method; contact testing is portable and can be done anywhere.

questions.q: The three ultrasonic CONTACT systems are:
questions.expl: The three contact systems are pulse echo, through transmission, and resonance.

questions.q: The PULSE-ECHO ultrasonic system uses:
options: One transducer that both generates and receives the sound; good for small defects
questions.expl: Pulse echo uses a single transducer to both generate and receive the sound pulse; it is sensitive to small defects.

questions.q: The THROUGH-TRANSMISSION ultrasonic system uses:
options: Two transducers on opposite sides; less sensitive to small defects
questions.short: Two transducers, opposite sides (less sensitive to small defects)
questions.expl: Through transmission uses two transducers on opposite sides of the part; it is less sensitive to small defects than pulse echo.

questions.q: The RESONANCE ultrasonic system is characterized by:
options: Continuously varying the frequency, used for thickness measurement (like a micrometer)
questions.expl: Resonance continuously varies the frequency and is used for thickness measurement, acting much like a micrometer.

questions.q: Radiographic (X-ray) inspection requires:
options: Special equipment/certifications, with the radiation area roped off and warnings posted
questions.short: Special equipment/certs; area roped off + warnings
questions.expl: Radiography needs special equipment and certifications; the radiation area is roped off and posted with warnings.

questions.q: Magnetic particle inspection (MPI) detects flaws that are:
options: Surface and slightly subsurface, in ferromagnetic materials
questions.expl: MPI reveals surface and slightly subsurface discontinuities in ferromagnetic (iron/nickel/cobalt and some alloys) materials.

questions.q: In MPI, what forms at the edges of a crack to reveal it?
options: Magnetic poles, where iron particles gather to form an indication
questions.short: Magnetic poles; iron particles gather there
questions.expl: A magnetic field is introduced; magnetic poles form at the edges of cracks, and iron particles gather there to form a visible indication.

questions.q: MPI indications are made visible by which lighting?
questions.expl: Indications are made visible under ultraviolet light (with fluorescent media) or under visible light (with color media).

questions.q: In NDT terminology, a flaw is called a:
questions.expl: A flaw is a discontinuity - a disruption in the part normal physical/structural continuity.

questions.q: MPI is capable of detecting which discontinuities?
options: Cracks, splits, seams, voids, cold shuts, and inclusions
questions.short: Cracks, splits, seams, voids, cold shuts, inclusions
questions.expl: MPI detects cracks, splits, seams, voids, cold shuts, and inclusions in ferromagnetic parts.

questions.q: Typical aircraft parts inspected by MPI include:
options: Landing gear, bolts, and high-stress steel supports
questions.short: Landing gear, bolts, high-stress steel supports
questions.expl: MPI is used on landing gear, bolts, and high-stress steel structural supports.

questions.q: MPI can ONLY be used on:
questions.expl: MPI works only on ferrous/ferromagnetic materials; non-magnetic materials cannot be inspected this way.

questions.q: For a flaw to be detected by MPI, the magnetic flux lines must run:
questions.expl: Flux must be perpendicular to the flaw; faults running parallel to the flux are not detected.

questions.q: Faults that run PARALLEL to the magnetic flux are:
questions.expl: Because flux must be perpendicular to a flaw, faults running parallel to the flux are not detected - hence two magnetizing directions.

questions.q: Because flaws can lie in any direction, the part is magnetized in:
questions.expl: To catch flaws in any orientation, the part is magnetized both longitudinally and circularly.

questions.q: Good MPI media (the iron particles) must be:
options: Ferromagnetic, finely divided iron oxides with high permeability and low retentivity
questions.short: High permeability, low retentivity iron oxides
questions.expl: The media must be ferromagnetic, finely divided iron oxides with high permeability and low retentivity (so they move freely and do not stay magnetized).

questions.q: The DRY MPI method is best suited to:
questions.expl: Dry media is used under field conditions; particles may be gray/black/red or fluorescent.

questions.q: Dry MPI media is applied using:
options: A hand shaker, spray bulb, or powder gun
questions.short: Hand shaker, spray bulb, or powder gun
questions.expl: Dry media is applied with a hand shaker, spray bulb, or powder gun in field conditions.

questions.q: The WET MPI method uses particles that are:
options: Suspended in a liquid bath (stationary equipment) with continuous agitation
Blown on dry from a gun
questions.short: Suspended in a liquid bath, continuous agitation
questions.expl: Wet method flows particles suspended in a bath on stationary equipment, with continuous agitation to keep them suspended.

questions.q: RESIDUAL magnetism MPI means the magnetizing force is:
options: Removed BEFORE applying particles/inspecting (relies on retained field)
questions.expl: Residual method removes the magnetizing force before testing; it relies on the field retained by hardened/heat-treated steel parts.

questions.q: The residual MPI method is typically used on:
options: Heat-treated (hardened) steel parts that retain a field
questions.expl: Residual magnetism is used on heat-treated/hardened steel parts because they retain enough magnetism after the force is removed.

questions.q: CONTINUOUS magnetism MPI keeps the magnetizing force:
options: Applied continuously during testing (greater subsurface sensitivity)
questions.expl: Continuous method keeps the magnetizing force applied during testing, giving greater sensitivity to subsurface flaws.

questions.q: In a Magnaflux machine, which current MAGNETIZES vs. DEMAGNETIZES the part?
questions.expl: Per the notes, in the Magnaflux machine DC magnetizes the part and AC demagnetizes (degausses) it.

questions.q: The two methods of magnetizing a part in MPI are:
options: Passing current through a coil the part sits in (DC coil), or passing current through the part via copper clamp electrodes/pads
questions.short: DC coil pass-through, or current through the part via clamps
questions.expl: A part is magnetized by a DC coil it passes through, or by passing current through the part itself using copper clamp electrodes/pads.

questions.q: The magnetic field indicator (pie gauge) is used to:
options: Check the amount of residual magnetic flux in the part
questions.expl: The magnetic field indicator (pie gauge) checks the amount of residual magnetic flux remaining in the part.

questions.q: After MPI, parts must be returned to a usable state by:
questions.expl: After inspection, parts are AC-demagnetized (degaussed) so residual magnetism does not attract debris or disturb instruments.

questions.q: LONGITUDINAL magnetization is produced by:
options: Placing the part inside a coil and passing current through the coil
Passing current through the part itself
questions.short: Part in a coil; current through the coil
questions.expl: Longitudinal magnetization places the part inside a coil; current through the coil runs the flux lengthwise through the part.

questions.q: CIRCULAR magnetization is produced by:
options: Placing the part in a coil
Passing current through the part (between heads, or via a central conductive rod; flat objects use test probes)
questions.expl: Circular magnetization passes current through the part itself - between contact heads, through a central rod, or via test probes on flat objects.

questions.q: In the magnetizing-current summary, which current MAGNETIZES (and at what range)?
questions.expl: Direct current (DC) at about 110-440 V is used to magnetize the part.

questions.q: In the magnetizing-current summary, which current DEMAGNETIZES the part?
questions.expl: Alternating current (AC) degausses/demagnetizes the part; reversing (decaying) DC can also be used.

questions.q: A hand-held YOKE for portable field MPI uses:
options: Dry media plus a magnetic field, with its legs placed across the flaw
questions.short: Dry media + magnetic field, legs across the flaw
questions.expl: The portable yoke applies a magnetic field with dry media; its legs straddle the flaw to drive flux across it.

questions.q: With a yoke, the magnetic field indicator is placed how relative to the principal direction?
options: At 90 degrees to the principal field direction
questions.expl: The magnetic field indicator is placed at 90 degrees to the principal direction, and the part is checked in two directions.

questions.q: A FATIGUE crack indication in MPI typically appears as:
questions.expl: Fatigue cracks show up as sharp, clear, uniform indications.

questions.q: A HEAT-TREAT crack indication in MPI typically appears as:
questions.expl: Heat-treat cracks appear short, jagged, and less clear than fatigue cracks.

questions.q: A SHRINK-crack indication in MPI typically appears as:
questions.expl: Shrink cracks show jagged indications that are close together.

questions.q: A SEAM indication in MPI typically appears as:
questions.expl: Seams appear as symmetrical, fine, sharp lines.

questions.q: A HAIRLINE indication in MPI is described as:
questions.expl: Hairline indications look like very fine, closely spaced seams.

questions.q: INCLUSION indications in MPI typically appear as:
options: Straight intermittent / fuzzy lines of variable size
questions.expl: Inclusions show as straight intermittent or fuzzy indications of variable size.

questions.q: Test bars with KNOWN flaws are used in MPI to:
options: Verify the system/process is performing correctly
questions.expl: Test bars containing known flaws are used to verify that the MPI system and process are performing correctly.

questions.q: The correct ORDER of the MPI procedure is:
options: Magnetize, pre-clean, demagnetize, apply media, interpret, clean, disposition
Pre-clean, introduce magnetic field, apply media, interpret, demagnetize, clean, disposition
Apply media, magnetize, interpret, pre-clean, clean, demagnetize, disposition
Interpret, magnetize, apply media, pre-clean, clean, disposition, demagnetize
questions.short: Pre-clean > magnetize > media > interpret > demag > clean > disposition
questions.expl: MPI order: (1) pre-clean, (2) introduce the magnetic field, (3) apply media, (4) interpret, (5) demagnetize, (6) clean, (7) disposition of the part.

questions.q: Which is the FIRST step of the MPI procedure?
questions.expl: Step 1 of MPI is pre-cleaning the part before any magnetic field is introduced.

questions.q: After interpreting the indications, the next two MPI steps are:
questions.expl: After interpretation come demagnetizing (step 5) and cleaning (step 6), before final disposition (step 7).

questions.q: The final (7th) step of the MPI procedure is:
questions.expl: The last MPI step is disposition of the part (serviceable vs. rejected, with the proper tag).

questions.q: During fluorescent UV inspection in the field, tarps are used to:
options: Block ambient light so the fluorescent glow is visible
questions.short: Block ambient light for UV reading
questions.expl: Tarps block ambient light so the fluorescent indications can be seen clearly under UV.

questions.q: Which two methods detect only SURFACE discontinuities?
questions.expl: Visual and dye penetrant detect surface discontinuities only; other methods are needed for subsurface flaws.

questions.q: Which two methods detect BOTH surface and subsurface discontinuities?
questions.expl: Magnetic particle and eddy current both detect surface AND subsurface flaws; visual/penetrant are surface-only.

questions.q: Which method is limited to FERROUS materials, and which is limited to CONDUCTIVE materials?
options: Magnetic particle is ferrous-only; eddy current is conductive-only
Penetrant is ferrous-only; ultrasonic is conductive-only
Eddy current is ferrous-only; magnetic particle is conductive-only
questions.expl: Magnetic particle works only on ferromagnetic (ferrous) materials; eddy current works only on electrically conductive materials.

questions.q: Which method can find MINUTE cracks too small for X-ray and also works on plastics/ceramics/composites?
questions.expl: Ultrasonic finds minute cracks too small for X-ray and is the only listed method usable on plastics, ceramics, composites, and most metals.

questions.q: Which statement correctly pairs an induction-based vs. sound-based method?
options: Eddy current is sound-based; ultrasonic is induction-based
Eddy current is based on electromagnetic induction; ultrasonic is based on high-frequency sound waves
Both are based on capillary action
questions.expl: Eddy current is based on electromagnetic induction; ultrasonic is based on high-frequency sound waves.

questions.q: Aircraft operating in a corrosive environment require a more stringent corrosion preventive/control program than aircraft in a dry environment. Which is one of the corrosive environments AC 43-4B identifies?
questions.expl: AC 43-4B lists salt air, heavy atmospheric industrial pollution, and over-water (over-the-ocean) operations as the corrosive environments. Dry/desert air is far less corrosive.

questions.q: Per AC 43-4B, which over-the-surface operation is named as a corrosive environment requiring a stricter corrosion-control program?
questions.expl: The three corrosive environments named are salt air, heavy atmospheric industrial pollution, and over-water (over-the-ocean) operations. Marine salt is the chief driver.

questions.q: Heavy atmospheric INDUSTRIAL pollution is a corrosive environment chiefly because it deposits:
options: Acidic, salt- and sulfur-bearing contaminants that act as electrolytes
questions.short: Acidic/sulfur-bearing contaminants that act as electrolytes.
questions.expl: Industrial pollution deposits acidic and sulfur-bearing contaminants onto the surface; these act as corrosive agents/electrolytes. The driver is chemical, not abrasion or UV.

questions.q: AC 43-4B names THREE corrosive environments. They are salt air, over-water operations, and:
questions.expl: The complete list of corrosive environments in para. 1.8.1 is: salt air, heavy atmospheric industrial pollution, and over-water (over-the-ocean) operations.

questions.q: AC 43-4B lists factors that influence the rate of metal corrosion. Which of the following is one of those factors?
options: Heat treatment and grain direction of the metal
The altitude where the part was machined
questions.expl: Listed factors include type of metal; heat treatment and grain direction; presence of a dissimilar less-corrodible metal; temperature; presence of electrolytes; and more.

questions.q: Which is NOT one of the corrosion-rate factors listed in AC 43-4B?
options: Presence of electrolytes (salt water, battery fluids)
questions.expl: Para. 2.4.1 lists metal type, heat treatment/grain direction, dissimilar metals, temperature, electrolytes, oxygen availability, electrolyte concentration, anode/cathode area, biological organisms, mechanical stress, and time of exposure. Paint color is not a factor.

questions.q: The presence of a dissimilar, LESS corrodible metal in contact with a metal increases corrosion because it sets up:
questions.expl: A dissimilar, less-corrodible (more noble) metal in electrical contact through an electrolyte makes the active metal the anode, accelerating galvanic corrosion. This is one of the listed corrosion-rate factors.

questions.q: Which of the following corrosion-rate factors describes how long the metal has been exposed to the corrosive condition?
questions.expl: Among the factors in para. 2.4.1 is the time of exposure of the metal to the corrosive environment — longer exposure means more corrosion.

questions.q: Per AC 43-4B, the availability of which gas at the metal surface is one of the factors influencing corrosion rate?
questions.expl: Availability of oxygen is a listed corrosion-rate factor. Differences in oxygen availability drive oxygen concentration (crevice) cells.

questions.q: Which is a listed corrosion-rate factor relating to LIVING contamination on the surface?
questions.expl: Presence of biological organisms (microbial growth, e.g. in fuel tanks) is one of the factors in para. 2.4.1 that influences corrosion and its rate.

questions.q: Galvanic (dissimilar-metal) corrosion requires four conditions. Besides an anode and a cathode, the other two are:
options: An electrolyte and electrical contact between the metals
questions.expl: The four requirements are: an anode (more active metal), a cathode (less active metal), an electrolyte, and electrical contact between anode and cathode.

questions.q: In a galvanic couple, corrosion is RAPID and SEVERE when the surface area of the corroding (anodic) metal is:
options: Coated with a thick oxide film
questions.short: Smaller than the cathode (unfavorable area ratio).
questions.expl: If the anode area is SMALLER than the cathode, corrosion is rapid and severe. If the anode is LARGER than the cathode, the attack is slow and superficial.

questions.q: In a galvanic couple, corrosion is SLOW and SUPERFICIAL when the corroding (anodic) metal is:
questions.expl: When the corroding metal (anode) is larger than the less-active metal (cathode), the corrosion is slow and superficial — a favorable area ratio.

questions.q: A form of corrosion that first appears as a white or gray powdery deposit, similar to dust, blotching the surface — and reveals tiny holes when cleaned away — is:
questions.expl: AC 43-4B: pitting is the most common effect of corrosion on aluminum and magnesium alloys; first noticeable as a white/gray powdery deposit, and cleaning it reveals tiny pits or holes.

questions.q: Pitting corrosion is the MOST COMMON effect of corrosion on which metals?
questions.expl: Pitting is the most common effect of corrosion on aluminum and magnesium alloys. Steel shows reddish-brown rust, not the white/gray powder of pitting.

questions.q: After the powdery deposit of pitting corrosion is cleaned away, what is revealed in the surface?
questions.expl: When the white/gray powdery deposit of pitting is cleaned away, tiny pits or holes in the surface can be seen — the defining feature of pitting corrosion.

questions.q: Uniform surface (etch) corrosion attacks the metal surface:
options: Evenly/uniformly over a wide area, producing a general roughening or etched look
In a worm-like line under paint
questions.short: Evenly over a wide area (general roughening/etch).
questions.expl: Uniform surface corrosion is the most common type — a direct chemical or electrochemical attack spread evenly over the surface, leaving a roughened, etched, often frosted appearance.

questions.q: Concentration cell (crevice) corrosion develops where there is a difference in concentration of ions or oxygen. Oxygen concentration cells typically form under all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A freshly anodized, sealed surface with no deposits
Metallic or nonmetallic deposits on the surface
Faying surfaces such as riveted lap joints
Gaskets, wood, rubber and similar materials in contact with the metal
questions.expl: Oxygen cells form under deposits, faying/riveted lap joints, and gaskets/wood/rubber. A clean, properly sealed surface with no trapped material creates no oxygen-starved anodic site.

questions.q: Per the worksheet, oxygen concentration cells develop under which of these surface conditions?
options: Bare polished metal in dry air
Faying surfaces such as riveted lap joints
questions.short: Faying surfaces such as riveted lap joints.
questions.expl: The three oxygen-cell locations from para. 2.5.4.2 are: metallic/nonmetallic deposits; faying surfaces (riveted lap joints); and gaskets, wood, rubber and similar materials in contact with the metal.

questions.q: Active-passive cells (a concentration-cell variant) rapidly attack metals that rely on a tightly adhering passive film, such as:
options: Corrosion-resistant (stainless) steel under a dirt particle
questions.short: Corrosion-resistant (stainless) steel under a dirt particle.
questions.expl: Active-passive cells attack metals depending on a passive oxide film (e.g. CRES/stainless). A salt deposit or dirt particle breaks the film locally; that small active area becomes anodic and pits rapidly. (This is the source for the worksheet Q4 'fourth' location.)

questions.q: Corrosion that attacks along the grain boundaries of an alloy — where boundary and grain center act as anode and cathode — is called:
questions.expl: Intergranular corrosion attacks the grain boundaries, which differ chemically from the grain center. High-strength aluminum alloys 2014 and 7075 are especially susceptible if improperly heat-treated.

questions.q: High-strength aluminum alloys 2014 and 7075 become more susceptible to intergranular corrosion when they have been:
options: Improperly heat-treated and then exposed to a corrosive environment
questions.short: Improperly heat-treated, then exposed to corrosion.
questions.expl: AC 43-4B specifically calls out 2014 and 7075 as more susceptible to intergranular corrosion if improperly heat-treated and then exposed to a corrosive environment.

questions.q: An advanced form of intergranular corrosion in which surface grains are lifted/swelled by expanding corrosion products at the grain boundaries is:
questions.expl: Exfoliation is an advanced intergranular corrosion that lifts and swells surface grains (a flaky, layered look). It occurs on extruded, rolled, wrought, and forged high-strength aluminum/magnesium parts.

questions.q: A special form of oxygen concentration cell corrosion that occurs under an organic (paint) coating and is recognized by a worm-like trace beneath the paint film is:
questions.expl: Filiform is a special oxygen concentration / crevice corrosion under organic coatings, recognized by worm-like threads of corrosion product beneath the paint film.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion occurs when the atmospheric relative humidity is between:
questions.expl: AC 43-4B: filiform occurs when relative humidity is between 78 and 90 percent and the surface is slightly acidic. Storing below 70 percent RH helps prevent it.

questions.q: Filiform corrosion occurs at 78-90% relative humidity AND when the metal surface is:
questions.expl: Filiform requires relative humidity between 78 and 90 percent AND a slightly acidic surface; corrosion starts at breaks in the coating and moves underneath it.

questions.q: Which storage condition helps PREVENT filiform corrosion?
options: Leaving acidic contaminants on the surface
questions.expl: Prevention includes storing below 70 percent RH, using low-diffusion coatings, and washing off acidic contaminants. High humidity promotes filiform; bare metal is not the fix.

questions.q: Stress-corrosion cracking is best described as:
options: Intergranular cracking caused by combined stress and corrosion
questions.short: Intergranular cracking from combined stress + corrosion.
questions.expl: AC 43-4B: stress-corrosion cracking is an intergranular cracking of a metal caused by combined stress and corrosion. Stress may be internal (residual) or external (applied).

questions.q: Which process creates COMPRESSIVE surface stresses that help REDUCE stress-corrosion cracking?
questions.expl: Shot peening a metal surface creates compressive surface stresses that reduce stress-corrosion cracking and increase the threshold stress level.

questions.q: Abrasive wear at the interface of two highly-loaded surfaces held immobile but caused to rub by vibration — removing protective film and exposing fresh metal — is:
questions.expl: Fretting occurs where two highly-loaded surfaces rub due to vibration; the rubbing removes protective film, prevents oxide reformation, and can cause severe pitting and fatigue cracking.

questions.q: AC 43-4B identifies the MOST COMMON corrosive agents/media as:
options: Acids, alkalis, and salts (plus atmosphere and water)
questions.short: Acids, alkalis, and salts (plus atmosphere and water).
questions.expl: The most common corrosive agents are acids, alkalis, and salts; the atmosphere and water also act as corrosive agents. Worksheet Q5 lists all five.

questions.q: Besides acids, alkalis, and salts, which TWO also act as corrosive agents per AC 43-4B?
questions.expl: AC 43-4B: the atmosphere and water may act as corrosive agents as well, in addition to the most-common acids, alkalis, and salts.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the FIVE corrosive media on the worksheet (Q5)?
questions.expl: The five corrosive media for worksheet Q5 are: acids, alkalis, salts, the atmosphere, and water.

questions.q: The recommended corrosion-prevention treatment for ALUMINUM alloy parts generally consists of:
options: Anodize or conversion coating, then prime (and topcoat)
questions.short: Anodize or conversion coat, then prime/topcoat.
questions.expl: Aluminum is protected by anodizing or a chemical conversion coating, then primed and topcoated (some later aircraft use IVD aluminum plating). Bare aluminum is not the recommended protection.

questions.q: BRASS and BRONZE parts are typically protected against corrosion by:
options: Cadmium plating, zinc plating, IVD aluminum coating, or a conversion coating
questions.expl: AC 43-4B lists cadmium plating, zinc plating, IVD aluminum coating, or a conversion coating as the protective measures for brass and bronze.

questions.q: MAGNESIUM alloys are among the most chemically active aircraft metals and therefore:
options: Always require protective coatings (special chemical/electrochemical treatments and paint)
Are best protected by bare polishing
Should be cadmium-plated with a wire brush
questions.expl: Magnesium is highly active and ALWAYS requires protective coatings — special chemical and electrochemical treatments plus paint finishes.

questions.q: Anodizing is the most common surface treatment used to protect which metal?
questions.expl: Anodizing (an electrolytic oxide coating) is the most common surface treatment for non-clad aluminum. It is then primed/topcoated for full protection.

questions.q: Per AC 43-4B Table 3-1, MAGNESIUM is highly susceptible to which attack, and how does its corrosion appear?
options: Pitting; white, powdery, snow-like mounds and white spots
questions.short: Pitting; white powdery snow-like mounds/white spots.
questions.expl: Table 3-1 lists magnesium as highly susceptible to pitting, with white powdery snow-like mounds and white spots. Worksheet Q7's answer is 'pitting.'

questions.q: Magnesium is susceptible to what TYPE of corrosive attack (worksheet Q7)?
questions.expl: Magnesium, the most chemically active aircraft metal, is highly susceptible to pitting-type corrosion that appears as white powdery snow-like mounds and lifts the paint film.

questions.q: Per Table 3-1, how does corrosion on STAINLESS steels (300/400 series) typically appear (worksheet Q8)?
options: Classic red rust like carbon steel
A rough surface, sometimes with a uniform red, brown, or stain
questions.short: Rough surface; sometimes a uniform red, brown, or stain.
questions.expl: Stainless does NOT show classic red rust. Table 3-1 describes a rough surface, sometimes with a uniform red, brown, or stain. It is prone to crevice/pitting (marine) and intergranular (300 series).

questions.q: Per Table 3-1, the corrosion product appearance for ALUMINUM is:
questions.expl: Aluminum corrosion appears as white-to-gray powder. Reddish-brown is low-alloy steel rust; blue/blue-green is copper-based; green is nickel.

questions.q: Per Table 3-1, copper-based BRASS and BRONZE corrosion appears as:
options: No visible product below 700 deg F
questions.expl: Brass/bronze (copper alloys) show surface and intergranular corrosion appearing as a blue or blue-green powdery deposit.

questions.q: Per Table 3-1, low-alloy STEEL corrosion appears as:
questions.expl: Low-alloy steel shows the classic reddish-brown oxide (rust). This is distinct from aluminum's white-gray powder or copper's blue-green deposit.

questions.q: Per Table 3-1, TITANIUM produces 'no visible corrosion products at low temperature,' but colored surface oxides develop above:
questions.expl: Titanium is highly corrosion resistant; colored surface oxides develop above 700 deg F (370 deg C). Repeated contact with chlorinated solvents can degrade it at high temperature.

questions.q: Per Table 3-1, CADMIUM is described as 'used as sacrificial plating to protect steel,' and its corrosion ranges from a white powdery deposit to:
options: Brown or black mottling of the surface
questions.short: Brown or black mottling of the surface.
questions.expl: Cadmium gives uniform surface corrosion ranging from white powder to brown or black mottling. The mottling SIGNALS that sacrificial protection is working — do not remove it for appearance.

questions.q: WHEEL WELLS and landing gear are among the MOST corrosion-prone areas on an aircraft primarily because they:
options: Are always painted with the thinnest coatings
Are exposed to mud, water, salt, gravel and runway debris, with complex shapes hard to coat
questions.short: Runway debris exposure + complex shapes hard to coat.
questions.expl: Wheel wells/landing gear receive more punishment than any other area: mud, water, salt, gravel and debris thrown from runways, plus complicated shapes/fittings that make complete paint coverage difficult.

questions.q: Why is complete protective-paint coverage difficult on the landing gear / wheel well area (worksheet Q9)?
options: Their complicated shapes, assemblies, and fittings
The area is too hot to paint
Paint is banned there by regulation
questions.expl: Beyond debris exposure, the complicated shapes, assemblies, and fittings of the landing gear/wheel well make complete protective-coating coverage difficult — leaving bare spots for corrosion.

questions.q: ENGINE FRONTAL areas and cooling-air vents are corrosion-prone mainly because (worksheet Q10):
options: They are sealed from all airflow
Constant abrasion by dirt/gravel/rain removes protection while moist salt/pollutant-laden air flows over unpainted surfaces
questions.short: Abrasion strips protection; contaminated air flows over bare surfaces.
questions.expl: Constant abrasion by airborne dirt, gravel and rain strips protective surfaces, and unpainted areas (cooling fins, machined mounting bases) are continuously exposed to moist, salt- or pollutant-laden air.

questions.q: Exhaust trail areas are a known corrosion trouble spot mainly because exhaust deposits combine with moisture to form:
options: Mildly corrosive acids that attack the surface
questions.short: Mildly corrosive acids on the surface.
questions.expl: Exhaust gas deposits trap and hold other corrosive agents and, with moisture, can form mildly corrosive acids — making the exhaust trail a high-priority corrosion control point.

questions.q: Battery compartments and vents are corrosion-prone because the electrolyte and its fumes:
options: Spread corrosive agents to nearby metal and trapped areas
questions.short: Spread corrosive agents to nearby metal.
questions.expl: Battery acid (or alkaline) fumes and electrolyte spills corrode the battery box and nearby structure; vents can carry corrosive fumes downstream. Spills must be neutralized promptly.
questions.cite: AC 43-4B p. 4-29, para. 4.13.2.8 (cleaning agents); see also areas guidance

questions.q: A protective coating that corrodes ITSELF to protect the underlying base metal is called a:
questions.expl: Sacrificial coatings (cadmium, zinc, aluminum) corrode preferentially to protect the base metal. Non-sacrificial coatings protect by barrier action.

questions.q: Which group lists ONLY sacrificial protective coatings (worksheet Q11)?
questions.expl: Sacrificial coatings are cadmium, zinc, and aluminum. Hard chromium/nickel plating, conversion coatings, primers/sealants, and topcoats are non-sacrificial.

questions.q: Hard CHROMIUM plating and NICKEL plating are examples of:
questions.expl: Hard plating (chromium, nickel) protects by forming a hard barrier, not by sacrificing itself — so it is non-sacrificial. Cadmium/zinc/aluminum are the sacrificial coatings.

questions.q: Which of the following is a NON-sacrificial protective finish per worksheet Q11?
questions.expl: Non-sacrificial finishes include hard plating (chromium, nickel), chemical conversion coatings, sealant/primers, and topcoat. Cadmium, zinc, and aluminum are sacrificial.

questions.q: Primers, sealants, and the topcoat protect the base metal by:
options: Forming a barrier that keeps electrolytes off the metal (non-sacrificial)
questions.expl: Sealant/primers and topcoat are non-sacrificial: they shield the metal from moisture and electrolytes by barrier action, rather than corroding in its place.

questions.q: Which cleaning agent is correctly matched to neutralizing an electrolyte spill from a SULFURIC-ACID (lead-acid) battery?
questions.expl: Sodium bicarbonate neutralizes acidic (lead-acid/sulfuric) spills. Monobasic sodium phosphate and boric acid are for nickel-cadmium (alkaline) spills.

questions.q: Monobasic sodium phosphate and boric acid are used to neutralize electrolyte spills from which battery type?
questions.expl: Nickel-cadmium electrolyte is alkaline, so it is neutralized with an acidic agent (monobasic sodium phosphate and boric acid). Sodium bicarbonate handles acidic lead-acid spills.

questions.q: Per worksheet Q12, a MILD ABRASIVE used to polish out scratches in canopy/transparency materials is:
questions.expl: The miscellaneous cleaning agents in para. 4.13.2.8 include plastic polish (a mild abrasive to polish out scratches in canopy materials), alkaline chemicals, sodium bicarbonate, and monobasic sodium phosphate/boric acid.

questions.q: Per worksheet Q12, ALKALINE chemicals among the miscellaneous cleaning agents are used to:
questions.expl: Alkaline chemicals are listed as miscellaneous cleaning agents used to neutralize specific acidic soils on the aircraft surface.

questions.q: In addition to routine maintenance, an aircraft operating in a MODERATE corrosive environment should be inspected for corrosion every:
questions.expl: Suggested intervals: severe = 15 days, moderate = 45 days, mild = 90 days. The 45-day moderate interval is the worksheet Q13 answer and a classic exam number.

questions.q: Per AC 43-4B suggested intervals, an aircraft in a SEVERE corrosive environment should be inspected every:
questions.expl: Severe = 15 days, moderate = 45 days, mild = 90 days. Severe zones get the shortest interval.

questions.q: Per AC 43-4B suggested intervals, an aircraft in a MILD corrosive environment should be inspected every:
questions.expl: Mild zones = 90 days, moderate = 45 days, severe = 15 days. Milder environment, longer interval.

questions.q: Before performing a corrosion inspection, the aircraft should be (worksheet Q14):
questions.expl: AC 43-4B directs that the aircraft be washed prior to ANY inspection for corrosion, so contaminants do not hide developing corrosion. Worksheet Q14 answer = True.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the nondestructive inspection (NDI) methods listed in AC 43-4B?
questions.expl: NDI methods include visual/fiber-optic, liquid (dye) penetrant, magnetic particle, eddy current, X-ray (radiographic), ultrasonic, and acoustic emission. Tensile/hardness-file are destructive/mechanical.

questions.q: Which NDI method uses a dye drawn into surface-breaking flaws, then a developer to make the flaw visible, and is LIMITED to flaws open to the surface?
questions.expl: Liquid penetrant reveals only surface-breaking flaws (the dye must enter an opening). Eddy current and ultrasonic find some subsurface flaws; X-ray sees internal/volumetric defects.

questions.q: Which NDI method can ONLY be used on ferromagnetic (magnetizable) materials such as steel?
questions.expl: Magnetic particle inspection requires a ferromagnetic material; it cannot be used on aluminum or other nonmagnetic metals. Penetrant, eddy current, and ultrasonic work on nonmagnetic metals too.

questions.q: Which NDI method uses penetrating radiation to reveal internal (subsurface/volumetric) defects?
questions.expl: Radiographic (X-ray) inspection sees internal/volumetric defects, including hidden corrosion and inclusions. Penetrant and magnetic particle are limited to surface or near-surface flaws.

questions.q: Which NDI method uses high-frequency SOUND waves to detect subsurface flaws and measure remaining thickness?
questions.expl: Ultrasonic uses high-frequency sound to find subsurface flaws and measure remaining thickness (useful for hidden corrosion). Penetrant/magnetic particle are surface methods.

questions.q: Which inspection method uses a borescope/fiber-optic device to look inside areas the eye cannot directly reach (worksheet Q15 'A')?
questions.expl: Visual inspection, aided by fiber-optic borescopes, lets the inspector see hidden cavities and ducts. The worksheet Q15 list begins with visual/fiber-optic then the six AC methods.
questions.cite: AC 43-4B p. 5-12, para. 5.4.1.2 (fiber optics also p. 4-14, para. 4.11.6)

questions.q: Which NDI method induces eddy currents in a conductive part and detects flaws by changes in those currents (works on nonmagnetic metals like aluminum)?
questions.expl: Eddy current inspection works on conductive metals (including nonmagnetic aluminum) and can find surface and near-surface cracks and corrosion thinning.

questions.q: When mixing acid and water, the correct and safe procedure is to:
options: Never add water to acid - always add acid to water
Heat the acid first, then pour in water
questions.short: Add acid to water; never add water to acid.
questions.expl: Never add water to acid - the violent exothermic reaction can spatter acid. Always add acid slowly to water. Worksheet Q16(a) = True.

questions.q: When MIXING ALKALIS with water, AC 43-4B requires that the containers used:
options: Be made to withstand the chemical heat generated
questions.short: Be made to withstand the heat generated.
questions.expl: Mixing alkalis with water generates heat; use containers made to withstand the chemical heat generated. Worksheet Q16(b) = True.

questions.q: When using solvents, cleaners, strippers, etchants, or conversion coatings, AC 43-4B requires that you:
options: Work without gloves for better feel
Apply with bare hands and rinse afterward
questions.expl: Rubber or plastic gloves are required when handling solvents, cleaners, strippers, etchants, or conversion coatings. Worksheet Q16(d) = True.

questions.q: If a paint stripper, etchant, or conversion-coating material contacts your skin or clothing, you should:
options: Wash it from the body/skin/clothing immediately
Let it dry, then brush it off
questions.expl: Wash any paint stripper, etchant, or conversion coating material immediately from body, skin, or clothing - these are corrosive to tissue. Worksheet Q16(c) = True.

questions.q: Pits remaining after removal of corrosion products must be blended out when they are found in:
questions.expl: In critical and highly-stressed areas, remaining pits must be blended out to prevent stress risers that cause stress-corrosion cracking. Worksheet Q17 answer.

questions.q: When reworking a corrosion dish-out, AC 43-4B specifies blending the depression using a length-to-depth ratio of:
questions.expl: Rework depressions with a smoothly-blended dish-out using a length-to-depth ratio of 20:1. NOTE: AC 43-4B specifies only this 20:1 ratio; it does NOT state any separate 10:1 width ratio.

questions.q: Blending out remaining pits in critical/highly-stressed areas is required mainly to prevent:
options: Stress risers that lead to stress-corrosion cracking
questions.short: Stress risers that lead to stress-corrosion cracking.
questions.expl: Sharp pits act as stress risers (concentrators) and can initiate stress-corrosion cracking or fatigue cracks in loaded structure, so they are blended smooth.

questions.q: Why does AC 43-4B require a smoothly blended dish-out (20:1) rather than a sharp gouge when removing corrosion?
options: To make it look better only
To avoid creating a sharp stress concentration in the structure
questions.short: To avoid a sharp stress concentration.
questions.expl: A gradual 20:1 blended contour spreads load smoothly; an abrupt edge would concentrate stress and could initiate cracking. The ratio controls the smoothness of the cleanup.

questions.q: Before resorting to chemical testing, the PRIMARY reference used to identify an aircraft metal is the:
questions.expl: AC 43-4B: the primary method of identifying metal is the aircraft SRM. Chemical testing is used only after other methods are exhausted. Worksheet Q18 answer.

questions.q: Chemical testing to determine an aircraft metal's composition should be used:
options: Only when all other identification methods are exhausted
questions.short: Only when all other methods are exhausted.
questions.expl: The SRM is primary; chemical testing is a last resort used only when all other identification methods have been exhausted.

questions.q: Which of the following must NEVER be used to remove corrosion from an ALUMINUM surface?
options: Steel wool or a steel wire brush
questions.short: Steel wool or steel wire brush.
questions.expl: Steel wool, steel wire brushes, copper alloy brushes, and severe abrasives must never be used on aluminum - they embed dissimilar particles that start galvanic corrosion. Worksheet Q19.

questions.q: Per worksheet Q19, which of these is on the 'never use on aluminum' list?
questions.expl: The four prohibited items for aluminum are: steel wool, steel wire brushes, copper alloy brushes, and severe abrasive materials.

questions.q: Using a steel wire brush on aluminum is harmful chiefly because it:
options: Embeds steel particles that set up dissimilar-metal (galvanic) corrosion
questions.expl: Steel (or copper) particles embedded in aluminum become future corrosion sites by forming galvanic couples. That is why steel/copper brushes and steel wool are prohibited on aluminum.

questions.q: Which materials are ACCEPTABLE for removing corrosion from aluminum?
options: Copper alloy brushes and crocus cloth
Aluminum wool, fiber (Tampico) bristle brushes, and mild abrasives
questions.short: Aluminum wool, fiber bristle brushes, mild abrasives.
questions.expl: Acceptable choices are aluminum wool, fiber (Tampico) bristle brushes, and mild abrasives. Steel/copper brushes, steel wool, and severe abrasives are prohibited on aluminum.

questions.q: Austenitic (300-series) stainless steels resist corrosion because of a tightly adhering oxide (passive) film. If that film is broken, re-passivation does NOT restore it, and serviceability is affected, AC 43-4B says you must:
questions.expl: If corrosion occurs on austenitic steel and the part's structural integrity or serviceability is affected (film not restored by re-passivation), the part must be removed. Worksheet Q20.

questions.q: Austenitic 300-series stainless steels are protected from corrosion primarily by:
options: A tightly adhering passive oxide film
questions.short: A tightly adhering passive oxide film.
questions.expl: Non-magnetic austenitic (300-series) stainless relies on a tight, self-healing passive oxide film. If that film is broken and cannot re-passivate, active corrosion can begin.

questions.q: Stainless steel that shows a brown/tea-colored stain on the surface is best assessed as:
options: A rough/stained surface — evidence of corrosion attack on the passive film
questions.short: Rough/stained surface = corrosion of the passive film.
questions.expl: Stainless does not show classic red rust; tea-staining/brown discoloration indicates the passive film has been attacked (crevice/pitting). Table 3-1 describes a rough surface, sometimes red/brown/stained.

questions.q: Cadmium- and zinc-plated parts must NEVER have their coatings cleaned with a:
questions.expl: AC 43-4B: under no condition should a cadmium (or zinc) coating be cleaned with a wire brush - it would remove the thin sacrificial layer. Worksheet Q21.

questions.q: Why must a cadmium-plated part never be wire-brushed?
options: The wire brush removes the thin sacrificial cadmium layer that protects the steel
questions.short: It removes the sacrificial cadmium layer.
questions.expl: The cadmium layer is thin and sacrificial. A wire brush would strip it, exposing the base steel to corrosion. Clean with non-abrasive methods instead.

questions.q: Properly functioning cadmium plating may show white-to-brown-to-black MOTTLING on its surface. This mottling indicates (worksheet Q22):
options: The plating has failed and must be stripped
Sacrificial protection is taking place - do not remove it for appearance
questions.short: Sacrificial protection working - leave it alone.
questions.expl: Mottling (white to brown to black) shows the cadmium is sacrificially protecting the base metal; under no condition should it be removed merely for appearance.

questions.q: Cadmium-plate mottling ranges in color from white to:
questions.expl: The sacrificial-protection mottling on cadmium plate ranges from white through brown to black spots. It signals the coating is working and should not be removed for appearance.

questions.q: Before applying soap and water to a plastic (transparent) surface, you should FIRST (worksheet Q23):
options: Flush it with fresh water to dissolve salt deposits and wash away dust
Polish it with a dry cloth
questions.expl: Flush plastic surfaces with fresh water first to dissolve salt and remove grit, then wash with soap and water, preferably by hand. Dry cloths and solvents scratch or craze plastic.

questions.q: To remove oil and grease from a plastic windshield/window, you should (worksheet Q24):
options: Rub gently with a cloth wet with soap and water
questions.short: Rub gently with a soap-and-water-wet cloth.
questions.expl: Remove oil and grease by rubbing gently with a soap-and-water cloth. Solvents like carbon tetrachloride, lacquer thinner, and gasoline soften the plastic and cause crazing.

questions.q: Which of the following must NOT be used on aircraft plastics because it softens the plastic and causes crazing (worksheet Q25)?
questions.expl: Acetone, benzene, carbon tetrachloride, lacquer thinners, window-cleaning sprays, gasoline, fire-extinguisher fluid, and deicer fluid all craze plastic. Soap, water, and a chamois are safe.

questions.q: Worksheet Q25 lists three solvents NOT to use on plastics. They are acetone, benzene, and:
questions.expl: The worksheet's three blanks are acetone, benzene, and carbon tetrachloride (first three of the full FAA list). All soften the plastic and cause crazing.

questions.q: Crazing of an aircraft plastic windshield refers to:
options: A network of fine cracks/clouding caused by solvent attack or stress
questions.short: A network of fine cracks/clouding in the plastic.
questions.expl: Crazing is fine cracking/clouding of plastic, commonly caused by incompatible solvents (acetone, benzene, carbon tetrachloride, etc.). It is unrelated to metal corrosion.

questions.q: To avoid scratching a plastic windshield, you should NOT:
options: Rub it with a dry cloth
Wash it by hand with soap and water
Dry it with a clean chamois
questions.short: Rub it with a dry cloth.
questions.expl: Because the surface is soft, do not rub plastic with a dry cloth - it scratches and builds static that attracts dust. Wash by hand and dry with a chamois or absorbent cotton.

questions.q: Which of the following IS safe to use when drying a cleaned plastic transparency?
options: A clean, damp chamois or absorbent cotton
questions.short: A clean, damp chamois or absorbent cotton.
questions.expl: Dry plastic with a clean damp chamois or soft absorbent cotton - never a dry/abrasive cloth and never solvents, which scratch or craze the surface.

questions.q: Per AC 43-4B, which cleaning method is specifically noted to CRAZE plastic and should not be used on transparencies?
questions.expl: AC 43-4B notes that steam cleaning crazes plastic. (The detailed solvent list that crazes plastic is in FAA-H-8083-30B p. 8-24.)

questions.q: A mechanic finds a worm-like trail of corrosion under the painted skin of a stored aircraft kept in a humid hangar (~85% RH). The MOST likely corrosion and a good prevention step are:
options: Galvanic corrosion; add a sacrificial anode
Filiform corrosion; store below 70% RH and use low-diffusion coatings
questions.expl: A worm-like trace under paint at 78-90% RH is filiform corrosion. Prevention includes storing below 70% RH, low-diffusion coatings, and washing off acidic contaminants.

questions.q: During a corrosion inspection a mechanic plans to clean an aluminum panel using a power wire brush from the steel-parts bench. What is wrong with this plan?
options: Nothing - wire brushing aluminum is preferred
Aluminum cannot be cleaned at all
A steel wire brush embeds steel particles into aluminum, causing galvanic corrosion; use aluminum wool or a fiber brush
questions.short: Steel brush -> embedded steel -> galvanic corrosion.
questions.expl: Never use a steel wire brush (or steel wool/copper brush) on aluminum; embedded steel particles start dissimilar-metal corrosion. Use aluminum wool, fiber (Tampico) brushes, and mild abrasives.

questions.q: A lead-acid battery has leaked electrolyte into its box. The correct neutralizing agent, and the reason cadmium mottling on the box plating should be left alone, are:
options: Sodium bicarbonate; mottling shows the cadmium is sacrificially protecting the steel
Monobasic sodium phosphate; mottling must be wire-brushed off
questions.short: Sodium bicarbonate; mottling = sacrificial protection working.
questions.expl: Sodium bicarbonate neutralizes acidic (lead-acid) electrolyte. Cadmium mottling (white-to-brown-to-black) shows sacrificial protection is working and must not be removed for appearance.

questions.q: A 7075 aluminum fitting was improperly heat-treated and then exposed to salt air. Which corrosion is it now MOST susceptible to, and what advanced form could follow?
options: Intergranular corrosion, which can advance to exfoliation
questions.expl: Improperly heat-treated 2014/7075 are prone to intergranular corrosion along grain boundaries, which can advance to exfoliation (lifting/swelling of surface grains).

questions.q: A mechanic finds tiny holes under a white powdery deposit on a magnesium gearbox housing. The corrosion form is ___ and magnesium is described as ___ to it.
questions.expl: White/gray powder over tiny holes is pitting, the most common corrosion of aluminum and magnesium. Table 3-1 lists magnesium as highly susceptible to pitting.

questions.q: To repair a shallow corrosion pit in a highly-stressed wing spar cap, the mechanic should blend the area to a smooth dish-out with a length-to-depth ratio of:
options: No blending - just paint over it
questions.expl: In critical/highly-stressed areas, pits must be blended out (not left as stress risers) using a smoothly contoured 20:1 length-to-depth dish-out.

questions.q: Anything that has mass and volume — the substance physical objects are made of — is defined as:
questions.expl: Matter is anything that has mass and volume and is the substance of which physical objects are composed. Its four states are solids, liquids, gases, and plasma; the technician deals with solids, liquids, and gases.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A substance that cannot be reduced to a simpler form by chemical means — such as iron, gold, or oxygen — is called a(n):
questions.expl: An element cannot be reduced to a simpler form by chemical means. A compound, by contrast, is a chemical combination of two or more elements (e.g., water = 2 hydrogen + 1 oxygen).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A chemical combination of two or more elements — such as water (two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom) — is a:
questions.expl: A compound is a chemical combination of two or more elements. Water is the most common example. Substances composed of only one type of atom are elements.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: What is the smallest particle of matter that can exist and still retain the identity of a substance such as water?
questions.expl: The molecule (e.g., H2O) is the smallest particle of matter that can exist and still retain its identity. The atom is the basic building block, but the molecule retains the substance's identity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The most basic building block of all matter, composed of protons, neutrons, and electrons, is the:
questions.expl: The atom is the most basic building block of all matter and is composed of three subatomic particles: protons, neutrons, and electrons.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which two subatomic particles are located in the nucleus of an atom?
questions.expl: At the center of the atom is the nucleus, which contains the positively-charged protons and the neutrally-charged neutrons. The electrons orbit at the periphery.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which subatomic particle carries a NEGATIVE charge and has a very small mass compared to a proton?
questions.expl: The electron is the negatively-charged particle with a very small mass. The proton's mass is approximately 1,837 times greater than that of the electron.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Approximately how many times greater is the mass of a proton compared to the mass of an electron?
questions.expl: The proton's mass is approximately 1,837 times greater than the electron's. Because of this, the number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus determines an atom's overall weight; the electron's weight is not considered.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: An atom is said to have a NEUTRAL charge when:
