options: The positive proton charge equals the negative electron charge
It has more electrons than protons
It has more protons than electrons
questions.expl: When the total positive charge of the protons in the nucleus equals the total negative charge of the orbiting electrons, the atom has a neutral charge.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The maximum number of electrons that any shell can hold is defined by the equation:
questions.expl: Electron capacity = 2n^2, where n is the energy level. The first shell holds 2 electrons, the second 8, the third 18, and so on.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The number of chemical bonds an atom can form — determined by the number of electrons in its outermost shell — is called its:
questions.expl: Valence is the number of chemical bonds an atom can form; the electrons in the outermost shell are the valence electrons and determine valence.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The process by which an atom loses or gains electrons is called:
questions.expl: Ionization is the process by which an atom loses or gains electrons, making it a positive or negative ion.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: An atom that has LOST an electron becomes a positively charged particle called a:
questions.expl: Dislodging an electron leaves the atom net positive — a positive ion or cation. An atom that GAINS an electron becomes a negative ion or anion.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: An atom that has GAINED an extra electron is negatively charged and is called a(n):
questions.expl: An atom that gains an extra electron is negatively charged and is called a negative ion or anion. Losing an electron yields a positive ion (cation).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The loosely-bound outermost electrons that can drift from atom to atom and make up current flow are called:
questions.expl: Free electrons are the loosely-bound outermost electrons that can be easily dislodged and travel atom-to-atom, becoming the flow of electrons called current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which group of materials makes the BEST electrical conductors because of their many free electrons?
questions.expl: Gold, copper, and silver possess many free electrons and make good conductors. Copper and silver have just one electron in their outer orbit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Glass, ceramic, and plastic do NOT conduct electrical current well because, under normal conditions, their atoms:
questions.expl: Insulators such as glass, ceramic, and plastic do not produce free electrons under normal conditions; the absence of free electrons means current cannot be conducted.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When an insulator is placed in an extremely strong electrical field and its outer electrons are dislodged, the resulting action that usually causes physical damage is called:
questions.expl: Only in an extremely strong electrical field will an insulator's outer electrons be dislodged; this action is called breakdown and usually damages the insulator.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Silicon and germanium — the two most widely used materials for transistors and integrated circuits — are classified as:
questions.expl: Semiconductor properties fall between conductors and insulators; in their pure state they neither conduct nor insulate well. Silicon and germanium are the most widely used.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In electrical work, the prefix 'kilo' (k) means:
questions.expl: Kilo (k) means one thousand. Mega (M) = one million; milli (m) = one-thousandth; micro (mu) = one-millionth.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The prefix 'micro' used in electrical calculations means:
questions.expl: Micro (mu) means one-millionth (1/1,000,000). Milli means one-thousandth. These appear constantly with farads (microfarad) and amps.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Electricity in which the electrons are at rest (a buildup of charge on the surface of an object, with no current flowing) is called:
questions.expl: Static electricity is a buildup of charge on the surface of an object; it is 'static' because no current is flowing. Dynamic electricity has electrons in motion.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Per the fundamental law of static electricity (and magnetism), how do charges behave?
options: Like charges attract; unlike charges repel
Like charges repel; unlike charges attract
questions.short: Like charges repel; unlike charges attract
questions.expl: One of the most fundamental laws of static electricity and magnetism: like charges repel each other and unlike charges attract each other.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The field of force that exists around a charged body, represented by lines extending in all directions, is called the:
questions.expl: A field of force around a charged body is an electrostatic field (sometimes called a dielectric field), represented by lines extending in all directions.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: By arbitrary agreement, the direction of an electrostatic field is determined using a small:
questions.expl: It is arbitrarily agreed that a small positive test charge is used to determine field direction; the field around a positive charge points away from it.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On an irregularly-shaped charged object, the greatest charge concentrates at:
options: The points or areas of sharpest curvature
questions.expl: The charge on an irregularly-shaped object is not evenly distributed; the greatest charge is at the points, or areas of sharpest curvature.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One of the most frequent causes of damage to a solid-state component or integrated circuit when handled is:
questions.expl: ESD from the human body is one of the most frequent causes of damage to solid-state components. ESD-sensitive parts ship in special protective bags.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To safely protect a solid-state component from ESD while handling it, a technician should:
options: Wear a grounding wrist strap and discharge body static
questions.expl: ESD damage is avoided by discharging body static (touch the chassis of the removed box), wearing a grounding wrist strap, and good professional handling.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Magnetism is defined as the property of an object to attract certain metallic substances, generally those composed of iron or iron alloys, which are called:
questions.expl: Magnetism is the property of an object to attract certain metallic substances — generally ferrous materials (iron or iron alloys such as soft iron, steel, and alnico).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which three nonferrous materials are magnetic to a limited degree?
questions.expl: Magnetic materials include at least three nonferrous materials magnetic to a limited degree: nickel, cobalt, and gadolinium.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Substances that are REPELLED by both poles of a magnet are classified as:
questions.expl: A few non-magnetic substances (e.g., bismuth) are diamagnetic, having a permeability less than one; they are repelled by both poles of a magnet.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The 'N' end of a freely-suspended bar magnet is more precisely described as the:
questions.expl: If a magnet swings freely it aligns with the earth; the 'N' end is the North-seeking pole, which points toward the North magnetic pole.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The individual lines making up a magnetic field are called lines of force, also referred to as:
questions.expl: The invisible lines making up the magnetic field are the lines of force, also called magnetic flux. They are concentrated at the North and South poles.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When no more lines of force can be induced into a piece of magnetic material, the material is said to be:
questions.expl: There is a limit to the number of lines of force a magnet can hold; when no more can be induced, the material is saturated.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Magnetic lines of force behave such that:
options: They never cross one another and actually repel each other
questions.short: They never cross and repel each other
questions.expl: Lines of force are separate, individual forces; one line never crosses another — they actually repel one another and remain parallel.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When storing a magnet, 'keeper bars' are installed across the poles to:
options: Provide an easy flux path and promote retention of magnetism
questions.short: Provide an easy flux path / retain magnetism
questions.expl: Keeper bars provide an easy path for flux lines from one pole to the other, promoting retention of the molecules' North-South alignment.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Heating, hammering, or dropping a magnet causes the strength of the magnet to:
questions.expl: Heating or sudden shock creates misalignment of the molecules, causing the magnet's strength to decrease.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The measure of the EASE with which magnetic flux can penetrate a material is called:
questions.expl: Permeability is the measure of the ease with which magnetic flux can penetrate a material. Soft iron and ferrous metals have high permeability. A vacuum is rated 1.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The permeability scale is based on a perfect vacuum, which is given a rating of:
questions.expl: The permeability scale is based on a perfect vacuum with a rating of one; air and nonmagnetic materials are so close they are also considered to have a rating of one.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Iron and its alloys, which have by far the greatest permeability, are termed:
questions.expl: Ferromagnetic applies to iron and its alloys, which have by far the greatest permeability. Nonferrous metals with permeability greater than one (nickel, cobalt) are paramagnetic.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The measure of OPPOSITION a material offers to magnetic lines of force — comparable to resistance in an electrical circuit — is called:
questions.expl: Reluctance is the measure of opposition to lines of force, comparable to electrical resistance. The reluctance of soft iron is much lower than that of air.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A magnet that retains its magnetism long after the magnetizing force is removed is classified as a:
questions.expl: Permanent magnets retain their magnetism long after the magnetizing force is removed; temporary magnets quickly lose most of their magnetism.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The ability of a magnet to HOLD its magnetism, which varies with the type of metal, is known as:
questions.expl: Retentivity is the ability of a magnet to hold its magnetism. Soft iron magnetizes easily but loses most of its magnetism quickly when the force is removed.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The small amount of magnetism remaining in soft iron after the magnetizing force is removed — important to generator operation — is called:
questions.expl: The small amount of magnetism remaining after the force is removed is residual magnetism, of great importance in such applications as generator operation.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which alloy of iron, aluminum, nickel, and cobalt is considered one of the very best permanent-magnet materials?
questions.expl: Alnico (aluminum-nickel-cobalt) is considered one of the very best permanent-magnet materials. Others include ferrites, neodymium-iron-boron, and samarium-cobalt.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In 1820, who discovered that a compass needle is deflected near a current-carrying conductor, demonstrating a link between electricity and magnetism?
questions.expl: In 1820, Danish physicist Hans Christian Oersted discovered that a compass needle is deflected near a current-carrying conductor, linking electricity and magnetism.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor takes what shape?
options: A straight line along the wire
A single loop at the end
A series of concentric circles around the conductor
questions.expl: A series of concentric circles around the conductor represents the field; if all lines were shown it would appear as a continuous cylinder of circles.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Per the handbook, which rule determines the direction of the magnetic field lines around a current-carrying conductor when the direction of current flow is known?
questions.expl: Grasp the conductor in the LEFT hand with the thumb pointing in the direction of current flow; the fingers wrap in the direction of the magnetic field lines. This is the left-hand rule.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A coil of wire loops with a soft-iron core inside, which concentrates the magnetic flux, is called a(n):
questions.expl: An iron core in a coil of wire loops is an electromagnet; the soft iron (greater permeability than air) concentrates the flux in the center and the poles act like a bar magnet.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Adding a soft-iron core to a current-carrying coil does which TWO things?
options: Increases the magnetic flux and concentrates the flux lines
questions.expl: The soft-iron core does two things: it increases the magnetic flux and concentrates the flux lines.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Per the handbook, the polarity (North/South) of a current-carrying coil/electromagnet is found by grasping the coil with the LEFT hand so the fingers point in the direction of electron flow (minus to plus); the thumb then points to the:
questions.expl: Using the left-hand rule for a coil, with fingers curved in the direction of electron flow (minus to plus), the thumb points to the North pole.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The strength of an electromagnet's field can be increased by increasing the current flow or by:
options: Increasing the number of loops in the wire
questions.expl: Field strength is increased by increasing current flow or the number of loops; doubling either approximately doubles the field strength. The core metal type is also a factor.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A device in which a spring-loaded iron core is pulled to a center position to complete a circuit when a coil is energized is a:
questions.expl: The solenoid (also called solenoid switch or relay) uses a spring-loaded off-center iron core that is rapidly pulled to center when the coil is energized, completing a circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The conventional current theory, advanced by Benjamin Franklin, holds that current flows:
questions.expl: Conventional current theory, advanced by Franklin, visualizes current as flowing from positive (+) to negative (-). Electron flow is the opposite.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How does the FAA officially define the direction of current flow?
questions.expl: The FAA officially defines current flow using electron theory: negative to positive. Electron flow is what actually happens — electrons flow from the negative source to the positive.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The electromotive force (emf) — the push or pressure that moves electrons through a circuit — is represented by which symbol and measured in what unit?
questions.expl: The symbol for emf is the capital letter 'E', and it is measured in volts. It is the electrical pressure that moves electrons.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Voltage (potential difference) is best described as a value that is:
questions.expl: Unlike current (a flow), voltage is determined between two points — it is the potential difference or emf felt across two points.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Electrons in motion making up an electric current are measured as a rate of charge flow. What is the symbol used for current in formulas?
questions.expl: The symbol used to indicate current in formulas or on schematics is the capital letter 'I'. Current is a measure of the rate at which charge flows.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One ampere (A) of current is equivalent to how much charge passing a point in one second?
questions.expl: 1 A = 1 coulomb per second. One ampere equals one coulomb of charge passing through a conductor in one second.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One coulomb of charge is equal to approximately how many electrons?
questions.expl: The handbook states one coulomb of charge equals 6.28 billion electrons. (Note: 1 A = 1 C/s.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When current flows in only ONE direction through a circuit, it is called:
questions.expl: When current flow is in one direction it is direct current (DC). Current that periodically oscillates back and forth is alternating current (AC).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The relatively slow average velocity at which electrons drift toward the positive end of a conductor is called the:
questions.expl: The average velocity of the charge is called drift velocity — a relatively slow speed — even though the effect of current is nearly instantaneous.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Ohm's Law states that current equals voltage divided by resistance. Which equation expresses this?
questions.expl: Ohm's Law: I = E/R, where I is current (A), E is voltage (V), and R is resistance (ohms). Transposed: E = I x R and R = E/I.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Transposing Ohm's Law to solve for voltage gives which equation?
questions.expl: Multiplying I = E/R by R gives the second form of Ohm's Law: E = I(R).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Transposing Ohm's Law to solve for resistance gives which equation?
questions.expl: Dividing E = I(R) by I gives the third form of Ohm's Law: R = E/I.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using the handbook's graph example with a constant 20-ohm resistance, if 60 volts are applied the current is:
questions.expl: I = E/R = 60 V / 20 ohms = 3 A. This matches the handbook's linear-relationship graph (E=10 gives 0.5 A, E=60 gives 3 A, E=120 gives 6 A).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A circuit has 28 volts applied across a 14-ohm resistor. How much current flows?
questions.expl: I = E/R = 28 V / 14 ohms = 2 A. When two of the three quantities are known, the third is found by transposing Ohm's Law.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using Ohm's Law, what voltage is required to push 5 amperes through a 4-ohm resistor?
questions.expl: E = I x R = 5 A x 4 ohms = 20 V.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: If 24 volts produces 3 amperes of current, what is the resistance?
questions.expl: R = E/I = 24 V / 3 A = 8 ohms.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The unit used to measure resistance is the ohm, whose symbol is:
questions.expl: The unit of resistance is the ohm, symbolized by the Greek letter omega. In formulas, the capital letter 'R' refers to resistance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which material is usually considered the BEST available conductor for a given wire diameter?
questions.expl: Copper is usually considered the best available conductor; a copper wire of a given diameter offers lower resistance than aluminum of the same diameter.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Aluminum is often used as a conductor instead of copper when which factor is important?
questions.expl: Aluminum is much lighter than copper and, for weight and cost reasons, is often used when the weight factor is important — even though it has higher resistance per diameter.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How does the resistance of a metallic conductor change as its length increases?
options: Resistance varies with the square of length
questions.expl: Resistance is DIRECTLY proportional to length — double the length, double the resistance. (It is INVERSELY proportional to cross-sectional area.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: If the cross-sectional area of a conductor is doubled, the resistance to current flow is:
questions.expl: Resistance is inversely proportional to cross-sectional area. Doubling the area cuts resistance in half because electrons have more room to move without collision.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: List the FOUR major factors that determine the resistance of a conductor.
questions.expl: The four properties that determine resistance are the material, length, cross-sectional area, and temperature.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: For most materials used as conductors, what happens to resistance as temperature rises?
questions.expl: Most materials used as conductors increase in resistance as temperature increases. Carbon is an exception (it decreases); constantan and Manganin change very little.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which substance is an EXCEPTION, showing a DECREASE in resistance as temperature increases?
questions.expl: Although most conductors increase in resistance with temperature, carbon shows a decrease in resistance as the temperature increases.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The amount of resistance increase of a 1-ohm sample per degree rise in temperature above 0 degrees C is called the:
questions.expl: The temperature coefficient of resistance is the increase in resistance of a 1-ohm sample per degree rise above 0 degrees C. For copper it is approximately 0.00427 ohm.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In American Wire Gauge (AWG), each DECREASE of one gauge number equals approximately what change in cross-sectional area?
questions.expl: Each decrease of one gauge number equals a ~25 percent increase in cross-sectional area. A decrease of three gauge numbers approximately doubles the area (2:1).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In AWG, a decrease of THREE gauge numbers does what to the conductor's resistance for a given length?
questions.expl: A decrease of three gauge numbers approximately doubles the cross-sectional area (2:1), which cuts the resistance of a given length in half.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A change of TEN wire gauge numbers represents approximately what change in cross-sectional area?
questions.expl: A change of ten wire gauge numbers represents a 10:1 change in cross-sectional area.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In wire measurement standards used in North America, one 'mil' equals:
questions.expl: The mil (one-thousandth of an inch) is the unit of diameter; the foot is the unit length. A wire of 1 mil diameter and 1 foot length is the unit size (mil-foot).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Power, the rate of energy consumption or conversion, is measured in watts. One watt equals a rate of:
questions.expl: The watt (W) is a rate of energy conversion of 1 joule/second. The number of joules consumed in 1 second equals the number of watts.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One horsepower of mechanical power is equivalent to how many watts of electrical power?
questions.expl: An electric motor rated at 1 horsepower requires 746 watts of electrical energy. (33,000 ft-lb/min and 550 ft-lb/s describe the mechanical definition of horsepower.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One horsepower is defined as the power required to move how much weight one foot in one minute?
questions.expl: One horsepower is required to move 33,000 pounds 1 foot in 1 minute, which works out to 550 ft-lb/s.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using the general power formula P = I x E, what is the power in a circuit with 2 amps at 24 volts?
questions.expl: P = I(E) = 2 A x 24 V = 48 W. This is the handbook's own worked example of the general power formula (Watt's Law).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A circuit carries 2 amps through a 100-ohm resistor. Using P = I^2 R, what is the power dissipated?
questions.expl: Second form of the power equation: P = I^2 R = (2 A)^2 x 100 ohms = 4 x 100 = 400 W. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using the third form of the power equation, P = E^2 / R, what is the power for 24 volts across 20 ohms?
questions.expl: P = E^2/R = (24 V)^2 / 20 ohms = 576 / 20 = 28.8 W. This matches the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How is total power found in BOTH series and parallel circuits?
options: It equals the sum of the power dissipated in each resistor
It equals the largest single power dissipation
It equals the source voltage times resistance
questions.expl: Total power dissipated in both series and parallel circuits equals the sum of the power dissipated in each resistor: PT = P1 + P2 + ... + PN. Power is simply additive.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A familiar unit of electrical ENERGY (not power), seen on an electric bill, is the:
questions.expl: Energy = power x time. Because power is in watts and time in seconds, a unit of energy is the watt-second or, more familiarly, the kilowatt-hour (kWh).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: What are the four most common methods of producing electrical energy?
questions.expl: The four most common methods of producing electrical energy are pressure (piezoelectric), chemical (battery), thermal (thermocouple), and light (solar cell).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Producing a voltage by applying mechanical pressure to a crystal such as quartz is called the:
questions.expl: Piezoelectric generation results from mechanical pressure on a dielectric crystal (e.g., quartz, Rochelle salt). The crystal produces a voltage proportional to the stress.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A thermocouple generates a voltage proportional to temperature at the junction of two dissimilar metals. This thermal-electric phenomenon is the:
questions.expl: Thermocouples generate a voltage proportional to junction temperature — the Seebeck effect. They are used to measure cylinder head temperature and Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A device that converts light energy directly into electricity is a:
questions.expl: A solar cell or photovoltaic cell converts light energy into electricity; photons absorbed by semiconducting material (silicon) break electrons loose to produce current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The general purpose of a resistor in a circuit is to:
options: Limit the amount of current flow
questions.short: Limit the amount of current flow
questions.expl: The general use of a resistor is to limit the amount of current flow. Heat is typically the unwanted loss of energy (except in heating elements).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A carbon composition resistor's ohmic (resistive) value is determined by:
options: The amount of graphite relative to the insulation material
questions.expl: The carbon composition resistor is a mixture of carbon/graphite, insulation filler, and binder; the amount of graphite relative to insulation determines the ohmic value.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On the resistor color code, what numerical value does the color RED represent?
questions.expl: Color code values: Black 0, Brown 1, Red 2, Orange 3, Yellow 4, Green 5, Blue 6, Violet 7, Gray 8, White 9.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On the resistor color code, what numerical value does the color GREEN represent?
questions.expl: Green = 5 on the resistor color code. (Black 0, Brown 1, Red 2, Orange 3, Yellow 4, Green 5, Blue 6, Violet 7, Gray 8, White 9.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The percentage variation allowed between a resistor's marked value and its actual value is known as its:
questions.expl: Tolerance is the allowable percentage variation between marked and actual value. A 5 percent resistor is not more than 5 percent higher or lower than the marked value.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On a resistor, a GOLD fourth (tolerance) band indicates what tolerance?
questions.expl: A gold fourth band = 5 percent tolerance; silver = 10 percent; no fourth band = 20 percent.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On a resistor, a SILVER fourth (tolerance) band indicates what tolerance?
questions.expl: A silver fourth band = 10 percent tolerance. Gold = 5 percent; no band = 20 percent.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On a 4-band resistor, if there is NO fourth (tolerance) color band, what tolerance is understood?
questions.expl: If there is no fourth band, the tolerance is understood to be 20 percent.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On an end-to-center band resistor, the THIRD color band normally indicates:
options: The number of zeros to add to the first two digits
questions.expl: The third band indicates the number of zeros to add to the two digits from the first and second bands (except gold = x 0.10, silver = x 0.01).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A resistor reads red, green, yellow. Its resistance value is:
questions.expl: Red=2, Green=5, Yellow=4 (number of zeros). So 25 followed by four zeros = 250,000 ohms. With no fourth band, tolerance is 20 percent (50,000 ohms).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Wire-wound resistors are typically used in applications that require:
options: Handling large amounts of current and high power ratings
questions.expl: Wire-wound resistors typically control large amounts of current and have high power ratings; they are constructed by winding resistance wire around an insulating rod.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which variable resistor is used to VARY the current in a circuit and is connected in series?
questions.expl: A rheostat is a variable resistor used to vary current; connected in series, increasing its resistance increases total resistance and decreases current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A potentiometer is considered a THREE-terminal device and is used to vary the:
questions.expl: The potentiometer is a three-terminal device (terminal 3 is the wiper). While a rheostat varies current, the potentiometer is used to vary the voltage in a circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A thermistor is a variable resistor with a NEGATIVE temperature coefficient, which means that as temperature increases its resistance:
questions.expl: The thermistor is temperature-sensitive with a negative temperature coefficient: as sensed temperature increases, the thermistor's resistance decreases.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a LINEAR potentiometer, one quarter turn of the wiper results in:
options: One quarter of the total resistance
questions.short: One quarter of the total resistance
questions.expl: In a linear potentiometer, resistance varies linearly with wiper position; one quarter turn gives one quarter of the total resistance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A situation in which a point at full system voltage comes into direct contact with the ground/return side, creating a path with virtually no resistance, is called a:
questions.expl: A direct short describes full system voltage coming into direct contact with ground, establishing a path with virtually no resistance and causing rapid heat generation.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In most fuses, the metal strip that melts to open the circuit is made of an alloy of:
questions.expl: In most fuses, the metal strip is made of an alloy of tin and bismuth, which melts and opens the circuit when current exceeds the rated capacity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A circuit-protection device similar to a fuse but made of copper, used in heavy-current circuits (30 amp or greater) and which must be replaced once opened, is a:
questions.expl: The current limiter's link is usually copper and stands a considerable overload briefly; it opens in over-current heavy circuits (30 A+), is used to sectionalize a bus, and must be replaced.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A circuit-protection device that interrupts current on an overload and CAN be reset (unlike a fuse) is a:
questions.expl: A circuit breaker opens when current exceeds a set value and can be reset; a fuse or current limiter must be replaced after it opens.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which type of circuit breaker is NOT allowed in aircraft?
questions.expl: Automatic reset type circuit breakers are not allowed in aircraft. Most circuit breakers must be reset by hand.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A thermal protector (thermal switch) used to protect a MOTOR works by opening the circuit when the motor:
questions.expl: A thermal protector opens the circuit automatically whenever the motor becomes excessively hot, using a bimetallic disk/strip that bends when heated.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a switch's designation, the number of POLES indicates:
options: The number of toggle rest positions
The number of circuits (paths) that can be completed at one time
questions.expl: Pole = the switch's movable blade/contactor; the number of poles equals the number of circuits (paths) that can be completed at any one time.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a switch's designation, the number of THROWS indicates:
options: The number of circuits each pole can complete
questions.expl: Throw indicates the number of circuits, or paths for current, that it is possible to complete through the switch with each pole or contactor.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A switch that allows a connection between two contacts — either open in one position or closed in the other — is designated:
questions.expl: The single-pole, single-throw (SPST) switch allows a connection between two contacts; the circuit is open in one position and closed in the other.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A switch that makes a connection from one set of contacts to either of two other sets of contacts is designated:
questions.expl: The double-pole, double-throw (DPDT) switch makes a connection from one set of contacts to either of two other sets of contacts.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A switch that stays open except when held in the closed position is identified as:
questions.expl: A switch that stays open except when held in the closed position is a normally open (NO) switch. One that stays closed except when held open is normally closed (NC).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A switch that opens or closes a circuit with a very small movement (1/16 inch or less) of the tripping device — often used as a limit switch for landing gear — is a:
questions.expl: A microswitch opens or closes a circuit with a very small movement (1/16 inch or less). They are used primarily as limit switches for landing gear, actuator motors, etc.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: An electromechanical switch in which a SMALL current energizes a coil to control a LARGE amount of current is a:
questions.expl: A relay is an electromechanical switch where a small amount of current can control a large amount of current; the energized electromagnet pulls the common (C) arm.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On a relay, when the coil is energized and the arm is pulled down, the contacts between the arm and the normally closed (NC) contacts are:
questions.expl: When energized, the electromagnetic field pulls the arm down: the circuit between the arm and the NC contacts opens, and between the arm and the NO contacts closes.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Components connected end-to-end so the same current flows through each, with only one path for current, form a:
questions.expl: A series circuit connects components end-to-end so the same current flows through each; there is only one path for the current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a SERIES circuit, what is true about the current?
options: Current is the same through every component and at every point
Current is greatest at the source only
Current is zero in the resistors
questions.expl: In a series circuit the current is always the same value at every point because there is only one path.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a SERIES circuit, the total resistance equals:
options: The sum of the individual resistors
questions.expl: For resistors in series, total resistance = R1 + R2 + R3 + ... + RN (a simple sum).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Three resistors of 10, 20, and 30 ohms are connected in SERIES. What is the total resistance?
questions.expl: Series total resistance is the simple sum: 10 + 20 + 30 = 60 ohms.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A 12-volt source is in series with R1 = 10 ohms and R2 = 30 ohms. Using the handbook's example, the circuit current is:
questions.expl: RT = 10 + 30 = 40 ohms; I = E/RT = 12 V / 40 ohms = 0.3 A. This is the handbook's worked series-circuit example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The loss in electrical pressure (emf) caused by forcing electrons through a resistor is called the:
questions.expl: Voltage drop refers to the loss in electrical pressure or emf caused by forcing electrons through a resistor; each resistor has its own drop.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In the handbook example, a current of 1 mA flows through R1=1k, R2=3k, R3=5k in series. The source voltage equals:
questions.expl: Voltage drops: 1 V + 3 V + 5 V = 9 V. Or ES = I x RT = 1 mA x 9 k = 9 V. The sum of the drops equals the source voltage.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Three 1.5-volt cells are connected in series with the SAME polarity. The total voltage is:
questions.expl: Voltage sources in series add algebraically: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = +4.5 volts when all polarities are in the same direction.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law states that around any closed loop, the algebraic sum of all voltages is:
questions.expl: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law: the algebraic sum of all voltages around a closed loop is zero. Equivalently, the sum of all voltage drops equals the total source voltage.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Stated another way, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law tells us the source voltage equals:
options: The sum of the voltage drops
questions.expl: The source voltage equals the sum of the voltage drops: ES = E1 + E2 + E3 + ... + EN.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a series circuit, one resistor drops 14 volts and another drops 10 volts. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, the source voltage is:
questions.expl: The source voltage equals the sum of the drops: 14 V + 10 V = 24 V. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A 24-volt source feeds three series resistors. Two drops are 12 V and 10 V. Using KVL, the third drop (E3) is:
questions.expl: 24 - 12 - 10 - E3 = 0, so E3 = 2 volts. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A device that makes it possible to obtain more than one voltage from a single power source — usually resistors connected in series with fixed/movable contacts — is a:
questions.expl: Voltage dividers make it possible to obtain more than one voltage from a single power source; they consist of resistors in series with tap contacts.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a voltage divider, the small current that flows through the divider in addition to the load currents is called the:
questions.expl: In addition to the load currents (I1, I2, I3), some bleeder current (IB) flows. The total current IT is the sum of all currents.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using the voltage divider formula EX = (RX/RT) x ES with RT = 1,000 ohms, ES = 100 V, the drop across a 300-ohm resistor is:
questions.expl: EX = (300/1000) x 100 = 30 V. The voltage drop across a resistor equals the ratio of its resistance to total resistance, times the source voltage.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a parallel circuit, each separate path for current flow is called a:
questions.expl: A parallel circuit provides more than one path for current; each of these parallel paths is called a branch.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a PARALLEL circuit, what is the same across every branch?
questions.expl: The voltage across any branch equals the voltage across all the other branches; branches are connected across the same source. Current divides among branches.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a PARALLEL circuit, the total resistance is always:
options: Less than the value of the smallest resistor
Equal to the sum of the branches
questions.expl: The total resistance of a parallel combination is always less than the value of the smallest resistor, because adding branches gives current more paths.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a parallel circuit, the total current of the circuit equals:
options: The current in the smallest branch
The sum of the current in all branches
questions.expl: The total current of a parallel circuit is the sum of the current in all branches, and is greater than that of any given branch.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The total resistance of two resistors in parallel equals:
options: The sum divided by the product
The product of the two resistors divided by their sum
questions.expl: For two resistors in parallel, RT = (R1 x R2) / (R1 + R2) — the product of both resistors divided by their sum.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using the product-over-sum formula, a 500-ohm and 400-ohm resistor in parallel give a total resistance of approximately:
questions.expl: RT = (500 x 400) / (500 + 400) = 200,000 / 900 = 222.22 ohms. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Three equal 30-ohm resistors are connected in parallel. The total resistance is:
questions.expl: For N equal resistors in parallel, RT = R/N = 30/3 = 10 ohms. (Handbook troubleshooting example uses three 30-ohm branches giving RT = 10 ohms.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Kirchhoff's Current Law states that at any junction (node):
options: The sum of currents entering equals the sum of currents leaving
Current is the same in every branch
questions.expl: Kirchhoff's Current Law: the sum of the currents into a junction equals the sum of the currents flowing out of that same junction.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using Kirchhoff's Current Law: if branch currents I1 = 5 mA and I2 = 12 mA enter a node, the total current is:
questions.expl: IT = I1 + I2 = 5 mA + 12 mA = 17 mA. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Total current into a node is 75 mA. Two of three branches carry 30 mA and 20 mA. The third branch carries:
questions.expl: IT = I1 + I2 + I3, so 75 = 30 + I2 + 20, giving I2 = 25 mA. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a parallel circuit acting as a current divider, branches with higher resistance carry:
options: The same current as low-resistance branches
questions.expl: Branch currents are inversely proportional to the ohmic values: branches with higher resistance have less current than those with lower resistance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A circuit that is a combination of resistors in series and in parallel is called a:
questions.expl: Most real circuits are series-parallel — combinations of resistors in parallel and in series; the same series and parallel rules apply.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In the handbook's series-parallel example (R1 = 1k in series with R2=2k parallel R3=3k, ES = 24 V), the total resistance RT is:
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In that same series-parallel example (RT = 2.2 k-ohm, ES = 24 V), the total current IT is approximately:
questions.expl: IT = ES/RT = 24 V / 2.2k = 10.9 mA (handbook worked example).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: An unintentional low-resistance path between two points (or to ground) that usually creates high current flow is called a:
questions.expl: A short circuit is an unintentional low-resistance path; it usually creates high current flow that burns out or damages the conductor or components.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A circuit that is not a complete or continuous path — representing an infinitely large resistance so no current flows — is a(n):
questions.expl: An open circuit is not a complete or continuous path; it represents an infinitely large resistance. Switches and burned-out components are common causes.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The state of being continuous, uninterrupted, or connected together (a circuit that is not broken) is called:
questions.expl: Continuity is the state of being continuous, uninterrupted, or connected together — the opposite of a broken or open circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When tracing an OPEN in a SERIES circuit with a voltmeter, the open component will show:
questions.expl: With an open in a series circuit, no current flows so good components drop 0 V; the total source voltage appears across the open component (per Kirchhoff's Voltage Law).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When checking for an open in a PARALLEL circuit, why can a voltmeter NOT be used effectively?
options: Across an open resistor it reads the voltage drop of the parallel branch
questions.expl: A voltmeter cannot be used in a parallel circuit for an open, since placed across an open resistor it reads the voltage drop in a parallel branch. Use an ammeter or modified ohmmeter.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To accurately check the resistance of a component with an ohmmeter, the component must first be:
questions.expl: When using an ohmmeter, the component must be isolated and the power source removed, otherwise parallel paths cause inaccurate readings or meter damage.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a SERIES circuit, a shorted resistor causes the circuit current to:
questions.expl: A short across a series circuit produces a greater-than-normal current flow; usually this blows the fuse or burns out a component.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A cell whose energy-releasing electrochemical reaction is NOT reversible — so it cannot be safely recharged — is a:
questions.expl: In a primary cell (e.g., dry cell), one metal is consumed during discharge; the reaction is not reversible and recharging is usually dangerous and can cause explosion.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A cell in which the electrochemical reaction that releases energy is REVERSIBLE — so it can be recharged — is a:
questions.expl: A secondary cell (e.g., lead-acid, Ni-Cad) has a reversible reaction and can be recharged.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a lead-acid cell, the electrolyte is sulfuric acid, the positive electrode is lead peroxide (lead dioxide), and the negative electrode is:
questions.expl: The lead-acid cell uses sulfuric acid electrolyte, positive plates of lead dioxide (PbO2), and negative plates of spongy lead.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How many lead-acid cells in series make up a typical 12-volt battery?
questions.expl: Each lead-acid cell produces approximately 2 volts; a 12-volt battery uses 6 cells in series, and a 24-volt battery uses 12 cells.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: What is the approximate normal RATED voltage of a single lead-acid cell?
questions.expl: A lead-acid cell is normally rated at approximately 2 volts (about 2.2 V right off the charger).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A lead-acid cell just removed from the charger reads approximately:
questions.expl: Although a lead-acid cell is rated at approximately 2 volts, just off the charger it reads about 2.2 volts.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The most common battery capacity rating, found by multiplying current in amperes by discharge time in hours, is the:
questions.expl: The most common battery rating is the ampere-hour rating: current (amps) multiplied by discharge time (hours).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A battery rated at 1 ampere-hour can ideally supply 1 amp for 1 hour, or 1/2 amp for how long?
questions.expl: Ampere-hour = amps x hours. A 1 A-h battery supplies 1 A for 1 hour, or 1/2 A for 2 hours, or 2 A for 1/2 hour.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: For rating airplane battery capacity, what discharge time period has been established as the basis?
questions.expl: For airplane batteries, a period of 5 hours has been established as the discharge time used in rating battery capacity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Connecting batteries in PARALLEL increases which rating, while voltage stays the same?
questions.expl: Connecting batteries in parallel increases ampere-hour capacity (capacity), not voltage. Connecting in series increases total voltage but not capacity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Connecting batteries in SERIES increases:
questions.expl: Connecting batteries in series increases the total voltage but not the ampere-hour capacity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Battery life cycle is defined as the number of complete charge/discharge cycles before normal capacity falls below what percentage of initial rated capacity?
questions.expl: Battery life cycle is the number of complete charge/discharge cycles before normal charge capacity falls below 80 percent of its initial rated capacity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The state of charge of a lead-acid battery is indicated by the density (specific gravity) of the electrolyte, measured with a(n):
questions.expl: State of charge is indicated by the specific gravity of the electrolyte, measured with a hydrometer — a sealed glass tube weighted to float upright.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The scale range on a typical hydrometer used for lead-acid batteries runs from:
questions.expl: The hydrometer's paper scale has a range of 1.100 to 1.300. The more dense the electrolyte, the higher the hydrometer floats.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a new, fully-charged aircraft battery, the electrolyte is approximately 30 percent acid and 70 percent water, with a specific gravity of about:
questions.expl: A new fully-charged battery's electrolyte is ~30 percent acid/70 percent water and is about 1.300 (1.270 at 60 F). During discharge it drops below 1.300 (to ~1.150 discharged).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When taking a hydrometer reading, no temperature correction is necessary as long as the electrolyte temperature is between:
questions.expl: No correction is necessary when the temperature is between 70 F and 90 F. Above 90 F or below 70 F, a correction factor must be applied.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When servicing a lead-acid battery, hydrometer readings should always be taken:
options: Only when the battery is warm
questions.expl: Always take hydrometer readings BEFORE adding distilled water, never after, to allow the water time to mix and avoid a non-representative sample.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The two standard methods of charging a battery are the constant current method and the:
questions.expl: Batteries are charged by either the constant voltage or the constant current method. In constant voltage, charging current tapers off as the battery nears full charge.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In the aircraft, the storage battery is charged by the aircraft generator system using which method (because generator voltage is held constant by a regulator)?
questions.expl: In the aircraft, the battery is charged by the constant voltage method, since the generator voltage is held constant by a voltage regulator.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When a lead-acid battery is being charged, it generates an explosive mixture of:
questions.expl: When charging, a battery generates hydrogen and oxygen, an explosive mixture. Loosen the vent caps and prevent open flames, sparks, or ignition sources.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The electrolyte used in a nickel-cadmium (Ni-Cad) battery is:
questions.expl: The Ni-Cad electrolyte is a potassium hydroxide (KOH) solution (20-34 percent by weight pure KOH in distilled water).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: What is the approximate typical open-circuit cell voltage of a single nickel-cadmium cell?
questions.expl: The typical open-circuit cell voltage of a nickel-cadmium battery is about 1.25 volts — distinctly lower than the ~2 V lead-acid cell.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Toward the end of charging (or if overcharged), a nickel-cadmium cell emits gas. To completely charge a Ni-Cad, some of this 'gassing':
questions.expl: To completely charge a Ni-Cad, some gassing, however slight, must take place; thus some water is used. The gas is hydrogen and oxygen from decomposition of water in the electrolyte.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: For a nickel-cadmium battery, water should be added to the cells only when the battery is:
questions.expl: During discharge the plates absorb electrolyte; on recharge the level rises and is highest at full charge. Therefore water should be added only when the battery is fully charged.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The undesirable Ni-Cad characteristic caused by constant overcharge and infrequent discharge, technically called 'voltage depression' but commonly (and erroneously) called:
questions.expl: Ni-Cads exhibit 'voltage depression,' commonly but erroneously called 'memory effect.' SLA batteries do not have this characteristic.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One of the first aircraft to utilize lithium-ion batteries is the:
questions.expl: The FAA certified lithium-ion batteries for aircraft; one of the first to use them is the Boeing 787, which uses two 32V 8-cell lithium-ion batteries.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A major reason AC has largely replaced DC in commercial power systems is that AC voltages can be readily increased or decreased by means of:
questions.expl: AC can be transmitted economically over long distances because AC voltages can be increased or decreased by transformers.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which two effects occur in AC circuits that do NOT occur in DC circuits?
questions.expl: Because AC constantly changes direction and intensity, inductive reactance and capacitive reactance occur in AC circuits but not in DC circuits.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In 1831, who discovered that a magnetic field could create a current flow in a conductor (electromagnetic induction)?
questions.expl: In 1831, Faraday discovered that a magnetic field could create a current flow in a conductor — the basis of generator operation.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Lenz's Law states that the induced current caused by relative motion of a conductor and magnetic field always flows in a direction such that its magnetic field:
options: Has no effect on the motion
questions.expl: Lenz's Law: the induced current always flows in such a direction that its magnetic field OPPOSES the motion that produced it.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Using the generator LEFT-hand rule, the SECOND finger of the left hand points in the direction of the:
questions.expl: Generator left-hand rule: first finger = magnetic lines of force (N to S), thumb = movement of conductor, second finger = direction of induced emf.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The value of an induced emf depends on three factors: the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of rotation, and:
options: The number of wires moving through the field
questions.short: Number of wires in the field
questions.expl: Induced emf depends on (1) number of wires moving through the field, (2) strength of the magnetic field, and (3) speed of rotation.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Generators that produce alternating current are called AC generators or:
questions.expl: Generators used to produce alternating current are called AC generators or alternators.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a simple AC generator, current is taken from the rotating loop by brushes riding on two metal:
questions.expl: In a simple AC generator the ends of the loop connect to two slip rings (collector rings) C1 and C2; brushes take the current from these rings.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A repetition of a pattern in which voltage rises to a maximum positive, falls to zero, rises to a maximum negative, and returns to zero is called one:
questions.expl: A cycle is a complete series of changes that returns to the starting point. There are two alternations in a complete cycle: positive and negative.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How many alternations are there in one complete cycle of AC?
questions.expl: There are two alternations in a complete cycle: the positive alternation and the negative alternation. Each is half a cycle.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The number of complete AC cycles occurring per second is the frequency, whose standard unit is the:
questions.expl: Frequency is the number of cycles of AC per second; the standard unit is the hertz (Hz).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A 2-pole generator turning at 3,600 rpm produces what frequency?
questions.expl: F = (P/2) x (rpm/60) = 1 x 60 = 60 cps. The handbook gives this exact worked example (a 4-pole, 1,800-rpm generator also yields 60 cps).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The time required for a sine wave to complete one full cycle is called the:
questions.expl: The period is the time required for a sine wave to complete one full cycle.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The period of a sine wave and its frequency are related how?
questions.expl: Period is inversely proportional to frequency: t = 1/f, and f = 1/t. The higher the frequency, the shorter the period.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The distance a waveform travels during one period, indicated by the Greek letter lambda, is the:
questions.expl: Wavelength (lambda) is the distance a waveform travels during a period, measured from one point on the waveform to the corresponding point on the next.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Two AC waveforms that go through their maximum and minimum points at the SAME time and in the same direction are said to be:
questions.expl: When two waves are exactly in step — reaching maximum and minimum at the same time and direction — they are in phase.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When two waveforms reach their maximum/minimum values at different times, the waveform that reaches its maximum first is said to:
questions.expl: When out of phase, the waveform that reaches its maximum or minimum value first is said to lead the other; the other lags.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How many electrical degrees are there in one complete cycle of an AC sine wave?
questions.expl: One full sine-wave cycle spans 360 degrees: zero at 0, max at 90, zero at 180, max (opposite) at 270, zero at 360.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The three values of AC are instantaneous, peak, and:
questions.expl: There are three values of AC: instantaneous, peak, and effective root mean square (RMS).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The largest instantaneous value of a sine wave, occurring at 90 degrees (and 270 degrees in the opposite direction), is the:
questions.expl: The peak value is the largest instantaneous value; the largest positive value occurs at 90 degrees and the largest negative at 270 degrees.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The effective value of a sine wave is also known as the:
questions.expl: The effective value is also known as the RMS (root mean square) value, which most AC voltmeters display. It is a measure of the heating effect.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The effective (RMS) value of an AC sine wave equals what fraction of the peak value?
questions.expl: RMS (effective) value = 0.707 x peak. It represents the heating effect — the DC voltage producing the same heat. (Do not confuse with 0.637.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Peak voltage can be found from RMS voltage using the relationship:
questions.expl: Algebraically rearranging Vrms = 0.707 x Vp gives Vp = 1.414 x Vrms. Peak is the larger value.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Household AC is 110 volts RMS. Approximately what is the PEAK voltage?
questions.expl: Vp = 1.414 x 110 = ~155 volts. The handbook gives this exact example: the 110 V supplied to homes is only 0.707 of the ~155 V peak.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Unless otherwise specified, any values given for AC current or voltage are assumed to be:
questions.expl: In the study of AC, any values for current or voltage are assumed to be effective (RMS) values unless otherwise specified. AC meters read effective values.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Besides resistance, which TWO factors oppose current flow ONLY in AC circuits (not DC)?
questions.expl: Resistance opposes both AC and DC. Inductive reactance (XL) and capacitive reactance (XC) oppose current only in AC circuits and can create a phase shift.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In its most basic form, a capacitor is constructed of two parallel plates separated by a nonconductor called a:
questions.expl: A capacitor is two parallel plates separated by a nonconductor called a dielectric. In a circuit, a capacitor serves as a reservoir/storehouse for electricity.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A good capacitor connected to a DC source, once charged, will:
options: Block DC and pass the effects of pulsing DC or AC
questions.expl: There can be no continuous movement of DC through a capacitor. A good capacitor blocks DC and passes the effects of pulsing DC or AC.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The time constant of a series resistor/capacitor (RC) circuit equals:
questions.expl: The RC time constant tau equals the product of resistance (ohms) and capacitance (farads): tau = RC.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In one RC time constant, a capacitor charges to approximately what percentage of the source voltage?
questions.expl: One time constant is the time required to charge to 63 percent of the source voltage. About 5 time constants brings it to ~99 percent.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A capacitor reaches approximately 99 percent of full charge after how many RC time constants?
questions.expl: The time to reach about 99 percent of source voltage is approximately 5 time constants (5 tau).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The basic unit of capacitance is the:
questions.expl: The basic unit of capacitance is the farad (f): one farad is one coulomb of charge stored with one volt across the plates.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: One farad is defined as one coulomb of charge stored with how much voltage across the plates?
questions.expl: By definition, one farad is one coulomb of charge stored with one volt across the plates of the capacitor (C = Q/E).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The two more common practical units of capacitance, smaller than the farad, are the microfarad and the:
questions.expl: Because one farad is large, smaller units are used: the microfarad (10^-6 F) and the picofarad (10^-12 F).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The maximum DC voltage that can be applied across a capacitor without damage is called its breakdown voltage, working voltage, or simply the:
questions.expl: The voltage rating (breakdown voltage / working voltage) is the maximum DC voltage that can be applied without risk of dielectric breakdown and arcing.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A capacitor should be selected so that its working voltage is at least what percentage greater than the highest voltage to be applied?
questions.expl: The capacitor's working voltage should be at least 50 percent greater than the highest voltage to be applied.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The capacitance of parallel plates is DIRECTLY proportional to which factor?
questions.expl: Capacitance is directly proportional to plate area: a larger plate area produces larger capacitance. Doubling the area doubles the capacitance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The capacitance of parallel plates is INVERSELY proportional to which factor?
questions.expl: Capacitance is inversely proportional to plate spacing: increasing the spacing (dielectric thickness) decreases capacitance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The three factors that affect the capacitance of parallel plates are plate area, plate spacing, and the:
questions.expl: Capacitance is affected by (1) plate area, (2) plate spacing, and (3) the dielectric material. The dielectric constant of a vacuum is defined as 1 (air is very close to 1).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which capacitor type is generally POLARIZED (marked + and -) and should be used only with DC or pulsating DC?
questions.expl: Electrolytic capacitors are generally polarized (+ and - leads marked) and should be subjected to direct or pulsating direct voltage only. They provide large capacitance in small size.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When capacitors are connected in SERIES, the total capacitance is:
options: Less than that of the smallest capacitor
questions.expl: In series, the effective plate separation increases, so total capacitance is less than the smallest capacitor (uses the reciprocal formula — opposite of resistors).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Capacitors connected in PARALLEL combine how (to find total capacitance)?
questions.expl: Capacitors in parallel add directly: CT = C1 + C2 + C3... (the effective plate area increases). This is OPPOSITE to resistors in parallel — a common trap.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Two capacitors, 330 uF and 220 uF, are connected in PARALLEL. The total capacitance is:
questions.expl: In parallel, capacitances add directly: 330 uF + 220 uF = 550 uF. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a circuit containing ONLY capacitance, the current:
options: Leads the applied voltage by 90 degrees
Lags the applied voltage by 90 degrees
Is in phase with the voltage
questions.expl: In a circuit with only capacitance, the current leads the applied voltage by 90 degrees (one-fourth cycle). In a circuit with only inductance, current lags voltage by 90 degrees.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Capacitive reactance (XC), measured in ohms, is found with the formula XC = 1 / (2 pi f C). As frequency INCREASES, XC:
questions.expl: XC = 1/(2 pi f C) is inversely proportional to frequency — as frequency rises, capacitive reactance DECREASES (opposite of inductive reactance).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When capacitors are connected in SERIES, their total capacitive reactance is found by:
questions.expl: When capacitors are connected in series, the total reactance equals the SUM of the individual reactances (Xct = Xc1 + Xc2 + ...). Note this is opposite to how their capacitance combines.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Faraday's Law (the Law of Electromagnetic Induction) states the induced emf in a closed loop of wire is proportional to:
options: The rate of change of the magnetic flux through the coil
questions.short: Rate of change of magnetic flux
questions.expl: Faraday's Law: the induced emf in a closed loop of wire is proportional to the rate of change of the magnetic flux through a coil of wire.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The property of a coil to oppose any CHANGE in current through it is called:
questions.expl: The primary aspect of a coil's operation is its property to oppose any change in current through it; this property is called inductance (symbol L).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a series RL circuit, the current increases to what percentage of full value in 1 time constant after the circuit is closed?
questions.expl: In a series RL circuit, current increases to 63 percent of full value in 1 time constant, and reaches 99 percent after the 5th time constant — like an RC charging curve.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: As a general rule, the inductance of a coil varies as:
options: The square root of the turns
The square of the number of turns
questions.short: The square of the number of turns
questions.expl: Doubling the number of turns produces a field twice as strong for the same current, but as a general rule the inductance varies as the SQUARE of the number of turns.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Doubling the LENGTH of a coil while keeping the same number of turns does what to the inductance?
questions.expl: Doubling the length of a coil, while keeping the same number of turns, halves the value of inductance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Lenz's Law (1833), which gives the direction of the induced emf, states the induced emf always opposes the:
questions.expl: Lenz's Law: the induced emf always acts in such a direction that the current it drives produces a magnetic field which OPPOSES the change in magnetic flux.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The basic unit of inductance is the:
questions.expl: The henry (H) is the basic unit of inductance. A circuit has one henry when current changing at one ampere per second induces one volt.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When inductors are connected in SERIES, the total inductance is:
options: The sum of the individual inductors
questions.expl: When inductors are connected in series, the total inductance LT is the SUM of the individual inductors: LT = L1 + L2 + ... + LN (like resistors in series).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When inductors are connected in PARALLEL, the total inductance is:
questions.expl: When inductors are connected in parallel, the total inductance is less than the smallest inductance (uses the reciprocal formula, like resistors in parallel).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Inductive reactance, the opposition an inductor offers to AC, is symbolized XL and measured in:
questions.expl: Inductive reactance (XL) is measured in ohms, just as resistance is. Inductance itself is measured in henries.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Inductive reactance is calculated with XL = 2 pi f L. As frequency INCREASES, XL:
questions.expl: XL = 2 pi f L is directly proportional to frequency and inductance — raise the frequency and inductive reactance rises (opposite of XC).
