sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In the handbook example, an inductance of 0.146 H at 60 cps gives an inductive reactance (XL = 2 pi f L) of approximately:
questions.expl: XL = 6.28 x 60 x 0.146 = 55 ohms. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In AC series circuits, inductive reactances are combined how?
questions.expl: In AC series circuits, inductive reactances are added like resistances in series; total reactance equals the sum of the individual reactances.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The combined total opposition to current flow in an AC circuit, made up of resistance and reactance, is called:
questions.expl: The combined effects of resistance, inductive reactance, and capacitive reactance make up the total opposition called impedance (Z), measured in ohms.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Ohm's Law for an AC circuit is written as:
questions.expl: For AC, Ohm's Law uses impedance: I = E/Z. When the circuit is resistance only, Z = R and it is the same as the DC form.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: For a series AC circuit with resistance and inductance, impedance is found by:
questions.expl: Resistance and reactance act at right angles, so by the Pythagorean theorem Z = sqrt(R^2 + XL^2). Substitute XC for circuits with capacitance.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In the handbook example with R = 6 ohms and XL = 8 ohms, the impedance Z is:
questions.expl: Z = sqrt(6^2 + 8^2) = sqrt(36 + 64) = sqrt(100) = 10 ohms. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A series AC circuit has 10 ohms resistance and 13 ohms capacitive reactance. Using Z = sqrt(R^2 + XC^2), the impedance is approximately:
questions.expl: Z = sqrt(10^2 + 13^2) = sqrt(100 + 169) = sqrt(269) = ~16.4 ohms. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When a circuit contains resistance, inductance, AND capacitance, the reactances are combined by:
options: Subtracting the smaller reactance from the larger (X = XL - XC)
questions.expl: Because their effects are exactly opposite, inductive and capacitive reactance are combined by subtraction (smaller from larger): then Z = sqrt(R^2 + (XL - XC)^2).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: For a series RLC circuit with R = 4 ohms, XL = 10 ohms, XC = 7 ohms, the impedance Z is:
questions.expl: Z = sqrt(R^2 + (XL - XC)^2) = sqrt(4^2 + (10-7)^2) = sqrt(16 + 9) = sqrt(25) = 5 ohms. Handbook worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: At the particular frequency where inductive reactance equals capacitive reactance and they cancel, the circuit is said to be:
questions.expl: At the resonant frequency, XL = XC; since they are opposite, they cancel, leaving only resistance to oppose current. The circuit is 'in resonance'.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In an AC circuit, the power actually consumed by the resistance portion (the working power, indicated by a wattmeter) is called:
questions.expl: True power (symbol P, measured in watts) is the working power consumed by the resistance, indicated by a wattmeter in the circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The power consumed by the ENTIRE AC circuit (both resistance and reactance), measured in volt-amps, is called:
questions.expl: Apparent power (symbol S, measured in volt-amps) is the power consumed by the entire circuit, including resistance and reactance — the product of effective voltage and current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The ratio of true power to apparent power, usually expressed as a percent, is called the:
questions.expl: Power factor (PF) is the ratio of true power to apparent power, usually expressed in percent: PF = (watts / volt-amperes) x 100.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A 220-volt AC motor draws 50 amperes; a wattmeter reads 9,350 watts. What is the apparent power?
questions.expl: Apparent power = volts x amperes = 220 x 50 = 11,000 watts (volt-amperes). This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: For that 220-volt motor (true power 9,350 W, apparent power 11,000 VA), the power factor is:
questions.expl: PF = (9,350 / 11,000) x 100 = 85 percent. This is the handbook's worked example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: True power equals apparent power ONLY when the AC circuit is made up of:
questions.expl: Only when the AC circuit is pure resistance is apparent power equal to true power. With reactance present, current and voltage are out of phase and true power is less.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A transformer changes electrical energy from one voltage to another by means of:
questions.expl: A transformer's two coils are not electrically connected; an alternating voltage on one coil creates a voltage in the other by mutual induction.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Why can a transformer NOT be used with pure (steady) DC voltage?
options: Transformers only pass AC current directly
Only a varying voltage creates the varying magnetic field needed for mutual induction
questions.short: Pure DC produces no varying field
questions.expl: A transformer needs a varying magnetic field for mutual induction. Pure DC produces a steady field, so only AC (or pulsating DC) can be used.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The three basic parts of a transformer are an iron core, a primary winding, and a:
questions.expl: A transformer has three basic parts: an iron core (low-reluctance flux path), a primary winding (receives source energy), and a secondary winding (receives energy by induction).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The factor that determines whether a transformer is step-up or step-down is the:
questions.expl: The turns ratio — primary turns to secondary turns — determines step-up vs. step-down. The voltage ratio equals the turns ratio in an ideal transformer.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A step-down transformer with a 5-to-1 turns ratio, fed with 10 volts on the primary, produces what secondary voltage?
questions.expl: Voltage ratio = turns ratio. A 5:1 step-down with 10 V on the primary gives 10/5 = 2 V on the secondary. Handbook example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A transformer has a 1-to-4 turns ratio (step-up). If 10 volts are applied to the primary, what is the secondary output?
questions.expl: Voltage ratio = turns ratio. With a 1:4 step-up and 10 V primary, secondary = 10 x 4 = 40 V. Handbook example.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: If a transformer steps UP the voltage, it steps DOWN the:
questions.expl: If a transformer steps up voltage, it steps down current by the same ratio, because the power (I x E) of the secondary equals that of the primary minus losses.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A transformer's primary develops 10,000 lines of force but only 9,000 cut the secondary. The transformer efficiency (coefficient of coupling) is:
questions.expl: 9,000 / 10,000 = 0.9, so the coefficient of coupling is 0.9 and the transformer is 90 percent efficient.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: No transformer is 100 percent efficient because some of the magnetic flux escapes the magnetic circuit. This lost flux is called:
questions.expl: A certain amount of magnetic flux, called leakage flux, leaks out of the magnetic circuit; thus no transformer is 100 percent efficient (though iron-core types approach it).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Current transformers are connected how in the circuit, and voltage transformers how?
options: Current transformers in parallel; voltage transformers in series
Current transformers in series; voltage transformer primaries in parallel
questions.expl: Voltage transformer primaries are connected in PARALLEL across the supply voltage; current transformer primaries are connected in SERIES in the primary circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The 'iron' losses in a transformer are of two types: eddy current loss and:
questions.expl: Iron losses are hysteresis loss (energy to re-magnetize the core each cycle) and eddy current loss (currents induced in the core). Copper loss is a separate conductor loss.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To reduce eddy current losses in a transformer, the core is made of:
questions.expl: To reduce eddy current losses, transformer cores are made of laminations coated with insulation, which reduces the circulation of induced currents.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The basic DC meter movement (used in the ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter) that deflects a pointer in proportion to current through a coil is the:
questions.expl: The d'Arsonval meter movement is the basic DC current-measuring device used in the ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter; the pointer deflects in proportion to coil current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The amount of current required to drive a meter movement to full-scale deflection is called the meter's:
questions.expl: Current sensitivity is the amount of current required to drive the movement to full-scale deflection (e.g., a 1 mA movement needs 1 mA for full scale).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To extend an ammeter's range so it can read currents larger than the movement allows, a resistor is added that bypasses the excess current. This is called a:
questions.expl: A shunt resistor is added in parallel to bypass current exceeding the movement's limit, extending the ammeter's range.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How is an AMMETER connected to measure current in a circuit?
options: In series with the element being measured
questions.expl: Always connect an ammeter in SERIES with the element whose current is to be measured. NEVER connect an ammeter across a voltage source — its very low resistance allows excessive current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Why must an ammeter NEVER be connected directly across a source of voltage such as a battery?
options: Its very low resistance allows excessive current that can damage it
questions.expl: The ammeter's resistance is extremely low; connected across a voltage source, even a volt or so causes very high current to flow through the meter, damaging it.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The most accurate ammeter readings are obtained at approximately what point on the scale?
questions.expl: The most accurate readings are obtained at approximately half-scale deflection; start with the highest range and work down.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To extend a VOLTMETER's range, a resistor in series with the movement that drops excess voltage is added; this is called the:
questions.expl: For a voltmeter, a series multiplier resistor (RM) drops any excess voltage above what the movement needs for full-scale deflection.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Voltmeter sensitivity is defined in terms of:
questions.expl: Voltmeter sensitivity is defined in terms of resistance per volt (ohms/volt). A 50 microamp movement has a sensitivity of 20k ohms/volt.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: How is a VOLTMETER connected to measure the voltage across a component?
questions.expl: Voltmeters are connected in PARALLEL — across the two points being measured. (Ammeter = series; voltmeter = parallel. A classic exam trap.)
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To prevent shunting (drawing off current and changing the reading) when measuring voltage, a technician should use a voltmeter with:
questions.expl: A low-resistance voltmeter draws off significant current and lowers the circuit's effective resistance. Use a HIGH-resistance voltmeter to prevent shunting.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The function of the ohmmeter is to measure resistance; unlike the ammeter and voltmeter, it contains its own:
questions.expl: The ohmmeter is self-excited — it contains a battery and a variable resistor in series with the movement to produce current through the resistance being measured.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On an ohmmeter, when the leads are OPEN (not connected), the pointer indicates:
questions.expl: When the ohmmeter leads are open, the meter indicates infinite resistance (an open circuit). When the leads are shorted, it reads zero.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The ohmmeter scale, where values decrease from left to right, is often called a:
questions.expl: Because the ohmmeter values decrease from left to right, the scale is often called a back-off scale; it is nonlinear (more compressed toward the left).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A high-range ohmmeter containing a hand-operated generator, used to measure insulation resistance with a high 'breakdown' voltage, is a:
questions.expl: The megger (megohmmeter) is a high-range ohmmeter with a hand-operated generator, used to measure insulation resistance, ground, continuity, and short circuits at high potential.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The chief advantage of the megger over an ordinary ohmmeter is its capacity to measure resistance with a high potential, or:
questions.expl: The megger's chief advantage is its capacity to measure resistance with a high potential, or 'breakdown' voltage, ensuring insulation will not short or leak under electrical stress.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A standard d'Arsonval (DC) meter connected in an AC circuit reads:
options: Zero (the average of the AC cycle)
questions.expl: A DC d'Arsonval meter in an AC circuit reads zero, because the current reverses too rapidly for the coil to follow, so it assumes an average position (zero).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To measure AC with a d'Arsonval movement, additional circuitry is required that converts AC to DC. This device is a:
questions.expl: For AC measurements, additional circuitry with a rectifier (half-wave or full-wave) converts AC to DC so the movement can respond.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A meter movement that can measure either AC or DC using two concentric iron vanes (one fixed, one movable) inside a solenoid, relying on repulsion, is the:
questions.expl: The moving iron vane meter uses repulsion between two concentric iron vanes inside a solenoid; it depends on induced magnetism and works on AC or DC.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Electric power is measured by means of a wattmeter, which has two elements (one for current, one for voltage) and is usually of which type?
questions.expl: Because power is the product of current and voltage, a wattmeter needs two elements and is usually of the electrodynamometer type; it can measure AC or DC power.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The instrument used to view and quantify waveform characteristics (phase, amplitude, duration), typically using a cathode-ray tube, is the:
questions.expl: The oscilloscope makes it possible to see and quantify waveform characteristics such as phase relationships, amplitudes, and durations, usually using a CRT.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The combined analog instrument that includes the voltmeter, ammeter, and ohmmeter is commonly called a multimeter or:
questions.expl: The combined voltmeter/ammeter/ohmmeter is called a multimeter or a VOM (volt-ohm-milliammeter). Digital versions are the DMM and DVM.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Compared with older analog meters, digital multimeters (DMM) and digital voltmeters (DVM) offer greater accuracy and:
options: No need for a power source
questions.expl: The DVM/DMM offers greater accuracy, less ambiguity, and higher input resistance, which gives it less of a loading effect on a circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The two most common materials used for semiconductors, each having four valence electrons, are germanium and:
questions.expl: The two most common semiconductor materials are germanium and silicon; each atom has four valence electrons. Silicon is preferred for its wider temperature range.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The very stable bond in which two silicon atoms each share one electron equally is called a:
questions.expl: Two silicon atoms share electrons equally in a covalent bond, which is very stable and holds the atoms together tightly.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The process of adding small amounts of impurities to a semiconductor material to increase its current flow is called:
questions.expl: Doping is the process by which small amounts of additives (impurities) are added to a semiconductor to increase current flow by adding electrons or holes.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A semiconductor doped with a donor impurity (such as phosphorus or arsenic) that produces an EXCESS of electrons is called:
questions.expl: An N-type semiconductor is doped with a donor impurity, producing an excess of electrons; the electrons are the majority current carriers.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A semiconductor doped with an acceptor impurity (such as boron or aluminum) that creates 'holes' is called:
questions.expl: A P-type semiconductor is doped with an acceptor impurity having only three valence electrons; this creates holes, which act as positive charges (the majority carrier).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A two-element semiconductor device that uses the rectifying properties of a PN junction to permit current flow in one direction only is the:
questions.expl: The semiconductor diode (PN junction diode) is a two-element device that converts AC into DC by permitting current flow in one direction only.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The region around a PN junction that lacks free electrons and holes is called the:
questions.expl: The area around the junction barrier lacks (is depleted of) free electrons and holes and is called the depletion region; the electrostatic field there is the barrier.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A semiconductor diode allows current to flow easily in which condition?
questions.expl: A forward-biased diode (negative source to N-type, positive source to P-type) conducts easily. Reverse bias blocks current. This one-way action makes a diode a rectifier.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a FORWARD biased diode, the negative voltage source connects to the N-type material and the positive source connects to the:
questions.expl: In a forward biased PN junction, the negative source connects to N-type and the positive source connects to P-type material, allowing current to flow easily.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When a diode is REVERSE biased, the most noticeable effect is that the depletion zone becomes:
questions.expl: When reverse biased, holes and electrons move away from the junction, widening the depletion zone and preventing current flow by the majority carriers.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A device that transforms AC into DC by limiting or regulating the direction of current flow is a:
questions.expl: A rectifier transforms AC into DC by limiting/regulating current direction. The two methods discussed are the half-wave rectifier and the full-wave rectifier.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A rectifier that produces output from only one half (e.g., the positive swing) of each AC cycle is a:
questions.expl: The half-wave rectifier reproduces the input minus the negative voltage swing — it uses only one half of each cycle.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A full-wave bridge rectifier uses how many diodes, with the output being a continuous sequence of pulses?
questions.expl: The full-wave bridge rectifier uses four diodes (D1-D4). During each half cycle, two diodes conduct so current passes through the load in the same direction both alternations.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A diode designed to break down (conduct) at a specific reverse-bias voltage and act as a voltage regulator is a:
questions.expl: Zener (breakdown) diodes conduct when reverse-bias voltage reaches a designed value, then behave like a constant voltage source — functioning as a voltage regulator.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a forward-biased diode where energy is dissipated as LIGHT rather than heat, the device is a:
questions.expl: In an LED, the energy released as electrons fall into holes is dissipated as light. Advantages over incandescent lamps: longer life, lower voltage, faster operation, less heat.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A variable-capacitance diode whose capacitance is controlled by the reverse voltage applied is a:
questions.expl: The varactor (varicap) is a variable-capacitance diode; the reverse voltage applied controls its capacitance. It often replaces mechanically tuned capacitors.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A three-terminal device used primarily to amplify signals and control current is the:
questions.expl: The transistor is a three-terminal device used primarily to amplify signals and control current within a circuit.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The three regions of a transistor are the collector, the emitter, and the:
questions.expl: A transistor's three regions are the collector (moderately doped), the emitter (heavily doped), and the base (significantly less doped and thinnest).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which transistor region is HEAVILY doped?
questions.expl: The emitter is heavily doped, the collector is moderately doped, and the base is significantly less doped (and thinnest).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Transistors are classified as either NPN or PNP according to:
options: The arrangement of their N and P materials
questions.short: Arrangement of N and P materials
questions.expl: Transistors are classified NPN or PNP according to the arrangement of their N and P materials. NPN has P sandwiched between two N regions; PNP is the opposite.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: On a transistor schematic symbol, how are NPN and PNP distinguished?
options: By the direction of the arrow on the emitter
By the color of the symbol
By the thickness of the base line
questions.expl: The arrow is on the emitter. Arrow pointing IN = PNP; arrow pointing OUT = NPN.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: To use a transistor as an amplifier, the emitter-base junction is biased in the forward direction (low resistance) and the collector-base junction is:
questions.expl: For amplification, the emitter-base junction is forward biased (low resistance) and the collector-base junction is reverse biased (high resistance), producing power gain.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a PNP transistor, the majority current carriers are:
questions.expl: In a PNP transistor the majority carriers are holes (the opposite of the NPN, where electrons are the majority carriers). To bias a PNP, the bias network polarity is reversed.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The name 'transistor' is a combination of which two words?
questions.expl: Because the device transfers current from a low-resistance path to a high-resistance one, its name combines 'transfer' and 'resistor' — transistor.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A transistor design with only ONE PN junction that is a VOLTAGE-controlled device (rather than current-controlled) is the:
questions.expl: The field-effect transistor (FET) has only one PN junction and is a voltage-controlled device, unlike the bipolar transistor which has two junctions and is current-controlled.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The transistor configuration that provides the best combination of voltage and current gain and is the only one giving a 180-degree phase reversal is the:
questions.expl: The common-emitter (CE) configuration provides good gains for voltage, current, and power, and is the only configuration that provides a 180-degree phase reversal.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A logic gate whose output is HIGH only when ALL of its inputs are high is the:
questions.expl: The AND gate output is high only when all inputs are high. If any input is low, the output is low — it determines when conditions are met at the same time.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A logic gate whose output is HIGH when ANY ONE (or more) of its inputs is high is the:
questions.expl: The OR gate output is high whenever any one of its inputs is high; the output is low only when no input is high.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A logic gate (also called a NOT gate) that converts one logic state into its opposite is the:
questions.expl: The inverter (NOT gate) converts one logic state into the opposite — a 1 to a 0 or a 0 to a 1.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A NAND gate produces a LOW output only when:
questions.expl: NAND = NOT-AND (AND with inverted output). The NAND output is low only when all inputs are high; if any input is low, the output is high.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Replacing the slip rings of a basic AC generator with two half-cylinders (a commutator) produces a:
questions.expl: Replacing the slip rings with a commutator (two half cylinders) makes a basic DC generator; the commutator action produces a DC voltage.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The rotating parts of a DC generator — the coil and commutator together — are called the:
questions.expl: The rotating coil and commutator of a DC generator are together called the armature.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: What are the three basic types of DC motors?
questions.expl: The three basic types of DC motors are series motors, shunt motors, and compound motors. They differ in how the field and armature coils are connected.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Which DC motor has its field windings connected in SERIES with the armature, producing HIGH starting torque (used as an aircraft engine starter)?
questions.expl: In a series motor the field windings are in series with the armature; the large starting current produces high starting torque — its principal advantage. Used as engine starters and for gear/flap operation.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: What is the danger if a SERIES motor has its load removed entirely?
options: It may overspeed until the armature flies apart
questions.short: It may overspeed and fly apart
questions.expl: A series motor runs at high speed under light load; if the load is removed entirely, the motor may operate at such a high speed that the armature flies apart.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A DC motor whose field winding is connected in PARALLEL (shunt) with the armature, giving fairly constant speed, is a:
questions.expl: In a shunt motor the field winding is connected in parallel (shunt) with the armature; starting torque is less than a series motor's, but speed is more constant.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A DC motor that combines a series winding and a shunt winding in the field is a:
questions.expl: The compound motor combines series and shunt motors; it has both a shunt winding and a series winding in the field. Its starting torque is higher than a shunt but lower than a series motor.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: When a DC motor's armature rotates in the magnetic field, a voltage is induced that opposes the applied voltage. This is called:
questions.expl: The voltage induced in the armature windings as it rotates is the counter (back) emf, opposite in direction to the applied voltage. It limits the armature current.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: As a DC motor's armature speed INCREASES, the counter emf increases and the current through the armature:
questions.expl: The faster the armature rotates, the greater the counter emf; counter emf opposes the applied voltage, so armature current decreases as speed increases. A motor draws high current on starting.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: An AC-series motor is a single-phase motor that resembles a DC motor in that it has brushes and a commutator, and it can operate on:
questions.expl: The AC-series motor resembles a DC motor (brushes and commutator) and operates on either AC or DC circuits. Modifications (laminations, compensating winding) make it work well on AC.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The frequency of an alternator's output voltage depends on the number of poles and the:
options: Speed of rotation of the rotor
questions.expl: Alternator frequency depends on the number of poles and the speed of rotation: the faster the speed (or the more poles), the higher the frequency.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: A device used to convert a portion of the aircraft's DC power to AC (mainly for instruments, radio, radar, lighting) is an:
questions.expl: An inverter converts DC power to AC, used mainly for instruments, radio, radar, lighting, and accessories — usually at 400 cps.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: Aircraft inverters are usually built to supply AC current at a frequency of:
questions.expl: Inverters are usually built to supply current at a frequency of 400 cps. Some provide more than one voltage (e.g., 26 V AC and 115 V AC).
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: The two basic types of inverters are the rotary inverter and the:
questions.expl: The two basic types of inverters are the rotary and the static (solid-state). Static inverters have no moving parts, are smaller, lighter, and more efficient.
sources: Ch.12 General — Fundamentals of Electricity & Electronics

questions.q: In a series circuit, which quantity is the SAME at every point in the circuit?
questions.expl: A series circuit has only one path, so the same current flows through every part. Voltage drops differ; current does not.

questions.q: Three resistors of 1 kΩ, 3 kΩ, and 5 kΩ are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
questions.expl: In series, total resistance is the simple sum: 1 + 3 + 5 = 9 kΩ. Adding series resistors always increases the total.

questions.q: In a parallel circuit, which quantity is the SAME across every branch?
questions.expl: Every branch connects across the same two source terminals, so each branch sees the same voltage. The branch currents differ.

questions.q: Compared to the smallest branch resistor, the total resistance of a parallel circuit is always:
options: Equal to the sum of all branches
questions.expl: Adding a parallel path gives current another road, lowering total resistance. The result is always less than the smallest individual branch — the opposite of series.

questions.q: Two resistors of 500 Ω and 400 Ω are connected in parallel. Using product-over-sum, what is the total resistance?
questions.expl: Product/sum = (500×400)/(500+400) = 200,000/900 = 222.2 Ω. Note it is less than the smaller resistor (400 Ω), confirming the parallel rule.

questions.q: Two 60 Ω resistors are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance?
questions.expl: For equal resistors in parallel, divide the value by the number of branches: 60 ÷ 2 = 30 Ω, which is less than the smallest branch.

questions.q: The correct product-over-sum formula for two resistors in parallel is:
questions.expl: Total resistance for two parallel resistors equals the product of the two divided by their sum: (R1×R2)/(R1+R2).

questions.q: In a series circuit, the individual voltage drops across the resistors must add up to:
questions.expl: Each resistor uses a share of the push; the shares add back to the applied source voltage (this is Kirchhoff's Voltage Law).

questions.q: A 12 V source feeds two series resistors: R1 = 100 Ω and R2 = 300 Ω. What is the voltage drop across R2?
questions.expl: Rt = 400 Ω, I = 12/400 = 0.03 A, E2 = 0.03 × 300 = 9 V. R2 holds 3/4 of the resistance so it drops 3/4 of the 12 V.

questions.q: A device that uses series resistors to obtain more than one voltage from a single power source is called a:
questions.expl: A voltage divider is a series resistor string with taps; different voltages appear between the taps because the source voltage divides among the resistors.

questions.q: Kirchhoff's Voltage Law states that around any closed loop, the source voltage minus all the voltage drops equals:
questions.expl: KVL: applied voltage minus the sum of all drops equals zero — every bit of the source's push is used up by the loads around the loop.

questions.q: A 100 V source feeds four series resistors. Three drops are 2 V, 10 V, and 20 V. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, the fourth drop is:
Kirchhoff's Current Law states that the current flowing into a junction (node):
options: Equals the current flowing out of it
Is greater than the current out
questions.expl: At any junction, current in equals current out — nothing accumulates. For parallel branches this gives It = I1 + I2 + I3.

questions.q: 75 mA flows into a node. Two branches leaving carry 30 mA and 20 mA. By Kirchhoff's Current Law, the third branch carries:
questions.expl: Current in = current out: 75 = 30 + 20 + I2, so I2 = 75 − 50 = 25 mA.

questions.q: In a parallel circuit, the total current drawn from the source equals:
options: The sum of all branch currents
The same current in every branch
questions.expl: The source current splits among the branches and rejoins, so It = I1 + I2 + I3 (Kirchhoff's Current Law applied to a parallel circuit).

questions.q: To solve a series-parallel circuit, the FIRST step is usually to:
options: Reduce each parallel group to its single equivalent resistor
questions.expl: Solve the parallel groups first and replace each with one equivalent resistor; the circuit then collapses into a simple series circuit you can sum.

questions.q: R1 = 1 kΩ is in series with a parallel pair (2 kΩ and 3 kΩ). What is the total resistance of the circuit?
questions.expl: Parallel pair: (2k×3k)/(2k+3k) = 1.2 kΩ. Then series add: 1 kΩ + 1.2 kΩ = 2.2 kΩ.

questions.q: An OPEN circuit is best described as:
options: A break or incomplete path so no current flows
A path with exactly the rated resistance
questions.expl: An open is an incomplete path for current — a break, blown fuse, or burned-open part — so no current flows in that path.

questions.q: A SHORT circuit is best described as:
options: An unwanted low-resistance path that lets current bypass the load
questions.expl: A short allows current to flow across a part that should insulate, bypassing the load. It causes excessive current and rapid heating.

questions.q: What is the typical symptom of a SHORT circuit in terms of current?
options: Excessive current that can blow a fuse or breaker
questions.expl: A short is a near-zero-resistance shortcut, so current spikes and generates heat quickly — this is what trips circuit protection.

questions.q: A voltmeter placed across a suspected break in an energized circuit reads the FULL source voltage. This indicates:
options: A blown branch resistor in parallel
questions.expl: With an open, no current flows, so no voltage is dropped elsewhere — the entire source voltage appears across the break.

questions.q: With power removed, an ohmmeter across a component reads infinite (∞) ohms. The component is:
questions.expl: Infinite resistance means no complete path — an open. A short would read near 0 Ω.

questions.q: Which fault can occur in a resistor or fuse WITHOUT any visible sign of damage?
questions.expl: The NCATT guide notes a resistor or fuse can open with no visible damage, so a meter/continuity check is needed to confirm it.

questions.q: Which meter is used to find a circuit problem with the power REMOVED from the circuit?
questions.expl: The ohmmeter measures resistance and runs continuity checks with power off. The voltmeter and ammeter are used with power on.

questions.q: Which meter is used to find a circuit problem with the power ON, and is a good first check for input voltage?
questions.expl: The voltmeter works on a live circuit; checking that input voltage is even present is a recommended starting point.

questions.q: A continuity check is performed to verify:
options: That a complete electrical path exists between two points
The peak value of an AC waveform
questions.expl: A continuity check confirms a complete path exists between two points; it is usually done with an ohmmeter or voltmeter, testing at each terminal.

questions.q: Which single instrument combines a voltmeter, ammeter, and ohmmeter and is the most versatile tool the technician uses?
questions.expl: A multimeter contains a voltmeter, ammeter, and ohmmeter, measuring voltage, current, and resistance — the most versatile electrical instrument.

questions.q: In the troubleshooting process, the recommended FIRST action is to:
options: Isolate the problem by asking how and when it occurs
Start replacing components one by one
Apply maximum voltage to the circuit
questions.expl: The NCATT process begins by isolating the problem — is it intermittent, worse during taxi/takeoff/cruise/landing, weather-related — before touching parts.

questions.q: When narrowing down where a fault is along a wire run, the efficient measurement strategy is to:
options: Measure at the middle, decide which half holds the fault, then split again
Test every single terminal in order from the source
questions.expl: Measuring at the midpoint and discarding the good half each time corners the fault in a few moves instead of testing every point.

questions.q: Direct current (DC) differs from alternating current (AC) in that DC:
options: Flows constantly in one direction with constant polarity
questions.expl: DC flows constantly in one direction with constant polarity; AC reverses direction at regular intervals, tracing a sine wave.

questions.q: The number of complete AC cycles that occur each second is called the:
questions.expl: Frequency is cycles per second, measured in hertz (Hz). The time for one cycle is the period.

questions.q: If an aircraft AC system operates at 400 Hz, the period of one cycle is:
questions.expl: Period = 1 ÷ frequency = 1 ÷ 400 = 0.0025 s = 2.5 ms. Period and frequency are reciprocals.

questions.q: One reason aircraft use AC rather than DC for many systems is that AC voltage:
options: Can be easily stepped up or down with transformers
questions.expl: AC can be raised or lowered by transformers, transmits over distance more easily, and AC motors are smaller, lighter, and brushless.

questions.q: What is the primary job of a resistor in a circuit?
options: To oppose (limit) the amount of current flow
questions.expl: A resistor's general use is to limit the amount of current flow.

questions.q: In the standard resistor color code, what digit does BLACK represent?
questions.expl: The color sequence starts Black=0, Brown=1, Red=2...

questions.q: What digit does the color WHITE represent?
questions.expl: White is the highest digit, 9, at the end of the 0-9 color sequence.

questions.q: Reading from the end toward the center of a 4-band resistor, what does the THIRD band represent?
options: The multiplier (number of zeros to add)
questions.expl: Band 3 tells how many zeros to add to the first two digits.

questions.q: On a standard 4-band resistor, what does the FOURTH band indicate?
questions.expl: The fourth band gives the percentage tolerance; no fourth band means +/-20%.

questions.q: A GOLD tolerance band means the resistor is accurate to within:
questions.expl: Gold tolerance band = +/-5%.

questions.q: A SILVER tolerance band means the resistor tolerance is:
questions.expl: Silver tolerance band = +/-10%.

questions.q: A resistor has NO fourth (tolerance) band. What tolerance is understood?
questions.expl: If there is no fourth band, the tolerance is understood to be 20 percent.

questions.q: Decode Brown-Black-Red-Gold. What is the value and tolerance?
questions.expl: Brown=1, Black=0 -> 10; Red x100 -> 1,000 ohms; Gold = +/-5%.

questions.q: Decode Yellow-Violet-Orange-Silver.
questions.expl: Yellow=4, Violet=7 -> 47; Orange x1,000 -> 47,000 ohms; Silver = +/-10%.

questions.q: Decode Green-Blue-Brown-Gold.
questions.expl: Green=5, Blue=6 -> 56; Brown x10 -> 560 ohms; Gold = +/-5%.

questions.q: Decode Red-Red-Red-Gold.
Decode Brown-Green-Green-Silver.
questions.expl: Brown=1, Green=5 -> 15; Green x100,000 -> 1,500,000 ohms; Silver = +/-10%.

questions.q: What is the correct order of the first five color-code colors (digits 0 through 4)?
questions.expl: 0=Black, 1=Brown, 2=Red, 3=Orange, 4=Yellow.

questions.q: Which color represents the digit 5?
questions.expl: Green=5. (Blue=6, Yellow=4, Violet=7.)

questions.q: Which color represents the digit 7?
questions.expl: Violet=7. A common confusion is Blue=6 vs Violet=7.

questions.q: A resistor's first two bands are Blue then Gray. What two digits is that?
questions.expl: Blue=6, Gray=8 -> '68'.

questions.q: Which resistor type is built from finely ground carbon/graphite mixed with an insulating filler and binder, pressed into a rod?
questions.expl: Carbon composition uses graphite + filler + binder; graphite amount sets the resistance.

questions.q: Which resistor type typically handles LARGE current and has HIGH power ratings, built by winding resistance wire on a porcelain rod?
questions.expl: Wire-wound resistors control large currents and have high power ratings.

questions.q: A film resistor is made by:
options: Winding wire on a ceramic rod
Pressing graphite and filler into a rod
Depositing a thin resistive material evenly on a ceramic rod
questions.short: Thin film deposited on a ceramic rod.
questions.expl: Film resistors deposit resistive material (graphite, nickel-chromium, etc.) on a ceramic rod.

questions.q: What is the difference between a rheostat and a potentiometer?
options: A rheostat has 3 terminals; a potentiometer has 2
A rheostat varies current (2 terminals); a potentiometer varies voltage (3 terminals)
A rheostat is temperature-sensitive; a potentiometer is light-sensitive
questions.short: Rheostat varies current; potentiometer varies voltage.
questions.expl: Rheostat = 2-terminal, varies current. Potentiometer = 3-terminal (wiper), varies voltage.

questions.q: A thermistor is a variable resistor that responds to:
questions.expl: A thermistor is temperature-sensitive with a negative temperature coefficient.

questions.q: Before measuring a resistor's value with an ohmmeter, you must:
questions.expl: An ohmmeter uses its own internal battery; circuit power must be off or it damages the meter and gives false reads.

questions.q: When testing a resistor in-circuit, why should you disconnect (isolate) at least one lead?
options: So parallel components don't provide a sneak path that falsifies the reading
To increase the voltage across it
Because the ohmmeter needs two batteries
questions.expl: Other components in parallel give the meter a second path, reading lower than the resistor alone; lifting a lead isolates it.

questions.q: An ohmmeter across an isolated resistor reads INFINITE (OL). The resistor is:
questions.expl: Infinite resistance means a broken path with no continuity = an open resistor.

questions.q: An ohmmeter across an isolated resistor reads 0 ohms. The resistor is:
questions.expl: Zero ohms means current passes where it should be opposed = a shorted resistor.

questions.q: The NCATT guide says an OPEN resistor is a fault caused by:
options: A foreign metal bridging two traces
An incomplete path for current from one terminal to another
questions.expl: An open is an incomplete path for current; it can occur with no visible signs of damage.

questions.q: The NCATT guide describes a SHORTED resistor as a fault that:
options: Allows current to flow across a part of the circuit that should act as an insulator
questions.short: Current flows where it should be blocked.
questions.expl: A short allows current across a portion that should insulate; often from foreign material or close traces.

questions.q: A resistor marked Brown-Black-Red-Gold (1,000 ohms +/-5%) measures 1,180 ohms after isolation. It is:
questions.expl: +/-5% of 1,000 = 950-1,050 ohms. 1,180 is above the range, so it is out of tolerance.

questions.q: A 470 ohm +/-10% resistor is within tolerance if it measures between:
questions.expl: +/-10% of 470 = +/-47, so the legal range is 423 to 517 ohms.

questions.q: You need a 330 ohm +/-5% resistor. Which color bands?
questions.expl: 3,3 = Orange,Orange; x10 (one zero) = Brown; +/-5% = Gold -> 330 ohms +/-5%.

questions.q: What does a resistor's POWER RATING (in watts) tell you?
options: The maximum heat it can dissipate without being damaged
questions.expl: The power rating is the most heat (watts) the resistor can shed without overheating; P = I-squared x R.

questions.q: Three resistors of 100, 220, and 330 ohms are in SERIES. The total resistance is:
questions.expl: In series, resistances add: 100 + 220 + 330 = 650 ohms.

questions.q: Two 100 ohm resistors are connected in PARALLEL. The total resistance is:
questions.expl: Two equal resistors in parallel = half of one: (100x100)/(100+100) = 50 ohms.

questions.q: On a color-banded resistor, the body (base) color of the resistor:
options: Has nothing to do with the resistance value
questions.expl: The body/base color has nothing to do with the color code and is never the same as a value band.

questions.q: The first color band of a standard 4-band resistor is never which colors?
questions.expl: The first (and second) digit band is never gold or silver in color.

questions.q: What does an inductor oppose, and where does it store energy?
options: A change in current; stored in a magnetic field
A change in voltage; stored in an electric field
A change in resistance; stored as heat
A change in frequency; stored in the dielectric
questions.expl: An inductor opposes a CHANGE in current and stores energy in a magnetic field. (The capacitor is the voltage/electric-field one.)

questions.q: What is the basic unit of inductance?
questions.expl: The henry (H) is the unit of inductance: 1 H when current changing 1 A/sec induces 1 V.

questions.q: Which change INCREASES a coil's inductance?
options: Stretching the coil to twice its length
Removing the iron core and using air
questions.expl: More turns increases inductance (it varies as the square of the turns). Longer coil, air core, and smaller area all DECREASE inductance.

questions.q: Doubling the number of turns on a coil (same current) changes the inductance by about what factor?
questions.expl: Inductance varies as the SQUARE of the number of turns, so doubling turns gives roughly 4x the inductance.

questions.q: Three inductors are connected in SERIES. How do you find total inductance?
options: Add them: LT = L1 + L2 + L3
questions.expl: Inductors in series add directly, just like resistors in series: LT = L1 + L2 + L3.

questions.q: The formula for inductive reactance is:
questions.expl: Inductive reactance XL = 2πfL, measured in ohms. (The 1/(2πf...) form is capacitive reactance.)

questions.q: As frequency increases, inductive reactance (XL):
questions.expl: XL = 2πfL, so higher frequency means higher inductive reactance. (Capacitive reactance does the opposite.)
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Find XL for L = 0.146 H at 60 cps.
With 110 V across an inductor whose XL = 55 ohms, the current is:
questions.expl: I = E / XL = 110 / 55 = 2 amperes.

questions.q: In the memory aid 'ELI the ICE man,' the 'ELI' part means:
options: Current leads voltage in an inductor
Voltage equals current in an inductor
Voltage (E) leads current (I) in an inductor (L)
questions.short: ELI: E leads I in L
questions.expl: ELI: in an inductor (L), voltage (E) leads current (I) - current lags the voltage.

questions.q: In a purely inductive AC circuit, the current:
options: Is exactly in phase with voltage
questions.expl: Inductance causes the current to lag the applied voltage by 90 degrees (ELI).

questions.q: What does a capacitor oppose, and where does it store energy?
options: A change in current; in a magnetic field
A change in frequency; as heat
A change in voltage; in an electric (electrostatic) field
questions.expl: A capacitor opposes a CHANGE in voltage and stores energy in an electric (electrostatic) field between its plates.

questions.q: A capacitor is basically constructed of:
options: A coil of many turns of wire
Two parallel plates separated by a dielectric
An iron core with two windings
questions.expl: A capacitor is two parallel plates separated by an insulator called the dielectric.

questions.q: The basic unit of capacitance is the farad, defined as:
options: 1 coulomb of charge stored at 1 volt
questions.expl: One farad is one coulomb of charge stored with one volt across the plates.

questions.q: One microfarad (µF) equals:
questions.expl: A microfarad is 10^-6 farad; a picofarad (pF) is 10^-12 farad.

questions.q: Which change INCREASES capacitance?
options: Using a dielectric with a lower K
questions.expl: Closer plate spacing increases capacitance. Larger plate area and a higher-K dielectric also increase it.

questions.q: How do capacitors combine when connected in PARALLEL?
options: They simply add: CT = C1 + C2 + C3
The total is the smallest value
questions.expl: Capacitors in parallel ADD directly (effective plate area increases) - the OPPOSITE of resistors in parallel.

questions.q: C1 = 330 µF and C2 = 220 µF are wired in parallel. Total capacitance?
questions.expl: In parallel, capacitances add: 330 + 220 = 550 µF.

questions.q: Capacitors connected in SERIES are calculated:
options: By the reciprocal rule, like resistors in parallel
The same as inductors in series
questions.expl: Capacitors in series use the reciprocal rule (1/CT = 1/C1 + 1/C2 ...), like resistors in parallel; total is less than the smallest.

questions.q: The formula for capacitive reactance is:
questions.expl: Capacitive reactance XC = 1/(2πfC), in ohms.

questions.q: As frequency increases, capacitive reactance (XC):
questions.expl: Because XC = 1/(2πfC), higher frequency means LOWER capacitive reactance - opposite of inductive reactance.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Find XC for C = 200 µF (0.000200 F) at 60 cps.
The 'ICE' half of 'ELI the ICE man' means:
options: Voltage leads current in a capacitor
Current equals voltage in a capacitor
Current (I) leads voltage (E) in a capacitor (C)
questions.short: ICE: I leads E in C
questions.expl: ICE: in a capacitor (C), current (I) leads voltage (E).

questions.q: In a purely capacitive AC circuit, the current:
questions.expl: In a capacitive circuit the current reaches maximum ahead of voltage - it leads by 90 degrees (ICE).

questions.q: The RC time constant is the time for the capacitor voltage to reach what percent of the applied voltage?
questions.expl: One time constant (τ = R × C) is the time to reach 63.2% of applied voltage; ~5 time constants for full charge.

questions.q: For R = 10,000 ohms and C = 100 µF (0.0001 F), the time constant (τ = RC) is:
Before handling or testing a capacitor, you should always:
options: Discharge it (short the leads through a resistor)
questions.expl: A charged capacitor can hold a dangerous voltage; always discharge it before handling or testing.

questions.q: You test a capacitor with an ohmmeter. A GOOD capacitor will:
options: Read a steady mid-scale resistance forever
Momentarily read low, then climb to high/infinite
questions.expl: A good cap charges (brief low reading) and then the reading rises toward high/infinite as charging current stops.

questions.q: An ohmmeter on a capacitor reads 0 ohms and never changes. The capacitor is:
questions.expl: A reading stuck at 0 ohms means the plates are touching - the capacitor is shorted; its electrical qualities are gone.

questions.q: An ohmmeter on a capacitor reads infinite immediately with NO initial charging dip. This indicates:
questions.expl: No momentary charging current (no dip) means the cap never charges - it is open.

questions.q: Electrolytic capacitors are POLARIZED, which means:
options: They only work on DC of any polarity
They must be installed observing + and - polarity
questions.expl: Electrolytic caps have marked + and - leads and must be installed with correct polarity or the dielectric breaks down.

questions.q: By what process does energy cross from a transformer's primary to its secondary?
options: Mutual induction (a shared changing magnetic field)
questions.expl: The windings are not electrically connected; the changing magnetic field of the primary induces voltage in the secondary by mutual induction.

questions.q: A transformer will NOT operate on:
questions.expl: A transformer needs a CHANGING voltage to create a changing magnetic field. Pure steady DC produces no change, so no transformation.

questions.q: The transformer turns-ratio relationship is:
questions.expl: Voltage divides in proportion to turns: Vp/Vs = Np/Ns.

questions.q: A transformer with MORE turns on the secondary than the primary is a:
questions.expl: More secondary turns gives a higher secondary voltage - a step-UP transformer.

questions.q: If a transformer steps the voltage UP, what happens to the current?
options: It steps up by the same ratio
It steps down by the same ratio
questions.expl: Current is inverse to voltage (power in ≈ power out). Step voltage up = step current down by the same ratio.

questions.q: Primary = 240 V on 100 turns; secondary = 25 turns. Secondary voltage?
questions.expl: Vs = Vp × (Ns/Np) = 240 × (25/100) = 60 V (a step-down).

questions.q: Primary = 10 V on 50 turns; secondary = 500 turns. Secondary voltage?
A 4:1 step-up transformer is fed 10 V at 4 A on the primary. The secondary output is:
questions.expl: 4:1 step-up: voltage 10×4 = 40 V; current 4/4 = 1 A. Power (40 W) is unchanged.

questions.q: You check a transformer winding with an ohmmeter and it reads INFINITE ohms. The winding is:
questions.expl: A healthy winding shows continuity (low resistance). Infinite ohms means an open winding - the wire is broken.

questions.q: A transformer secondary that is OPEN will produce what output voltage with power on the primary?
questions.expl: An open secondary has no coupling action, so the fault shows as zero volts output.

questions.q: When checking for SHORTED turns, the affected winding's resistance will typically read:
questions.expl: Shorted turns bypass part of the winding, so its measured resistance reads lower than the normal specified value (and it overheats).

questions.q: A resistance check between a transformer's primary winding and its core should read:
options: The same as the winding resistance
questions.expl: Windings must be insulated from the core and each other, so that reading should be infinite. A low reading means insulation breakdown.

questions.q: The safest way to verify a transformer's ratio is correct is to:
options: Apply DC and measure resistance only
Leave the current-transformer secondary open under load
Apply safe AC and compare secondary/primary voltage to the rated turns ratio
questions.short: Compare V ratio to N ratio
questions.expl: Apply safe AC and compare the measured Vs/Vp to the rated Ns/Np. Never leave a current-transformer secondary open while energized.

questions.q: A semiconductor's electrical behavior places it where compared to other materials?
options: It is between a conductor and an insulator
It is a perfect insulator like glass
It has no electrons in its outer shell
It is a better conductor than copper
questions.expl: A semiconductor conducts better than an insulator but worse than a true conductor; its usefulness is that we can control when it conducts.

questions.q: Which two elements are the common semiconductor materials?
questions.expl: Silicon (Si) is the most common semiconductor; germanium (Ge) is the other. Boron and phosphorus are doping impurities, not the base material.

questions.q: What is doping?
options: Coating the crystal with copper oxide
Adding small amounts of impurities to increase current flow
Removing all free electrons from the crystal
questions.expl: Doping adds impurities that contribute either extra electrons or extra holes, creating N-type or P-type material.

questions.q: An N-type semiconductor's majority current carriers are:
questions.expl: N-type is doped with 5-electron (donor) impurities, leaving an excess of electrons as the majority carrier.

questions.q: A P-type semiconductor's majority current carriers are:
questions.expl: P-type is doped with 3-electron (acceptor) impurities, leaving 'holes' (missing electrons) as the majority carrier.

questions.q: A 'hole' in a semiconductor is best described as:
options: A physical crack in the crystal
An empty place where an electron would go, which acts like a positive charge
An extra electron that bonds to silicon
questions.short: An empty electron position acting positive
questions.expl: A hole is the absence of an electron; as electrons shift to fill holes, the hole appears to move as a positive charge.

questions.q: How many PN junctions does a basic diode have?
questions.expl: A diode is a single PN junction formed by joining P-type and N-type material. The '1N' prefix on diode part numbers means one junction.

questions.q: The fundamental job of a diode is to:
options: Store electric charge like a capacitor
Allow current to flow in one direction only
questions.expl: A diode is a one-way valve; it conducts when forward biased and blocks when reverse biased, which lets it rectify AC into DC.

questions.q: To FORWARD bias a diode, you connect:
options: Positive to the P side, negative to the N side
Both terminals to the same polarity
Nothing; a diode needs no bias
Positive to the N side, negative to the P side
questions.short: Positive to P, negative to N
questions.expl: Forward bias puts positive on the P (anode) and negative on the N (cathode), shrinking the barrier so current flows.

questions.q: A diode connected positive-to-N and negative-to-P is:
questions.expl: Positive to N / negative to P widens the depletion region and blocks current (only tiny leakage). This is reverse bias.

questions.q: Approximately what forward voltage drop does a SILICON diode have?
questions.expl: A silicon PN junction turns on at about 0.7 V; germanium is about 0.3 V.

questions.q: Approximately what forward voltage drop does a GERMANIUM diode have?
questions.expl: Germanium turns on at roughly 0.3 V, lower than silicon's 0.7 V.

questions.q: A PN-junction diode offers maximum resistance when it is:
questions.expl: Forward bias = very little resistance; reverse bias = maximum resistance (essentially blocks current).

questions.q: When a diode is reverse biased, the depletion region:
questions.expl: Reverse bias pulls carriers away from the junction, widening the depletion region and blocking current.

questions.q: The marked band (or dot) on a diode body indicates which terminal?
questions.expl: Manufacturers mark the cathode (N side) with a band, dot, colored stripe, or the letter 'k'.

questions.q: What is the primary function of a Zener diode?
options: Store energy in a magnetic field
questions.expl: A Zener operates in reverse breakdown and holds a constant voltage, acting as a voltage regulator.

questions.q: In what bias condition is a Zener diode normally operated?
questions.expl: Unlike an ordinary diode, the Zener is run in reverse at its rated breakdown voltage, where it conducts and regulates.

questions.q: A Zener diode behaves like what once its rated reverse voltage is reached?
questions.expl: At breakdown the Zener conducts and clamps the voltage, behaving like a constant-voltage source for regulation.

questions.q: An LED produces light when it is:
questions.expl: In a forward-biased LED, electrons falling into holes release energy as light instead of heat.

questions.q: Compared to an incandescent lamp, an LED offers all of these advantages EXCEPT:
questions.short: It does NOT produce more heat
questions.expl: LEDs produce LESS heat, not more; they also have longer life, lower voltage, and faster switching.

questions.q: How many terminals does a transistor have?
questions.expl: A transistor is a three-terminal device: emitter, base, and collector.

questions.q: The three terminals of a bipolar transistor are:
questions.expl: Emitter (heavily doped), base (thin, lightly doped control layer), and collector (moderately doped).

questions.q: The two primary functions of a transistor are to:
questions.expl: A transistor is used primarily to amplify signals and to switch (control) current within a circuit.

questions.q: Which transistor terminal is the CONTROL terminal?
questions.expl: The thin base layer is the control terminal; a small base current controls a large collector current.

questions.q: In a working transistor, a small current at the base controls a large current at the:
questions.expl: The core transistor rule: a small base current controls a large collector current (the basis of amplification).

questions.q: For a transistor to amplify, the emitter-base junction is ___ and the collector-base junction is ___.
questions.expl: Emitter-base is forward biased (low resistance) and collector-base is reverse biased (high resistance), producing power gain.

questions.q: An NPN transistor is built as:
options: P material between two N layers
N material between two P layers
questions.expl: NPN = a thin P layer sandwiched between two N layers. PNP is the opposite.

questions.q: On a schematic symbol, an NPN transistor's emitter arrow points:
questions.expl: NPN emitter arrow points OUT ('Not pointiN in'); PNP points IN ('Points iN').

questions.q: A transistor symbol whose emitter arrow points INWARD is which type?
questions.expl: Arrow pointing IN = PNP; arrow pointing OUT = NPN. The arrow is always on the emitter.

questions.q: The transistor terminal that is the most heavily doped and emits the carriers is the:
questions.expl: The emitter is heavily doped and emits carriers; the collector is moderately doped; the base is lightly doped and thin.

questions.q: Unlike the bipolar transistor (current-controlled), a FET is:
questions.expl: A FET (JFET/MOSFET) uses gate VOLTAGE to control source-to-drain current and has very high input resistance.

questions.q: Gain, as used with transistor amplifiers, means:
options: The magnitude of amplification (output vs. input)
questions.expl: Gain expresses how much the transistor amplifies; the common-emitter (CE) configuration gives the best voltage/current gain combination.

questions.q: A relay is best described as:
options: An electrically controlled switch that opens/closes contacts to operate another circuit
A pure mechanical hinge with no coil
questions.expl: A relay uses a small coil current to open/close contacts controlling a larger circuit, much like a transistor used as a switch.
questions.cite: NCATT AET Study Guide Std 14 p.21

questions.q: 'Normally Open' (NO) relay contacts are:
options: Held closed by a spring until energized
Held open by a spring until energized
questions.short: Held open by spring until energized
questions.expl: NO contacts are held open by a spring and close when the relay is energized; NC contacts are the opposite.
questions.cite: NCATT AET Study Guide Std 14 p.21

questions.q: 'DPDT' describes a switch that is:
questions.expl: DPDT = Double Pole Double Throw: two separate circuits, each able to connect to either of two positions.
questions.cite: NCATT AET Study Guide Std 14 p.20

questions.q: Which switch type is described as the MOST susceptible to shock and vibration in an aircraft?
questions.expl: The Study Guide notes the toggle switch is most susceptible to shock/vibration; its contacts may open momentarily then re-close.
questions.cite: NCATT AET Study Guide Std 14 p.21

questions.q: What is the overall job of a power supply circuit?
options: Turn messy AC into clean, steady DC
questions.expl: A power supply steps down, rectifies, filters, and regulates so an avionics box gets steady DC.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: In the power-supply chain, which order is correct?
questions.expl: Transformer steps down, diodes rectify, cap/choke filters, Zener/regulator holds it steady.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A rectifier converts AC into DC using which component?
questions.expl: Diodes allow current to flow one direction only, which is rectification.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: How many diodes are in a HALF-WAVE rectifier?
questions.expl: A half-wave rectifier uses a single diode and passes only one half of the AC wave.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: How many diodes are in a full-wave BRIDGE rectifier?
questions.expl: A full-wave bridge uses four diodes and needs no center-tapped transformer.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters
AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: How many diodes does a full-wave CENTER-TAP rectifier use?
questions.expl: A center-tap full-wave rectifier uses two diodes with a center-tapped transformer.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Which rectifier produces the MOST ripple (roughest output)?
questions.expl: Half-wave throws away half the AC cycle, leaving big gaps and the most ripple.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Why is a full-wave rectifier smoother than a half-wave?
options: It flips the negative half-cycle up, filling the gaps
questions.expl: Full-wave conducts on both alternations so current always flows the same direction through the load with no gaps.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: The output of a rectifier (before filtering) is best described as:
questions.expl: Rectified output is DC that pulses; it never reverses but is not yet smooth.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: 'Ripple' in a power supply refers to:
options: The leftover AC bumpiness riding on the DC output
The cutoff frequency of a filter
questions.expl: Ripple is the residual AC variation in a rectifier's DC output; less ripple = cleaner DC.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, a diode is formed when:
options: Two resistors are placed in series
An N-type material is joined with a P-type material
A coil is wound on iron
questions.expl: A diode is the simplest semiconductor, an N-type joined to a P-type (a PN junction).
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: About how much forward voltage does a silicon diode need to start conducting?
questions.expl: It takes roughly 0.7 VDC of correct polarity to forward-bias a silicon diode.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A reverse-biased diode behaves like:
questions.expl: Reverse bias is a high-resistance state; the diode blocks current and acts like an open switch.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: What does the FILTER stage of a power supply do?
options: Smooths pulsating DC into nearly flat DC
questions.expl: The filter removes ripple, turning pulsating DC into nearly steady DC.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: In a capacitor filter, how is the capacitor connected to the load?
questions.expl: The filter capacitor is connected in parallel with the load to bypass AC ripple to ground.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: How does a capacitor smooth the rectifier output?
options: It charges on the pulse peaks and discharges into the load during the valleys
It converts DC back to AC
questions.short: Charges on peaks, discharges in valleys
questions.expl: Like a reservoir, the cap fills the valleys by discharging when the rectifier output dips, keeping voltage constant.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: In a filter, the inductor (choke) is connected:
questions.expl: A filter choke is placed in series; it offers high impedance to AC ripple and low impedance to DC.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Why does a series choke help filter ripple?
options: High impedance to AC ripple, low impedance to DC
It shorts the DC to ground
questions.expl: An inductor opposes current change, so it blocks the AC ripple while passing DC.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A filter shaped C-choke-C, resembling a Greek letter, is called a:
questions.expl: A pi-type filter (capacitor, choke, capacitor) resembles the Greek letter pi and gives strong smoothing.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, a PASSIVE filter:
options: Produces a current or voltage gain
Produces no gain and needs no external power
questions.expl: Passive filters use only R, L, and C, produce no gain, and require no external power source.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, an ACTIVE filter:
options: Produces a current or voltage gain
Is the same as a rectifier
questions.expl: An active filter produces a current or voltage gain and uses an amplifier plus R/C.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: What is the job of the REGULATION stage?
options: Keep the output voltage constant as the load current changes
questions.expl: Regulation keeps output voltage constant despite load or input changes.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 15 (Power Supply Circuits); FAA-H-8083-30B p.12-103
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Which component is the classic voltage regulator that holds DC steady at a set value?
questions.expl: A Zener diode breaks down at a chosen reverse voltage and acts like a constant-voltage source.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: How is a Zener diode operated when used as a regulator?
options: Reverse biased at its breakdown voltage
questions.expl: A Zener regulates by operating in reverse breakdown, where it holds a constant voltage.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A LOW-PASS filter:
questions.expl: A low-pass filter passes low frequencies and attenuates frequencies above the cutoff.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters
AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A HIGH-PASS filter:
questions.expl: A high-pass filter passes high frequencies and attenuates those below the cutoff; it is the opposite of low-pass.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A BAND-PASS filter:
options: Rejects a middle band of frequencies
Passes a middle band while attenuating high and low frequencies
questions.expl: A band-pass filter passes a chosen middle band and attenuates frequencies above and below it.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A BAND-STOP (band-reject / notch) filter:
options: Passes most frequencies but attenuates one middle band
Is the same as a low-pass filter
questions.expl: A band-stop/notch filter passes most frequencies but strongly attenuates one band; it is the opposite of band-pass.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A band-pass filter is built by combining a high-pass and low-pass filter connected:
questions.expl: Connecting a low-pass and high-pass in series forms a band-pass filter.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A band-stop filter is built by combining a high-pass and low-pass filter connected:
questions.expl: Connecting a low-pass and high-pass in parallel forms a band-stop (notch) filter.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: The Study Guide says frequency-sensitive filters act as:
questions.expl: Filters sit between source and load and act as frequency-sensitive voltage dividers.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, the 'cutoff frequency' is:
options: The frequency where attenuation starts to increase rapidly
questions.expl: Cutoff frequency is the frequency at which attenuation begins to increase rapidly.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Quartz crystals used in precise filters work because they:
options: Vibrate at a specific resonant frequency when voltage is applied
questions.expl: Piezoelectric quartz crystals vibrate at a set resonant frequency when voltage is applied, giving very selective filtering.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: A three-phase rectifier output, compared to a single-phase half-wave output, has:
questions.expl: Overlapping phases make three-phase rectifier output very smooth, with low ripple even before filtering.
sources: AET Academy M6 — Power Supplies & Filters

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, an oscillator is an electronic circuit that:
options: Converts AC into DC at a fixed voltage
Increases only the current of a signal
Converts DC into AC at predetermined frequencies and amplitudes
questions.expl: The Study Guide defines an oscillator as a circuit that converts DC into AC at predetermined frequencies and amplitudes.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of an oscillator?
options: To rectify AC into pulsating DC
To regulate a constant DC output voltage
To generate a repetitive waveform at a constant amplitude and specific frequency
questions.expl: Its primary purpose is to generate a repetitive waveform at constant (peak-to-peak) amplitude and a specific frequency, and hold it within limits.

questions.q: Which three things must an oscillator have to keep oscillating?
options: Amplification, positive feedback, and a frequency-determining network
A transformer, a rectifier, and a filter
Two resistors and one capacitor only
A battery, a switch, and a relay
questions.expl: An oscillator needs amplification (gain), positive/regenerative feedback, and a frequency-determining LC/RC/crystal network.

questions.q: Why does an oscillator need an amplifier?
options: To convert its output to DC
To raise the supply voltage to 115 V
To replace energy lost to wiring resistance so oscillation does not damp out
questions.expl: Without the amplifier, oscillation would quickly be damped out by the resistance in the circuit wiring.

questions.q: In an oscillator, the type of feedback that 'makes any amplifier an oscillator' is:
questions.expl: A regenerative (positive) feedback path makes any amplifier an oscillator.

questions.q: The part of an oscillator that sets the output frequency is the:
questions.expl: An LC tank, RC network, or crystal is the frequency-determining network that sets the oscillator's frequency.

questions.q: An LC tank oscillator produces its sinusoidal output because it:
options: Stores energy alternately in the inductor and the capacitor
Switches a transistor fully on and off
Rectifies the input with four diodes
questions.expl: The LC tank stores energy alternately in the inductor and capacitor, which produces a sinusoidal (sine-wave) output.

questions.q: A Hartley oscillator controls its frequency using a:
options: Tapped coil in parallel with a capacitor
questions.expl: A Hartley oscillator uses a tapped coil in parallel with a capacitor to control the AC frequency.

questions.q: In a crystal-controlled oscillator, the output frequency is determined by:
options: The resonant frequency of the piezoelectric crystal
questions.expl: The output frequency is set by the resonant frequency of the piezoelectric crystal in the oscillator.

questions.q: Which oscillator is the MOST stable and accurate in frequency?
questions.expl: A quartz crystal's resonant frequency barely drifts, so a crystal-controlled oscillator is the most stable/accurate.

questions.q: A piezoelectric quartz crystal works because it:
options: Stores DC charge like a capacitor
Vibrates at a specific resonant frequency when voltage is applied
questions.expl: A piezoelectric crystal vibrates at one specific resonant frequency when voltage is applied, giving precise frequency control.

questions.q: A crystal-controlled oscillator typically produces what waveform?
questions.expl: A crystal-controlled oscillator produces a sine wave.
