questions.q: Which waveform ramps up steadily and then drops back sharply to zero?
questions.expl: A sawtooth waveform ramps up gradually then snaps back down; it is used to sweep CRT/radar displays.

questions.q: A waveform that snaps between two flat levels (HIGH and LOW) with vertical sides is a:
questions.expl: A square wave has flat tops/bottoms and vertical transitions; it is common for clocks and digital timing.

questions.q: A multivibrator is best described as a circuit in which:
options: Four diodes rectify an AC input
Two transistors alternate conducting to make square/pulse waveforms
A coil and capacitor store energy to make a sine wave
A single diode clips a waveform
questions.expl: A multivibrator uses two transistors that take turns conducting, producing square waves and pulses.

questions.q: An ASTABLE multivibrator has:
options: Two conditions of temporary stability and no permanent stable state
questions.expl: An astable multivibrator has two temporarily stable conditions and no permanent stable state, so it free-runs.

questions.q: A MONOSTABLE multivibrator (one-shot) has:
options: One permanent stable mode of operation
questions.expl: A monostable multivibrator has one permanent stable mode; a trigger produces a single pulse, then it returns.

questions.q: In a BISTABLE multivibrator, the transistors:
options: Have no stable state and oscillate freely
One conducts until an external pulse stops it, then the other takes over
questions.expl: A bistable multivibrator has one transistor conduct until a pulse stops it, then the other takes over; it has two stable states (a flip-flop).

questions.q: Which multivibrator is 'free-running' and produces a continuous output without any trigger?
questions.expl: The astable multivibrator has no stable state, so it free-runs and continuously generates an output.

questions.q: A multivibrator that acts as a memory cell / flip-flop, needing a trigger to change and staying put either way, is the:
questions.expl: The bistable (flip-flop) has two stable states and needs a trigger to switch between them, making it a memory element.

questions.q: What is the main job of a limiter (clipper) circuit?
options: To clip/limit a waveform's amplitude to a set level
To store charge for smoothing ripple
To step a voltage up or down
questions.expl: A limiter (clipper) clips or limits the amplitude of a waveform to protect a circuit or shape the signal.

questions.q: Limiter/clipper circuits are built mainly from which component?
questions.expl: Clippers are built mostly from diodes (sometimes zeners), using forward/reverse bias to clip the wave.

questions.q: A diode clips a waveform because, depending on the signal polarity, it is either:
questions.expl: A clipping diode conducts when forward biased and blocks when reverse biased, which removes part of the wave.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 19 (Forward/Reverse Bias); FAA-H-8083-30B p.12-103

questions.q: In a SHUNT (parallel) clipper, the diode is connected:
options: In parallel across the load and shorts part of the signal to ground when it conducts
In series with the load only
In place of the load resistor
questions.expl: A shunt clipper places the diode in parallel with the load; when it conducts it shunts that part of the signal away, holding the output flat.

questions.q: In a SERIES clipper, the diode is connected:
options: In series (in line) with the signal path to the load
questions.expl: A series clipper places the diode in line with the signal; when reverse biased it opens the path and blocks that part of the wave.

questions.q: What does adding a bias voltage to a diode clipper do (a 'biased clipper')?
options: Removes the need for the diode
Converts the clipper into a rectifier
Sets the level at which clipping begins
questions.expl: A bias voltage in series with the diode raises the clip 'ceiling', setting where the waveform begins to be clipped.

questions.q: Per the FAA handbook, a zener diode can be used as a:
questions.expl: The handbook states the zener functions as a voltage regulator, waveform clipper, and related functions.

questions.q: To clip BOTH the top and bottom of an AC waveform with zener diodes, you must use:
options: Two zener diodes connected in opposite directions
questions.expl: A zener clips only one direction, so two zeners in opposite directions are needed to clip both alternations of AC.

questions.q: What is the key difference between a clipper and a clamper?
options: A clipper removes part of the wave; a clamper shifts the whole wave up or down
They are two names for the same circuit
A clipper uses transformers; a clamper uses diodes
A clamper only works on DC; a clipper only on AC
questions.expl: A clipper cuts off part of the waveform (changes its shape); a clamper shifts the entire waveform by a DC level (keeps its shape).

questions.q: Which component, present in a clamper but not in a basic clipper, stores the DC offset?
questions.expl: A clamper uses a capacitor to store and add the DC level that shifts the waveform; a basic clipper does not need one.

questions.q: If you clip a sine wave hard on both the top and bottom, its shape begins to resemble a:
questions.expl: Heavily clipping a sine wave flattens both peaks, so it starts to look like a square wave; clippers shape signals this way.

questions.q: One practical reason to put a limiter ahead of a sensitive stage is to:
options: Protect that stage from over-amplitude (too-large) spikes
questions.expl: A limiter caps the amplitude so an oversized spike cannot reach and damage the downstream circuit.

questions.q: Why does digital electronics use the binary number system?
options: Because a switch (transistor) has only two states: on or off
Because binary is faster to write than decimal
Because binary can store decimal points
questions.expl: Binary is based on the fact that switches/valves have two states, open or closed (on/off) - matching a transistor that is either on (1) or off (0).

questions.q: What base (radix) does the BINARY numbering system operate in?
questions.expl: Binary operates in Base 2 and is composed of two digits, 1 and 0; its math is based on powers of two.

questions.q: Which numbering system uses the digits 0 through 7 and operates in Base 8?
questions.expl: The octal system is composed of eight digits (0-7) and operates in Base 8; each octal digit represents three bits (a triad).

questions.q: What base does the DECIMAL system operate in, and what digits does it use?
questions.expl: The decimal numbering system uses numbers 0-9 and operates in Base 10.

questions.q: The HEXADECIMAL system operates in Base 16. What does it use after the digit 9?
questions.expl: Hex is composed of 16 units: decimal 0-9 and the letters A-F standing for decimal 10-15.

questions.q: In hexadecimal, what decimal value does the letter C represent?
Convert the binary number 1011 to decimal.
questions.expl: Place values 8,4,2,1: add the 1s -> 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11.

questions.q: Convert the binary number 1101 to decimal.
Convert the binary number 10110011 to decimal.
questions.expl: 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 179 (the handbook's worked example).

questions.q: What is the place value of the leftmost bit in an 8-bit binary number?
questions.expl: An 8-bit number's place values are 128,64,32,16,8,4,2,1; the leftmost (most significant) bit is 128.

questions.q: Per the FAA method, how do you convert a binary number to decimal?
options: Add up the place values that have a 1 (ignore the zeros)
questions.expl: To convert binary to decimal, add up the place values that have a 1; places with a zero contribute nothing.

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, one way to convert a decimal number to binary is to:
options: Look it up in a hex table
Sequentially divide the number by 2 and record each remainder
questions.expl: A decimal number may be converted to binary by sequentially dividing by 2 and recording each remainder.

questions.q: Convert the decimal number 13 to binary.
questions.expl: 13 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 1101. (Divide-by-2 remainders read bottom-up also give 1101.)

questions.q: Convert the decimal number 233 to binary.
Convert the hexadecimal number 2F to decimal.
Convert the hexadecimal number 1A to decimal.
What is a 'bit'?
options: A single binary digit - one 0 or one 1
A group of four binary digits
questions.expl: A binary digit (bit) is each single zero or one.

questions.q: How many bits are in a BYTE?
questions.expl: A byte consists of eight bits.

questions.q: How many bits are in a NIBBLE?
questions.expl: A nibble is 4 bits - half a byte - and equals exactly one hexadecimal digit.

questions.q: One hexadecimal digit represents how many binary bits?
questions.expl: Each hexadecimal digit represents four bits (one nibble), which is why hex is shorthand for groups of 4 bits.

questions.q: In Binary Coded Decimal (BCD), how is each decimal digit handled?
options: Each decimal digit is written as its own 4-bit binary group
The whole number is converted to binary at once
Each digit is written in hexadecimal
questions.expl: BCD writes each decimal digit separately as a 4-bit nibble, so 47 = 0100 0111.

questions.q: A logic gate's behavior is described by a:
questions.expl: A truth table describes the behavior of a logic gate, listing the output for every input combination.

questions.q: An AND gate produces a 1 (high) output only when:
questions.expl: An AND gate outputs 1 only when ALL inputs are 1 - like two switches in series.

questions.q: An AND gate has inputs A=1 and B=0. What is the output?
questions.expl: AND requires ALL inputs to be 1; since B=0, the output is 0.

questions.q: An OR gate produces a 1 output when:
questions.expl: A high on any one of the inputs produces a high output; OR outputs 0 only when all inputs are 0.

questions.q: An OR gate has inputs A=0 and B=0. What is the output?
questions.expl: An OR gate outputs 0 only when ALL inputs are 0.

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, which statement about the NOT gate / inverter is correct?
options: It is a gate with two inputs
It outputs 1 only when both inputs are 1
It has one input and one output that are always in opposition (1 in gives 0 out)
It requires inputs to be different
questions.expl: The inverter performs the NOT function, has one input and one output always in opposition (1 in -> 0 out), and is a logic device, not a gate.

questions.q: The NAND gate is equivalent to:
options: An OR gate followed by an inverter
An AND gate followed by an inverter
questions.expl: NAND means NOT AND; it is an AND gate usually followed by an inverter, so its output is the AND result flipped.

questions.q: A NAND gate has inputs A=1 and B=1. What is the output?
questions.expl: NAND outputs 0 only when ALL inputs are 1 (it is the opposite of AND, which would output 1 here).

questions.q: The NOR gate is equivalent to:
options: An AND gate followed by an inverter
An OR gate followed by an inverter
questions.expl: NOR means NOT OR; it is an OR gate followed by an inverter, so its output is the OR result flipped.

questions.q: A NOR gate outputs a 1 only when:
questions.expl: NOR is the opposite of OR: its output is 1 only when ALL inputs are 0; any 1 makes it 0.

questions.q: An EXCLUSIVE OR (XOR) gate produces a 1 output when:
options: The two inputs are the same
questions.expl: The EXCLUSIVE OR gate requires its inputs to be different to obtain an output.

questions.q: An XOR gate has inputs A=1 and B=1. What is the output?
questions.expl: XOR outputs 1 only when inputs differ; with both inputs the same (1 and 1), the output is 0.

questions.q: Which two gates are called the 'universal' gates?
questions.expl: NAND and NOR are universal because any other logic function can be built using only NAND gates or only NOR gates.
questions.cite: FAA-H-8083-30B p.12-125 to 12-126; Study Guide Std 21

questions.q: In Boolean logic used by digital circuits, the two truth values true and false are represented by:
questions.expl: Boolean logic uses only two truth values, true and false, represented by the binary digits 1 and 0.

questions.q: Under POSITIVE logic, which voltage level represents a 1?
questions.expl: In positive logic the more positive (higher) voltage stands for 1 and the lower voltage stands for 0; negative logic reverses this.

questions.q: An energized electrical fire (live wiring, motors, panels) is which class of fire?
questions.expl: Class C fires involve energized electrical wiring and equipment; use a non-conductive agent.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Which extinguisher must you AVOID on a Class C (electrical) fire?
questions.expl: Water conducts electricity; using it on energized equipment can electrocute the operator. Use CO2, Halon, or dry chemical.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Class A fires involve:
options: Flammable liquids like fuel and solvents
Ordinary combustibles like wood, paper, and cloth
questions.expl: Class A fires involve ordinary combustible materials such as wood, cloth, paper, and upholstery.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Class B fires involve:
options: Flammable petroleum products, greases, solvents, and paints
questions.expl: Class B fires involve flammable liquids: fuel, oil, grease, solvents, and paint.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: A Class D fire involves what, and which extinguishing agent is correct?
options: Flammable metals like magnesium; dry powder only
questions.expl: Class D fires involve flammable metals (e.g., magnesium). Use dry powder only; water or CO2 can cause explosive expansion of the hot metal.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: A CO2 extinguisher is effective on which fire classes?
questions.expl: CO2 extinguishers are used on Class A, B, and C fires by smothering (removing oxygen). Never use CO2 on Class D.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Three things must be present at once for a fire (the fire triangle). They are:
questions.expl: Fire requires fuel, heat, and oxygen; remove any one and the fire goes out.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: In an electrical shock, what actually injures or kills the body?
options: The current (amperes) flowing through the body
questions.expl: It is the current passing through the body that causes injury; voltage is only the push that drives it. Very small currents can be lethal.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: What is the FIRST action when someone is being electrically shocked?
options: Pull them off with your bare hands immediately
Turn off the power as quickly as possible
questions.expl: Turn off the power first. If you can't, do not touch the victim or the current passes through you too.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 22 (First Aid for Electrical Shock)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: If you cannot shut off the power, how should you move a shock victim clear of the source?
options: Splash water to break the contact
Use a dry, non-conducting object like a dry wooden pole or broom
questions.expl: Use a dry, non-conducting object (wooden pole, broom, chair) to push or pull the victim clear without touching them.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 22 (First Aid for Electrical Shock)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Locking a switch or breaker OFF and tagging it so no one can re-energize the circuit is called:
questions.expl: Lockout/tagout devices shut down, isolate, block, and secure equipment to control hazardous energy sources.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 22 (Elimination/Termination of Circuit Power)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, PPE selection considers the ways hazards enter the body. Which list is correct?
options: Inhalation, skin absorption, ingestion, eye/skin contact
questions.expl: PPE addresses inhalation, skin absorption, ingestion, and eye or skin contact, and is hazard-specific.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Circuit protection devices listed in the Study Guide include:
options: Fuses, current limiters, thermal protectors, and circuit breakers
questions.expl: Circuit protection devices include fuses, current limiters, thermal protectors (switches), and circuit breakers.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: A Safety Data Sheet (SDS/MSDS) provides information such as:
options: Toxicity, health effects, first aid, storage, disposal, and spill handling
questions.expl: An SDS lists physical data, toxicity, health effects, first aid, reactivity, storage, disposal, protective equipment, and spill handling.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 23 (Material Safety Data Sheet)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Who is legally required to supply the SDS to the purchaser of a chemical?
questions.expl: Hazardous chemical suppliers are required by law to supply MSDSs/SDSs to purchasers.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Which is a hard rule for hazardous-waste disposal?
options: Pour solvents down the sink if diluted
Burn oily rags in the open
Never pour hazardous waste down a drain or storm drain
questions.expl: Never pour hazardous materials down a drain or storm drain; it damages wastewater plants or contaminates lakes and streams.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 23 (Specifics to Follow at Jobsite)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Which federal agency has authority to control hazardous waste?
questions.expl: The EPA controls the generation, transportation, treatment, storage, and disposal of hazardous waste.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, every chemical container label must list:
options: Substance/trade name, manufacturer, hazards, and date
questions.expl: Containers must carry waterproof labels listing the substance/trade name, manufacturer, hazards, and date.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Acetone is described in the Study Guide as:
questions.expl: Acetone is an organic solvent that is colorless, volatile, and extremely flammable; use with ventilation and away from ignition.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: In FOD terminology, 'Foreign Object Debris' refers to:
options: The damage caused to an engine
A substance or article alien to the system that could cause damage
questions.expl: Foreign Object Debris is any substance, debris, or article alien to a vehicle/system that has invaded the product and could cause damage.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: A painted outline showing exactly where each tool belongs, so a missing tool is obvious, is called a:
questions.expl: A shadow box/board is a tool-control method: an empty outline immediately shows a tool is missing.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 24 (Tool Control and Management)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Why is a single dropped washer or safety-wire clipping treated so seriously on an aircraft?
options: It violates a paperwork rule only
It can be ingested by an engine, jam a control, or short a connector and cause catastrophic damage
questions.expl: Small FOD can be ingested by an engine, jam flight controls, or short electrical connectors, causing catastrophic damage.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: The Study Guide's 'clean as you go' rule includes which famous instruction?
options: Leave debris for the cleanup crew
If you drop something or hear something drop, pick it up
Only clean at the end of the month
questions.expl: Clean as you go: inspect/clean tools after each job and 'if you drop something or hear something drop, pick it up.'
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 24 (Clean As You Go)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, ESD (electrostatic discharge) is:
options: A slow leak of current through a resistor
The instantaneous discharge of static electricity from one surface to another
questions.expl: ESD is the instantaneous discharge of static electricity built up on one point and discharged to another.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Why is ESD so dangerous to microelectronics?
options: A static zap too small for a person to feel can still destroy a circuit board or chip
questions.expl: Static-sensitive devices can be damaged by a discharge far below what a person can feel, causing catastrophic, delayed, or degraded failure.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 27 (Handling of Electrostatic Devices)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: For an ESD wrist strap to protect a part, it must be:
questions.expl: A conductive wrist strap must connect the body to earth ground so static charge drains off; ungrounded, it does nothing.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Which shop condition makes static-electricity (ESD) buildup WORSE?
questions.expl: Dry, low-humidity air lets static build up; higher humidity bleeds charge off and reduces ESD risk.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 27 (Handling of Electrostatic Devices)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: The three kinds of electrostatic damage listed in the Study Guide are:
options: Catastrophic failure, delayed failure, and degradation
questions.expl: Electrostatic damage includes total catastrophic failure, delayed failure, and degradation to the circuit.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Why is safety wire (lock wire) installed on aircraft fasteners?
options: To make the fastener look finished
So vibration can only tighten the fastener, never loosen it
questions.expl: Safety wire secures non-self-locking fasteners/connectors so the constant vibration of flight cannot loosen them.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: What is the general-purpose diameter of safety wire used for securing equipment fasteners?
questions.expl: 0.032-inch safety wire is the general-purpose size; use 0.020-inch on electrical connectors and small screws.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 29 (Types and Sizes of Safety Wire)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, safety-wire clippings are:
options: Harmless and can be left in place
A serious FOD hazard that must be tracked and disposed of
Recycled into new wire on the spot
questions.expl: Keep track of safety-wire clippings and dispose of them properly because they pose a serious FOD hazard.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 29 (Acceptable Safety Wire Practices)
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: What is the main difference between a fuse and a circuit breaker?
options: A fuse is resettable; a breaker melts
A breaker only works on AC and a fuse only on DC
A fuse melts and is replaced; a circuit breaker trips and can be reset
questions.expl: Both open the circuit on overcurrent to protect wiring/equipment; a fuse is sacrificial (replace it), a breaker is resettable.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: A cotter pin is used to:
options: Filter ripple from a power supply
Mechanically lock a castle nut or bolt so it cannot unscrew
questions.expl: A cotter pin is inserted through a hole and bent over to mechanically lock a nut/bolt against loosening; it is single-use.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Shear wire is used on emergency equipment because it is designed to:
options: Break when a force is applied
questions.expl: Shear wire (copper/brass) is used on switch handles, exit-handle guards, and fire extinguishers; it breaks when force is applied.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: A label or sign warning of a hazard or stating a limitation (e.g., 'HIGH VOLTAGE') is a:
questions.expl: Warning placards communicate hazards or limits and are a key safety device for alerting personnel.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Roughly how much current through the chest can be enough to stop a person's heart?
questions.expl: As little as roughly 0.1-0.2 A across the chest can cause fatal heart fibrillation; current, not voltage, is the killer.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: Before working on a circuit, a 'job briefing' (Study Guide) covers all of these EXCEPT:
options: Energy source controls and PPE requirements
The current resale value of the aircraft
questions.expl: A job briefing covers job hazards, work procedures, special precautions, energy-source controls, and PPE requirements.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: The standard way to operate most fire extinguishers is best summarized as:
options: Aim at the top of the flames and hold still
Pull the pin, aim at the base of the fire, squeeze, and sweep side to side
questions.expl: Pull the pin, aim at the base of the fire, squeeze the lever, and sweep side to side (the PASS method), standing about 8 feet back.
sources: AET Academy M9 — Safety & Shop Practices

questions.q: What is the number-one rule when performing any maintenance task?
options: Always work to the approved data / manufacturer's instructions
Work from memory once you've done it before
Ask another tech instead of reading the manual
questions.expl: You always consult the manuals and approved data; never guess from memory.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Which document is the FAA legal order that REQUIRES you to fix an unsafe condition?
questions.expl: An AD is mandatory FAA action with a compliance timeframe to correct an unsafe condition.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Compliance with a manufacturer's Service Bulletin (SB) is:
options: Not required unless the SB is referenced by an AD
questions.expl: An SB is a recommendation; it is not FAR-required unless an AD makes it mandatory.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Which publication shows how components are connected electrically using standardized symbols, used to troubleshoot and repair circuits?
questions.expl: Wiring diagrams use standardized symbols to show electrical connections for troubleshooting/repair.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: A SCHEMATIC diagram is best described as one that:
options: Shows the exact physical location of every part
Lists all the aircraft's part numbers
Uses symbols (not pictures) and does not show physical location; used mainly for troubleshooting
Is the FAA's legal repair order
questions.expl: A schematic uses symbols rather than pictures and does not show physical locations; it's for troubleshooting.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: A diagram that shows functional units as BLOCKS with arrows for the flow between them is a:
questions.expl: A block diagram shows functional blocks and signal/energy flow; high-level, used for troubleshooting or teaching.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Which publication uses callouts to identify and locate each component so you can find part numbers and order parts?
questions.expl: The IPC/installation diagram pictures the system with callouts identifying and locating components.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, what should you consult if NO maintenance manual exists for maintaining an aircraft?
questions.expl: The Study Guide says to consult AC 43.13-1B/2B when no maintenance manual exists.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: An FAA-approved list of equipment that may be inoperative while the aircraft still legally flies is the:
questions.expl: The MEL lists installed equipment that may be inoperative while operation continues (14 CFR 91.213).
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: A Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) is an FAA document that approves:
options: A design alteration to an aircraft (e.g., a new avionics installation)
questions.expl: An STC approves an aircraft type-design alteration, such as installing new equipment.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Aircraft maintenance manuals are organized by a standardized numbering scheme so each system is filed the same way. That scheme is the:
questions.expl: The ATA 100 chapter system standardizes how systems are numbered across manufacturers.
questions.cite: FAA tech-pub practice; Study Guide Std 25
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: To quickly find a procedure in a manual, the Study Guide recommends you:
options: Read every page front to back
Check the Table of Contents and index, and use the legend/key and callouts
questions.expl: The Guide lists checking the TOC/index, reading the legend/key for symbols, and following callouts.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: The NCATT proficiency rules for common tools include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Choose the tool best suited for the job
Recognize some tools have more than one use
Use any tool that fits, even if it's the wrong one
Handle and care for tools safely
questions.expl: You must choose the tool best suited for the job, not just any tool that fits.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Why is applying the correct TORQUE to a fastener important?
options: Too little lets it vibrate loose; too much strips/cracks the part
questions.expl: Under-torque vibrates loose (intermittents); over-torque strips threads or cracks the part.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: A torque value is listed as 360 inch-pounds. In foot-pounds that is:
questions.expl: Divide inch-pounds by 12: 360 / 12 = 30 ft-lb.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: To convert inch-pounds to foot-pounds you:
questions.expl: There are 12 inches in a foot, so ft-lb = in-lb / 12.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Which is NOT one of the three common torque-wrench types in the handbook?
questions.expl: The handbook shows flexible-beam, rigid-frame (dial), and ratchet (click) torque wrenches.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Per the standard torque table rules, you should generally:
options: Always exceed the maximum value for safety
Tighten by rotating the nut (not the bolt head) when possible
Use any extension without changing the reading
questions.expl: Tighten by rotating the nut when possible; don't lubricate unless told; never exceed max.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Using a drive-end extension on a torque wrench:
options: Has no effect on the reading
Changes the dial reading needed, so it must be recomputed with the proper formula
questions.expl: An extension changes the effective lever length, so the wrench reading must be recomputed.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Which measuring tool gives the most precise reading of a small thickness or diameter (about 0.001 in)?
questions.expl: A micrometer reads to about 0.001 in (vernier mic to 0.0001 in), finer than a rule.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: What is the purpose of CALIBRATION of a torque wrench or multimeter?
options: To check it against a known standard so its readings are accurate
questions.expl: Calibration verifies a tool against a known standard; out-of-cal tools are removed from service.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: A crimping tool is used to:
options: Form a gas-tight mechanical/electrical bond between a contact and a wire
questions.expl: A crimper forms a solid gas-tight connection between a terminal/contact and the wire; the right die matters.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: When stripping a wire, why must you avoid nicking the conductor strands?
options: A nicked strand is a weak, high-resistance, corrosion-prone spot that can fail
questions.expl: A nick reduces cross-section, raises resistance, and invites corrosion and breakage.
questions.cite: Study Guide Std 26; FAA wiring practice
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: The Study Guide defines corrosion as the:
options: Heating of a wire under load
Chemical or electrochemical deterioration of a material (usually metal) due to its environment
questions.expl: Corrosion is chemical or electrochemical deterioration of metal reacting with its environment; may be visible or not.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Why is corrosion a special concern for an AVIONICS technician?
options: It only affects the airframe, not avionics
It corrodes connector pins, terminals, and grounds, causing intermittent faults and lost reliability
It only matters on the engine
questions.expl: Corrosion adds resistance at pins/terminals/grounds, causing intermittent faults; it's a major reliability killer.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: In a galvanic couple, which metal corrodes?
options: The more active (less noble) metal becomes the anode and corrodes
questions.expl: The more active/less noble metal becomes the anode and corrodes; the noble metal is the cathode.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Electrochemical corrosion requires which combination to occur?
options: An anode, a cathode, and a conductive liquid (electrolyte)
questions.expl: You need a difference in potential (anode/cathode) plus an electrolyte bridging them.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Removing which single factor will stop electrochemical corrosion and is the basis of corrosion control?
questions.expl: Removing the conductive medium (moisture) breaks the cell so corrosion cannot occur.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Pitting corrosion is best recognized as:
options: Concentrated localized pits, usually with white or gray powder
A green powder over the whole part
questions.expl: Pitting is concentrated cell corrosion localized in pits, usually with white or gray powder.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Corrosion that forms WITHIN the metal along its grain boundaries is called:
questions.expl: Intergranular corrosion attacks along the metal's grain boundaries, often hidden inside the metal.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Exfoliation corrosion is:
options: An extreme form of intergranular corrosion that lifts the metal into layers, destroying strength
The same as rust on steel
questions.expl: Exfoliation is severe intergranular corrosion that separates/lifts metal layers and ruins strength.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: You find a white, powdery deposit on an aluminum part. By contrast, corrosion on a STEEL part appears as:
questions.expl: Aluminum/magnesium corrosion is white/gray powder; steel/iron corrosion is reddish-brown rust.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Corrosion on copper, brass, or bronze typically appears as:
options: A green or blue-green powdery deposit
questions.expl: Copper-base metals form a green or blue-green powdery corrosion product.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Per the handbook, every corrosion inspection begins with:
options: A thorough cleaning of the area to be inspected
questions.expl: All corrosion inspections start with a thorough cleaning of the area, then a visual inspection.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Which is the MOST effective barrier for preventing corrosion on a metal surface?
questions.expl: A good intact paint finish is the most effective barrier between metal and corrosive media.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: The BEST way to prevent galvanic corrosion at a joint of two dissimilar metals is to:
options: Insulate/separate the two metals (finishes, sealant) so no electrolyte can bridge them
questions.expl: Breaking the cell by insulating/separating the metals (Study Guide: insulation and sealants) prevents galvanic corrosion.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Avionics copper conductors are plated with tin, silver, or nickel mainly to:
questions.expl: Conductor plating (tin, silver, nickel) protects copper from corrosion/oxidation.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: Why should you NEVER use steel wool or a steel brush to clean corrosion off an aluminum part?
options: Embedded steel particles start NEW galvanic corrosion on the aluminum
questions.expl: Steel particles left on aluminum form dissimilar-metal couples and start fresh galvanic corrosion.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: The general corrosion TREATMENT sequence is:
options: Clean/strip, remove corrosion, neutralize residue, restore the protective film, recoat
Only wipe it with a dry rag
questions.expl: Clean/strip, remove corrosion, neutralize residue in pits, restore surface film, apply protective coating.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: If corrosion damage EXCEEDS the manufacturer's allowable limits, you must:
options: Ignore it until the next check
Replace the part (or repair per the structural repair manual)
questions.expl: When corrosion exceeds damage limits, the part must be replaced or repaired per the manual.
sources: AET Academy M10 — Tools, Pubs & Corrosion

questions.q: In the American Wire Gauge (AWG) system, as the gauge NUMBER gets larger, the wire diameter:
questions.expl: AWG is backwards from intuition: a larger gauge number means a smaller-diameter wire (e.g., #22 is thin, #4 is fat).
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Per the Study Guide, which is NOT one of the wire-sizing factors for AWG?
questions.expl: Wire sizing factors are allowable power loss, permissible voltage drop, and current-carrying ability.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Per AC 43.13-1B, wires must be sized so they all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Do not exceed allowable voltage drop
Are always the smallest gauge available
Are protected by the circuit protection device
questions.expl: Wires must have mechanical strength, stay within voltage-drop limits, be protected by the breaker/fuse, and meet current requirements; smallest gauge is not a goal.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: The voltage drop in main power wires should not exceed what percentage of the regulated voltage?
questions.expl: AC 43.13-1B limits main power-wire voltage drop to 2 percent of the regulated voltage.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Which conductor material has higher conductivity but is heavier and more expensive?
questions.expl: Copper has higher conductivity than aluminum but is heavier and more costly; aluminum has only ~60% of copper's conductivity.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A coaxial aircraft wire consists of:
options: One center conductor surrounded by a metallic braided shield
One insulated wire in a jacket
questions.expl: Coax is one center conductor surrounded by a metallic braided shield, used for data and RF signal distribution.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Twisted pair aircraft wire is typically used for:
options: Onboard communications and low-level signal circuits
questions.expl: Twisted pair (two separately insulated wires twisted together) is used for onboard communications and circuits such as the standby compass light.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Per AC 43.13-1B, aircraft wire must contain at least how many strands (no wire with fewer may be used)?
questions.expl: Wire containing fewer than 19 strands must not be used; stranding provides flexibility and vibration resistance.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: The golden rule for routing signal wiring relative to power wiring is:
options: Keep signal wiring away from power wiring
Always run them in the same connector
questions.expl: Keep sensitive signal wiring away from power wiring; EMI increases with parallel-run length and decreases with separation.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Wires of redundant aircraft systems should be:
options: Routed in the same bundle for neatness
Routed in separate bundles and separate connectors
Grounded to a single common point
questions.expl: Redundant systems are routed in separate bundles and connectors so a single fault cannot disable multiple systems.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Wire bundles are physically supported along their length by:
questions.expl: Clamps support wire bundles at intervals to prevent sag, chafing, and vibration damage.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: BONDING differs from grounding in that bonding primarily:
options: Provides a low-impedance path to keep metal parts at the same potential
questions.expl: Bonding provides a low-impedance path connecting metal parts so they share the same potential; it usually does not carry working current.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Per AC 43.13-1B, inadequate bonding or grounding can cause all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Reduced fuel flow to the engine
Personnel shock hazard or lightning-strike damage
questions.expl: Bad bonding/grounding causes EMI, ESD damage, shock hazard, and lightning damage — it is an electrical, not a fuel, concern.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A bonding jumper connection's resistance should not exceed:
questions.expl: Bonding jumpers must be as short as practicable, with each connection's resistance not exceeding .003 ohm.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Shielding is best described as:
options: Applying a metallic covering to wiring to eliminate interference from stray electromagnetic energy
questions.expl: Shielding applies a metallic covering to wiring/equipment to eliminate interference caused by stray electromagnetic energy.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: For shielding to actually work, the shield must be:
options: Connected to ground (typically at both ends or at connectors)
questions.expl: A shield only suppresses EMI when it is grounded; shielded wire is typically grounded at both ends or at connectors.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: The most important rule when STRIPPING insulation from aircraft wire is:
options: Avoid nicking, cutting, or breaking the conductor strands
Strip as much insulation as possible
Heat the insulation with a torch
questions.expl: Strip with sharp, size-adjusted tools so you avoid nicking, cutting, or breaking strands; remove no more insulation than necessary.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A properly made crimp connection is described as:
questions.expl: A correct crimp squeezes the barrel onto the conductor to form a gas-tight joint that resists corrosion.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Per AC 43.13-1B, crimp terminal lugs and splices must be installed using:
options: A high-quality, calibrated ratchet-type crimping tool sized to the wire
questions.expl: Crimps require a high-quality, calibrated, ratchet-type crimp tool with the correct positioner/adjustment for the wire size.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: To verify a crimp's dimensions are correct, a technician uses a:
questions.expl: Suitable Go/No-Go gauges are used to confirm crimp dimensions before and during crimping operations.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A 'cold' solder joint is one that:
options: Was not heated enough, so solder did not bond, leaving a dull joint that cracks open
Is shiny and smooth with good wetting
questions.expl: A cold joint forms when the metal isn't hot enough; the dull, grainy joint never truly bonds and later cracks into an intermittent open.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: For most aircraft wire terminations, the generally preferred method over soldering is:
questions.expl: A proper crimp is gas-tight, repeatable, and verifiable with a gauge, so crimping is generally preferred; solder is used where specified.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Pre-insulated crimp-type terminals on a stud: how many terminal lugs (max) may be placed on one stud?
questions.expl: No more than four terminal lugs (or three lugs plus a bus bar) should be connected to any one stud.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A continuity check with an ohmmeter is used to:
options: Confirm a complete path and identify open/shorted conductors, good/bad fuses, and switch operation
questions.expl: A continuity check traces circuit paths to verify current can flow and identifies opens/shorts, good vs. bad fuses, and switch operation.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: The Study Guide calls which instrument the 'most versatile electrical measuring instrument used by aircraft technicians'?
questions.expl: The multimeter is the most versatile meter; it can be set as a voltmeter, ammeter, or ohmmeter to read voltage, current, and resistance.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A voltmeter is connected to a circuit:
questions.expl: A voltmeter is connected in parallel (across) the component, with power on.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment
AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An ammeter is connected to a circuit:
options: In series by breaking the circuit and inserting the meter
questions.expl: An ammeter is connected in series with the element through which current flows, by opening the circuit and inserting the meter.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment
AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Why must you NEVER connect an ammeter across a voltage source?
options: Its very low resistance allows huge current that damages the meter
questions.expl: An ammeter has extremely low resistance; placed across a source, even a small voltage drives a very high current that destroys it.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: When using an OHMMETER to check a component, the circuit must be:
options: De-energized (power off) with the component isolated
questions.expl: An ohmmeter supplies its own power, so the circuit must be de-energized and the component disconnected to avoid damage and false readings.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: On an analog meter, the most accurate readings are taken:
questions.expl: The most accurate analog readings occur at about half-scale (mid-scale) deflection; start high and work down to reach it.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: An oscilloscope is different from a DMM because it primarily:
options: Displays the waveform — voltage versus time — so you can see the signal's shape
Supplies test power to a circuit
questions.expl: A scope displays voltage over time (the waveform), revealing amplitude, period/frequency, and phase — a DMM gives only one number.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: The Study Guide says an oscilloscope measures voltage as which value, plus time to find frequency?
questions.expl: Oscilloscopes measure voltage (AC peak-to-peak) and time to determine frequencies, and show phase relationships.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A function (signal) generator is used to:
options: Produce a known test signal (sine, square, sawtooth) to inject into a circuit
questions.expl: A signal/function generator produces a known waveform at a chosen frequency and amplitude to drive a circuit under test.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: The megger (megohmmeter) is specifically used to measure:
options: Insulation resistance and other very high resistances using a high test voltage
questions.expl: A megger is a high-range ohmmeter with a hand-cranked generator; it measures insulation resistance with a high 'breakdown' voltage.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: What gives the megger its advantage over an ordinary ohmmeter?
options: It applies a high potential (breakdown voltage) to test insulation under stress
It is connected in series with the load
questions.expl: The megger measures resistance with a high 'breakdown' voltage, proving insulation won't leak or short under electrical stress.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: To measure the very low resistance of a bonding/grounding connection, AC 43.13-1B recommends:
options: A bonding tester or millivolt-drop test (high-quality low-resistance instrument)
questions.expl: Bond resistances are in milliohms; AC 43.13-1B recommends a bonding tester (or millivolt-drop test) to measure them accurately.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Per both the Study Guide and AC 43.13-1B, before performing continuity checks or using test equipment, you must ensure the equipment is:
questions.expl: The Study Guide and AC 43.13-1B both stress ensuring test equipment and tools are calibrated before use.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Compared to an analog multimeter, a digital multimeter (DMM) generally offers:
options: Greater accuracy, easier reading, and higher input resistance (less loading)
questions.expl: DMMs are easier to read, more accurate, and have higher input resistance, so they load the circuit less than analog meters.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: A continuity tester (or ohmmeter) shows a GOOD, complete connection as:
questions.expl: A good continuous path reads near-zero resistance; an open path reads infinity (very high resistance).
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: When choosing the right meter: to see a signal's SHAPE and timing you use a scope; to test wire INSULATION you use a:
questions.expl: Match the tool to the job: oscilloscope for waveforms, megger for insulation resistance, DMM for V/A/ohms, bonding tester for bonds.
sources: AET Academy M11 — Wiring & Test Equipment

questions.q: Motion about the LONGITUDINAL axis is called what, and which surface controls it?
questions.expl: The longitudinal axis runs nose-to-tail; motion about it is roll, commanded by the ailerons.

questions.q: Motion about the LATERAL axis (wingtip to wingtip) is called what, and which surface controls it?
questions.expl: The lateral axis runs wingtip-to-wingtip; motion about it is pitch, commanded by the elevator.

questions.q: Motion about the VERTICAL axis is called what, and which surface controls it?
questions.expl: The vertical axis runs top-to-bottom; motion about it is yaw, commanded by the rudder.

questions.q: Which control surface is operated by the rudder PEDALS?
questions.expl: The rudder is operated by foot pedals; right pedal yaws the nose right.

questions.q: Flaps are SECONDARY flight controls that primarily:
options: Increase lift and drag for takeoff and landing
questions.expl: Flaps lower to increase lift, drag, and effective wing area for slower takeoff and landing.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a trim tab?
options: To reduce pilot effort by holding a primary control in position
To act as the main lift device
questions.expl: Trim tabs on a control's trailing edge aerodynamically hold the control, reducing pilot effort and workload.

questions.q: The central body of the aircraft, aft of the firewall and forward of the empennage, is the:
questions.expl: The fuselage is the central body containing the cockpit/cabin, passengers, cargo, and most avionics.

questions.q: The empennage is another name for the aircraft's:
questions.expl: The empennage is the tail: it holds the vertical fin (rudder) and horizontal stabilizer (elevator).

questions.q: In a wing, the main spanwise member that carries the flight and landing loads is the:
questions.expl: The spar is the principal spanwise beam of the wing; it carries the flight and landing loads.

questions.q: Which fuselage construction is described as the MOST COMMON type?
questions.expl: Semimonocoque, which derives strength from internal members plus the skin, is the most common fuselage type.

questions.q: A semimonocoque fuselage derives its strength from internal parts such as:
options: Bulkheads, longerons, keel beams, and drag struts
questions.expl: Semimonocoque combines the stressed skin with internal members: bulkheads, longerons, keel beams, and drag struts.

questions.q: For an avionics tech, a 'Fuselage Station (FS)' identifies a location's:
questions.expl: FS gives the fore-and-aft position in inches aft of the datum, pinning a component along the fuselage length.

questions.q: Why does an avionics technician need to understand aircraft zones and stations?
options: To locate equipment, antennas, wiring, and access panels in the manual
questions.expl: Stations give exact coordinates and zones give general regions, so the tech can find boxes, antennas, wiring, and panels.

questions.q: During the avionics preflight, antennas must be inspected for security, missing fasteners, skin cracks, deteriorated seals, and:
questions.expl: Paint over an antenna degrades its performance; the Study Guide lists it as a required antenna inspection item.

questions.q: Around a running TURBINE (jet) engine, the area in front of the inlet must be kept clear because the inlet can:
options: Suck in people, debris, or property (FOD)
questions.expl: A turbine inlet creates strong suction that can ingest personnel, equipment, and FOD; keep it clear.

questions.q: The hazard directly BEHIND a running turbine engine is:
options: A blast of hot, high-velocity exhaust gas
questions.expl: Turbine exhaust is a high-velocity, high-temperature blast that can burn, knock down personnel, and throw debris.

questions.q: Why should you treat any propeller as if it could turn at any moment?
options: A hot magneto can fire and start a 'dead' engine
questions.expl: A faulty/hot magneto can ignite the engine even when it appears off, so every prop is treated as live.

questions.q: Wheel chocks are correctly placed:
options: Fore and aft of the main-gear wheels
questions.short: Fore and aft of main wheels
questions.expl: Chocks go in front of and behind the main-gear wheels to keep the aircraft from rolling.

questions.q: During towing, the qualified person seated in the cockpit is there to:
options: Operate the brakes only in an emergency, e.g. if the tow bar fails
questions.expl: That person is on the brakes for emergencies (tow bar failure); they do NOT steer the aircraft.

questions.q: During a tow, aircraft brakes should be applied:
options: Only on an emergency command from a ground tow-team member
questions.expl: Brakes are applied only on an emergency command from a tow-team member on the ground.

questions.q: When jacking an aircraft, you must lift only at the:
questions.expl: Jack only at the approved jack points, on level ground, with jacks of adequate capacity and safety locks engaged.

questions.q: When mooring (tying down) a parked aircraft, you should head it:
options: Toward the hangar regardless of wind
questions.expl: Head the aircraft into the prevailing/forecast wind and tie down at the proper tie-down rings.

questions.q: Tie-down rope should never be tied to a:
questions.expl: Tying to a lift strut can bend it if the rope slips; use the provided tie-down rings/loops. Use anti-slip knots like the bowline.

questions.q: Before fuel begins to flow, the single most important electrical safety step is to:
options: Turn on the radar to monitor
Bond and ground the aircraft and fueling unit
Disconnect the battery and discard it
questions.expl: Bonding/grounding equalizes potential so static electricity cannot spark and ignite fuel vapor.

questions.q: Which item must NEVER be used as a grounding point when fueling?
options: A wing tie-down loop with bare metal
questions.expl: Never ground to the propeller or exhaust; use bare metal or proper grounding points.

questions.q: AVGAS and Jet A fuel must be:
questions.expl: Mixing the wrong fuel can cause power loss, detonation, or engine damage; never mix AVGAS and jet fuel.

questions.q: FOD stands for foreign object damage/debris. A key FOD-prevention practice is:
options: Carrying spare hardware in your shirt pocket
Keeping the area clear and accounting for all tools/items
questions.short: Keep area clear, account for tools
questions.expl: Pick up loose items, keep jet inlets clear, and inventory tools so nothing is ingested or blown into people.

questions.q: A red 'REMOVE BEFORE FLIGHT' streamer is attached to items such as pitot covers and gear pins because:
options: Leaving them on can make systems inoperative and cause accidents
questions.expl: RBF streamers warn personnel and flag items that, if left on, render systems inoperative (e.g. covered pitot = no airspeed).

questions.q: When working on an energized electrical system, you should first:
options: Increase the voltage to test it
Pull and red-tag the breaker / open and tag the disconnect
questions.expl: De-energize, then red-tag/placard so no one re-energizes the system; residual charge can remain in caps and coils.

questions.q: A Class C fire involves:
questions.expl: Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment and wiring. (A fire needs the triangle of fuel + heat + oxygen.)
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Which extinguishing agent must you AVOID on an energized (Class C) electrical fire?
questions.expl: Plain water conducts electricity; use a non-conductive agent like CO2 or Halon on energized electrical fires.

questions.q: A Class D fire (burning metal such as magnesium) is best fought with:
questions.expl: Dry powder is used on Class D metal fires; water or CO2 cooling causes explosive expansion of the hot metal.

questions.q: During engine start, the fireguard must stand by with a CO2 extinguisher of at least:
questions.expl: A fireguard familiar with starting procedures stands by with a CO2 extinguisher of at least 5-pound capacity.

questions.q: Bernoulli's Principle, the main source of wing lift, states that as a fluid's velocity increases, its pressure:
questions.expl: Faster airflow over the cambered top of the wing lowers pressure there, producing lift. A stabilator is a one-piece horizontal stabilizer-plus-elevator.

questions.q: A 24 V source is across a 6 ohm resistor. What is the current?
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Which formula gives power?
questions.expl: Watt's law: power equals current times voltage (P = I x E).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: 2 mA equals how many amps?
questions.expl: Milli means times 0.001, so 2 mA = 0.002 A. Watch your prefixes.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: In a SERIES circuit, the current is:
questions.expl: A series circuit has one path, so the same current flows through every component.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: In a PARALLEL circuit, the voltage across each branch is:
questions.expl: All parallel branches share the same two nodes, so they see the same voltage.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: In the resistor color code, the digit for GREEN is:
questions.expl: Black0 Brown1 Red2 Orange3 Yellow4 Green5 Blue6 Violet7 Gray8 White9.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A GOLD tolerance band means:
questions.expl: Gold = +/-5%, Silver = +/-10%, no band = +/-20%.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An open resistor in a series string causes:
questions.expl: An open breaks the only path, so series current stops.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: In 'ELI the ICE man,' for an inductor (L):
questions.expl: ELI: in an inductor, E (voltage) leads I (current).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: In 'ELI the ICE man,' for a capacitor (C):
questions.expl: ICE: in a capacitor, I (current) leads E (voltage).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: The unit of capacitance is the:
questions.expl: Capacitance is measured in farads; inductance in henries.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A transformer with a primary:secondary turns ratio of 1:3 fed 10 V AC outputs about:
questions.expl: Voltage scales with turns ratio: 10 V x 3 = 30 V.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A silicon diode needs about how much forward voltage to conduct?
questions.expl: A silicon diode forward-biases at roughly 0.7 VDC.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An NPN transistor's three terminals are:
questions.expl: A bipolar junction transistor has an emitter, base, and collector.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A small base current controlling a large collector current is what transistor action?
questions.expl: A transistor amplifies: a small base signal controls a larger collector current.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: The filter capacitor in a power supply is connected:
questions.expl: A filter capacitor sits in parallel with the load to bypass ripple.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Which component is the classic voltage regulator?
questions.expl: A Zener diode regulates by holding a constant reverse-breakdown voltage.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An oscillator's job is to:
questions.expl: An oscillator produces a continuous repeating output signal.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A limiter (clipper) circuit:
options: Removes part of a waveform above/below a set level
questions.expl: A limiter clips off the portion of a waveform beyond a set amplitude.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: For oscillation to be sustained, a circuit needs gain plus:
questions.expl: Oscillators use positive feedback to sustain a continuous output.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An AND gate outputs a 1 only when:
questions.expl: AND outputs 1 only when every input is 1.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An OR gate outputs a 1 when:
questions.expl: OR outputs 1 if at least one input is 1.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A NOT gate (inverter):
options: Outputs the opposite of its input
questions.expl: A NOT gate outputs the logical opposite of its single input.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: The decimal number 5 in binary is:
questions.expl: 5 = 4 + 1 = 101 in binary.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: An XOR gate outputs a 1 when:
questions.expl: XOR (exclusive OR) outputs 1 only when its inputs differ.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Hexadecimal is base:
questions.expl: Hexadecimal is base 16 (digits 0-9 and A-F).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Which fire class is FLAMMABLE LIQUIDS?
questions.expl: Class B fires involve flammable liquids like fuel and solvents.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: When handling an electrostatic-sensitive device, you should:
options: Store it loose in a pocket
Wear a grounded wrist strap at a grounded ESD bench
questions.expl: ESD parts require a grounded wrist strap, ESD bench, and anti-static bags.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: FOD stands for:
questions.expl: FOD = Foreign Object Debris (and the Damage it causes).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A safety wire (lock wire) is installed so that it:
options: Tends to tighten the fastener / prevents loosening
questions.expl: Safety wire is routed to resist loosening of the secured fastener.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: The authoritative source for a maintenance procedure is the:
questions.expl: Always use the current manufacturer technical publication for procedures.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Dissimilar metals in contact with an electrolyte cause:
questions.expl: Dissimilar metals plus an electrolyte create galvanic (dissimilar-metal) corrosion.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A torque wrench is used to:
questions.expl: A torque wrench applies a precise, specified tightening force.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: In American Wire Gauge (AWG), a SMALLER gauge number means the wire is:
questions.expl: Smaller AWG number = larger diameter = more current capacity.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Bonding (electrical bonding) primarily:
options: Ties metal parts to a common potential to control static, lightning, and EMI
questions.expl: Bonding joins metal parts to one potential, controlling static, lightning, and EMI.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A wire crimp terminal must be:
options: Sized for the wire gauge and crimped with the proper tool
Larger than the wire by two sizes
questions.expl: Crimp terminals must match the wire gauge and be crimped with the correct tool.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: A semi-monocoque structure carries loads through:
options: A combination of skin and internal frames/stringers
questions.expl: Semi-monocoque shares loads between the skin and internal frames/stringers.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Before working near aircraft, you should always:
options: Follow ground-handling and safety procedures (chocks, grounding, hazards)
questions.expl: Aircraft handling requires following ground-safety procedures and respecting hazard zones.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: To pass the 90-question AET at 73%, you must answer at least how many correctly?
questions.expl: 90 x 0.73 = 65.7, so you need 66 correct (may miss up to 24).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: Which credential is required BEFORE you can take any avionics endorsement (AEIT, ANS, DNS, OCS, RCS)?
questions.expl: Each endorsement requires passing the AET first.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: The DNS endorsement covers navigation systems that:
options: Depend on external/ground signals (VOR, ILS, DME)
questions.expl: DNS = Dependent Navigation Systems, which rely on ground-based aids.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: AET plus one avionics endorsement satisfies the formal-training prong of which regulation toward an FAA Repairman Certificate?
questions.expl: AET + an endorsement meets the formal-training prong of 14 CFR 65.101(a)(5)(ii).
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: If you pass the AET but your employer wants the CAET, you can:
options: Take a ~$49 upgrade exam (50 q, 80% pass)
Do nothing; they are identical names
questions.expl: A ~$49 upgrade exam (50 q, 80%) converts AET to CAET.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements

questions.q: During the exam, the smartest move on a question that stalls you for over 90 seconds is to:
options: Guess, flag it, and return at the end
questions.expl: Guess your best, flag it, move on, and revisit; never leave a blank.
sources: AET Academy M13 — Exam Prep & Endorsements
